The compressive force on the disc is the resultant of the axial spine force and the muscle-induced axial load:

$$F_{\text{disc}} = W + F_m = 250 + 750 = 1000 \text{ N (axial only)}$$

Including the pre-existing 600 N compressive load:

$$F_{\text{total}} = 1000 + 600 = 1600 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Moment about L5-S1: $M = 37.5$ N·m
- Erector spinae force: $F_m = 750$ N
- Compressive force on disc: $F_{\text{disc}} = 1600$ N **[Total: 14 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Equilibrium Problems (Module 1):**
> 
> 1. **Skipping the FBD:** Examiners specifically check for a properly drawn free body diagram. Skipping it costs **at least 2–3 marks** even if the final numerical answer is correct.
> 
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors:** Mixing clockwise and counter-clockwise signs inconsistently. KTU expects **CCW = positive** as the standard convention. Reversing this midway through a solution leads to wrong signs cascading through all subsequent equations.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Lever Arm:** Using the **distance along the segment** instead of the **perpendicular distance to the line of action of the force**. For inclined forces, the lever arm is $d = r \sin\theta$, not simply $r$.
> 
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m within the same equation. Always convert to SI units (m) **before** substituting into equations.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting Body Weight:** When asked to find the force on a limb holding a load, many students compute only the load and forget the segment's own weight acting at its CG.
> 
> 6. **Choosing a Poor Reference Point:** Taking moments about an arbitrary point instead of strategically at a joint where two unknowns act → produces a moment equation with two unknowns that cannot be solved directly.  
> 
> 7. **Omitting Direction of Joint Reaction:** KTU expects the **direction** of the joint reaction (angle or qualifier like "vertically downward") in addition to magnitude. Reporting only magnitude may cost 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Equilibrium of Individual Body Components**

- ☐ Equilibrium of a body segment requires **two conditions**: $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ and $\sum \vec{M}_O = 0$.
- ☐ In 2D, this gives **3 scalar equations**: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$.
- ☐ In 3D, this gives **6 scalar equations**.
- ☐ A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is **mandatory** as the first step in every problem.
- ☐ All forces (body weight, muscle, joint, external load) must be shown at the correct point of application.
- ☐ Standard sign convention: **CCW positive, CW negative**.
- ☐ **Varignon's Theorem** allows decomposition of a force into components for easier moment calculation.
- ☐ **Lami's Theorem** applies when **3 concurrent coplanar forces** are in equilibrium: $\dfrac{F_1}{\sin \alpha} = \dfrac{F_2}{\sin \beta} = \dfrac{F_3}{\sin \gamma}$.
- ☐ **Choose the moment reference point at a joint** where the maximum number of unknowns act — this eliminates them from the moment equation.
- ☐ The moment of a force is $M_O = F \cdot d$, where $d$ is the **perpendicular** lever arm.
- ☐ The moment of a couple is **independent of the reference point**: $M = F \cdot d$.
- ☐ $W = mg$ with $g = 9.81$ m/s² (textbook may use 9.8 or 10 m/s²).
- ☐ Joint reaction magnitude: $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$; direction: $\phi = \tan^{-1}(R_y / R_x)$.
- ☐ Biceps-like muscles operate with **large mechanical disadvantage** — they must produce forces 5–10× the held load.
- ☐ The elbow and knee joints typically experience large compressive joint reaction forces during static holds.
- ☐ Equilibrium of individual components is a building block for **whole-body equilibrium** in later modules (e.g., gait, posture, lifting).

> [!TIP]  
> **Final Exam Tip:** In the KTU ESE, always:
> 1. State the governing equations first.
> 2. Draw a clean, fully-labelled FBD.
> 3. State the chosen reference point and justify it.
> 4. Show all substitutions in SI units.
> 5. Report magnitude **and** direction for any reaction force.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic and Kinetic Concepts for Analyzing Human Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic and Kinetic Concepts for Analyzing Human Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematics** is the branch of *classical mechanics* that describes the geometry of motion of a body in terms of **position, velocity, and acceleration** with respect to time, **without reference to the forces** that produce that motion. In biomechanics, kinematics describes *how* the body segments move.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinetics** is the branch of *classical mechanics* that analyzes the relationship between the **forces acting on a body** and the **motion** that results. In biomechanics, kinetics answers the question *why* the body segments move the way they do.

In the KTU 2024 framework, human motion analysis is performed using these two complementary pillars:

- **Kinematics** $\rightarrow$ *Descriptive* (what the body does)
- **Kinetics** $\rightarrow$ *Explanatory* (what causes the body to do it)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Football Throw:**
> - When you watch a cricket ball thrown by a bowler, you observe its **path** (curved), **speed** (fast/slow), and **landing point**. That description is **kinematics**.
> - When an engineer calculates the **force** the bowler applied through the shoulder, the **torque** at the elbow, and the **gravity** pulling the ball down, that explanation is **kinetics**.
> - Kinematics is the *movie*; Kinetics is the *physics engine* that rendered the movie.

### 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in Engineering

| Aspect | Kinematics | Kinetics |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | *How* does it move? | *Why* does it move? |
| **Variables Studied** | Position, velocity, acceleration | Forces, moments, energy |
| **Engineering Use** | Gait analysis, motion capture | Prosthetic design, orthotics, sports injury prevention |
| **Standard Tools** | High-speed cameras, IMU sensors | Force plates, EMG, inverse dynamics |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A motion that is kinematically correct (looks right) can still be **kinetically wrong** (injury-causing). This is the foundation of ergonomic and rehabilitation engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Logical Steps in Analyzing Human Motion

**Step 1 — Establish a Reference Frame**
- Every kinematic/kinetic description is meaningless without a **coordinate system**.
- In biomechanics, three standard frames are used: **Global (Lab) frame**, **Segmental (Local) frame**, and **Joint frame**.
- Choice of origin and axes is critical: typically, the **right-hand rule** Cartesian system with **X (medio-lateral), Y (antero-posterior), Z (longitudinal/vertical)**.

**Step 2 — Define Position and Displacement**
- **Position vector** $\vec{r}(t)$ locates a body point in space.
- **Displacement** $\Delta \vec{r} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1$ is a *vector* (not the same as distance, which is a scalar).

**Step 3 — Differentiate to Get Velocity and Acceleration**
- Velocity is the time-rate of change of position.
- Acceleration is the time-rate of change of velocity (or the second derivative of position).

**Step 4 — For Rotational Segments, Use Angular Analogues**
- Joint motion is rotational. We replace linear quantities with **angular** ones: $\theta$ (rad), $\omega$ (rad/s), $\alpha$ (rad/s²).

**Step 5 — For Kinetics, Apply Newton's Laws**
- Net force = mass × acceleration (translational).
- Net moment = moment of inertia × angular acceleration (rotational).

**Step 6 — Quantify Energy and Power for Performance Analysis**
- Work done by muscles, kinetic and potential energy of segments, mechanical power output.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why a reference frame?** Because the same arm raise can be "moving forward" in one frame and "stationary" in another (relative motion principle).
- **Why vectors and not just numbers?** Human joints rotate in 3D — direction matters as much as magnitude (e.g., ACL injury depends on *direction* of knee force).
- **Why energy?** Energy is conserved (in closed systems), letting us equate muscle work to changes in body segment KE + PE.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference for Module 1**

| Domain | Quantity | Formula | Units | Vector Form |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | Position | $s$ or $\vec{r}$ | m (metres) | $\vec{r}(t)$ |
| Linear | Displacement | $\Delta s = s_f - s_i$ | m | $\Delta \vec{r}$ |
| Linear | Velocity | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | m/s | $\vec{v} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$ |
| Linear | Acceleration | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | m/s² | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$ |
| Angular | Angular Displacement | $\theta$ | rad | $\vec{\theta}$ |
| Angular | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | rad/s | $\vec{\omega}$ |
| Angular | Angular Acceleration | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | rad/s² | $\vec{\alpha}$ |
| Kinetics | Newton's 2nd Law | $F = m \cdot a$ | N | $\sum \vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ |
| Kinetics | Moment / Torque | $M = F \cdot d$ | N·m | $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ |
| Kinetics | Rotational Equivalent | $M = I \cdot \alpha$ | N·m | $\sum \vec{M} = I\vec{\alpha}$ |
| Energy | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | scalar |
| Energy | Potential Energy | $PE = m g h$ | J | scalar |
| Energy | Work Done | $W = F \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta$ | J | scalar |
| Power | Mechanical Power | $P = F \cdot v$ | W | scalar |
| Circular | Centripetal Acc. | $a_c = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$ | m/s² | directed toward center |
| Circular | Tangential Acc. | $a_t = r \alpha$ | m/s² | along motion path |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants Used:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ (or $9.8 \; m/s^2$ for exam simplification).
> - 1 radian $= \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 57.3^\circ$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Inverse dynamics uses kinetic formulas to compute joint forces from measured accelerations.
- **Sports Engineering:** Power output formula helps optimize bat/club swing speed.
- **Ergonomics:** Lever-arm calculations using $\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ determine spinal loading during lifting.
- **Prosthetic Design:** Moment of inertia (I) of artificial limbs must match the biological limb to prevent gait asymmetry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Linear and Angular Quantities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Show that for a particle moving in a circle of radius $r$, the linear velocity $v$ and linear acceleration $a_t$ are related to the angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$.

**Starting point:** Consider a rigid link of length $r$ rotating about a fixed point O. Let the arc length swept be $s$.

By the definition of angle in radians:

$$
s = r \theta
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to time** to find the velocity relationship.

$$
\frac{ds}{dt} = r \frac{d\theta}{dt}
$$

Since $\frac{ds}{dt} = v$ (linear velocity) and $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$ (angular velocity):

$$
v = r \omega
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate again** to find the tangential acceleration.

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} = r \frac{d\omega}{dt}
$$

Since $\frac{dv}{dt} = a_t$ (tangential acceleration) and $\frac{d\omega}{dt} = \alpha$ (angular acceleration):

$$
a_t = r \alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Combine with centripetal acceleration.**

For a body moving along a circular path, the total linear acceleration has two orthogonal components: the tangential component $a_t$ along the motion direction, and the centripetal component $a_c$ directed toward the center.

$$
a_c = \frac{v^2}{r} = r \omega^2
$$

The magnitude of total acceleration is:

$$
a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_c^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In human walking, the foot moves in a complex curve. The ankle joint must provide *both* tangential force (to swing the foot forward) *and* centripetal force (to keep the foot on its arc). This is why ankle sprains happen during sudden direction changes — the centripetal demand spikes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Equation of Motion Under Constant Acceleration (Projectile Analogy for Human Center of Mass)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the displacement-time relation for uniformly accelerated motion — the foundation of all biomechanical motion equations.

**Given:** Initial position $s_0$, initial velocity $u$, constant acceleration $a$, and time $t$.

**Step 1 — Definition of acceleration:**

$$
a = \frac{v - u}{t}
$$

Rearranging to express final velocity:

$$
v = u + a t
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of average velocity** (for constant acceleration, average velocity is the arithmetic mean of initial and final velocities):

$$
v_{avg} = \frac{u + v}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** the expression for $v$ from Step 1 into the average velocity equation:

$$
v_{avg} = \frac{u + (u + a t)}{2} = \frac{2u + a t}{2} = u + \frac{1}{2} a t
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the kinematic definition** of displacement as the product of average velocity and time:

$$
s = v_{avg} \cdot t
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute** the expression for $v_{avg}$ from Step 3:

$$
s = \left( u + \frac{1}{2} a t \right) t
$$

Expanding:

$$
s = u t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2
$$

**Step 6 — Eliminating time** using $v = u + a t \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{v - u}{a}$, we get the velocity-displacement relation:

$$
v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The three kinematic equations of uniformly accelerated motion (SUVAT form) are:**
> 1. $v = u + a t$
> 2. $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$
> 3. $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$

**Engineering Application in Biomechanics:** When a sprinter pushes off the starting block, the foot's motion during the brief ground contact is approximately uniformly accelerated. Coaches use these equations to estimate the *block clearance velocity* required to win a 100 m race.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Kinetic Energy from Work-Energy Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the expression for translational kinetic energy of a body of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$.

**Step 1 — Work done by a net force** on a body moving through a small displacement $ds$ is:

$$
dW = F \, ds
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Newton's 2nd Law** $F = m a$:

$$
dW = m a \, ds
$$

**Step 3 — Use the kinematic identity** $a \, ds = v \, dv$ (derived from $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ and $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$):

$$
dW = m v \, dv
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate** from initial velocity $u$ to final velocity $v$:

$$
W = \int_{u}^{v} m v \, dv = m \left[ \frac{v^2}{2} \right]_{u}^{v}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limits:**

$$
W = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 - \frac{1}{2} m u^2
$$

**Step 6 — Define kinetic energy** as the work done to accelerate the body from rest to velocity $v$:

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

Therefore, the **Work-Energy Theorem** states:

$$
W_{net} = \Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomechanics Application:** When a gymnast lands from a vault, the kinetic energy at touchdown is absorbed by the knee extensors. A landing with $v = 6$ m/s and effective body mass $m = 60$ kg gives $KE = 0.5 \times 60 \times 36 = 1080$ J — enough to tear ligaments if absorbed over too short a time.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Concept

| Scenario | Primary Concept | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Measuring reach of a basketball player's arm | Kinematics (position) | $\Delta \vec{r}$ |
| Computing wrist speed during a tennis serve | Kinematics (velocity) | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ |
| Determining angular velocity of the knee during walking | Angular Kinematics | $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ |
| Calculating force on a patella tendon during squat | Kinetics (Newton's law) | $F = m a$ |
| Finding torque required at shoulder to hold a weight | Kinetics (Moment) | $M = F \cdot d$ |
| Estimating work done by calf muscle during push-off | Kinetics (Work) | $W = F \cdot d$ |
| Power output of a cyclist's leg | Kinetics (Power) | $P = F \cdot v$ |
| Energy stored in a stretched Achilles tendon | Kinetics (Elastic PE) | $PE = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: Human Motion Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OBSERVE HUMAN MOTION] --> B{ANALYSIS TYPE}
    B -- DESCRIBE --> C[KINEMATICS]
    B -- EXPLAIN --> D[KINETICS]
    C --> C1[POSITION]
    C --> C2[VELOCITY]
    C --> C3[ACCELERATION]
    C1 --> E1[DISPLACEMENT DELTA r]
    C2 --> E2[ANGULAR OMEGA]
    C3 --> E3[ANGULAR ALPHA]
    D --> D1[NEWTON LAWS]
    D --> D2[MOMENT EQUILIBRIUM]
    D --> D3[ENERGY METHODS]
    D1 --> F1[F equals m times a]
    D2 --> F2[M equals I times alpha]
    D3 --> F3[WORK ENERGY THEOREM]
    E1 --> G[BIOMECHANICAL OUTPUT]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[CLINICAL OR SPORTS DECISION]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Reference Frame Hierarchy in Biomechanics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RF1[GLOBAL LAB FRAME] --> RF2[SEGMENTAL LOCAL FRAME]
    RF2 --> RF3[JOINT FRAME]
    RF1 --> RF4[GROUND REACTION PLANE]
    RF3 --> RF5[ANGULAR KINEMATICS]
    RF4 --> RF6[FORCE VECTORS]
    RF5 --> OUT[INVERSE DYNAMICS OUTPUT]
    RF6 --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Force-Motion Relationship (Newtonian Coupling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MASS[MASS m] --> K1[INERTIA]
    ACC[ACCELERATION a] --> K1
    K1 --> F1[FORCE F equals m a]
    FORCE[F APPLIED] --> M1[MOMENT M equals r cross F]
    LEVER[LEVER ARM r] --> M1
    M1 --> OUT[JOINT TORQUE]
    F1 --> OUT
    OUT --> MVT[RESULTING MOTION]
```

### 4.4 Schematic of Vector Decomposition for Joint Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[NET VECTOR] --> V1[COMPONENT 1 PROJECTION]
    V --> V2[COMPONENT 2 PROJECTION]
    V1 --> APP[APPLIED TO MUSCLE LINE OF ACTION]
    V2 --> APP
    APP --> RES[RESOLVED JOINT FORCE]
    RES --> CLIN[CLINICAL INTERPRETATION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *logical transformation step*. The arrows indicate the **direction of data flow** — observation to analysis to clinical or engineering decision. This is the exact workflow used in a biomechanics gait laboratory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between Kinematics and Kinetics with one biomechanical example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Kinematics | Kinetics |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Study of motion *without* reference to force | Study of motion *with* reference to force |
| Variables | Position, velocity, acceleration | Force, moment, energy |
| Biomech Example | Measuring knee joint angle during walking using goniometer (3 marks) | Calculating the moment produced by the quadriceps at the knee during extension (3 marks) |

**Mark Split:** 1 mark for each difference, 1 mark for example.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the terms (a) angular velocity, and (b) centripetal acceleration. State their SI units.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(a) Angular velocity ($\omega$):** The rate of change of angular displacement with respect to time, defined as $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$. **SI unit:** rad/s.
- **(b) Centripetal acceleration ($a_c$):** The acceleration directed toward the center of a circular path, given by $a_c = \dfrac{v^2}{r} = r \omega^2$. **SI unit:** m/s². (1 mark each concept + 1 mark for units.)

---

### **PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A long jumper takes off with a horizontal velocity of $7.5$ m/s and a vertical velocity of $3.2$ m/s. Compute:
- (i) The magnitude of the resultant velocity at takeoff. (3 marks)
- (ii) The angle of projection of the resultant velocity with respect to the horizontal. (2 marks)
- (iii) The maximum height reached above the takeoff point. (2 marks)

**(b)** Define **torque** (moment of a force) and derive its rotational equation of motion $M = I\alpha$ from Newton's second law. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Resultant velocity magnitude:**

$$
v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt{7.5^2 + 3.2^2}
$$

$$
v = \sqrt{56.25 + 10.24} = \sqrt{66.49}
$$

$$
v \approx 8.15 \; m/s
$$

**(a) (ii) Angle of projection:**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v_y}{v_x}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3.2}{7.5}\right)
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.4267) \approx 23.1^\circ
$$

**(a) (iii) Maximum height:**

At maximum height, vertical velocity $v_y = 0$. Using $v^2 = u^2 - 2gh$:

$$
0 = 3.2^2 - 2 \times 9.81 \times h
$$

$$
h = \frac{3.2^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{10.24}{19.62}
$$

$$
h \approx 0.522 \; m
$$

---

**(b) Torque and derivation of $M = I\alpha$ (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Torque (or moment) is the rotational equivalent of force. It is defined as the cross product of the position vector $\vec{r}$ from the axis of rotation to the point of force application and the force vector $\vec{F}$:

$$
\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}
$$

The magnitude is $M = r F \sin\phi$ where $\phi$ is the angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$. **SI unit:** N·m.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis. Consider a small particle of mass $m_i$ at distance $r_i$ from the axis.

**Step 1 — Force on the particle** causes a tangential acceleration $a_{t,i} = r_i \alpha$ (same $\alpha$ for all particles in rigid body).

**Step 2 — Tangential force** required on that particle (Newton's 2nd law):

$$
F_{t,i} = m_i a_{t,i} = m_i r_i \alpha
$$

**Step 3 — Torque** about the axis from this tangential force:

$$
M_i = F_{t,i} \cdot r_i = m_i r_i^2 \alpha
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the torques** from all particles in the rigid body:

$$
M = \sum_i m_i r_i^2 \alpha = \left( \sum_i m_i r_i^2 \right) \alpha
$$

**Step 5 — Define moment of inertia** $I = \sum_i m_i r_i^2$ (or $I = \int r^2 \, dm$ for continuous body):

$$
M = I \alpha
$$

This is the rotational analogue of $F = m a$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice for Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a free body diagram, explain the **three Newton's Laws of Motion** as applicable to a human body in motion. Give one biomechanical example for each law. (7 marks)

**(b)** A body segment of mass $2.5$ kg moves with a velocity of $3$ m/s. It accelerates uniformly to $7$ m/s over a displacement of $4$ m. Calculate: (i) the work done, (ii) the force applied, and (iii) the power developed, if the time taken is $1.5$ s. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Newton's Three Laws in Biomechanics (7 marks):**

**1st Law (Law of Inertia) — 2 marks:**
A body at rest stays at rest, and a body in uniform motion stays in uniform motion, unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force.
*Biomechanical Example:* A football lying on the field remains stationary until a player kicks it. A runner's leg continues its swing momentarily after the hip muscles stop contracting (which is why we need eccentric muscle action to decelerate limbs).

**2nd Law (F = ma) — 3 marks:**
The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net external force applied.
*Mathematical Form:* $\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$
*Biomechanical Example:* During sprinting, a sprinter with mass 70 kg accelerating at $4$ m/s² must generate a ground reaction force of $F = 70 \times 4 = 280$ N (per leg).

**3rd Law (Action-Reaction) — 2 marks:**
For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.
*Biomechanical Example:* When you walk, your foot pushes the ground backward (action), and the ground pushes your foot forward (reaction). This ground reaction force is what propels you.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m = 2.5$ kg, $u = 3$ m/s, $v = 7$ m/s, $s = 4$ m, $t = 1.5$ s.

**(b)(i) Work Done (3 marks):**

Using the work-energy theorem:

$$
W = \Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2
$$

$$
W = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 7^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times 3^2
$$

$$
W = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 2.5 \times (49 - 9) = 1.25 \times 40
$$

$$
W = 50 \; J
$$

**(b)(ii) Force Applied (2 marks):**

$$
F = \frac{W}{s} = \frac{50}{4} = 12.5 \; N
$$

**(b)(iii) Power Developed (2 marks):**

Average power:

$$
P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{50}{1.5} \approx 33.33 \; W
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Misuse (–1 mark):** Students often write torque in "Joules" instead of N·m. Energy and torque have the *same dimensional units* but are *physically different*. Energy is a scalar, torque is a vector.
> 2. **Vector vs Scalar Confusion (–1 mark):** Distance is *not* displacement. Speed is *not* velocity. Examiners look for this distinction.
> 3. **Skipping Free Body Diagrams (–2 marks):** In any kinetics problem worth more than 5 marks, you **must** draw a labeled FBD with all force vectors clearly identified. Skipping this costs heavily.
> 4. **Sign Convention Errors in Moments (–1 mark):** Clockwise and counter-clockwise moments must be assigned consistent signs. Mixing them leads to a wrong direction of $\alpha$.
> 5. **Forgetting to Square in KE (–2 marks):** A classic error: writing $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv$ instead of $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$. Examiners will deduct.
> 6. **Neglecting g in PE Calculations (–1 mark):** Always use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- **Kinematics** = describes motion (position, velocity, acceleration) — *no forces involved*.
- **Kinetics** = explains motion using forces — *forces involved*.
- **Three fundamental kinematic variables** for linear motion: $s$, $v$, $a$ — linked by differentiation/integration with time.
- **Three fundamental kinematic variables** for angular motion: $\theta$, $\omega$, $\alpha$.
- **Bridge equations (linear ↔ angular):** $v = r\omega$ and $a_t = r\alpha$.
- **Newton's 2nd Law** has two forms:
  - Translational: $F = ma$
  - Rotational: $M = I\alpha$
- **Kinetic Energy:** $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ (translational); $KE_{rot} = \tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^2$ (rotational).
- **Work-Energy Theorem:** $W_{net} = \Delta KE$.
- **Power:** $P = F \cdot v$ (instantaneous) or $P = W/t$ (average).
- **SUVAT equations** apply only when acceleration is **constant**:
  - $v = u + at$
  - $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$
  - $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$
- **SI Units to memorize cold:** m, m/s, m/s², rad, rad/s, rad/s², N, N·m, J, W, kg.
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ (use $9.8$ in exams if it simplifies arithmetic).
- **Always draw a Free Body Diagram (FBD)** in any kinetics problem — it is the single biggest mark-saving habit in KTU exams.
- **Vectors matter:** displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, and torque are all *vectors*. Their directions are graded.
- **Biomechanics-specific reminder:** the human body is a *system of rigid links* (bones) connected by *pin joints*. This is why rotational dynamics ($M = I\alpha$) is more relevant than translational dynamics for joint analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Kinematics** is the branch of classical mechanics that describes the **motion of points, bodies (objects), and systems of bodies** without considering the forces that cause the motion. In the context of **Biomechanics**, kinematic concepts deal purely with the **geometric and time-based description** of human body segments, joints, and the overall body in motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBMT601 – Module 1):**
> *Kinematic Concepts* refers to the analytical framework used to describe motion in terms of **position, displacement, velocity, and acceleration** — both in **linear (translational)** and **angular (rotational)** domains — applied to anatomical segments and joints.

A **kinematic concept** is a measurable quantity that characterizes how a body or its segments move through space and time. The four foundational kinematic concepts are:

1. **Position** — *where* a point is located in space.
2. **Displacement** — the *change* in position.
3. **Velocity** — the *rate of change* of position (how fast and in which direction).
4. **Acceleration** — the *rate of change* of velocity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Bus Stop" Story:**
> Imagine you are sitting in a KSRTC bus travelling from Thiruvananthapuram to Kochi. The **bus stop number** (e.g., Aluva, Kalamassery) is your **position**. The **distance covered** from the previous stop to the current one is your **displacement** along the route. The **speed at which the bus is moving** (e.g., 60 km/h on NH66) is your **velocity**. The moment the driver **presses the accelerator or brake**, the change in that speed is your **acceleration**.
>
> In biomechanics, replace the "bus" with a **human forearm**, the "bus stops" with **joint angles** (e.g., elbow at 45°), the "speed" with **angular velocity** (rad/s), and the "acceleration pedal" with **muscle contraction force**. You have just translated transport kinematics into body kinematics!

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Position (s or x):** Measured in **metres (m)** for linear, **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)** for angular.
- **Displacement (Δs or Δx):** **metres (m)** or **radians (rad)**.
- **Linear Velocity (v):** **metres per second (m/s)**.
- **Angular Velocity (ω):** **radians per second (rad/s)**.
- **Linear Acceleration (a):** **metres per second squared (m/s²)**.
- **Angular Acceleration (α):** **radians per second squared (rad/s²)**.
- **Time (t):** **seconds (s)**.
- **Standard gravity on Earth (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (used in vertical kinematics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scalars vs. Vectors — A Critical Distinction:**
> *Distance* and *speed* are **scalars** (magnitude only). *Displacement* and *velocity* are **vectors** (magnitude + direction). Acceleration is **always a vector**. KTU examiners *frequently* test this distinction for 3-mark Part-A questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Theoretical Framework

The complete kinematic description of any human movement unfolds across **four hierarchical layers**:

**Layer 1 — Spatial Reference (Frame of Reference)**
- A **coordinate system** must be defined *before* any motion can be quantified.
- KTU biomechanics conventionally uses a **right-handed, 3-axis Cartesian frame**: X (medio-lateral), Y (antero-posterior), Z (vertical/longitudinal).
- For joint analysis, a **joint coordinate system (JCS)** following the **Grood & Suntay (1983)** convention is the gold standard.

**Layer 2 — Position and Displacement**
- **Position vector** $\vec{r}(t)$ locates a segment's centre of mass or a joint centre in 3D space.
- **Displacement** $\Delta \vec{r} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1$ is the *shortest straight-line vector* from initial to final position.
- **Path length** (a scalar) ≠ displacement (a vector) unless the path is a straight line.

**Layer 3 — Velocity (Linear and Angular)**
- **Linear velocity** $\vec{v} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$ — instantaneous rate of change of position.
- **Angular velocity** $\vec{\omega} = \frac{d\vec{\theta}}{dt}$ — instantaneous rate of change of angular position.
- **Average velocity** $\vec{v}_{avg} = \frac{\Delta \vec{r}}{\Delta t}$.
- For a rotating rigid body, every point has a **linear velocity** $\vec{v} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$.

**Layer 4 — Acceleration (Linear and Angular)**
- **Linear acceleration** $\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \frac{d^2\vec{r}}{dt^2}$.
- **Angular acceleration** $\vec{\alpha} = \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} = \frac{d^2\vec{\theta}}{dt^2}$.
- Acceleration has **two independent components**:
  - **Tangential acceleration** $a_t = r\alpha$ (changes the *speed*).
  - **Normal/centripetal acceleration** $a_n = \frac{v^2}{r} = r\omega^2$ (changes the *direction*).
- **Total acceleration magnitude:** $a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer

> [!NOTE]
> *Why is this layered approach important for a biomedical engineer?*
> - **Gait analysis** (Layer 1–2) is used to detect pathological walking patterns.
> - **Sports biomechanics** (Layer 3) optimizes sprint start, javelin release angles.
> - **Rehabilitation engineering** (Layer 4) measures joint accelerations to design assistive exoskeletons that respond to human motion.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield consolidated reference** for the KTU ESE. Memorize all entries; they appear almost every cycle.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Defining Equation | Type | SI Unit |
|---|----------|--------|-------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | Position (linear) | $\vec{r}$ | — | Vector | m |
| 2 | Position (angular) | $\theta$ | — | Scalar/Vector | rad |
| 3 | Displacement | $\Delta \vec{r}$ | $\vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1$ | Vector | m |
| 4 | Angular displacement | $\Delta \theta$ | $\theta_2 - \theta_1$ | Vector | rad |
| 5 | Linear velocity | $\vec{v}$ | $\dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$ | Vector | m/s |
| 6 | Angular velocity | $\vec{\omega}$ | $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Vector | rad/s |
| 7 | Linear acceleration | $\vec{a}$ | $\dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$ | Vector | m/s² |
| 8 | Angular acceleration | $\vec{\alpha}$ | $\dfrac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt}$ | Vector | rad/s² |
| 9 | Tangential acceleration | $a_t$ | $r\alpha$ | Vector | m/s² |
| 10 | Centripetal acceleration | $a_n$ | $\dfrac{v^2}{r} = \omega^2 r$ | Vector | m/s² |
| 11 | v–u relation | $v$ | $u + at$ | Scalar | m/s |
| 12 | Position relation (no t) | $s$ | $ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ | Scalar | m |
| 13 | Velocity–position relation | $v^2$ | $u^2 + 2as$ | Scalar | m²/s² |
| 14 | Angular v–ω relation | $\omega$ | $\omega_0 + \alpha t$ | Scalar | rad/s |
| 15 | Angular position | $\theta$ | $\omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^2$ | Scalar | rad |
| 16 | Relative velocity | $\vec{v}_{AB}$ | $\vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B$ | Vector | m/s |
| 17 | Period of rotation | $T$ | $\dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | Scalar | s |
| 18 | Frequency | $f$ | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Scalar | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Beyond the Exam Hall:**
> - In **prosthetic limb design**, $\omega$ and $\alpha$ of knee joints are measured to mimic natural gait.
> - In **crash-test dummy design** (used by ARAI, Pune), peak $a$ values dictate injury thresholds (e.g., HIC – Head Injury Criterion).
> - In **sports performance**, javelin release velocity $\vec{v}$ at angle $\theta$ follows projectile kinematics to maximize range.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Three Linear Kinematic Equations from First Principles

Let us derive the **three equations of uniformly accelerated motion** starting from the *definition of acceleration*. These are the **most-tested derivations** in KTU biomechanics and physics papers.

**Starting Premise:**
Acceleration is the time-derivative of velocity, and velocity is the time-derivative of position.

**Step 1 — Express acceleration as a constant:**
Assume the body (e.g., a centre of mass of a torso during a fall) experiences a *constant* acceleration $a$. Then:

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate with respect to time to get the first equation:**
Rearranging and integrating from $t = 0$ (where $v = u$) to time $t$ (where $v = v$):

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = \int_{0}^{t} a \, dt$$

Because $a$ is constant, it can be taken out of the integral:

$$v - u = a t$$

This gives the **first kinematic equation**:

$$v = u + a t$$

**Step 3 — Express velocity as the time-derivative of position:**
Velocity, by definition, is:

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$$

For a body under constant acceleration, the *average* velocity over the interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{u + v}{2}$$

(Logical basis: for a constant $a$, $v$ varies linearly with $t$, so its mean is the arithmetic mean of the endpoints.)

**Step 4 — Use average velocity to find displacement:**
Displacement is the integral of velocity:

$$s = \int_{0}^{t} v \, dt = v_{avg} \cdot t$$

Substituting $v_{avg} = \frac{u + v}{2}$ and $v = u + a t$:

$$s = \left(\frac{u + v}{2}\right) t = \left(\frac{u + (u + at)}{2}\right) t$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$s = \left(\frac{2u + at}{2}\right) t = ut + \frac{1}{2} a t^{2}$$

This gives the **second kinematic equation**:

$$s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate time to obtain the third equation:**
From Equation 1: $t = \dfrac{v - u}{a}$.
Substituting this into Equation 2:

$$s = u \left(\frac{v - u}{a}\right) + \frac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{v - u}{a}\right)^{2}$$

Expand the first term:

$$s = \frac{uv - u^{2}}{a} + \frac{1}{2} a \cdot \frac{(v - u)^{2}}{a^{2}}$$

The $a$ in the denominator of the second term cancels one power:

$$s = \frac{uv - u^{2}}{a} + \frac{(v - u)^{2}}{2a}$$

Multiply through by $2a$:

$$2 a s = 2(u v - u^{2}) + (v - u)^{2}$$

Expand $(v - u)^2 = v^2 - 2uv + u^2$:

$$2 a s = 2 u v - 2 u^{2} + v^{2} - 2 u v + u^{2}$$

The $2uv$ terms cancel:

$$2 a s = v^{2} - u^{2}$$

Rearranging, we obtain the **third kinematic equation**:

$$v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Angular ↔ Linear Correspondence

Every linear kinematic quantity has a direct angular analogue. KTU examiners love to give questions asking for the "angular equivalent" of a linear formula. The full mapping is:

| Linear Quantity | Linear Symbol | Angular Counterpart | Angular Symbol | Substitution Rule |
|-----------------|---------------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Displacement | $s$ | Angular displacement | $\theta$ | Replace $s \to \theta$ |
| Velocity | $v$ | Angular velocity | $\omega$ | Replace $v \to \omega$ |
| Acceleration | $a$ | Angular acceleration | $\alpha$ | Replace $a \to \alpha$ |
| Mass × accel. (force) | $F$ | Torque | $\tau$ | $F \to \tau$ |

Using this substitution rule, the **three angular kinematic equations** are obtained by inspection:

$$\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$$

$$\theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$$

$$\omega^{2} = \omega_0^{2} + 2 \alpha \theta$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Knee Joint During Squat

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
A subject begins a squat from rest. During the descent phase, the knee joint rotates through an angular displacement of $\theta = 1.2$ rad in a time of $t = 0.6$ s, starting from rest ($\omega_0 = 0$). Find (a) the average angular velocity, (b) the angular acceleration, and (c) the linear velocity of a point on the tibia $r = 0.25$ m from the knee centre.

**Part (a) — Average Angular Velocity:**

$$\omega_{avg} = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t} = \frac{1.2 \text{ rad}}{0.6 \text{ s}} = 2.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part (b) — Angular Acceleration (starting from rest):**

Using the angular kinematic equation $\theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$ with $\omega_0 = 0$:

$$1.2 = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha (0.6)^{2}$$

$$1.2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha \cdot 0.36 = 0.18 \alpha$$

$$\alpha = \frac{1.2}{0.18} = 6.667 \text{ rad/s}^{2}$$

**Part (c) — Linear Velocity of Tibial Point:**

For pure rotation, the tangential linear velocity at radius $r$ is:

$$v = r \omega = 0.25 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ rad/s} = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** In a real knee, the patellar tendon inserts at roughly $r \approx 4$ cm, so the linear velocity of the tendon at the same $\omega$ is only $0.08$ m/s. This is why **knee extensor moment arms** are a major focus in orthopaedic biomechanics — they translate $\omega$ into vastly different linear $v$ at different anatomical sites.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Kinematic Analysis Workflow

The diagram below maps the **complete analytical workflow** that a biomedical engineer follows when performing a kinematic analysis of a human movement (e.g., gait, jump, reach).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Define Anatomical Body Segment] --> nodeB[Establish Global Reference Frame X Y Z]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Define Joint Coordinate System JCS]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Capture Motion Data via Motion Capture or IMU]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Compute Position Vector r of t]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Linear Velocity v equals dr over dt]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Compute Angular Displacement theta of t]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Compute Angular Velocity omega equals dtheta over dt]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Compute Linear Acceleration a equals dv over dt]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Compute Angular Acceleration alpha equals d omega over dt]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Resolve Tangential and Normal Components]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Apply Linear Angular Equivalence v equals r omega]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Generate Kinematic Report and Plots]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Clinical or Engineering Interpretation]
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU examiner's perspective):**
- `nodeA`–`nodeC` (top tier) are the **setup** steps (frame of reference).
- `nodeD`–`nodeH` (middle tier) are the **data-acquisition and first-derivative** computations.
- `nodeI`–`nodeK` (lower tier) are the **second-derivative and vector resolution** computations.
- `nodeL` is the **rotational equivalence bridge** connecting linear and angular worlds.
- `nodeM`–`nodeN` (bottom tier) are the **clinical/engineering outputs**.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Linear vs. Angular Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LINEAR[LINEAR TRANSLATIONAL DOMAIN]
        L1[Position s] --> L2[Velocity v]
        L2 --> L3[Acceleration a]
    end
    subgraph ANGULAR[ANGULAR ROTATIONAL DOMAIN]
        A1[Angle theta] --> A2[Angular Velocity omega]
        A2 --> A3[Angular Acceleration alpha]
    end
    L2 <--> A2
    L3 <--> A3
    BRIDGE[Bridge Equations: v equals r omega and a_t equals r alpha and a_n equals omega squared r] --- LINEAR
    BRIDGE --- ANGULAR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for the bridge block:**
> - The left block (LINEAR) is what you measure with a **linear encoder** or **GPS**.
> - The right block (ANGULAR) is what you measure with a **goniometer** or **IMU gyroscope**.
> - The **BRIDGE** equations allow you to convert between them, which is essential when, for example, you measure $\omega$ at the elbow but need to compute $v$ of the hand holding a cricket bat.

### 4.3 Coordinate System Reference Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ORIGIN[Origin at Joint Centre] --> XAXIS[X axis Medio Lateral]
    ORIGIN --> YAXIS[Y axis Antero Posterior]
    ORIGIN --> ZAXIS[Z axis Vertical Longitudinal]
    XAXIS --> XNOTE[Right is positive X]
    YAXIS --> YNOTE[Anterior is positive Y]
    ZAXIS --> ZNOTE[Upward is positive Z]
```

This is a *block-level schematic* of the **standard biomechanical reference frame** used in KTU lab sessions and in published gait-analysis literature. For complex anatomical drawings (e.g., detailed muscle moment arms), this block representation is the recommended substitute to avoid Mermaid syntax conflicts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define the terms (i) displacement, (ii) velocity, and (iii) acceleration. State whether each is a scalar or a vector quantity, and give their SI units.*

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Displacement:** The shortest straight-line vector from the initial position to the final position of a body. It is a **vector** quantity, denoted $\Delta \vec{r}$, and has the SI unit **metre (m)**.

- **(ii) Velocity:** The time-rate of change of displacement, $\vec{v} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$. It is a **vector** quantity with SI unit **metre per second (m/s)**.

- **(iii) Acceleration:** The time-rate of change of velocity, $\vec{a} = \dfrac{d\vec{v}}{dt}$. It is a **vector** quantity with SI unit **metre per second squared (m/s²)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students frequently write that *velocity = displacement / time* and forget to specify that displacement is a *vector* divided by a *scalar*, giving a vector. Also, the unit is m/s **with a slash**, not "metres per second" written in three words. Examiners deduct ½ mark for sloppy unit notation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between average velocity and instantaneous velocity. A runner completes one full lap of a 400 m circular track in 50 s. Find (a) the average speed, (b) the average velocity.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Average Velocity | Instantaneous Velocity |
|----------|------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | $\vec{v}_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta \vec{r}}{\Delta t}$ | $\vec{v} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \dfrac{\Delta \vec{r}}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt}$ |
| Path | Considers net displacement only | Considers limit at a single instant |
| Vector | Yes | Yes |
| Use | Whole-trip analysis | Real-time motion capture |

**(a) Average speed** is total path length / total time (a scalar):

$$v_{speed} = \frac{400 \text{ m}}{50 \text{ s}} = 8.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Average velocity** is net displacement / total time. Since the runner returns to the starting point after one lap, the *net displacement* is **zero**, hence:

$$\vec{v}_{avg} = \frac{0}{50} = 0 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** The most common mistake is reporting the average velocity as 8 m/s (confusing it with average speed). This question is a KTU favourite precisely because it traps students who ignore the *vector* nature of displacement. Marks are deducted even if the numerical answer is right but the reasoning for "zero displacement" is missing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has a **Module-Level Internal Choice**. Solve any *one* of the two alternatives. The marks are split **7 + 7** across the two sub-parts. Show every step in the model solution; partial marking is the examiner's primary tool.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** (Module 1 Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Starting from the fundamental definitions of velocity and acceleration, derive the three equations of motion for a body moving with uniform acceleration. State clearly the meaning of every symbol used.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We define **velocity** as the time-rate of change of position and **acceleration** as the time-rate of change of velocity.

**Step 1.** $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$. For uniform (constant) acceleration, integrating from $t=0$ (with $v = u$) to time $t$ (with $v$):

$$\int_{u}^{v} dv = a \int_{0}^{t} dt \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v - u = a t$$

**Therefore Equation 1:** $\boxed{v = u + a t}$

**Step 2.** Average velocity for constant $a$ is the arithmetic mean of $u$ and $v$:

$$v_{avg} = \frac{u + v}{2}$$

Displacement = average velocity × time:

$$s = v_{avg} \, t = \left(\frac{u + v}{2}\right) t$$

Substituting $v = u + a t$:

$$s = \left(\frac{u + u + at}{2}\right) t = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$$

**Therefore Equation 2:** $\boxed{s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}}$

**Step 3.** Eliminate $t$ using $t = \dfrac{v - u}{a}$:

$$s = u \left(\frac{v-u}{a}\right) + \tfrac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{v-u}{a}\right)^{2}$$

Multiplying through by $2a$ and simplifying:

$$2 a s = 2 u v - 2 u^{2} + v^{2} - 2 u v + u^{2} = v^{2} - u^{2}$$

**Therefore Equation 3:** $\boxed{v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s}$

**Symbol meanings:**
- $u$ — initial velocity (m/s)
- $v$ — final velocity (m/s)
- $a$ — uniform acceleration (m/s²)
- $s$ — displacement (m)
- $t$ — time elapsed (s)

---

**(b)** *A rehabilitation patient is asked to perform a knee extension exercise. The lower leg (shank + foot) has an effective length of $L = 0.42$ m from the knee joint to the ankle. Starting from rest, the leg swings to a vertical position in $t = 0.35$ s with uniform angular acceleration. Calculate (i) the angular velocity at the end of the swing, (ii) the linear velocity of the ankle at that instant, and (iii) the centripetal acceleration experienced by the ankle.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ rad (90°), $\omega_0 = 0$, $t = 0.35$ s, $r = L = 0.42$ m.

**(i) Angular velocity (using $\theta = \omega_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2}\alpha t^{2}$ with $\omega_0 = 0$):**

$$\frac{\pi}{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha (0.35)^{2}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{\pi}{0.35^{2}} = \frac{3.1416}{0.1225} = 25.65 \text{ rad/s}^{2}$$

Then $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t = 0 + 25.65 \times 0.35 = 8.978$ rad/s.

$$\boxed{\omega \approx 8.98 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**(ii) Linear velocity of ankle (tangential, since leg is vertical):**

$$v = r \omega = 0.42 \times 8.978 = 3.771 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 3.77 \text{ m/s}}$$

**(iii) Centripetal acceleration of ankle:**

$$a_n = \omega^{2} r = (8.978)^{2} \times 0.42 = 80.60 \times 0.42 = 33.85 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{a_n \approx 33.85 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**Verification via $v^2 / r$:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^{2}}{r} = \frac{(3.771)^{2}}{0.42} = \frac{14.22}{0.42} = 33.85 \text{ m/s}^{2} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> 1. **Sign of $a$:** The acceleration is *positive* in the chosen direction of motion; do not introduce a negative sign unless the leg is decelerating.
> 2. **Units of $\omega$:** Report in **rad/s**, not in *revolutions per minute* (rpm). Conversion factors must be shown if rpm is used.
> 3. **Use of $L$ as radius:** Some students mistakenly use the length of the *foot* (typically 0.26 m) instead of the full shank length. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Step marking:** Skipping the integration step in part (a) costs 2 marks. Always show the integral setup explicitly.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** (Module 1 Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternative]**

**(a)** *Explain the terms (i) tangential acceleration, (ii) centripetal acceleration, and (iii) total acceleration for a particle undergoing curvilinear motion. Derive expressions for each, and compute the resultant acceleration for a cricket ball bowled with a release speed of 30 m/s at a curving trajectory of radius 1.2 m. Given $\alpha = 5 \text{ rad/s}^{2}$ at the release instant.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tangential acceleration ($a_t$):** The component of acceleration that changes the *magnitude* of velocity (i.e., the speed) along the direction of motion. For a rotating body at radius $r$:

$$a_t = r \alpha$$

**(ii) Centripetal (normal) acceleration ($a_n$):** The component of acceleration that changes the *direction* of velocity, always directed toward the centre of the curved path:

$$a_n = \frac{v^{2}}{r} = \omega^{2} r$$

**(iii) Total acceleration ($a$):** The vector sum of tangential and normal components. Since $a_t$ and $a_n$ are always *mutually perpendicular*:

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^{2} + a_n^{2}}$$

The direction of $\vec{a}$ makes an angle $\phi$ with the tangential direction:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{a_n}{a_t}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $v = 30$ m/s, $r = 1.2$ m, $\alpha = 5$ rad/s².

**Tangential acceleration:**

$$a_t = r \alpha = 1.2 \times 5 = 6.0 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Centripetal acceleration:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^{2}}{r} = \frac{(30)^{2}}{1.2} = \frac{900}{1.2} = 750 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

**Total acceleration:**

$$a = \sqrt{(6.0)^{2} + (750)^{2}} = \sqrt{36 + 562500} = \sqrt{562536} \approx 750.02 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 750.02 \text{ m/s}^{2} \approx 750 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**Direction angle:**

$$\tan \phi = \frac{750}{6} = 125 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \phi = \arctan(125) \approx 89.54^{\circ}$$

This confirms that the centripetal component *dominates* the tangential one for fast curvilinear motion — a key insight in designing cricket-bowling action analysis.

---

**(b)** *A sprinter starts from rest and reaches a top speed of 9 m/s in 3 seconds with uniform acceleration. After that, he decelerates uniformly to 7 m/s over the next 2 seconds while rounding a curve of radius 30 m. Find (i) the acceleration during the first 3 s, (ii) the angular velocity at the moment he begins the curve, (iii) the centripetal acceleration during the curve, and (iv) the total acceleration magnitude at the curve entry point.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Linear acceleration in first 3 s:**

Using $v = u + a t$ with $u = 0$, $v = 9$ m/s, $t = 3$ s:

$$9 = 0 + a \times 3 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a = 3 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{a = 3 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**(ii) Angular velocity at curve entry (speed = 7 m/s, radius = 30 m):**

The sprinter is moving in a circle, so $v = r \omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{7}{30} = 0.2333 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.233 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**(iii) Centripetal acceleration during the curve:**

$$a_n = \frac{v^{2}}{r} = \frac{(7)^{2}}{30} = \frac{49}{30} = 1.633 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{a_n = 1.633 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

**(iv) Total acceleration — compute tangential component first:**

Tangential acceleration in the 2 s deceleration phase:

$$a_t = \frac{v_f - v_i}{t} = \frac{7 - 9}{2} = -1.0 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

Magnitude $\vert a_t \vert = 1.0$ m/s².

Total acceleration magnitude (perpendicular $a_t$ and $a_n$):

$$a = \sqrt{a_t^{2} + a_n^{2}} = \sqrt{(1.0)^{2} + (1.633)^{2}} = \sqrt{1 + 2.667} = \sqrt{3.667} = 1.915 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 1.92 \text{ m/s}^{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. **Negative $a_t$:** In part (b)(iv), $a_t$ is negative because the sprinter is *decelerating*. Use the *magnitude* in the Pythagorean sum, but state the sign in the intermediate step.
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega$ and $f$:** Some students confuse angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) with frequency $f$ (Hz). The relation is $\omega = 2\pi f$. Showing this conversion explicitly is good practice.
> 3. **Forgetting the units of $a_n$:** $a_n = v^2/r$ has units of (m/s)² / m = m/s² — confirm by dimensional analysis if the question is lengthy.
> 4. **Skipping the direction angle $\phi$:** The KTU valuation key typically awards 1 mark for the angle $\phi = \arctan(a_n / a_t)$ in curvilinear problems. Do not omit it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Kinematic Concepts**

- **Kinematics ≠ Kinetics.** Kinematics *describes* motion (no forces); kinetics *explains* motion (forces, energy). This is a top-3 KTU-favourite distinction.
- The **four pillars** of kinematics: **Position, Displacement, Velocity, Acceleration** — remember them in this order of derivation.
- **Scalars** (distance, speed) have only magnitude. **Vectors** (displacement, velocity, acceleration) have magnitude + direction. *Acceleration is always a vector, even if speed is constant and only direction changes.*
- The **three linear kinematic equations** for uniform $a$ are: $v = u + a t$, $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^{2}$, $v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s$.
- The **angular analogues** are obtained by the substitution $s \to \theta$, $u \to \omega_0$, $v \to \omega$, $a \to \alpha$.
- The **rotational bridge equations** $v = r \omega$, $a_t = r \alpha$, $a_n = r \omega^{2} = v^{2}/r$ link the two domains.
- For **curvilinear motion**, $a_t$ and $a_n$ are *always perpendicular*; total $a = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_n^2}$.
- **Relative velocity** is the vector difference: $\vec{v}_{AB} = \vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B$.
- **Reference frames** must be defined *first*, before any kinematic quantity has meaning. Use the right-handed Cartesian system (X medio-lateral, Y antero-posterior, Z vertical).
- **Common biomechanical applications:** gait analysis, prosthetic design, crash biomechanics, sports performance, rehabilitation engineering.
- **SI unit memory trick:** *p*osition → m, *v*elocity → m/s, *a*cceleration → m/s²; *θ* → rad, *ω* → rad/s, *α* → rad/s². Each "rate" adds a 1/s in the denominator.
- **One-lap = zero displacement ≠ zero distance**: this distinction appears in KTU papers every cycle.
- For **uniform circular motion**, $a_t = 0$ but $a_n \neq 0$; this is the *only* case where total $a$ points purely toward the centre.
- The **Grood & Suntay Joint Coordinate System** is the KTU-recommended standard for 3D joint kinematics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinetic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinetic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Kinetics" in Biomechanics?

**Kinetics** is the branch of **Biomechanics** (a sub-discipline of Engineering Mechanics applied to biological systems) that deals with the study of the *causes* of motion — that is, the **forces and torques** that produce, modify, or prevent movement of the human body, its segments, or the external loads it interacts with.

It is formally contrasted with **Kinematics**, which only describes motion (displacement, velocity, acceleration) without worrying about *why* the motion happens.

| Aspect | Kinematics | Kinetics |
|---|---|---|
| Question Asked | *How* does the body move? | *Why* does the body move? |
| Variables | Position, velocity, acceleration | Force, moment, impulse, energy |
| Newton's Laws | Not required | Foundation of analysis |
| Output | Motion descriptors | Cause-effect relationships |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBMT601, Module 1):**
> *"Kinetics is the study of the relationships between the forces acting on a body and the motion that results. It forms the basis for analyzing human movement, joint loading, and external loads in clinical, sports, and rehabilitation engineering contexts."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Tug of War

Imagine two teams locked in a **tug-of-war**. You can:
- **Kinematically** describe the contest: *"Team A's rope marker is moving rightward at 0.4 m/s; the acceleration of the centre of the rope is 0.2 m/s²."* This tells you *how* the rope is moving.
- **Kinetically** explain it: *"Team B is exerting 2800 N along the rope, while Team A exerts 2500 N, plus the ground friction of 350 N on Team B's shoes and 280 N on Team A's."* This tells you *why* the rope is accelerating.

Kinetics = the **"why"** behind the motion. In biomechanics, "Team A" and "Team B" are replaced by **muscles, bones, ligaments, gravity, ground reaction forces, and external loads**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Quantities of Kinetics

The **SI base units** that govern every kinetic analysis are:

- **Mass ($m$)** — measured in **kilograms (kg)**. The amount of matter in a body; resistance to acceleration.
- **Force ($F$)** — measured in **newtons (N)**, where $\mathbf{1\ N = 1\ kg \cdot m \cdot s^{-2}}$.
- **Length ($s$ or $L$)** — measured in **metres (m)**.
- **Time ($t$)** — measured in **seconds (s)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Scalar vs. Vector Nature of Kinetic Quantities**
> Most kinetic quantities (force, velocity, momentum) are **vectors** — they have *magnitude* and *direction*. Only energy, mass, and time are pure **scalars**. Mistaking a vector for a scalar is the single most common KTU board-exam error in this module.

## 1.4 Concept Map of Kinetic Quantities

The kinetic universe of biomechanics can be organised around **four primary pillars**:

1. **Force & Moment** — the push–pull and the rotational tendency.
2. **Inertia & Mass** — the resistance of the body to motion change.
3. **Momentum & Impulse** — the "quantity of motion" and its change.
4. **Work, Energy & Power** — the transfer and rate of energy during movement.

All four are interconnected. Newton's Second Law ($\mathbf{F} = ma$) is the **bridge** between Force (Pillar 1) and Inertia (Pillar 2). The Work-Energy Theorem is the bridge between Force and Energy (Pillars 1 and 4). The Impulse-Momentum Theorem is the bridge between Force and Momentum (Pillars 1 and 3).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Newton's Three Laws — The Heart of Kinetics

### 2.1.1 Newton's First Law — The Law of Inertia
*"A body at rest stays at rest, and a body in motion stays in motion with the same velocity, unless acted upon by a net external force."*

**Biomechanical meaning:** Your knee does not bend by itself; you must *actively* contract your hamstrings or apply an external load. Likewise, once a sprinter leaves the blocks, friction with the ground is the *only* horizontal force (air resistance aside) that can decelerate them.

> [!NOTE]
> In biomechanics, **inertia** is not just a property of mass — it is also a property of **rotational mass (moment of inertia, $I$)**, which resists changes in *angular* motion. Both are essential for analysing joint rotations.

### 2.1.2 Newton's Second Law — The Fundamental Equation of Kinetics
The **net external force** acting on a particle equals the **time-rate of change of its linear momentum**.

$$
\mathbf{F}_{net} = \dfrac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt} = \dfrac{d(m\mathbf{v})}{dt}
$$

For a body of **constant mass**, this reduces to the famous:
$$
\mathbf{F}_{net} = m \mathbf{a}
$$

For **rotational motion** of a rigid body, the analogous form is:
$$
\mathbf{M}_{net} = I \boldsymbol{\alpha}
$$
where $I$ is the moment of inertia (kg·m²) and $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ is the angular acceleration (rad/s²).

### 2.1.3 Newton's Third Law — Action and Reaction
*"For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction."*

**Biomechanical meaning:** When your foot pushes down and backward on the ground ($\mathbf{F}_{foot \to ground}$), the ground pushes up and forward on your foot ($\mathbf{F}_{ground \to foot} = -\mathbf{F}_{foot \to ground}$). This **Ground Reaction Force (GRF)** is what propels a runner, jumper, or walker.

## 2.2 Force Systems — Classification

A **force system** is a collection of forces acting simultaneously on a body. The classification drives the **method of analysis**.

| Type of Force System | Description | Resultant Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Concurrent (Coplanar)** | All forces meet at a single point | Parallelogram / Triangle rule |
| **Parallel (Coplanar)** | All forces are parallel | Algebraic sum + moment balance |
| **General (Coplanar)** | Forces in 2-D, not concurrent/parallel | Component + Moment equations |
| **Concurrent (3-D)** | All forces meet at a point in space | Vector sum in 3 components |
| **Couple** | Two equal, opposite, parallel forces | Single equivalent moment |

## 2.3 Free Body Diagram (FBD) — The Most Important Skill in Kinetics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every KTU board exam problem in this module begins and ends with a correctly drawn Free Body Diagram.** Marks are explicitly awarded (typically 2–3 out of 14) just for an FBD.

**Steps to construct an FBD:**
1. **Isolate** the body of interest from all surroundings (imagine a "bubble" around it).
2. Replace all **supports** with the reactions they exert (normal force, tension, friction, pin).
3. Show the **weight** of the body acting at its centre of gravity.
4. Show all **applied** loads (muscle forces, external weights, contact forces).
5. Choose a **coordinate system** ($x$–$y$ or $n$–$t$) and label every vector with magnitude and direction.
6. Write the **equilibrium** or **dynamic** equations: $\sum F_x = m a_x$, $\sum F_y = m a_y$, $\sum M = I \alpha$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol/Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Second Law (linear) | $F = m a$ | $F$ in N, $m$ in kg, $a$ in m/s² | Constant mass only |
| 2 | Newton's Second Law (angular) | $M = I \alpha$ | $M$ in N·m, $I$ in kg·m², $\alpha$ in rad/s² | For fixed axis rotation |
| 3 | Weight | $W = m g$ | $W$ in N, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | On Earth only |
| 4 | Momentum (linear) | $\mathbf{p} = m \mathbf{v}$ | $p$ in kg·m/s | Vector quantity |
| 5 | Impulse–Momentum | $\mathbf{J} = \int \mathbf{F}\, dt = \Delta \mathbf{p}$ | $J$ in N·s | Equals change in momentum |
| 6 | Work done by constant force | $W = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{d}$ | $W$ in joules (J) | $W = F d \cos\theta$ |
| 7 | Kinetic Energy (translation) | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | $KE$ in J | $v$ is linear speed |
| 8 | Kinetic Energy (rotation) | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | $KE$ in J | $\omega$ is angular speed (rad/s) |
| 9 | Work-Energy Theorem | $W_{net} = \Delta KE$ | All in J | Conservative & non-conservative |
| 10 | Power | $P = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{v}$ | $P$ in watts (W) | Also $P = M \omega$ |
| 11 | Hooke's Law (spring) | $F = k x$ | $F$ in N, $x$ in m | $k$ in N/m |
| 12 | Friction (static, max) | $F_{s,max} = \mu_{s} N$ | $\mu_s$ is dimensionless | $F_s \leq \mu_s N$ |
| 13 | Friction (kinetic) | $F_{k} = \mu_{k} N$ | $\mu_k$ is dimensionless | $\mu_k < \mu_s$ typically |
| 14 | Resultant of two concurrent forces | $R = \sqrt{F_{1}^{2} + F_{2}^{2} + 2 F_{1} F_{2} \cos\theta}$ | $R$ in N | $\theta$ is angle between forces |
| 15 | Direction of resultant | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{F_{2} \sin\theta}{F_{1} + F_{2} \cos\theta}$ | $\alpha$ is angle from $F_1$ | Standard parallelogram law |
| 16 | Moment of a force | $M = r F \sin\theta$ | $M$ in N·m | $r$ is perpendicular distance |
| 17 | Varignon's Theorem | $\sum M_{O} = \sum (r_i \times F_i)$ | Sum of moments | Applies to any force system |
| 18 | Centripetal force | $F_{c} = \dfrac{m v^{2}}{r}$ | $F_c$ in N | Toward centre of circle |
| 19 | Gravitational acceleration | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Downward | Standard Earth value |
| 20 | Stress (axial) | $\sigma = F/A$ | $\sigma$ in Pa (N/m²) | $A$ is cross-section area |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Orthopaedic Biomechanics:** Computing the force across a hip implant while climbing stairs.
- **Sports Engineering:** Optimising cricket bat / tennis racket grip using impulse-momentum principles.
- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Designing a treadmill that provides controlled GRF to recovering patients.
- **Ergonomics:** Estimating L5/S1 disc compression force when a worker lifts a 20 kg box using static equilibrium.
- **Prosthetics:** Sizing a prosthetic limb's torque capacity using $M = I \alpha$ and the amputee's gait data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resultant of Two Concurrent Forces (Parallelogram Law)

**Statement:** The resultant of two coplanar concurrent forces $\mathbf{F}_1$ and $\mathbf{F}_2$ acting at an angle $\theta$ between them is the diagonal of the parallelogram formed by them.

**Given:** Two forces $\mathbf{F}_1$ and $\mathbf{F}_2$ meeting at point $O$ with angle $\theta$ between them.

**Step 1 — Choose a coordinate system.** Let $\mathbf{F}_1$ lie along the $x$-axis. So the components of $\mathbf{F}_1$ are $(F_1, 0)$.

**Step 2 — Resolve $\mathbf{F}_2$ into components.** The angle of $\mathbf{F}_2$ from the $x$-axis is $\theta$, so:

$$
F_{2x} = F_2 \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_{2y} = F_2 \sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components to obtain the resultant components.** Since forces are vectors, components add algebraically.

$$
R_x = F_{1x} + F_{2x} = F_1 + F_2 \cos\theta
$$

$$
R_y = F_{1y} + F_{2y} = 0 + F_2 \sin\theta = F_2 \sin\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the resultant** (Pythagorean combination):

$$
R = \sqrt{R_x^{2} + R_y^{2}}
$$

Substitute $R_x$ and $R_y$:

$$
R = \sqrt{(F_1 + F_2 \cos\theta)^{2} + (F_2 \sin\theta)^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the squared terms:**

$$
R = \sqrt{F_1^{2} + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta + F_2^{2} \cos^{2}\theta + F_2^{2} \sin^{2}\theta}
$$

**Step 6 — Use the trigonometric identity $\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta = 1$:**

$$
R = \sqrt{F_1^{2} + F_2^{2} + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 7 — Direction of $\mathbf{R}$:** Let $\alpha$ be the angle the resultant makes with $\mathbf{F}_1$. From the component ratio:

$$
\tan \alpha = \dfrac{R_y}{R_x} = \dfrac{F_2 \sin\theta}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\theta}
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{F_2 \sin\theta}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\theta}\right)
$$

This is the **law of cosines** applied to the parallelogram. ✔ **Verified.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Moment of a Force (Torque) — The Heart of Joint Kinetics

**Statement:** The moment of a force about a point $O$ equals the perpendicular component of the force times the distance from $O$ to the line of action.

**Step 1 — Definition.** Let a force $\mathbf{F}$ be applied at point $A$ and let $O$ be the moment centre. The position vector of $A$ from $O$ is $\mathbf{r}$.

**Step 2 — Vector definition of moment:**

$$
\mathbf{M}_O = \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{F}
$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude via cross product.** By the geometric definition of the cross product:

$$
\vert M_O \vert = \vert \mathbf{r} \vert \, \vert \mathbf{F} \vert \, \sin\theta
$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\mathbf{r}$ and $\mathbf{F}$.

**Step 4 — Introduce the perpendicular distance.** The perpendicular distance from $O$ to the line of action of $\mathbf{F}$ is $d = \vert \mathbf{r} \vert \sin\theta$. Therefore:

$$
M_O = F \, d
$$

**Step 5 — Sign convention.** Moment is **positive (counter-clockwise)** if it tends to rotate the body counter-clockwise about $O$, and **negative (clockwise)** otherwise. This is the **right-hand rule** for 2-D analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU pitfall:** Many students write $M = F \cdot r$ without the $\sin\theta$ term. This is **only** valid when $F$ is *perpendicular* to $r$. In a general 2-D or 3-D problem, **always** include $\sin\theta$ or use $\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{F}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work–Energy Theorem (Single Particle)

**Statement:** The net work done by all forces acting on a particle as it moves from position 1 to position 2 equals the change in its kinetic energy.

**Step 1 — Start with Newton's Second Law.** For a particle of mass $m$:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{net} = m \mathbf{a}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the dot product of both sides with velocity $\mathbf{v}$:**

$$
\mathbf{F}_{net} \cdot \mathbf{v} = m \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{v}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the chain-rule identity $a \cdot v = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{d}{dt}(v^{2})$:**

Proof: $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$, so $a \cdot v = v \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{d(v^{2})}{dt}$. ✔

Substituting:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{net} \cdot \mathbf{v} = \dfrac{m}{2} \dfrac{d(v^{2})}{dt} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}\right) = \dfrac{d(KE)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the left-hand side as instantaneous power** ($\mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{v} = P$):

$$
P = \dfrac{d(KE)}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $t_1$ to $t_2$:**

$$
\int_{t_1}^{t_2} P\, dt = KE_2 - KE_1
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $P = F \cdot v$ and use $v\, dt = ds$:**

$$
\int_{s_1}^{s_2} \mathbf{F} \cdot d\mathbf{s} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v_2^{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} m v_1^{2}
$$

$$
W_{net} = \Delta KE \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Impulse–Momentum Theorem

**Step 1 — Start with Newton's Second Law in momentum form:**

$$
\mathbf{F} = \dfrac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt} = \dfrac{d(m\mathbf{v})}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by $dt$:**

$$
\mathbf{F}\, dt = d(m\mathbf{v})
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from time $t_1$ to $t_2$:**

$$
\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathbf{F}\, dt = m\mathbf{v}_2 - m\mathbf{v}_1
$$

**Step 4 — Define the left-hand side as linear impulse $\mathbf{J}$:**

$$
\mathbf{J} = \Delta \mathbf{p} = m\mathbf{v}_2 - m\mathbf{v}_1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the basis for understanding why a **cricket fielder** pulls their hands back while catching a ball: by **increasing the time of impact** ($t_2 - t_1$), the **average force** experienced by the ball (and the hand) is *reduced*, since $\bar{F} = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Maximum Static Friction (Friction Cone Concept)

**Step 1 — Empirical observation (Coulomb's Law of Friction).** The maximum static friction force is proportional to the normal force $N$ between the surfaces in contact.

$$
F_{s,\max} = \mu_s N
$$

where $\mu_s$ is the **coefficient of static friction** (dimensionless).

**Step 2 — For kinetic (sliding) friction:**

$$
F_k = \mu_k N
$$

with $\mu_k < \mu_s$ (typically).

**Step 3 — Friction angle $\phi$:** The angle that the reaction force (normal + friction) makes with the normal is called the friction angle. It satisfies:

$$
\tan \phi = \mu_s
$$

**Step 4 — Friction cone:** If the line of action of the total reaction force $R$ lies *inside* a cone of half-angle $\phi$ about the normal, the body remains in static equilibrium. If it lies *on* the cone, the body is on the verge of slipping. If *outside*, slipping initiates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In a "block on inclined plane" problem, the **critical angle** at which the block begins to slide is given by $\tan \phi_c = \mu_s$, where $\phi_c$ is the incline angle. This is a 3-mark direct question that recurs almost every KTU exam cycle.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Problem Involves... | Use This Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Force and acceleration of a *particle* | $\mathbf{F} = m \mathbf{a}$ | Newton's Second Law (linear) |
| Angular acceleration of a *rigid body* | $\mathbf{M} = I \boldsymbol{\alpha}$ | Newton's Second Law (angular) |
| Velocity and force over *time* | Impulse–Momentum | Time integral of force |
| Velocity and force over *distance* | Work–Energy | Distance integral of force |
| Static equilibrium (no motion) | $\sum F = 0$, $\sum M = 0$ | Newton's First Law |
| Distributed body force | Centre of gravity / mass | Replace by equivalent resultant |
| Sliding / sticking | Coulomb friction | Empirical law |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — The Four Pillars of Kinetics

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **conceptual relationships** between the four pillars of kinetic concepts and Newton's Laws.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Newton First Law\nInertia] --> B[Newton Second Law\nF = m a]
    C[Force] --> B
    D[Mass] --> B
    B --> E[Acceleration]
    B --> F[Net Force Vector Sum]
    G[Newton Third Law\nAction Reaction] --> H[Ground Reaction Force]
    H --> I[Walking Running Jumping]
    B --> J[Work Energy Theorem]
    B --> K[Impulse Momentum Theorem]
    J --> L[Power = F dot v]
    K --> M[Impact and Collision Analysis]
    F --> N[Free Body Diagram]
    N --> O[Equilibrium Equations]
    O --> P[Kinetic Analysis of Body]
```

## 4.2 Force System Classification — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FS[Force System] --> CP[Coplanar]
    FS --> SP[Spatial 3D]
    CP --> CC[Concurrent]
    CP --> PP[Parallel]
    CP --> GP[General]
    CC --> R1[Resolve using\nparallelogram law]
    PP --> R2[Algebraic sum of magnitudes\nplus moment balance]
    GP --> R3[Resolve into components\nthen balance forces and moments]
    SP --> R4[Vector resolution in 3 components\nplus 3 moment equations]
```

## 4.3 Free Body Diagram Methodology — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify the body] --> S2[Step 2: Imagine bubble around it]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Remove all supports\nreplace with reaction forces]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Draw weight at CG]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Draw all applied loads\nmuscles, contact, external weights]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Choose xy coordinate system]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Label all forces\nmagnitude and direction]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Write Sigma Fx, Sigma Fy, Sigma M]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Solve for unknowns]
```

## 4.4 Friction Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Are surfaces\nin contact?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is there relative\nmotion?}
    Q1 -->|No| END1[No friction force]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Tendency to slip?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| KF[Kinetic friction F_k = mu_k N]
    Q3 -->|Yes| SFMAX[F_s max = mu_s N\nbody on verge of slipping]
    Q3 -->|No| EQ[F_s adjusts up to mu_s N\nstatic equilibrium]
    KF --> COMP[F opposes relative motion]
    SFMAX --> COMP2[F opposes impending motion]
    EQ --> COMP3[0 le F_s le mu_s N]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biomechanical Kinetic Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Biomechanical Problem\nHuman motion / joint load] --> IDEAL[Idealise body\nRigid link segments]
    IDEAL --> FBD[Draw Free Body Diagram]
    FBD --> COORD[Choose coordinate system]
    COORD --> EQL[Write equilibrium or\ndynamic equations]
    EQL --> SOLVE[Apply Newton Second Law\nF = m a and M = I alpha]
    SOLVE --> SOL[Solution: Joint forces\nmuscle forces accelerations]
    SOL --> INTERPRET[Interpretation for clinical\nsports or rehab use]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Kinetic Concepts**
> 1. **Forgetting the FBD** costs 2–3 marks instantly. Always draw it *first*, *labelled* and *isolated*.
> 2. **Sign errors** in moment equations are the most common mark-loss. Adopt a *strict* convention (CCW positive) and stick to it for the whole paper.
> 3. Confusing **mass ($m$, kg)** with **weight ($W = mg$, N)** is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty.
> 4. In friction problems, students often forget to check **all three** equilibrium equations and end up with an under-determined system.
> 5. Using $F = m a$ on a **rigid body in rotation** (instead of $M = I \alpha$) is a fatal conceptual error.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Newton's three laws of motion. Give one biomechanical example for each law.**

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia):**
*"A body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by an external unbalanced force to change its state."*
**Biomechanical example:** A sprinter on a frictionless surface, once set in motion, would continue moving at constant velocity. In practice, ground friction and air drag decelerate the runner, requiring continuous muscular effort.

**Newton's Second Law (Law of Acceleration):**
*"The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net external force applied and takes place in the direction of the force."*
Mathematically: $\mathbf{F} = m \mathbf{a}$.
**Biomechanical example:** A cricket batsman hitting a ball experiences a force from the bat; the resulting acceleration of the ball depends on the magnitude of the force and the ball's mass.

**Newton's Third Law (Law of Action–Reaction):**
*"For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction."*
