# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define "crimp" in tendon structure. What is its mechanical significance during the early phase of loading?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Crimp** refers to the *regular, planar sinusoidal waviness* of collagen fibers and fibrils in an unloaded (or lightly loaded) tendon. It is visible under polarized light microscopy and is typically 0°–5° off the tendon axis. Mechanically, when a tendon is first loaded (strain 0–2 %), the crimp **gradually straightens** ("uncrimping") without any stretching of the collagen molecules themselves. This produces the characteristic **toe region** of the stress–strain curve: very low stiffness, large strain for minimal stress. Once the crimp is fully straightened, the tendon enters the **linear-elastic region** where the collagen molecules themselves bear load and the modulus rises sharply (~ 100-fold). The crimp thus provides a *mechanical buffer* — a soft "give" at low loads — protecting the tissue from shock and allowing smooth force transmission onset.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between creep, stress relaxation, and hysteresis in tendon.**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Creep** is the *time-dependent increase in strain* under a *constant applied stress*. Example: when a tendon is held at a fixed load, it slowly elongates further with time. Governed by dashpot flow.
> - **Stress Relaxation** is the *time-dependent decrease in stress* under a *constant applied strain*. Example: when a tendon is stretched to a fixed length and held, the internal force decays with time.
> - **Hysteresis** refers to the *difference between the loading and unloading stress–strain paths*. The area enclosed by the loop represents energy *dissipated as heat*. In healthy tendon, hysteresis loss is only 5–10 % of stored energy — making the tendon an efficient elastic spring.
>
> All three phenomena arise from the **viscoelastic** (i.e., time- and rate-dependent) nature of tendon, modeled via spring–dashpot analogs (Maxwell, Kelvin-Voigt, SLS, QLV).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Modified Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **hierarchical structure of tendon** from the molecular level to the whole tissue. Add a note on the Hodge–Petruska quarter-stagger model. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat sketch of the stress–strain curve, explain the **five mechanical regions** of a tendon under uniaxial tension. List the typical values of Young's modulus, ultimate tensile strength, and failure strain. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The tendon is organized as a **five-level hierarchical structure** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Tropocollagen molecule (Level 1):** The fundamental unit. A right-handed triple helix of three left-handed polyproline-II-type α-polypeptide chains, each ~ 1000 amino acids long, with Gly-X-Y repeating triplets (X often = Pro, Y often = Hyp). Length ~ 300 nm, diameter ~ 1.5 nm.

2. **Microfibril (Level 2):** Formed by the staggered lateral assembly of **5 tropocollagen molecules** in the **Hodge–Petruska quarter-stagger model**. Each molecule is offset from its neighbor by **1/4 of its length (≈ 67 nm, the D-period)**. This staggered arrangement produces the characteristic **67 nm banding pattern** seen in EM and X-ray diffraction, and is the structural origin of tendon's tensile strength — because covalent and hydrogen bonds between adjacent molecules overlap along the entire fibril length.

3. **Collagen fibril (Level 3):** Bundles of microfibrils, diameter 50–500 nm, visible under electron microscopy. Fibrils are the load-bearing unit at the sub-micron scale.

4. **Collagen fiber (Level 4):** Bundles of fibrils, diameter 1–20 µm, visible under light microscopy. The functional unit of surgical sutures and most biomechanical tests.

5. **Fascicle → Whole tendon (Level 5):** Bundles of fibers wrapped by endotenon (inter-fascicular), then the whole tendon wrapped by epitenon and paratenon (outer sheaths). The paratenon allows the tendon to glide.

> **Insertion:** The tendon transitions into bone via a **fibrocartilaginous enthesis** (4 zones: tendon → fibrocartilage → mineralized fibrocartilage → bone) that minimizes stress concentration.

**Key points examiners look for:**
- 5-level hierarchy ✓
- 67 nm D-period ✓
- Gly-X-Y triplet and tropocollagen ✓
- Quaternary structure & covalent cross-links ✓
- Functional relevance of each level ✓

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Refer to the stress–strain curve in Section 4.2. The five regions are:

| Region | Strain (%) | Mechanism | Modulus Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Toe** | 0 – 2 | Crimp straightening | Very low (~ 0.1 GPa) |
| **II — Linear Elastic** | 2 – 6 | Intact collagen fiber stretching | Constant, high (~ 1.0 – 1.5 GPa) |
| **III — Yield** | 6 – 8 | Microfailure begins; first fiber breaks | Decreasing |
| **IV — Failure** | 8 – 10 | Macroscopic fiber rupture | Sharp stress drop |
| **V — Complete Rupture** | > 10 | Catastrophic failure | Zero |

**Standard values to quote:**
- Young's modulus (linear region): **E = 1.0 – 1.5 GPa**
- Ultimate tensile strength: **σ_uts = 50 – 150 MPa**
- Failure strain: **ε_f = 8 – 15 %**
- Storage modulus E': **1 – 2 GPa** (dynamic)
- Loss modulus E'': **20 – 200 MPa** (dynamic)

**In vivo working range:** Tendons normally operate between **0 % and 4 % strain** — i.e., within regions I–II. This provides a 2–3× safety factor before damage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **viscoelastic behavior of tendon** with reference to **creep, stress relaxation, and hysteresis**. Sketch the Maxwell, Kelvin–Voigt, and Standard Linear Solid models. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **strain energy density** stored in a tendon under uniaxial tensile loading. A tendon of length 25 cm and cross-sectional area 1.0 cm² is loaded to a stress of 30 MPa in the linear region (E = 1.2 GPa). Compute the **elongation** and **total strain energy stored**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

Tendon is a **viscoelastic** material: its mechanical response depends on *both* the magnitude and *rate* of loading, and shows time-dependent behavior even at constant load/displacement. Three key manifestations:

**(i) Creep — strain increases under constant stress.**
Under a step stress $\sigma_0$ applied at $t = 0$, the strain evolves as:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta} \, t \quad \text{(Maxwell)}
$$

or for a Kelvin–Voigt solid:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right), \quad \tau = \eta/E
$$

**(ii) Stress Relaxation — stress decreases under constant strain.**
For a Maxwell element under step strain $\varepsilon_0$:

$$
\sigma(t) = E \, \varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \tau = \eta/E
$$

For an SLS solid (most accurate for tendon):

$$
\sigma(t) = E_R \, \varepsilon_0 + (E_0 - E_R) \, \varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**(iii) Hysteresis — loading and unloading paths differ.**
The loop area = viscous dissipation per cycle. Healthy tendon hysteresis = **5 – 10 %** of stored energy. Pathological (tendinopathy, aging) tendons can show 20–30 %.

**Models (sketches):**

- **Maxwell:** Spring $E$ in *series* with dashpot $\eta$ — predicts stress relaxation but not bounded creep.
- **Kelvin–Voigt:** Spring $E$ in *parallel* with dashpot $\eta$ — predicts bounded creep but not stress relaxation (except in modified forms).
- **Standard Linear Solid (SLS / Zener):** Maxwell branch in parallel with a second spring $E_2$ — predicts both creep and stress relaxation with asymptotic limits.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (re-stated concisely for the answer sheet):**

Starting from Hooke's law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ in the linear region, the strain energy *per unit volume* (strain energy density) is the work done in stretching from $0$ to $\varepsilon$:

$$
U = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon' = \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} E \, \varepsilon' \, d\varepsilon' = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}
$$

**Numerical computation:**

Given:
- $L_0 = 25$ cm = 0.25 m
- $A_0 = 1.0$ cm² = $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- $\sigma = 30$ MPa = $30 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $E = 1.2$ GPa = $1.2 \times 10^{9}$ Pa

**Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{30 \times 10^{6}}{1.2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.025 = 2.5\%
$$

This is within the linear region. ✓

**Elongation:**

$$
\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = 0.025 \times 0.25 = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 6.25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Strain energy density:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} \times (30 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.025 = 3.75 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^{3} = 0.375 \text{ MJ/m}^{3}
$$

**Total strain energy in tendon:**

Volume $V_0 = A_0 L_0 = (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.25 = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³.

$$
W = U \cdot V_0 = (3.75 \times 10^{5}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-5}) = 9.375 \text{ J}
$$

**Final Answers:** $\Delta L = 6.25$ mm; $W = 9.375$ J.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCBMT601:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the toe region with elastic limit.** The toe region (0–2 % strain) is *NOT* the elastic limit — the elastic limit is at the boundary of the linear and yield regions (~ 6–8 %). Examiners deduct marks if you say "tendon is elastic only up to 2 %".
> 2. **Mixing up "ultimate tensile strength" with "yield strength".** UTS is the *maximum* stress reached; yield is where plastic (permanent) deformation begins. For tendon, UTS ≈ 50–150 MPa, but yield ≈ 30–50 MPa.
> 3. **Quoting muscle and tendon stiffness in the same units without conversion.** Tendon E ≈ 1 GPa; passive muscle E ≈ 0.05 MPa. A factor of ~ 20,000 difference. Stating them interchangeably is a major error.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet.** Always state $E$ in GPa or MPa — never write "E = 1.0" without units.
> 5. **Treating tendon as purely elastic.** Failing to mention viscoelasticity (creep/relaxation/hysteresis) in a question on tendon properties will lose ~ 3 marks.
> 6. **In passive muscle problems, forgetting the slack length.** Passive force = 0 below slack length — examiners will deduct if you write $F_{passive}(L) = F_0 e^{k(L/L_0)}$ for all $L$.
> 7. **Skipping the "in vivo working range" comment.** Always mention that tendons work at 0–4 % strain in vivo — this is the safety margin and is a high-yield talking point.
> 8. **In numerical problems, failing to verify linear-region assumption.** Always check that your computed strain (2.5 % in the example) lies *within* the assumed linear region. If it doesn't, the assumption is invalid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Properties of Tendons and Passive Muscles**

- [ ] Tendon = **5-level hierarchical collagen structure** (tropocollagen → microfibril → fibril → fiber → fascicle/tendon).
- [ ] **Hodge–Petruska quarter-stagger model** → 67 nm D-period.
- [ ] Tendon composition: **~ 90 % Type I collagen, ~ 2 % elastin, ~ 60–70 % water**.
- [ ] **5 regions of stress–strain curve:** Toe (0–2 %), Linear (2–6 %), Yield (6–8 %), Failure (8–10 %), Rupture (> 10 %).
- [ ] **Tendon E = 1.0–1.5 GPa**; **σ_uts = 50–150 MPa**; **ε_f = 8–15 %**.
- [ ] **In vivo strain range: 0–4 %** — within toe + linear regions (safety margin).
- [ ] **Viscoelasticity:** creep, stress relaxation, hysteresis (5–10 % loss in healthy tendon).
- [ ] **Hysteresis loss ratio** $\eta_{hys} = \Delta W / W_{load} \times 100\%$.
- [ ] **Models:** Maxwell (relaxation), Kelvin–Voigt (creep), SLS/Zener (both), QLV (most accurate).
- [ ] **Strain energy density:** $U = \tfrac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \sigma^{2} / 2E$.
- [ ] **Total energy in tendon:** $W = U \cdot V_0 = U \cdot A_0 L_0$.
- [ ] **Passive muscle** = muscle with no active stimulation — force comes from titin + connective tissue.
- [ ] **Titin** = largest known protein; I-band Ig domains + PEVK segment act as entropic springs.
- [ ] **Passive force–length:** $F_{passive}(L) = F_0 \exp[k (L - L_0)/L_0]$ — exponential rise beyond slack length.
- [ ] **Passive muscle stiffness:** $k_{muscle}(L) = (k/L_0) F_{passive}(L)$ — self-reinforcing.
- [ ] **Hill's model:** PEC (parallel elastic) + CC (contractile) + SEC (series elastic = tendon).
- [ ] **In series:** strains add, force is constant. **In parallel:** forces add, strain is constant.
- [ ] **Tendons are excellent energy-storage springs** — 90–95 % of stored elastic energy is returned.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** ACL grafts, prosthetics, orthotics, McKibben pneumatic muscles, sports biomechanics.
- [ ] **Titin dominates at intermediate sarcomere lengths; collagen ECM dominates at extreme lengths.**

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Properties of Tendons and Passive Muscles*

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Viscoelasticity** is the property of a material that exhibits both **viscous (fluid-like)** and **elastic (solid-like)** characteristics when subjected to an applied load. A **viscoelastic material** shows a **time-dependent mechanical response** under load, meaning its stress-strain behavior is dependent on the **rate of loading**, the **duration of loading**, and the **loading history**.

A **tendon** is a dense, fibrous **connective tissue** that functions as a biological **force-transmission cable** between skeletal muscle and bone. Tendons are classified as **anisotropic**, **inhomogeneous**, and **non-linear viscoelastic composites** composed primarily of **Type I collagen (≈ 70–80% of dry weight)**, **elastin (≈ 1–2%)**, **proteoglycans**, and **water (≈ 60–70% of total mass)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"The viscoelastic behavior of tendons describes the time- and rate-dependent mechanical response of collagenous connective tissue, characterized by the phenomena of creep, stress relaxation, hysteresis, and strain-rate sensitivity."*

The **hierarchical structure** of a tendon is responsible for its unique mechanical behavior:

| Hierarchical Level | Structural Unit | Approximate Diameter |
|:-------------------|:----------------|:---------------------|
| Level 1 | Tropocollagen molecule | 1.5 nm |
| Level 2 | Collagen microfibril | 3.5 nm |
| Level 3 | Collagen fibril | 50–500 nm |
| Level 4 | Collagen fiber | 1–20 μm |
| Level 5 | Fascicle (sub-tendon bundle) | 50–400 μm |
| Level 6 | Whole tendon | 0.1–15 cm |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Memory-Foam Pillow vs. The Rubber Ball**
>
> Imagine two everyday objects:
>
> 1. **A pure elastic rubber ball** — When you squeeze it, it deforms instantly, and when you release it, it pops back to its original shape immediately. The energy you put in is fully returned. The response is **time-independent**.
>
> 2. **A memory-foam pillow** — When you press your hand into it, it doesn't return to shape instantly. It slowly creeps back over several seconds, and if you keep your hand there, it continues to deform slightly more over time. It "remembers" its shape but on a **time delay**.
>
> A **tendon behaves like the memory foam**. It is neither purely elastic (rubber ball) nor purely viscous (honey), but a combination of both. If you pull on a tendon quickly, it feels stiffer. If you pull slowly, it feels more compliant. If you hold it at a constant length, the internal force gradually drops (relaxation). If you hold a constant weight on it, it slowly lengthens more (creep).

## 1.3 Why Tendons Are Viscoelastic (The Physical Origin)

The **molecular basis** of viscoelasticity in tendons arises from:

- **Collagen fiber sliding and uncrimping** — Under load, the wavy (crimped) collagen fibers straighten and slide past one another against the viscous resistance of the **ground substance (proteoglycan matrix)**.
- **Inter-fibrillar water flow** — Bound water within the tissue is forced through the dense collagen network, producing internal frictional (viscous) drag.
- **Progressive recruitment of fibers** — Not all fibers bear load simultaneously; with time and increasing strain, more fibers are recruited, contributing to the non-linear time-dependent stiffness.
- **Bond breaking and reformation** — Sacrificial bonds between collagen fibrils break and reform during loading, dissipating energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Why is tendon a viscoelastic material and not purely elastic?"* — your answer MUST include the words: *collagen fiber sliding, ground substance viscosity, inter-fibrillar fluid flow, and time-dependent recruitment*. These four keywords are worth 3 marks by themselves.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics the Examiner Expects

The following key material metrics **must be memorized** for KTU 2024 exams:

- **Young's Modulus (E)** of tendon: **≈ 1.0 – 1.5 GPa** (along fiber direction)
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** of tendon: **≈ 50 – 100 MPa**
- **Strain at failure** of tendon: **≈ 6 – 10%**
- **Storage Modulus (E')**: Represents the elastic (in-phase) component
- **Loss Modulus (E'')**: Represents the viscous (out-of-phase) component
- **Loss Tangent (tan δ) = E''/E'**: A measure of internal damping

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Hallmarks of Tendon Viscoelasticity

Any KTU 2024 question on this topic essentially tests the following four characteristic phenomena. You must be able to **define**, **graphically represent**, and **mathematically describe** each one.

### 2.1.1 Creep

**Definition:** Creep is the **progressive time-dependent increase in strain** experienced by a tendon when it is subjected to a **constant sustained stress**.

- **Test setup:** Apply a step stress $\sigma_0$ at $t = 0$ and hold it constant.
- **Observed response:** The strain $\varepsilon(t)$ rises rapidly initially (instantaneous elastic strain), then continues to increase gradually (delayed/viscous strain), and may approach a steady-state or, if the load is high enough, lead to tertiary creep and rupture.

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{instantaneous} + \varepsilon_{creep}(t)$$

- **Creep Compliance Function:**

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0} \quad \text{[units: Pa}^{-1}\text{] or [m}^2\text{/N]}$$

- **Clinical relevance:** Sustained low-load activities (e.g., standing, gripping) cause tendons to slowly elongate. This is critical in surgical grafts and tendon transfer surgeries.

### 2.1.2 Stress Relaxation

**Definition:** Stress relaxation is the **progressive time-dependent decrease in stress** experienced by a tendon when it is held at a **constant strain**.

- **Test setup:** Apply a step strain $\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$ and hold it constant.
- **Observed response:** The stress $\sigma(t)$ rises to an initial peak, then decays exponentially toward an asymptotic equilibrium value.

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_{initial} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{relax}}$$

- **Relaxation Modulus Function:**

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} \quad \text{[units: Pa]}$$

- **Clinical relevance:** A tendon held at a constant elongation (e.g., during surgical suturing) loses grip tension over time. Sutures must be retightened intraoperatively.

### 2.1.3 Hysteresis

**Definition:** Hysteresis is the **energy dissipation** that occurs when a tendon is loaded and then unloaded. The unloading curve does **not** retrace the loading curve; instead, it forms a closed loop.

- **Loading curve area** = Energy stored + Energy dissipated
- **Unloading curve area** = Energy returned
- **Hysteresis loop area** = **Energy dissipated as heat (lost)**

$$\Delta U_{dissipated} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

- **Hysteresis ratio:**

$$H = \frac{\text{Area of hysteresis loop}}{\text{Area under loading curve}}$$

- **Typical tendon hysteresis:** **≈ 5–10%** (relatively low, indicating high resilience)
- **Clinical relevance:** Hysteresis in tendons represents energy lost to internal friction. During running, tendons stretch during foot contact and recoil during push-off; less hysteresis means more efficient energy return.

### 2.1.4 Strain-Rate Sensitivity

**Definition:** The mechanical properties of tendon (stiffness, UTS, modulus) are **strongly dependent on the rate at which strain is applied**.

- At **high strain rates** (sudden impact, sports): tendon behaves **stiffer and stronger**.
- At **low strain rates** (slow movement, prolonged loading): tendon behaves **more compliant and weaker**.

Quantitatively, the modulus follows a power-law dependence on strain rate:

$$E = K \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^{n}$$

where $K$ is a constant, $\dot{\varepsilon}$ is the strain rate, and $n$ is the strain-rate sensitivity exponent (for tendon, $n \approx 0.05 - 0.10$).

- **Clinical relevance:** Tendon ruptures typically occur during **sudden, high-velocity muscle contractions** (e.g., Achilles tendon rupture during a sprint jump) — the tissue cannot dissipate energy fast enough at high strain rates.

## 2.2 The Three Building-Block Rheological Elements

To mathematically model the viscoelastic response, KTU expects you to know three fundamental elements:

| Element | Symbol | Stress–Strain Relation | Physical Analogy |
|:--------|:------:|:-----------------------|:-----------------|
| **Linear Spring** (Hookean) | Spring (E) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Rubber band — stores energy, no dissipation |
| **Dashpot** (Newtonian) | Dashpot (η) | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Syringe plunger — dissipates energy, no spring-back |
| **Friction slider** (St. Venant) | Friction block (μ) | $\sigma = \pm \mu \sigma_n$ | Static friction threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **spring** represents **purely elastic** behavior. A **dashpot** represents **purely viscous** behavior. **All real tendons are combinations of these two elements in series and/or parallel.**

## 2.3 The Three Classical Viscoelastic Models (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.3.1 Maxwell Model (Spring + Dashpot in Series)

- **Configuration:** Spring (E) and dashpot (η) connected end-to-end.
- **Constitutive equation:**

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

- **Behavior under creep:** Strain increases linearly with time (unbounded).
- **Behavior under stress relaxation:** Stress decays exponentially:
$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-Et/\eta} = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}$$
- **Limitation:** Does NOT predict equilibrium creep strain. **Poor fit for tendons.**

### 2.3.2 Kelvin–Voigt Model (Spring + Dashpot in Parallel)

- **Configuration:** Spring (E) and dashpot (η) connected side-by-side (both experience same strain).
- **Constitutive equation:**

$$\sigma(t) = E \varepsilon(t) + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}(t)$$

- **Behavior under creep:** Strain rises asymptotically to $\sigma_0 / E$:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)$$
- **Behavior under stress relaxation:** Stress remains constant (no relaxation). **Physically unrealistic for tendons.**
- **Limitation:** Does NOT predict stress relaxation. **Incomplete model for tendons.**

### 2.3.3 Standard Linear Solid (SLS) Model — **The KTU Gold Standard**

The **Standard Linear Solid model** (also called the **Zener model**) is the **best classical analog** for tendon viscoelastic behavior. It consists of a Maxwell element (E₁, η) in **parallel** with a second spring (E₂).

- **Constitutive equation (general form):**

$$\sigma + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\sigma} = E_R \left( \varepsilon + \tau_\sigma \dot{\varepsilon} \right)$$

- **Equivalent expanded form:**

$$\sigma(t) = E_2 \varepsilon(t) + E_1 \varepsilon(t) - \frac{E_1 \eta}{E_1} \dot{\varepsilon} \cdot (\text{from Maxwell branch})$$

The cleanest way to write it for the SLS (three-parameter Zener) is:

$$\dot{\sigma} + \frac{E_1 + E_2}{\eta} \sigma = E_2 \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{E_1 E_2}{\eta} \varepsilon$$

- **Behavior under creep:** Strain rises to a finite equilibrium $\sigma_0 / E_2$ (bounded) — **physically correct**.
- **Behavior under stress relaxation:** Stress decays to a finite equilibrium — **physically correct**.
- **Two characteristic time constants:** $\tau_1 = \eta / E_1$ and $\tau_2 = \eta / (E_1 + E_2)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SLS is the preferred model for KTU exams:** The Maxwell model alone fails under creep, the Kelvin–Voigt model alone fails under stress relaxation, and the Standard Linear Solid (Zener) model correctly captures BOTH responses — matching real tendon behavior in laboratory tests.

## 2.4 Fung's Quasi-Linear Viscoelastic (QLV) Theory

For KTU 2024 advanced questions, the **Quasi-Linear Viscoelastic (QLV) theory** developed by **Y.C. Fung** in 1972 is the most widely used **phenomenological** model for soft tissue (tendons, ligaments, skin).

- **Reduced relaxation function:**

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma(0)} = 1 - C \int_0^t e^{-s/\tau} \frac{ds}{\tau} = 1 - C\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right]$$

- **Stress response (general form):**

$$\sigma(t) = \int_0^t G(t - s) \, \frac{\partial \sigma^e(\varepsilon)}{\partial \varepsilon} \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial s} \, ds$$

- $\sigma^e(\varepsilon)$ is the **elastic (instantaneous) stress-strain response**.
- $G(t)$ is the **reduced relaxation function** — a property of the tissue independent of strain.
- **Clinical relevance:** QLV is used to model post-surgical tendon healing, ligament graft remodeling, and the strain-adaptive response of biological tissues.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. Memorize it. It is worth 5–7 marks on any KTU question from this topic.**

| Phenomenon | Governing Equation | Key Parameter | Units |
|:-----------|:-------------------|:--------------|:------|
| Hooke's Law (spring) | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's modulus | Pa |
| Newton's law (dashpot) | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$ | $\eta$ = viscosity | Pa·s |
| Maxwell creep | $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0 \left( \frac{1}{E} + \frac{t}{\eta} \right)$ | — | — |
| Maxwell stress relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_M}$ | $\tau_M = \eta / E$ | s |
| Kelvin–Voigt creep | $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / E)\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)$ | $\tau_K = \eta / E$ | s |
| SLS stress relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_\infty + (\sigma_0 - \sigma_\infty) e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau = \eta / (E_1 + E_2)$ | s |
| SLS creep | $\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} \left[ 1 - \frac{E_1}{E_1 + E_2} e^{-t/\tau} \right]$ | — | — |
| Creep compliance | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$ | — | Pa⁻¹ |
| Relaxation modulus | $G(t) = \sigma(t) / \varepsilon_0$ | — | Pa |
| Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ | $0 \leq \delta \leq \pi/2$ | dimensionless |
| Strain-rate modulus | $E = K \dot{\varepsilon}^{n}$ | $n$ = sensitivity exponent | dimensionless |
| Hysteresis energy loss | $\Delta U = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | — | J/m³ |
| QLV reduced relaxation | $G(t) = 1 - C \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$ | $0 \leq C \leq 1$ | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why Tendon Viscoelasticity Matters |
|:-----------------|:------------------------------------|
| **Orthopedic surgery** | Graft selection, suture tensioning, post-op rehab protocols |
| **Sports biomechanics** | Predicting rupture risk, designing footwear and orthotics |
| **Tissue engineering** | Designing scaffolds that mimic native tendon creep/relaxation |
| **Prosthetic design** | Tendon-driven robots and prosthetic limbs require viscoelastic actuators |
| **Forensic biomechanics** | Determining injury mechanism from tissue damage pattern |
| **Rehabilitation engineering** | Optimizing load progression based on stress-relaxation curves |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition below is fully explicit. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation: Maxwell Model Constitutive Equation

**Starting point:** A spring ($E$) and a dashpot ($\eta$) are connected **in series**, so they experience the **same stress** but their strains add.

**Step 1:** Write the strain compatibility equation.

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hooke's law to the spring.

$$\varepsilon_{spring} = \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's viscous law to the dashpot.

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate the spring strain with respect to time.

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} = \frac{1}{E} \dot{\sigma}$$

**Step 5:** Add the two strain rates (time derivative of step 1).

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{total} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} + \dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute from Steps 4 and 3.

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Final Maxwell constitutive equation:**

$$\boxed{\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Maxwell Stress Relaxation Response

**Setup:** A step strain $\varepsilon_0$ is applied instantaneously at $t = 0$ and held constant, so $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ for $t > 0$.

**Step 1:** Substitute $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ into the constitutive equation.

$$0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.

$$\dot{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \sigma$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables.

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta} dt$$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$, with $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$.

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma'}{\sigma'} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals.

$$\ln \left( \frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} \right) = -\frac{E}{\eta} t$$

**Step 6:** Define the Maxwell relaxation time $\tau_M = \eta / E$.

$$\ln \left( \frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} \right) = -\frac{t}{\tau_M}$$

**Step 7:** Exponentiate both sides.

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}}$$

**Step 8:** At $t = \tau_M$, the stress has decayed to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value.

## 3.3 Derivation: Kelvin–Voigt Model Constitutive Equation

**Setup:** A spring ($E$) and a dashpot ($\eta$) are connected **in parallel**, so they experience the **same strain** but their stresses add.

**Step 1:** Write the stress additivity equation.

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Hooke's law to the spring.

$$\sigma_{spring} = E \varepsilon$$

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's law to the dashpot.

$$\sigma_{dashpot} = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 4:** Add them.

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E \varepsilon(t) + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}(t)}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Kelvin–Voigt Creep Response

**Setup:** A step stress $\sigma_0$ is applied at $t = 0$ and held constant.

**Step 1:** Substitute $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0$ into the constitutive equation.

$$\sigma_0 = E \varepsilon(t) + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}(t)$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange into a standard first-order linear ODE.

$$\dot{\varepsilon}(t) + \frac{E}{\eta} \varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}$$

**Step 3:** The general solution is the sum of the homogeneous and particular solutions.

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{homogeneous}(t) + \varepsilon_{particular}$$

**Step 4:** Find the particular (steady-state) solution. At $t \to \infty$, $\dot{\varepsilon} \to 0$:

$$\varepsilon_{particular} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 5:** Find the homogeneous solution. Assume $\varepsilon_h = A e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$-\lambda A e^{-\lambda t} + \frac{E}{\eta} A e^{-\lambda t} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{E}{\eta}$$

**Step 6:** Define the Kelvin retardation time $\tau_K = \eta / E$.

$$\varepsilon_h(t) = A e^{-t/\tau_K}$$

**Step 7:** Apply the initial condition: at $t = 0$, the strain is zero (dashpot cannot move instantly).

$$\varepsilon(0) = 0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -\frac{\sigma_0}{E}$$

**Step 8:** Assemble the full solution.

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_K} \right)}$$

**Step 9:** Limiting behavior check.
- $t \to 0$: $\varepsilon \to 0$ (dashpot blocks initial motion) ✓
- $t \to \infty$: $\varepsilon \to \sigma_0 / E$ (spring fully extended) ✓

## 3.5 Derivation: Standard Linear Solid (SLS / Zener) Constitutive Equation

**Setup:** Maxwell branch ($E_1$ in series with $\eta$) is in **parallel** with a second spring $E_2$.

**Step 1:** Recognize that in the parallel arrangement, **total strain is the same in both branches** but **stresses add**.

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{Maxwell} = \varepsilon_{spring2}$$

$$\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{Maxwell} + \sigma_{spring2}$$

**Step 2:** Express the spring-2 stress.

$$\sigma_{spring2} = E_2 \varepsilon$$

**Step 3:** Express the Maxwell-branch stress using its constitutive equation (rearranged).

From $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dot{\sigma}_M / E_1 + \sigma_M / \eta$, solving for $\sigma_M$:

$$\sigma_M + \frac{\eta}{E_1} \dot{\sigma}_M = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 4:** The total stress is $\sigma = \sigma_M + E_2 \varepsilon$, so $\sigma_M = \sigma - E_2 \varepsilon$.

**Step 5:** Differentiate: $\dot{\sigma}_M = \dot{\sigma} - E_2 \dot{\varepsilon}$.

**Step 6:** Substitute into Step 3.

$$(\sigma - E_2 \varepsilon) + \frac{\eta}{E_1}(\dot{\sigma} - E_2 \dot{\varepsilon}) = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 7:** Expand and collect like terms.

$$\sigma + \frac{\eta}{E_1} \dot{\sigma} = E_2 \varepsilon + \frac{\eta E_2}{E_1} \varepsilon + \eta \dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{\eta E_2}{E_1} \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 8:** Group the $\varepsilon$ terms.

$$\sigma + \frac{\eta}{E_1} \dot{\sigma} = \left( E_2 + \frac{\eta E_2}{E_1} \right) \varepsilon + \eta \left( 1 + \frac{E_2}{E_1} \right) \dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 9:** Define the time constants.
- $\tau_\sigma = \eta / E_1$ (constant-stress relaxation time)
- $\tau_\varepsilon = \eta (E_1 + E_2) / (E_1 E_2)$ (constant-strain retardation time)
- $E_R = E_2$ (relaxed modulus, the long-term modulus)
- $E_U = E_1 + E_2$ (unrelaxed modulus, the instantaneous modulus)

**Step 10:** Final SLS constitutive equation in compact form.

$$\boxed{\sigma + \tau_\sigma \dot{\sigma} = E_R \left( \varepsilon + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\varepsilon} \right)}$$

## 3.6 Derivation: SLS Stress Relaxation Response

**Setup:** Step strain $\varepsilon_0$ applied, so $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ for $t > 0$, with $\varepsilon(0) = \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 1:** Substitute into the constitutive equation.

$$\sigma + \tau_\sigma \dot{\sigma} = E_R \varepsilon_0$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.

$$\dot{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\tau_\sigma} \sigma = \frac{E_R \varepsilon_0}{\tau_\sigma}$$

**Step 3:** The steady-state (relaxed) solution is $\sigma_\infty = E_R \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 4:** The homogeneous solution is $\sigma_h = B e^{-t/\tau_\sigma}$.

**Step 5:** The initial stress (at $t = 0^+$) is the unrelaxed stress $\sigma_0 = E_U \varepsilon_0 = (E_1 + E_2) \varepsilon_0$.

**Step 6:** Apply initial condition: $\sigma(0) = E_U \varepsilon_0$.

$$E_U \varepsilon_0 = E_R \varepsilon_0 + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = (E_U - E_R) \varepsilon_0 = E_1 \varepsilon_0$$

**Step 7:** Final solution.

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E_R \varepsilon_0 + E_1 \varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_\sigma} = \varepsilon_0 \left[ E_2 + E_1 \, e^{-t/\tau_\sigma} \right]}$$

**Step 8:** Limiting checks.
- $t = 0$: $\sigma = E_2 \varepsilon_0 + E_1 \varepsilon_0 = (E_1 + E_2) \varepsilon_0 = E_U \varepsilon_0$ ✓
- $t \to \infty$: $\sigma \to E_2 \varepsilon_0 = E_R \varepsilon_0$ ✓

## 3.7 Derivation: SLS Creep Response

**Setup:** Step stress $\sigma_0$ applied at $t = 0$, $\dot{\sigma} = 0$ for $t > 0$.

**Step 1:** Substitute into the constitutive equation.

$$\sigma_0 = E_R \left( \varepsilon + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\varepsilon} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange.

$$\dot{\varepsilon} + \frac{1}{\tau_\varepsilon} \varepsilon = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_R \tau_\varepsilon}$$

**Step 3:** Steady-state strain: $\varepsilon_\infty = \sigma_0 / E_R = \sigma_0 / E_2$.

**Step 4:** General solution: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_\infty + D e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon}$.

**Step 5:** Apply initial condition: $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / E_U$ (instantaneous elastic response in spring $E_2$ + Maxwell spring $E_1$ both deform).

$$\frac{\sigma_0}{E_U} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} + D \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sigma_0 \left( \frac{1}{E_1 + E_2} - \frac{1}{E_2} \right) = -\frac{\sigma_0 E_1}{E_2(E_1 + E_2)}$$

**Step 6:** Final creep solution.

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} - \frac{\sigma_0 E_1}{E_2(E_1 + E_2)} e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon} = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} \left[ 1 - \frac{E_1}{E_1 + E_2} e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon} \right]}$$

**Step 7:** Limiting checks.
- $t = 0$: $\varepsilon = (\sigma_0 / E_2)[1 - E_1/(E_1+E_2)] = \sigma_0 / (E_1 + E_2) = \sigma_0 / E_U$ ✓
- $t \to \infty$: $\varepsilon \to \sigma_0 / E_2 = \sigma_0 / E_R$ ✓

## 3.8 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Summary

| Model | Predicts Creep? | Predicts Stress Relaxation? | Equilibrium Strain | Strain Recovery | Best For |
|:------|:---------------:|:---------------------------:|:-------------------:|:---------------:|:---------|
| **Pure Spring** | No (instant only) | No (constant stress) | 0 | Instant | Hookean solids |
| **Pure Dashpot** | Unbounded linear | No (constant stress) | $\to \infty$ | None | Newtonian fluids |
| **Maxwell** | Yes (unbounded) | Yes (to zero) | $\to \infty$ | Partial | Synthetic polymers (qualitative) |
| **Kelvin–Voigt** | Yes (bounded) | No (constant) | $\sigma_0 / E$ | Complete | Foam, soil compaction |
| **SLS (Zener)** | Yes (bounded) | Yes (to $\sigma_\infty$) | $\sigma_0 / E_2$ | Partial | **Tendons & ligaments** |
| **QLV (Fung)** | Yes (non-linear) | Yes (non-linear) | Bounded | Partial | **Soft tissues (clinical)** |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA["APPLIED STEP STRESS SIGMA NAUGHT"]
    inpB["APPLIED STEP STRAIN EPSILON NAUGHT"]
    branchA{{CONDITION SWITCH}}
    cre["CREEP COMPLIANCE J OF T"]
    rel["RELAXATION MODULUS G OF T"]
    his["HYSTERESIS LOOP AREA"]
    srt["STRAIN RATE SENSITIVITY"]
    sls["STANDARD LINEAR SOLID MODEL"]
    res["OUTPUT STRAIN OR STRESS VERSUS TIME"]
    inpA --> branchA
    inpB --> branchA
    branchA --> cre
    branchA --> rel
    branchA --> his
    branchA --> srt
    cre --> sls
    rel --> sls
    his --> sls
    srt --> sls
    sls --> res
```

## 4.3 Architecture: Three Classical Viscoelastic Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    modA["MAXWELL MODEL<br/>SPRING E IN SERIES WITH DASHPOT ETA"]
    modB["KELVIN VOIGT MODEL<br/>SPRING E IN PARALLEL WITH DASHPOT ETA"]
    modC["STANDARD LINEAR SOLID<br/>MAXWELL BRANCH IN PARALLEL WITH SPRING E2"]
    behA["CREEP UNBOUNDED LINEAR<br/>RELAXATION EXPONENTIAL TO ZERO"]
    behB["CREEP BOUNDED EXPONENTIAL<br/>RELAXATION NONE"]
    behC["CREEP BOUNDED<br/>RELAXATION EXPONENTIAL TO NON ZERO"]
    useA["POLYMER MELTS"]
    useB["FOAM AND SOIL"]
    useC["TENDON AND LIGAMENT TISSUE"]
    modA --> behA
    modB --> behB
    modC --> behC
    behA --> useA
    behB --> useB
    behC --> useC
```

## 4.4 Stress–Strain Curve for Tendon (Block Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    reg1["REGION 1 TOE REGION<br/>CRIMP UNFOLDING<br/>STRAIN 0 TO 2 PERCENT"]
    reg2["REGION 2 LINEAR REGION<br/>COLLAGEN FIBER ELONGATION<br/>STRAIN 2 TO 6 PERCENT"]
    reg3["REGION 3 MICRORUPTURE<br/>FEW FIBERS FAIL<br/>STRAIN 6 TO 8 PERCENT"]
    reg4["REGION 4 FAILURE<br/>MACROSCOPIC RUPTURE<br/>STRAIN ABOVE 8 PERCENT"]
    unload["UNLOADING CURVE<br/>BELOW LOADING CURVE<br/>HYSTERESIS LOOP CLOSED"]
    reg1 --> reg2
    reg2 --> reg3
    reg3 --> reg4
    reg4 --> unload
    unload -.ENERGY LOST AS HEAT.-> reg1
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow: Viscoelastic Testing Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["STEP 1 PREPARE TENDON SPECIMEN<br/>MEASURE GEOMETRY"]
    s2["STEP 2 MOUNT IN BIAXIAL OR UNIAXIAL TESTER"]
    s3["STEP 3 APPLY PRECONDITIONING CYCLES"]
    s4["STEP 4A CREEP TEST<br/>CONSTANT STRESS FOR 600 SECONDS"]
    s4b["STEP 4B RELAXATION TEST<br/>CONSTANT STRAIN FOR 600 SECONDS"]
    s4c["STEP 4C CYCLIC LOAD UNLOAD<br/>TO OBTAIN HYSTERESIS"]
    s4d["STEP 4D RAMPED LOADING<br/>AT MULTIPLE STRAIN RATES"]
    s5["STEP 5 FIT DATA TO SLS OR QLV MODEL"]
    s6["STEP 6 EXTRACT PARAMETERS<br/>E1 E2 ETA TAU C"]
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4A
    s3 --> s4B
    s3 --> s4C
    s3 --> s4D
    s4A --> s5
    s4B --> s5
    s4C --> s5
    s4D --> s5
    s5 --> s6
```
