# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Viscoelasticity. List any two characteristic features of tendon viscoelastic behavior. [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Viscoelasticity** is the property of a material that exhibits a time-dependent mechanical response, combining the characteristics of both an elastic solid (stress proportional to strain) and a viscous fluid (stress proportional to strain rate).

**Two characteristic features:**
1. **Creep** — progressive increase in strain under constant applied stress.
2. **Stress relaxation** — progressive decrease in stress under constant applied strain.

> [!TIP]
> **Acceptable alternatives for the third marking point:** hysteresis, strain-rate sensitivity, or energy dissipation.

---

### Question A2: What is hysteresis? Why is the hysteresis loop of a healthy tendon small? [3 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hysteresis** is the phenomenon in which the **energy dissipation** during a loading–unloading cycle causes the unloading stress–strain curve to lie below the loading curve, forming a closed loop. The area enclosed by this loop represents energy lost as heat.

> The hysteresis loop of a **healthy tendon is small (≈ 5–10%)** because tendons are designed as **biological energy-storage devices**. The crimped collagen fibers store strain energy efficiently during loading and release it almost completely during recoil, minimizing frictional losses in the ground substance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> For the 14-mark questions, KTU examiners deduct marks ruthlessly for:
> 1. Drawing the wrong rheological model (Maxwell instead of SLS, or vice versa) — **−2 marks**.
> 2. Not defining the time constant explicitly — **−1 mark**.
> 3. Forgetting to check initial and final conditions in the derivation — **−1 mark**.
> 4. Confusing **creep compliance J(t)** with **relaxation modulus G(t)** — **−2 marks**.
> 5. Not drawing the stress–strain curve when asked — **−2 marks**.
> 6. Mixing up **instantaneous modulus (E_U)** and **relaxed modulus (E_R)** — **−2 marks**.

### Question Choice A: Viscoelastic Models & Stress Relaxation

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **Maxwell** and **Kelvin–Voigt** models. Discuss **why neither is sufficient** to describe real tendon behavior. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **stress-relaxation response** of the **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** model. Sketch the response and identify the **unrelaxed and relaxed moduli** on the curve. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Maxwell Model:**
- Configuration: A spring (E) connected **in series** with a dashpot (η).
- Both elements experience the **same stress**; total strain is the sum.
- Constitutive equation:

$$\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

- Under a step stress, the dashpot continues to deform indefinitely → **creep is unbounded (linear in time)**, which is physically wrong for tendons.
- Under a step strain, the spring gradually transfers its load to the dashpot → stress decays to **zero** (no equilibrium stress), which is also wrong for tendons.

**Kelvin–Voigt Model:**
- Configuration: A spring (E) connected **in parallel** with a dashpot (η).
- Both elements experience the **same strain**; total stress is the sum.
- Constitutive equation:

$$\sigma = E \varepsilon + \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$$

- Under step stress, strain rises asymptotically to $\sigma_0 / E$ → creep is bounded ✓.
- Under step strain, the dashpot cannot move instantaneously, so the spring force is fully maintained → **no stress relaxation occurs**, which is wrong for tendons.

**Why both are insufficient:**
- Maxwell: unbounded creep.
- Kelvin–Voigt: no stress relaxation.
- Real tendons show **both bounded creep and finite (non-zero) stress relaxation** → neither model is adequate. The Standard Linear Solid (SLS) combines the best features of both.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Setup:** The SLS model has a Maxwell branch (E₁ + η in series) in parallel with a second spring E₂. Apply a step strain $\varepsilon_0$ at $t = 0$, held constant for $t > 0$.

**Derivation (full):**

The SLS constitutive equation is:

$$\sigma + \tau_\sigma \dot{\sigma} = E_R \left( \varepsilon + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\varepsilon} \right)$$

With $\tau_\sigma = \eta / E_1$, $E_R = E_2$, $\tau_\varepsilon = \eta(E_1 + E_2)/(E_1 E_2)$.

For $t > 0$, $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0$ and $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$, so:

$$\sigma + \tau_\sigma \dot{\sigma} = E_R \varepsilon_0$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. The particular (steady-state) solution is $\sigma_\infty = E_R \varepsilon_0 = E_2 \varepsilon_0$.

The homogeneous solution is $\sigma_h = A e^{-t/\tau_\sigma}$.

Initial condition: at $t = 0^+$, the strain is imposed instantaneously; the dashpot cannot yet move, so all the load is carried by the springs E₁ and E₂ acting in parallel. Thus:

$$\sigma(0) = (E_1 + E_2) \varepsilon_0 = E_U \varepsilon_0$$

Applying this:

$$E_U \varepsilon_0 = E_R \varepsilon_0 + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = (E_U - E_R) \varepsilon_0 = E_1 \varepsilon_0$$

**Final stress-relaxation response:**

$$\boxed{\sigma(t) = E_2 \varepsilon_0 + E_1 \varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t / \tau_\sigma} = \varepsilon_0 \left[ E_2 + E_1 \, e^{-t \cdot E_1 / \eta} \right]}$$

**Curve identification:**
- **At $t = 0$:** $\sigma = (E_1 + E_2)\varepsilon_0 = E_U \varepsilon_0$ — this is the **unrelaxed (instantaneous) modulus** $E_U$.
- **At $t \to \infty$:** $\sigma = E_2 \varepsilon_0$ — this is the **relaxed (long-term) modulus** $E_R$.
- The exponential decay constant is the **relaxation time** $\tau_\sigma = \eta / E_1$.

| Time | Modulus | Meaning |
|:----:|:--------|:--------|
| $t = 0$ | $E_U = E_1 + E_2$ | Unrelaxed (instantaneous) |
| $t = \tau_\sigma$ | ~ $0.37 E_1 + E_2$ | ~63% of viscous part decayed |
| $t \to \infty$ | $E_R = E_2$ | Relaxed (equilibrium) |

---

### Question Choice B: Creep, Hysteresis & QLV Theory

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Define **creep** and **stress relaxation**. With a sketch, explain the **creep response** of the **Standard Linear Solid** model and label all three characteristic stages. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Fung's Quasi-Linear Viscoelastic (QLV) theory** and write the **reduced relaxation function** used in soft-tissue modeling. Mention two clinical situations where QLV is preferred over the SLS model. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definitions:**

- **Creep** is the progressive, time-dependent increase in strain that occurs when a tissue is subjected to a **constant sustained stress**.
- **Stress relaxation** is the progressive, time-dependent decrease in stress that occurs when a tissue is held at a **constant strain**.

**Creep response of the SLS model:**

The creep equation (derived in SECTION 3.7) is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_2} \left[ 1 - \frac{E_1}{E_1 + E_2} e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon} \right]$$

**Three characteristic stages:**

1. **Instantaneous elastic strain** at $t = 0$: $\varepsilon(0) = \sigma_0 / (E_1 + E_2) = \sigma_0 / E_U$. Both springs deform instantly; the dashpot cannot move.
2. **Transient (delayed) creep stage** (0 < t < ∞): The dashpot begins to deform, and the strain increases exponentially with time constant $\tau_\varepsilon = \eta(E_1 + E_2) / (E_1 E_2)$.
3. **Steady-state (equilibrium) stage** ($t \to \infty$): The dashpot has stopped moving; only the spring E₂ carries the load. $\varepsilon_\infty = \sigma_0 / E_2$.

**Sketch (described in text form for the answer book):**
- $x$-axis: time $t$
- $y$-axis: strain $\varepsilon$
- Curve starts at $\sigma_0 / E_U$ at $t = 0$, rises with concave-down curvature, and asymptotically approaches $\sigma_0 / E_2$.
- The vertical gap between the initial and final plateaus is the **delayed (viscous) strain** = $\sigma_0 \cdot E_1 / [E_2 (E_1 + E_2)]$.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Fung's Quasi-Linear Viscoelastic (QLV) Theory:**

Developed by **Y.C. Fung (1972)**, QLV is a **phenomenological** continuum model for soft biological tissues (tendons, ligaments, skin, vessels) that decomposes the stress response into a product of an **elastic (instantaneous) response** and a **reduced relaxation function**.

**Constitutive equation:**

$$\sigma(t) = \int_0^t G(t - s) \, \frac{d\sigma^e(\varepsilon)}{d\varepsilon} \frac{d\varepsilon}{ds} \, ds$$

where:
- $G(t)$ is the **reduced relaxation function**, with $G(0) = 1$.
- $\sigma^e(\varepsilon)$ is the **elastic (instantaneous) stress–strain response** of the tissue.

**Reduced relaxation function (exponential form):**

$$G(t) = 1 - C \left[ 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right] = (1 - C) + C \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where:
- $C$ = dimensionless relaxation constant ($0 \leq C \leq 1$)
- $\tau$ = characteristic relaxation time

**Why QLV is preferred over SLS in clinical settings:**

1. **Non-linearity:** QLV accommodates the **non-linear elastic response** $\sigma^e(\varepsilon)$ of soft tissue, which SLS cannot (SLS is purely linear in the spring elements).
2. **Strain-history dependence:** QLV correctly models the **load-history-dependent** behavior seen in surgical grafts and tendons during rehabilitation, where the prior loading sequence alters the future response.

**Two clinical situations where QLV is preferred:**

1. **Post-operative tendon graft remodeling** — the graft's mechanical response changes over weeks/months; QLV captures this gradual adaptation better than a fixed-parameter SLS.
2. **Rehabilitation of Achilles tendon rupture** — load progression protocols are designed using QLV-predicted creep and relaxation rather than linear viscoelastic extrapolations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Carry)**

- [ ] **Tendon = anisotropic, non-linear, viscoelastic composite** of Type I collagen + elastin + ground substance + water.
- [ ] **Hierarchical structure:** tropocollagen → microfibril → fibril → fiber → fascicle → tendon.
- [ ] **Four hallmarks:** creep, stress relaxation, hysteresis, strain-rate sensitivity.
- [ ] **Young's modulus of tendon ≈ 1.0–1.5 GPa**; **UTS ≈ 50–100 MPa**; **failure strain ≈ 6–10%**.
- [ ] **Three building blocks:** linear spring (Hookean), dashpot (Newtonian), friction slider.
- [ ] **Maxwell model** (spring + dashpot in series): predicts stress relaxation but unbounded creep.
- [ ] **Kelvin–Voigt model** (spring + dashpot in parallel): predicts bounded creep but no stress relaxation.
- [ ] **Standard Linear Solid (SLS / Zener)** = Maxwell branch in parallel with a second spring. **Best classical model for tendons.**
- [ ] SLS constitutive equation: $\sigma + \tau_\sigma \dot{\sigma} = E_R (\varepsilon + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\varepsilon})$.
- [ ] SLS stress relaxation: $\sigma(t) = E_2 \varepsilon_0 + E_1 \varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau_\sigma}$.
- [ ] SLS creep: $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0 / E_2)[1 - E_1/(E_1+E_2) e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon}]$.
- [ ] **Unrelaxed modulus $E_U = E_1 + E_2$** (instantaneous).
- [ ] **Relaxed modulus $E_R = E_2$** (long-term).
- [ ] **Two time constants:** $\tau_\sigma = \eta / E_1$ and $\tau_\varepsilon = \eta (E_1 + E_2) / (E_1 E_2)$.
- [ ] **Fung's QLV theory:** decomposes stress into elastic response × reduced relaxation function.
- [ ] **QLV reduced relaxation:** $G(t) = 1 - C(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- [ ] **Hysteresis ratio** for healthy tendon: **≈ 5–10%** (low → efficient energy return).
- [ ] **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta = E'' / E'$ quantifies internal damping.
- [ ] **Strain-rate sensitivity:** $E = K \dot{\varepsilon}^n$, with $n \approx 0.05 - 0.10$ for tendon.
- [ ] **Clinical relevance:** sutures must be retightened intraoperatively (stress relaxation); tendons rupture at high strain rates (e.g., sprint jumps); grafts remodel following QLV kinetics.
- [ ] **Examiner's red flags to avoid:** confusing J(t) with G(t), mixing E_U with E_R, using Maxwell where SLS is required, and omitting initial-condition checks in derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tendon Interaction with Surrounding Tissues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tendon Interaction with Surrounding Tissues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tendon Interaction with Surrounding Tissues** refers to the structural, mechanical, and biochemical relationships that exist between a tendon and the adjacent anatomical structures it contacts during physiological function. These interactions govern force transmission, tendon excursion, vascular perfusion, and friction management. The surrounding tissues include the **paratenon**, **epitenon**, **endotenon**, **synovial sheaths**, **vincula**, **pulleys**, **bursae**, **retinacula**, and adjacent neurovascular bundles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The interaction is fundamentally a *tribological* problem — tendons must glide with **minimal friction** while transmitting large tensile loads, a paradox resolved by specialized connective tissue architecture and synovial lubrication.

### 1.2 Key Surrounding Tissues — Defined

| Tissue | Definition |
|--------|------------|
| **Paratenon** | A loose, areolar connective tissue sleeve enveloping tendons that do **not** possess a synovial sheath (e.g., Achilles tendon). |
| **Epitenon** | A dense, fibrous connective tissue layer that **directly** ensheathes the entire tendon surface. |
| **Endotenon** | A thin connective tissue septa that subdivides the tendon into **fascicles** and carries neurovascular supply. |
| **Synovial Sheath** | A double-walled tubular structure (visceral + parietal layers) enveloping tendons at high-friction sites (digits, wrist). |
| **Vincula (Vinculae)** | Mesentery-like folds carrying blood vessels to the tendon within a sheath. |
| **Pulleys** | Fibrous/reinforced bands that hold tendons close to the bone to prevent *bowstringing* (e.g., A1–A5 pulleys of the finger). |
| **Bursae** | Closed, fluid-filled sacs that reduce friction between tendon and adjacent bone or ligament. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a **rope sliding through a curved metal pipe** that the rope must bend around. The pipe (surrounding tissue) holds the rope (tendon) in place, but if the rope just rubbed directly on metal, it would wear out. To fix this:
> 1. We **wrap the rope in a slippery plastic sleeve** (epitenon + synovial fluid).
> 2. We **inflate a thin balloon of oil between sleeve and pipe** (synovial cavity).
> 3. We **add oil holes that drip lubricant onto the rope** (vincula blood supply).
> 4. We **clamp the pipe with metal rings** at key points to stop the rope from jumping out (pulleys).
>
> This is exactly how tendons interact with their surrounding tissues.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Coefficient of friction (tendon gliding in synovial sheath): **μ ≈ 0.001 to 0.01** (one of the lowest in the human body).
- Coefficient of friction (tendon on paratenon): **μ ≈ 0.1 to 0.3**.
- Normal synovial fluid viscosity at 37 °C: **η ≈ 0.001 to 0.01 Pa·s**.
- Sheath fluid film thickness: **h ≈ 0.001 to 0.05 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of Tendon-Surrounding Tissue Interaction

The interaction is governed by a four-tier logical hierarchy:

### Tier 1 — *Micro-Scale Architecture*
- Tropocollagen molecules (length ≈ **300 nm**, diameter ≈ **1.5 nm**) assemble into microfibrils → fibrils → fibers.
- These fibers are bundled by **endotenon** septa into **fascicles** (50–400 μm diameter).

### Tier 2 — *Surface Encasement*
- The entire fascicle bundle is wrapped externally by **epitenon** (thickness ≈ 10–50 μm).
- The epitenon presents a **smooth, low-friction gliding surface**.

### Tier 3 — *Macro-Sleeve (Paratenon OR Synovial Sheath)*
- In **avascular environments with low excursion** (e.g., Achilles): paratenon acts as a sliding sleeve.
- In **high-excursion, angulated environments** (e.g., flexor digitorum): a closed synovial sheath forms.

### Tier 4 — *Anchoring & Guiding Structures*
- **Pulleys, retinacula, and fibrous bands** maintain tendon-bone apposition.
- **Bursae** cushion tendon-bone contact at pressure points.
- **Vincula** sustain blood supply across the closed sheath.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Friction Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is friction management critical?**
> - High friction → **tendon wear, fraying, adhesions** (e.g., post-surgical stiffness).
> - High friction → **energy loss** (up to 30 % of muscle work dissipated as heat).
> - Reduced excursion → **loss of joint range of motion (ROM)** and contracture.

> [!NOTE]
> **How is friction minimized?**
> 1. **Boundary lubrication** — lubricin (a glycoprotein, MW ≈ 227 kDa) coats tendon surfaces.
> 2. **Hydrodynamic lubrication** — synovial fluid is dragged into the contact zone during motion.
> 3. **Weeping lubrication** — interstitial fluid weeps from cartilage/bone under load to bathe the tendon.
> 4. **Elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** — a combined mechanism dominant in vivo.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{f} = \mu N$ | Classical Amontons' friction law (μ = coefficient, N = normal force) | N |
| 2 | $F_{glide} = \mu W$ | Gliding resistance of tendon under tension W | N |
| 3 | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | Newtonian shear stress in synovial film | Pa |
| 4 | $h_{min} \approx 4.9 \, R \, (\eta \, U / W)^{0.68}$ | Elastohydrodynamic minimum film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson) | m |
| 5 | $P_{syn} = \dfrac{2 T \sin(\theta/2)}{r}$ | Synovial sheath pressure (T = tendon tension, θ = bend angle, r = pulley radius) | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{pulley} = \dfrac{T}{R_{pulley}}$ | Contact pressure at pulley on tendon | Pa |
| 7 | $F_{bowstring} = T \sin\theta$ | Out-of-plane bowstringing force | N |
| 8 | $\mu_{eff} = \dfrac{F_{friction}}{W_{tendon}}$ | Effective coefficient of friction in vivo | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$ | Poiseuille synovial fluid flow through vincula (r = vessel radius) | m³/s |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Tensile stress in tendon (F = tension, A = CSA) | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU VALUATION:** In exams, **always state units** in your final answer box. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing SI unit on derivations.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This biomechanical framework is directly applied in:
- **Tendon repair surgery** — design of pulley reconstruction and A2/A4 pulley biomechanics.
- **Tissue engineering** — biomimetic tendon scaffolds mimicking endotenon/epitenon layering.
- **Rehabilitation engineering** — controlled passive motion (CPM) devices to prevent adhesion.
- **Robotic prosthetic design** — Bowden cables employ identical tribology principles.
- **Sport shoe design** — Achilles tendon-paratenon interaction informs heel counter geometry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Gliding Resistance of a Tendon Inside a Synovial Sheath

We model a tendon of tension $W$ (Newton) sliding through a fixed curved synovial sheath of bend angle $\theta$ (radians) wrapped around a pulley of radius $R$ (meters).

**Step 1: Free-body decomposition of the tendon at the pulley arc.**

Consider an infinitesimal arc element of tendon subtending angle $d\theta$ at the pulley center. The tension $W$ acts tangentially at both ends of the element; a normal reaction $dN$ acts radially outward from the pulley.

**Step 2: Equilibrium in the radial direction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum of forces in radial direction} &= 0 \\
dN - W \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) - \bigl[W \sin\!\bigl(\tfrac{d\theta}{2}\bigr)\bigr] &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The two tension components act on opposite sides of the arc; for an infinitesimal angle, each contributes $W \sin(d\theta/2)$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dN &= 2 W \sin\!\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) \\
\text{For } d\theta \to 0: \quad \sin(d\theta/2) &\approx d\theta/2 \\
\therefore dN &= 2 W \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = W \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Integrate to obtain total normal force over the wrap angle $\theta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= \int_{0}^{\theta} W \, d\theta = W \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Amontons' friction law to find the frictional resistance (capstan equation).**

The differential friction force over arc element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dF_{f} &= \mu \, dN = \mu W \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrate around the wrap angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{glide} &= \int_{0}^{\theta} \mu W \, d\theta = \mu W \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,F_{glide} = \mu \, W \, \theta\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **capstan (Euler-Eytelwein) equation** for tendons, identical in form to the classical belt-friction equation. It is the single most important equation in tendon biomechanics — used in KTU problems on flexor tendon excursion and pulley design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Synovial Sheath Pressure on the Tendon

**Step 1: Geometric setup.**

Tendon of tension $W$ bends around a pulley of radius $R$ over a contact arc angle $\theta$. The contact arc length is $s = R\theta$.

**Step 2: Contact pressure (force per unit area).**

Contact area $A = b \cdot s = b R \theta$, where $b$ = tendon width.

Average pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= \frac{N_{total}}{A} = \frac{W \theta}{b R \theta} = \frac{W}{b R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum pressure (at the apex of the wrap).**

By curved-contact Hertz-like analysis, peak pressure is approximately twice the average:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{max} \approx \frac{2W}{b R}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication Film Thickness (Hamrock-Dowson, for ball-in-socket analog)

For a tendon in a curved sheath (analogous to a cylinder in a socket), the minimum EHL film thickness is:

**Step 1: Isoviscous-elastic regime formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{min} &= 7.43 \, (1 - 0.85 \cdot e^{-0.31 k}) \cdot (U^{*})^{0.65} (W^{*})^{-0.21} \\
\text{where } U^{*} &= \frac{\eta_0 U}{E' R_x}, \quad W^{*} = \frac{W}{E' R_x^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Simplify for the in-vivo tendon case.**

For typical tendon values ($\eta_0 \approx 0.001$ Pa·s, $U \approx 0.01$ m/s, $E' \approx 10^{7}$ Pa, $R_x \approx 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U^{*} &\approx \frac{(10^{-3})(0.01)}{(10^{7})(5 \times 10^{-3})} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \\
W^{*} &\approx \frac{50}{(10^{7})(2.5 \times 10^{-5})} = 2 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substituting yields:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{min} &\approx 7.43 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-8})^{0.65} (2 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.21} \\
&\approx 7.43 \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 13.5 \\
&\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Converting to physical film thickness.**

$$
\boxed{\,h_{min} = H_{min} \cdot R_x \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \approx 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5 \,\mu\text{m}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **5 μm film thickness** is experimentally confirmed — the synovial fluid film between tendon and sheath is barely a few cell-widths thick, yet it reduces friction by **two orders of magnitude** compared to dry contact.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Friction Mechanisms

| Feature | Paratenon Type | Synovial Sheath Type |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| Tendon examples | Achilles, quadriceps | Flexor digitorum, FHL |
| Friction coefficient μ | **0.1 – 0.3** | **0.001 – 0.01** |
| Lubrication | Boundary (lubricin) | EHL + boundary |
| Vascular supply | Paratenon microvessels | **Vincula only** |
| Healing after injury | Good (vascular) | Poor (avascular zones) |
| Adhesion risk | Low | High (post-surgical) |
| Bowstringing risk | Medium | Low (pulleys present) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Tendon & Surrounding Tissue Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[COLLAGEN MOLECULE Level 1] --> B[Microfibril Level 2]
    B --> C[Subfibril Level 3]
    C --> D[Fibril Level 4]
    D --> E[Fiber Level 5]
    E --> F[Fascicle Level 6]
    F --> G[ENDOTENON septa between fascicles]
    G --> H[Whole Tendon Bundle]
    H --> I[EPITENON outer sheath]
    I --> J{Site Type Decision}
    J -->|Low excursion avascular| K[PARATENON loose areolar sleeve]
    J -->|High excursion angulated| L[SYNOVIAL SHEATH double walled]
    L --> M[Parietal Layer outer]
    L --> N[Visceral Layer inner]
    M --> O[Synovial Fluid Film h 1 to 50 microns]
    N --> O
    L --> P[VINCULA vascular mesentery folds]
    K --> Q[PULLEYS fibrous anchoring bands]
    L --> Q
    Q --> R[BONE Cortical Contact Surface]
    H --> S[BURSAE fluid filled cushion]
    S --> R
```

## 4.2 Force Transmission & Lubrication Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Muscle Force Fm] --> B[Myotendinous Junction]
    B --> C[Tendon Tension W]
    C --> D[Pulley Wrap Angle theta]
    D --> E[Normal Force N equal to W theta]
    E --> F[Friction Force mu W theta]
    F --> G[Gliding Resistance Fg]
    C --> H[Bowstringing Force T sin theta]
    H --> I[Pulley Constraint Reaction]
    I --> J[Anchored Tendon Path]
    K[Vincula Blood Supply] --> L[Synovial Fluid Production]
    L --> M[Lubricin Glycoprotein Secretion]
    M --> N[EHL Film Thickness h]
    N --> O[Reduced Friction mu down to 0.001]
    O --> G
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Functional Architecture of a Synovial Sheath

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Synovial Sheath Cross Section]
        T1[TENDON core] --> ET[EPITENON thin fibrous layer]
        ET --> SF1[Synovial Fluid Film h]
        SF1 --> VL[Visceral Layer]
        VL --> SC2[Synovial Cavity]
        SC2 --> PL[Parietal Layer]
        PL --> SF2[Fibrous Sheath Outer Wall]
        SF2 --> BT[Bone or Retinaculum]
        V1[Vinculum Longum] -.-> T1
        V2[Vinculum Breve] -.-> T1
        V3[Mesotenon] -.-> T1
    end
    V1 --> AS[Arteriole Supply]
    V2 --> AS
    V3 --> AS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagrams:**
> - The first diagram shows the **hierarchical build-up** of tendon from molecules to whole organ with surrounding layers.
> - The second diagram shows the **force and lubrication flow** — how muscle force becomes constrained tendon motion with minimal friction.
> - The third diagram shows the **closed-loop synovial sheath** with vincula as vascular lifelines (analogous to gut mesentery).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define paratenon and epitenon. State two differences between them.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Paratenon:** A loose, areolar connective tissue sleeve that envelopes tendons in regions where synovial sheaths are absent. It carries the tendon's blood supply and permits free gliding against adjacent fascia.
- **Epitenon:** A thin, dense fibrous connective tissue layer that **directly** wraps the entire outer surface of the tendon, providing a smooth gliding surface.

| Feature | Paratenon | Epitenon |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Tissue type | Loose areolar | Dense fibrous |
| Blood supply | Carries vessels | Avascular outer coat |

*(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**What are pulleys? State their biomechanical significance in the flexor tendon of the finger.**

**Model Answer:**

Pulleys are thickened, fibrous (or fibro-osseous) bands that hold the flexor tendons of the digits close to the phalangeal bones. They prevent **bowstringing** of the tendon during flexion, ensuring efficient conversion of tendon excursion into angular joint rotation.

Biomechanically, pulleys convert the linear pull of muscle into joint rotation, and the contact pressure $P = W/R$ (where $W$ is tendon tension and $R$ is pulley radius) generates the reaction force that holds the tendon against the bone.

*(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

### ✦ QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU ESE, Module 2, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **synovial sheath** of a flexor tendon. Explain the **role of vincula** in tendon vascularity. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **gliding resistance of a tendon** sliding around a curved pulley, starting from first principles. State the **clinical significance** of the result. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The synovial sheath is a double-walled tubular structure that envelops tendons in regions of high excursion and angulation (e.g., carpal tunnel, digital flexor sheaths).

**Structure:**
- **Outer (parietal) layer** — lines the inner surface of the fibrous sheath/bone.
- **Inner (visceral) layer** — adheres to the epitenon of the tendon.
- Between them lies the **synovial cavity** containing synovial fluid (hyaluronic acid + lubricin).
- The two layers are continuous at the proximal and distal ends of the sheath, forming a **closed system**.

**Vincula (Vinculae):**
- These are **mesentery-like folds** carrying arteries and veins from the parietal layer to the visceral layer of the tendon.
- **Vinculum longum** — long, slender fold at the proximal phalanx region.
- **Vinculum brevis** — short, stout fold near the insertion.
- They provide the **sole blood supply** to the tendon within the avascular sheath, particularly the **vincula brevia** of the flexor digitorum profundus near its insertion.

*(7 marks: 3 for structure + 2 for diagram + 2 for vincula)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

The gliding resistance is derived using the **capstan equation**. (Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above.)

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,F_{glide} = \mu \, W \, \theta\,}
$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction, $W$ is the tendon tension, and $\theta$ is the wrap angle in radians.

**Clinical significance:**
- Larger wrap angles $\theta$ → proportionally larger friction → more energy lost in flexor tendon work.
- Post-surgical adhesion formation effectively **increases $\mu$** by 10–100×, dramatically increasing $F_{glide}$ and limiting active finger flexion.
- Pulley design (smaller R) increases $P = W/R$ but does **not** change $\mu$ or $F_{glide}$ directly — however it elevates **contact pressure** that may damage the tendon.

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation + 1 for boxed result + 2 for clinical significance)*

---

### ✦ QUESTION B — 14 Marks [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **four lubrication mechanisms** that operate in tendon-sheath systems. Compare their relative effectiveness. (7 marks)

**(b)** The flexor digitorum profundus tendon in the finger wraps around a pulley of radius **6 mm** over an angle of **180°** under a muscle tension of **20 N**. Given $\mu = 0.005$, calculate:
   - (i) the normal force on the pulley,
   - (ii) the gliding resistance,
   - (iii) the maximum contact pressure (assume tendon width = 8 mm). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

The four lubrication mechanisms in tendon-sheath systems are:

1. **Boundary lubrication** — A mono-molecular layer of **lubricin** (a mucinous glycoprotein) adsorbs on both tendon (epitenon) and sheath (visceral layer) surfaces. Effective even at rest (zero velocity). Reduces $\mu$ from ~0.3 to ~0.05.

2. **Hydrodynamic lubrication** — During motion, the relative velocity drags synovial fluid into the converging gap, generating a pressure that **separates** the two surfaces. Velocity-dependent: thicker film at higher speeds.

3. **Elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** — Combines elastic deformation of tendon + hydrodynamic pressure of fluid. This is the **dominant in-vivo mechanism** for tendons. Film thickness $h \approx 1$–$5 \ \mu m$.

4. **Weeping lubrication** — Synovial fluid is expressed from cartilage and tendon pores under pressure, bathing the contact zone. Important at very low velocities and under sustained loads.

**Effectiveness ranking** (for typical flexor tendon):
- EHL > Hydrodynamic > Boundary > Weeping
- Combined, they reduce $\mu$ from 0.3 (dry) to **0.001–0.01** (lubricated).

*(7 marks: 4 for mechanisms + 2 for ranking + 1 for summary)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- $R = 6$ mm $= 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\theta = 180° = \pi$ rad
- $W = 20$ N
- $\mu = 0.005$
- Tendon width $b = 8$ mm $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**(i) Normal force on the pulley:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= W \theta = 20 \times \pi \\
\boxed{N_{total}} &= 20\pi \approx 62.83 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Gliding resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{glide} &= \mu \, W \, \theta = 0.005 \times 20 \times \pi \\
\boxed{F_{glide}} &= 0.1 \pi \approx 0.314 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum contact pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &\approx \frac{2W}{bR} = \frac{2 \times 20}{(8 \times 10^{-3})(6 \times 10^{-3})} \\
&= \frac{40}{48 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{40}{4.8 \times 10^{-5}} \\
\boxed{P_{max}} &\approx 8.33 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 833 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in $\theta$. The equation $F_{glide} = \mu W \theta$ uses **radians only**. Using degrees gives a wrong answer off by a factor of 57.3. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up bowstringing force** $T\sin\theta$ with gliding friction $\mu W\theta$ — these are different physical quantities. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting units in final answer** — KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks per missing SI unit** on numerical problems. Loss: up to **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing paratenon with epitenon** — paratenon is *external* to epitenon. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Missing vincula in synovial sheath diagrams** — examiners specifically look for these vascular folds. Loss: **1–2 marks**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- 🔑 **Tendon = hierarchical collagen structure** (molecules → fascicles) wrapped in **endotenon** (internal) and **epitenon** (external).
- 🔑 **Two types of outer covering:** **Paratenon** (loose, vascular, no sheath) and **Synovial sheath** (closed, double-walled, with fluid).
- 🔑 **Vincula** = sole blood supply to tendons inside synovial sheaths (analogous to gut mesentery).
- 🔑 **Pulleys** hold tendons close to bone; they prevent bowstringing; pressure $P = W/R$.
- 🔑 **Bursae** cushion tendon-bone contact; reduce local pressure.
- 🔑 **Friction coefficient:** $\mu \approx 0.001$–$0.01$ inside sheaths, $\mu \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ under paratenon.
- 🔑 **Capstan equation** (most-tested): $F_{glide} = \mu W \theta$ — **always use radians**.
- 🔑 **Four lubrication mechanisms:** Boundary, Hydrodynamic, Elastohydrodynamic, Weeping.
- 🔑 **EHL is dominant** in vivo; film thickness $h \approx 1$–$5\ \mu m$.
- 🔑 **Clinical corollaries:** Adhesions ↑μ, post-op rehab via CPM, pulley rupture → bowstringing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"PEPS-VPB"** = **P**aratenon, **E**pitenon, (endotenon implied), **P**ulleys, **S**heath, **V**incula, **P**aratenon, **B**ursae.
> **"BHEW"** = Lubrication order: **B**oundary, **H**ydrodynamic, **E**HL, **W**eeping.
> **"$\mu W\theta$"** = Gliding resistance. R-A-D-I-A-N-S!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties of Passive Muscles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties of Passive Muscles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Passive Muscle Mechanics

In the context of **Biomechanics (PCBMT601)**, the **passive mechanical properties of muscle** refer to the non-contractile, force-generating (or force-resisting) behavior exhibited by skeletal muscle tissue *in the absence of active neural stimulation or cross-bridge cycling between actin and myosin filaments*.

This passive behavior originates almost entirely from the **connective tissue scaffolding** surrounding and within the muscle, namely:

- **Epimysium** — the dense irregular connective tissue sheath enveloping the entire muscle belly.
- **Perimysium** — the connective tissue wrapping bundles of muscle fibers (fascicles).
- **Endomysium** — the delicate reticular fiber network surrounding individual muscle fibers.

These connective tissues, along with the giant structural protein **titin** (which spans from the Z-disc to the myosin thick filament), govern how a muscle responds to externally applied stretch when it is *electrically quiet* (i.e., no motor unit firing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The passive component is mathematically and physically distinct from the active (contractile) component. The total muscle force $F_{total}$ is the linear superposition:
> $$F_{total} = F_{passive}(L) + F_{active}(L, \, v, \, a)$$
> where $F_{passive}$ depends only on instantaneous length $L$, while $F_{active}$ depends on length $L$, shortening velocity $v$, and neural activation $a$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stretched Rubber Band Inside a Towel

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a wet cotton towel that you pull from both ends. The towel itself is soft, but as you stretch it further and further, you can feel increasing resistance. The towel never "decides" to resist — it just *is* that way because of how the cotton fibers are woven. Passive muscle behaves identically. Even when "asleep" (no neural signal), it has a built-in mechanical stiffness that grows as you stretch it.

Think of muscle as a **suspension bridge cable** with three parallel structural elements:

1. The **steel cables** → the **contractile element** (actin–myosin, active only when commanded).
2. The **outer rubberized weather-proofing sleeve** → the **parallel elastic element (PEE)** (epimysium, perimysium, endomysium, titin).
3. The **internal chains and pulleys** → the **series elastic element (SEE)** (tendons, aponeuroses, cross-bridge compliance).

When no signal reaches the muscle, *only* elements 2 and 3 are doing mechanical work. Their combined response is the **passive mechanical property** of muscle.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Metrics You Must Memorize:**
> - **Resting sarcomere length** $L_{s,0} \approx \mathbf{2.0\text{–}2.25 \, \mu m}$ (mammalian skeletal muscle).
> - **Optimal sarcomere length for active force** $L_{s,opt} \approx \mathbf{2.0\text{–}2.25 \, \mu m}$ (overlap zone peak).
> - **Yield (passive) force begins to rise sharply** at sarcomere lengths $\gtrsim \mathbf{2.7 \, \mu m}$ (toe-region limit).
> - **Passive muscle density** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1.06 \, g/cm^3}$ (similar to soft tissue average).
> - **Young's modulus of passive muscle (linear region)** $E \approx \mathbf{10\text{–}300 \, kPa}$ (highly strain-dependent).

## 1.4 Active vs. Passive — A Clear Distinction

| Aspect | Active Component | Passive Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Actin–myosin cross-bridges | Connective tissue, titin, tendons |
| **Energy input** | ATP hydrolysis required | None (purely mechanical) |
| **Depends on** | Length, velocity, activation $a$ | Length only (and strain rate for viscoelasticity) |
| **Existence when** $a=0$ | Zero force | Non-zero force beyond slack length |
| **Mathematical form** | Hill-type force–velocity | Exponential or polynomial spring |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Architecture of Passive Resistance

The passive load-bearing architecture of muscle is conventionally decomposed into **two elastic compartments** operating *in parallel* and *in series* with the contractile element. The Hill-type three-element muscle model expresses this decomposition rigorously.

### 2.1.1 Parallel Elastic Element (PEE)

The PEE represents the collective compliance of the **endomysium, perimysium, epimysium, sarcolemma, and titin**. It is engaged *in parallel* with the contractile element.

- Operates only when the muscle is stretched **beyond its slack length** $L_{slack}$.
- Below $L_{slack}$, the PEE carries **no force** (the tissue is slack/wavy).
- The PEE force is **independent of velocity** under purely elastic assumptions.

### 2.1.2 Series Elastic Element (SEE)

The SEE represents the compliance of **tendons, aponeuroses, and the cross-bridge attachments** themselves. It is engaged *in series* with the contractile element.

- Always present (even at short lengths), but in series the same force passes through it.
- SEE compliance is what allows **series elastic recoil** during the stretch–shortening cycle.
- SEE stiffness determines how quickly contractile force can be transmitted to bone.

### 2.1.3 Why Two Elements and Not One?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification:**
> PEE and SEE have *fundamentally different physiological roles*. The PEE determines the muscle's **resting length-tension relationship** and prevents over-stretching. The SEE determines the muscle's ability to **store and release strain energy** rapidly (e.g., during running, jumping, tendon stretch). Combining them into one would obscure their distinct biomechanical functions.

## 2.2 The Passive Force–Length Curve

The most experimentally measured and most-tested KTU property is the **passive force–length curve**, obtained by stretching a fully relaxed (deafferented, denervated, or post-mortem) muscle to progressively longer lengths and recording the resisting force.

The curve has **three distinct regions** — you must be able to identify and label each one in a KTU diagram:

| Region | Sarcomere Length Range | Mechanical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Toe Region** | $L_s \le L_{slack}$ | Negligible force; wavy collagen straightens |
| **Heel (Transition) Region** | $L_{slack} < L_s \le L_{s,0} + \Delta$ | Force begins to rise nonlinearly |
| **Linear (Stiff) Region** | $L_s \gtrsim 2.7 \, \mu m$ | Near-linear force vs. length; collagen fully taut |
| **Failure Region** | $L_s \gtrsim 3.5\text{–}4.0 \, \mu m$ | Micro-damage begins; sarcomere "popping" |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert or \mid for absolute value bars inside table cells. Never use raw | inside markdown tables.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Cauchy (engineering) stress; $A_0$ is resting cross-sectional area (often PCSA) | $Pa = N/m^2$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | Engineering (Lagrangian) strain; dimensionless | Unitless |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's modulus (valid in linear-elastic region only) | $Pa$ |
| 4 | $F_{PEE}(L) = k_{PEE}\,(L - L_{slack})^{n}$ | Power-law PEE force model; $n \approx 2\text{–}3$ | $N$ |
| 5 | $F_{PEE}(L) = a\,(e^{b\,(L - L_{slack})} - 1)$ | Exponential PEE force model (best fit) | $N$ |
| 6 | $F_{passive} = F_{PEE} + F_{SEE}$ | Total passive force as sum of PEE and SEE | $N$ |
| 7 | $L_{slack}$ | Length at which the PEE first begins to bear load | $m$ or $\mu m$ |
| 8 | $W_{el} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,k\,(\Delta L)^{2}$ | Elastic strain energy stored in spring of stiffness $k$ | $J$ |
| 9 | $\tau = \eta / E$ | Relaxation time constant (ratio of dashpot viscosity to spring modulus) | $s$ |
| 10 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}$ | Strain rate — controls viscoelastic response | $s^{-1}$ |

## 2.4 Viscoelasticity — The Time-Dependent Behavior

Pure passive muscle is **not a Hookean spring**. It is a **viscoelastic material** — its mechanical response depends on *both* the magnitude of the deformation *and* the rate at which the deformation is applied.

Two hallmark viscoelastic phenomena are repeatedly examined in KTU module 2:

### 2.4.1 Creep
When a **constant stress** $\sigma_0$ is suddenly applied, the strain $\varepsilon$ **continues to increase over time** rather than reaching a constant value instantly. This is **creep**.

### 2.4.2 Stress Relaxation
When a **constant strain** $\varepsilon_0$ is suddenly imposed and held, the stress **decays over time** from its initial peak $\sigma_0$ toward a steady-state value $\sigma_\infty$. This is **stress relaxation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Creep and stress relaxation are critical in **prosthetic design, surgical suture performance, ligament graft healing, and long-duration ergonomic loading** (e.g., a worker holding a tool overhead). The Hill-type models discussed in Section 3 mathematically reproduce both phenomena.

## 2.5 Hysteresis and Energy Dissipation

When a muscle is **loaded and then unloaded**, the unloading curve lies *below* the loading curve. The enclosed area represents **energy dissipated as heat** (viscous loss). This phenomenon is called **hysteresis** (or **mechanical hysteresis loop**).

$$\Delta W_{dissipated} = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Hysteresis:**
> The hysteresis area quantifies **energy lost per loading cycle**. For tendons and muscles acting as biological springs, the *stored* elastic energy minus the *dissipated* energy is the **net recoverable energy** available for efficient locomotion (e.g., galloping horses, kangaroo hopping, human running).

## 2.6 Three Canonical Viscoelastic Models

The mathematical models that students must know for KTU derivations are summarized below. Each is a specific arrangement of springs (elastic) and dashpots (viscous).

| Model Name | Topology | Stress–Strain Equation | Creep Response | Stress-Relaxation Response |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Maxwell** | Spring $\vert\vert$ Dashpot (in series) | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$ | Unbounded creep (like a fluid) | Exponential decay to zero |
| **Kelvin–Voigt** | Spring $\vert\vert$ Dashpot (in parallel) | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon + \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$ | Saturating exponential (no instantaneous strain) | **No stress relaxation** under step strain |
| **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** | Maxwell arm $\vert\vert$ single spring | $\sigma + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\sigma} = E_\infty \varepsilon + (E_0 + E_\infty)\tau_\sigma \dot{\varepsilon}$ | Saturating creep | Saturating stress relaxation (best fit for muscle) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** model — also called the **Zener model** — is the most accurate for skeletal muscle passive behavior because it predicts *both* finite creep *and* finite stress relaxation. KTU frequently asks students to identify the model or write its constitutive equation.

## 2.7 Strain-Rate Dependence

Because passive muscle is viscoelastic, the *same* final length reached at *different speeds* produces *different* resisting forces:

- **Fast stretch** (high $\dot{\varepsilon}$) → higher peak force (tissue behaves stiffer).
- **Slow stretch** (low $\dot{\varepsilon}$) → lower peak force (tissue has time to reorganize).

This rate sensitivity is mathematically captured by the dashpot term $\eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$ in the constitutive models above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear-Region Hookean Stress from a Passive Stretch Experiment

**Problem Setup (typical KTU Part B format):**
A passive muscle specimen of resting length $L_0 = 10 \, cm$ and cross-sectional area $A_0 = 2 \, cm^2$ is stretched to $L = 11.5 \, cm$. The measured tensile force is $F = 4 \, N$ in the linear region. Compute the engineering stress, engineering strain, and Young's modulus.

**Step 1 — Compute the engineering strain.**

The engineering strain is defined as the change in length divided by the original (reference) length.

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{11.5 \, cm - 10 \, cm}{10 \, cm} = \frac{1.5}{10} = 0.15
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the engineering stress.**

The engineering stress is the tensile force divided by the *original* (undeformed) cross-sectional area.

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{4 \, N}{2 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2} = 2 \times 10^{4} \, Pa = 20 \, kPa
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the linear-elastic Hooke's law relation.**

In the linear region of the stress–strain curve, stress and strain are proportional. The proportionality constant is the Young's modulus.

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{2 \times 10^{4} \, Pa}{0.15} = 1.333 \times 10^{5} \, Pa \approx 133.3 \, kPa
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check against typical passive muscle values.**

The computed $E \approx 133 \, kPa$ lies inside the empirical range of $10 \text{–} 300 \, kPa$ for passive muscle, so the result is physically reasonable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $E_{passive} \approx 133 \, kPa$, which is $\sim 1000$ times lower than structural steel ($E_{steel} \approx 200 \, GPa$) and $\sim 10$ times lower than cartilage ($E_{cartilage} \approx 1\text{–}10 \, MPa$). Muscle is one of the softest load-bearing biological tissues.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stress Relaxation in a Maxwell Element

**Problem Setup:**
A Maxwell element consists of a spring (modulus $E$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected in series. A step strain $\varepsilon_0$ is applied instantaneously and held. Derive the stress response $\sigma(t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the kinematic relation for series connection.**

In a series arrangement, the total strain is the sum of the strain in each element.

$$
\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot}
$$

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{total} = \dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} + \dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the constitutive laws for each element.**

For the linear spring:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E}
$$

For the linear (Newtonian) dashpot:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{total} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the step-strain boundary condition.**

A step strain is mathematically represented as:

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, H(t)
$$

where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function. The time derivative is:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \varepsilon_0 \, \delta(t)
$$

For $t > 0$ (after the step), $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ because the strain is held constant. Substituting $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ into the Maxwell equation:

$$
0 = \frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the resulting first-order ODE.**

Rearranging:

$$
\dot{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta}\,\sigma
$$

This is a first-order linear ODE with the solution:

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma(0)\, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the initial condition.**

At the instant the step is applied ($t = 0^+$), the dashpot has not yet deformed. All the strain is borne by the spring alone.

$$
\sigma(0^+) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0
$$

**Step 6 — Write the complete stress-relaxation response.**

$$
\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> - $E\,\varepsilon_0$ is the **instantaneous peak stress** at $t = 0$.
> - $\tau = \eta / E$ is the **relaxation time constant**. For passive muscle, $\tau$ is typically on the order of $\mathbf{1\text{–}10 \, s}$.
> - As $t \to \infty$, the stress decays to zero because the dashpot eventually carries all the deformation and the spring is unstressed.
> - In the Standard Linear Solid model (Section 2.6), the stress decays to a *non-zero* asymptotic value $E_\infty \varepsilon_0$, which is more realistic for muscle.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Creep Response of a Kelvin–Voigt Element

**Problem Setup:**
A Kelvin–Voigt element consists of a spring (modulus $E$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected in *parallel*. A step stress $\sigma_0$ is suddenly applied at $t = 0$. Derive the strain response $\varepsilon(t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the force/stress equilibrium for parallel connection.**

In a parallel arrangement, the total stress is the sum of the stress in each branch.

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sigma_{spring} + \sigma_{dashpot}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the constitutive laws.**

For the spring: $\sigma_{spring} = E\,\varepsilon$

For the dashpot: $\sigma_{dashpot} = \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{total} = E\,\varepsilon + \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the step-stress boundary condition.**

$$
\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, H(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma(t > 0) = \sigma_0
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_0 = E\,\varepsilon(t) + \eta\,\dot{\varepsilon}(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the first-order linear ODE.**

Rearranging into standard form:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}(t) + \frac{E}{\eta}\,\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta}
$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Its general solution is the sum of the homogeneous and particular solutions.

**Homogeneous solution:**

$$
\varepsilon_h(t) = C \, e^{-(E/\eta)\,t}
$$

**Particular solution** (constant, since RHS is constant):

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}
$$

**General solution:**

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C\, e^{-(E/\eta)\,t}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the initial condition.**

The Kelvin–Voigt element cannot deform *instantaneously* under a step stress, because the dashpot resists sudden motion. Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon(0) = 0
$$

Substituting $t = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{E} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = -\frac{\sigma_0}{E}
$$

**Step 6 — Write the complete creep response.**

$$
\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E}\,\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr), \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:**
> - $\sigma_0 / E$ is the **asymptotic equilibrium strain** (the strain reached as $t \to \infty$).
> - The strain **rises smoothly** from 0 toward the asymptote, with time constant $\tau$.
> - There is **no instantaneous jump** in strain (unlike the Maxwell element), because the parallel dashpot prevents any sudden deformation.
> - This is why a Kelvin–Voigt model cannot exhibit *pure* stress relaxation — the spring always carries some load.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Strain Energy Stored in the Toe Region

**Problem Setup:**
A passive muscle follows the exponential force–length relation $F_{PEE}(L) = a\,(e^{b(L - L_{slack})} - 1)$ for $L \ge L_{slack}$, and zero otherwise. Compute the strain energy stored when the muscle is stretched from $L_{slack}$ to $L_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Write the work integral.**

The work done in stretching the muscle equals the area under the force–length curve.

$$
W = \int_{L_{slack}}^{L_{max}} F_{PEE}(L) \, dL
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the force–length law.**

$$
W = \int_{L_{slack}}^{L_{max}} a\,\bigl(e^{b(L - L_{slack})} - 1\bigr) \, dL
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the integration term by term.**

For the exponential term, change variable $u = L - L_{slack}$, $du = dL$:

$$
\int_{L_{slack}}^{L_{max}} a\,e^{b(L - L_{slack})}\,dL = a \int_0^{L_{max} - L_{slack}} e^{b\,u}\,du = a \left[\frac{e^{b\,u}}{b}\right]_0^{L_{max} - L_{slack}} = \frac{a}{b}\,\bigl(e^{b(L_{max} - L_{slack})} - 1\bigr)
$$

For the constant term:

$$
\int_{L_{slack}}^{L_{max}} a\,dL = a\,(L_{max} - L_{slack})
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
W = \frac{a}{b}\,\bigl(e^{b\,\Delta L} - 1\bigr) - a\,\Delta L
$$

where $\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{slack}$. This can be rewritten compactly as:

$$
\boxed{W = a\left[\frac{e^{b\,\Delta L} - 1}{b} - \Delta L\right]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check (small $\Delta L$ limit):** Using $e^{b\,\Delta L} \approx 1 + b\,\Delta L + \tfrac{1}{2}b^2(\Delta L)^2$ for small $b\,\Delta L$, the expression reduces to $W \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\,a\,b\,(\Delta L)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,k\,(\Delta L)^2$ with effective stiffness $k = a\,b$, recovering Hookean spring behavior in the small-strain limit. This is a powerful consistency check for the model.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Model Selection

| Phenomenon | Maxwell | Kelvin–Voigt | Standard Linear Solid | Real Passive Muscle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Step-strain → stress relaxes? | Yes (to zero) | No (instantaneous) | Yes (to $E_\infty \varepsilon_0$) | Yes (to non-zero asymptote) |
| Step-stress → creep saturates? | No (linear forever) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Predicts hysteresis? | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Predicts strain-rate stiffness? | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Number of parameters | 2 ($E, \eta$) | 2 ($E, \eta$) | 3 ($E_0, E_\infty, \eta$) | $\ge 3$ |
| KTU recommendation | Teaching aid | Solid-like behavior | **Best fit for muscle** | Reference |

> - The **SEE** is in series with the contractile element (CE) — the same force passes through both.
> - The **PEE** branches are in parallel with the CE — they share the *length* but split the *force*.
> - When activation $a = 0$, only the SEE and PEE branches carry force — this combined force is the **passive force**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the parallel elastic element (PEE) in skeletal muscle. Why does it produce zero force at lengths below $L_{slack}$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The PEE represents the passive elastic resistance of the connective tissue surrounding muscle fibers (epimysium, perimysium, endomysium) and the structural protein titin. **(1 mark)** Below $L_{slack}$, the collagen fibers are in a wavy, crimped configuration and the tissue is mechanically slack, so the element carries no tensile load. **(1 mark)** Force develops only when the muscle is stretched sufficiently to straighten the crimped fibers, after which the PEE produces an exponentially rising restoring force. **(1 mark)**

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State two differences between Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt viscoelastic models in their response to a step stress.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The Maxwell model exhibits **unbounded (linear) creep** under step stress, because the dashpot can deform indefinitely while the spring reaches a fixed extension. **(1.5 marks)**
2. The Kelvin–Voigt model exhibits **bounded creep** that saturates at $\sigma_0 / E$ with time constant $\tau = \eta/E$, because the parallel dashpot prevents indefinite deformation. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **Question (a) [7 marks]:**  
> Derive the stress–strain constitutive equation for the Standard Linear Solid (SLS / Zener) model consisting of a Maxwell arm (spring $E_1$, dashpot $\eta$) in parallel with a spring $E_2$. Show that under a step strain $\varepsilon_0 H(t)$ the stress decays exponentially to a non-zero equilibrium value $E_2\,\varepsilon_0$.

> **Question (b) [7 marks]:**  
> A passive muscle specimen has resting length $L_0 = 12 \, cm$ and PCSA $A_0 = 3.5 \, cm^2$. When stretched to $L = 13.8 \, cm$ at low strain rate, the measured force is $F = 8.4 \, N$. Compute the engineering stress, engineering strain, and Young's modulus. State two assumptions made.

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Step 1 — Set up the parallel combination.**

Let $\sigma_1, \varepsilon_1$ denote the stress and strain in the Maxwell arm, and $\sigma_2, \varepsilon_2$ denote those in the parallel spring. For parallel connection:

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2, \quad \varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Maxwell arm constitutive equation.**

The spring and dashpot in the Maxwell arm are in series, so from Section 3.2:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} = \frac{\dot{\sigma}_1}{E_1} + \frac{\sigma_1}{\eta}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_1 = E_1 \left(\varepsilon - \int_0^t \frac{\sigma_1}{\eta}\,d\tau\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the parallel spring relation.**

$$
\sigma_2 = E_2\,\varepsilon
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the two contributions.**

$$
\sigma_{total} = \sigma_1 + E_2\,\varepsilon
$$

After algebraic rearrangement (taking derivatives and eliminating $\sigma_1$):

$$
\sigma_{total} + \tau_\varepsilon \dot{\sigma}_{total} = E_\infty \varepsilon + (E_0 + E_\infty)\tau_\sigma \dot{\varepsilon}
$$

where the four equivalent model parameters are:

$$
\tau_\varepsilon = \frac{\eta}{E_1}, \quad E_\infty = E_2, \quad E_0 = E_1 + E_2, \quad \tau_\sigma = \frac{\eta(E_1+E_2)}{E_1 E_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the step-strain boundary condition.**

For $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 H(t)$, we have $\dot{\varepsilon} = 0$ for $t > 0$. The ODE reduces to:

$$
\dot{\sigma} = -\frac{1}{\tau_\varepsilon}\sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma(t) = \sigma(0)\, e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 6 — Find the initial condition.**

At $t = 0^+$, both springs share the strain $\varepsilon_0$:

$$
\sigma(0^+) = (E_1 + E_2)\,\varepsilon_0
$$

**Step 7 — Final stress response.**

$$
\boxed{\sigma(t) = (E_1 + E_2)\varepsilon_0\, e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon} + E_2\,\varepsilon_0\,\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau_\varepsilon}\bigr)}
$$

As $t \to \infty$:

$$
\sigma(\infty) = E_2\,\varepsilon_0
$$

which is the **non-zero equilibrium stress** carried solely by the parallel spring $E_2$. The dashpot has relaxed, transferring the entire load to $E_2$. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Engineering strain.**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{13.8 - 12}{12} = 0.15
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Engineering stress.**

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{8.4}{3.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.4 \times 10^4 \, Pa = 24 \, kPa
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Young's modulus.**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^4}{0.15} = 1.6 \times 10^5 \, Pa = 160 \, kPa
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Assumptions.**

1. The muscle is in the **linear-elastic region** of its stress–strain curve. **(0.5 mark)**
2. The cross-section remains uniform during stretching (use of engineering stress, not true stress). **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]`

> **Question (a) [7 marks]:**  
> Explain the phenomenon of mechanical hysteresis in passive muscle. Sketch a typical loading–unloading force–length loop, and derive an expression for the energy dissipated per cycle.

> **Question (b) [7 marks]:**  
> A passive muscle is modeled as an exponential spring with $F_{PEE}(L) = a\,(e^{b(L - L_{slack})} - 1)$, where $a = 0.5 \, N$ and $b = 25 \, m^{-1}$. The slack length is $L_{slack} = 0.25 \, m$. Compute the strain energy stored when the muscle is stretched to $L = 0.27 \, m$.

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Step 1 — Define hysteresis.**

When a viscoelastic material is loaded to a given length and then unloaded back to the original length, the **unloading curve lies below the loading curve**. **(1 mark)** The closed area enclosed between the two curves represents **energy lost as heat** per cycle. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Sketch (described verbally for accessibility).**

A typical force–length hysteresis loop is an oval-shaped closed curve. The upper curve (loading) climbs steeply to the peak force $F_{max}$ at $L_{max}$. The lower curve (unloading) returns smoothly to the origin, reaching a lower force than the loading curve at every intermediate length. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Energy dissipated expression.**

The work done *on* the muscle during loading is:

$$
W_{load} = \int_{L_0}^{L_{max}} F_{load}(L)\,dL
$$

The work *recovered* during unloading is:

$$
W_{unload} = \int_{L_0}^{L_{max}} F_{unload}(L)\,dL
$$

The dissipated energy per cycle is the difference:

$$
\boxed{W_{dissipated} = W_{load} - W_{unload} = \oint F\,dL}
$$

**(2 marks)** This is also called the **hysteresis loss**, and for skeletal muscle it typically represents 5–15% of the stored elastic energy. **(1 mark)** Hysteresis is biologically essential in damping sudden impacts and protecting tissues from overload. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Compute the elongation.**

$$
\Delta L = L - L_{slack} = 0.27 - 0.25 = 0.02 \, m
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Set up the strain energy integral.**

$$
W = \int_{L_{slack}}^{L} a\,\bigl(e^{b(L' - L_{slack})} - 1\bigr)\,dL'
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
W = a\left[\frac{e^{b\,\Delta L} - 1}{b} - \Delta L\right]
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
e^{b\,\Delta L} = e^{25 \times 0.02} = e^{0.5} = 1.6487
$$

$$
\frac{1.6487 - 1}{25} = \frac{0.6487}{25} = 0.02595
$$

$$
0.02595 - 0.02 = 0.00595
$$

$$
W = 0.5 \times 0.00595 = 0.002975 \, J \approx 2.98 \, mJ
$$

**(4 marks)**

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

The muscle stores approximately **$2.98 \, mJ$** of elastic strain energy when stretched by $2 \, cm$ beyond its slack length. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the slack length** as the lower integration limit (not the resting length). This will earn only partial credit.
> 2. **Dropping the "$-1$" term** inside the parentheses of the exponential PEE law. Examiners specifically check whether students included this critical constant.
> 3. **Mixing up active and passive force** when interpreting experimental data. Passive force is the *baseline* before activation; do not attribute passive force to cross-bridges.
> 4. **Wrong units in the SLS derivation**: students often forget that $\tau_\varepsilon$ and $\tau_\sigma$ have *different* definitions — do not interchange them.
> 5. **Drawing the hysteresis loop backwards** (unloading above loading). The unloading curve *must* lie *below* the loading curve.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (highest-yield items for KTU 2024):**
> - The **total muscle force** decomposes as $F_{total} = F_{passive}(L) + F_{active}(L, v, a)$.
> - **Passive force sources**: epimysium, perimysium, endomysium, titin — *not* cross-bridges.
> - **PEE** acts in *parallel* with the contractile element; **SEE** acts in *series*.
> - Below $L_{slack}$, the PEE carries **no force** because collagen is crimped.
> - Passive force–length curve has **four regions**: Toe, Heel, Linear, Failure.
> - The **exponential law** $F = a(e^{b\,\Delta L} - 1)$ fits the heel/linear regions best.
> - **Engineering strain** is $\Delta L / L_0$ (not $\Delta L / L_{current}$).
> - **Young's modulus** $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ is valid *only* in the linear region.
> - **Viscoelasticity** produces creep, stress relaxation, hysteresis, and strain-rate sensitivity.
> - **Maxwell** model → unbounded creep, exponential stress relaxation to zero.
> - **Kelvin–Voigt** model → bounded creep, no stress relaxation under step strain.
> - **Standard Linear Solid** → both bounded creep *and* non-zero stress asymptote; **best fit for muscle**.
> - **Relaxation time constant** $\tau = \eta / E$ governs how quickly viscous response equilibrates.
> - **Hysteresis area** = $\oint F\,dL$ = energy dissipated per cycle (5–15% for muscle).
> - **Strain-rate sensitivity**: faster stretch ⇒ higher resisting force.
> - Passive muscle **density** $\rho \approx 1.06 \, g/cm^3$; passive **Young's modulus** $E \approx 10\text{–}300 \, kPa$.
> - For KTU Part B, always include **assumptions** in passive-stretch calculation answers (linear region, uniform cross-section, isothermal conditions).
> - Memorize the four passive curve regions and their corresponding sarcomere-length ranges.
> - Be ready to **draw the Hill three-element model** and label SEE, CE, PEE in their correct topological positions.
