Step 4 — Energy-rate hypothesis: Hill postulated that the total energy rate is constant and equal to the value at isometric maximum $P_0$ (where $v = 0$):

$$
E = (P_0 + a) \cdot 0 + bP_0 = bP_0
$$

Step 5 — Equating:

$$
(P + a)v + bP = bP_0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(P + a)v = b(P_0 - P)
$$

Step 6 — Final form:

$$
\boxed{v = \dfrac{b(P_0 - P)}{P + a}}
$$

Step 7 — Maximum velocity (when $P \to 0$):

$$
\boxed{v_{max} = \dfrac{bP_0}{a}}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **force-velocity curve** of skeletal muscle, including the eccentric, isometric, and concentric regions. Discuss how the curve is used in ergonomic design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A muscle has $P_0 = 200$ N, $a = 50$ N, $b = 0.2$ m/s. Calculate: (i) the maximum shortening velocity, (ii) the velocity of shortening when the muscle lifts a load of 100 N, and (iii) the maximum instantaneous mechanical power output. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Force-Velocity Curve (7 marks)**

The force-velocity curve is a hyperbola in the $P$-$v$ plane. It has three distinct regions:

| Region | Velocity | Force | Physiological Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eccentric** | $v < 0$ | $P > P_0$, up to ~$1.5$–$2.0\,P_0$ | Muscle forcibly lengthening; very high force |
| **Isometric** | $v = 0$ | $P = P_0$ | Static holding |
| **Concentric** | $v > 0$ | $P < P_0$, decreasing as $v$ rises | Active shortening under load |

**Sketch:**

```
  P
  |       Eccentric branch
  |        *
2.0 P0|------*
1.5 P0|----*         (Muscle lengthening)
1.0 P0|--*----------*-- (Isometric, v=0)
0.5 P0|*              
   0  +-----------|-----> v
      <-v       0     v_max ->
```

**Ergonomic applications (3 marks):**
- The maximum power point ($P \approx 0.3 P_0$, $v \approx 0.3 v_{max}$) tells the designer the **optimal speed–load combination** for repetitive tasks like lifting, hammering, or cycling.
- The eccentric branch shows the muscles can absorb much more force than they can produce — important for **catching and damping** tasks (e.g., conveyor-belt deceleration handles, fall-arrest harnesses).
- The hyperbolic shape dictates that **lighter loads can be moved quickly**; ergo-industrial tools are designed within the high-power region of the curve.

**(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

Given: $P_0 = 200$ N, $a = 50$ N, $b = 0.2$ m/s.

**(i) Maximum shortening velocity:**

$$
v_{max} = \dfrac{bP_0}{a} = \dfrac{0.2 \times 200}{50} = \dfrac{40}{50} = 0.8\ m/s
$$

**(ii) Velocity at $P = 100$ N:**

$$
v = \dfrac{b(P_0 - P)}{P + a} = \dfrac{0.2 \times (200 - 100)}{100 + 50} = \dfrac{20}{150} = 0.1333\ m/s
$$

**(iii) Maximum power output:**

First, the optimal load:

$$
P_{opt} = -a + \sqrt{a^2 + aP_0} = -50 + \sqrt{2500 + 10000} = -50 + \sqrt{12500}
$$

$$
\sqrt{12500} \approx 111.80
$$

$$
P_{opt} \approx 61.80\ N
$$

Corresponding velocity:

$$
v_{opt} = \dfrac{0.2 \times (200 - 61.80)}{61.80 + 50} = \dfrac{27.64}{111.80} \approx 0.2472\ m/s
$$

Maximum power:

$$
\mathcal{P}_{max} = P_{opt} \times v_{opt} = 61.80 \times 0.2472 \approx 15.28\ W
$$

**Verification with the 0.3 thumb-rule:**

$$
P_{opt}/P_0 = 61.80/200 = 0.309 \approx 0.3 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
v_{opt}/v_{max} = 0.2472/0.8 = 0.309 \approx 0.3 \quad \checkmark
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Functional Relations**

### Force-Length Curve
- [x] **Three regions**: descending (double overlap interference), plateau (max cross-bridges, $L_{opt} \approx 2.0$–$2.2\ \mu m$), ascending (reduced overlap).
- [x] **Total tension** = $F_{active}(L) + F_{passive}(L)$.
- [x] **Titin** is the principal source of passive tension.
- [x] **Sliding Filament Theory** (Huxley 1954) is the molecular basis.
- [x] Active force vanishes at $L_s \approx 3.6\ \mu m$ (no overlap).

### Force-Velocity Curve
- [x] **Hill's equation**: $(P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a)b$ — **hyperbola**.
- [x] **Units:** $a$ in **N (force)**, $b$ in **m/s (velocity)**.
- [x] **Three regimes:** concentric ($v>0$), isometric ($v=0$), eccentric ($v<0$, $P$ up to $\sim 1.5$–$2.0\,P_0$).
- [x] **Maximum velocity:** $v_{max} = bP_0/a$ (at zero load).
- [x] **Maximum power** occurs at $\approx 0.3\,P_0$ and $\approx 0.3\,v_{max}$.
- [x] Curve is measured **isometrically at $L_{opt}$** to isolate velocity dependence.

### Cross-Cutting Points
- [x] The two curves are **independent** by experimental design ($F$-$L$ at $v=0$; $F$-$V$ at $L_{opt}$).
- [x] **F-V curve** underlies Hill-type muscle models used in gait simulation, prosthetics, and ergonomics.
- [x] **F-L curve** underlies the **Frank–Starling law** of the heart (cardiac analog).
- [x] Engineering applications: **gait analysis, exoskeleton control, ergonomic tool design, sports biomechanics**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "3-2-1":**
> - **3 regions** in the F-L curve (descending, plateau, ascending).
> - **2 components** of total tension (active + passive).
> - **1 hyperbola** (Hill) describes the F-V curve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: History Effects in Muscle Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** History Effects in Muscle Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In KTU 2024 Scheme Biomechanics terminology, **History Effects in Muscle Mechanics** refer to the phenomenon in which the instantaneous mechanical state of an active (activated) skeletal muscle — its force output, stiffness, or length under a given load — depends not only on its *current* length, velocity, and level of activation, but also on the *prior sequence* of length changes and activation it has experienced.

The principal history-dependent properties recognized in the PCBMT601 syllabus are:

1. **Residual Force Enhancement (RFE)** — the elevated steady-state isometric force observed after an active stretch.
2. **Residual Force Depression (RFD)** — the depressed steady-state isometric force observed after an active shortening.
3. **Hysteresis** in the force–length work loop during cyclic length changes.
4. **Stress Relaxation** and **Creep** — the time-dependent viscoelastic response of passive and active muscle.
5. **Stretch–Shortening Cycle (SSC) Enhancement** — augmented work output during a concentric contraction preceded by an eccentric (lengthening) contraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme PCBMT601 Module 2 syllabus, History Effects are explicitly listed as a sub-topic under *Muscle Mechanics*. Questions frequently test (a) the *direction* of the effect, (b) the *physiological/molecular basis*, and (c) the *engineering utility* in rehabilitation robotics, prosthetics, and gait analysis.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Memory Rubber Band**
>
> Imagine a thick rubber band that "remembers" how it was pulled:
> - If you **stretch** the rubber band while it is already taut and active, then *pin it in place*, it remains **slightly stiffer and pulls harder** than if it had simply been stretched directly to that position without prior movement. This is **Force Enhancement**.
> - If you first **let the rubber band shorten** under tension, then pin it, it remains **slightly softer and pulls less** than expected. This is **Force Depression**.
> - The rubber band also **fails to spring back fully** — some energy is dissipated internally. This is **Hysteresis loss**.
> - If you *hold* it stretched at a constant length, the internal tension slowly *drops*. This is **Stress Relaxation**.
> - If you *hang a constant weight* on it, it keeps *slowly lengthening* with time. This is **Creep**.

A skeletal muscle behaves in exactly this manner. The "memory" is encoded at the level of **attached cross-bridges** between actin and myosin filaments and at the giant elastic protein **titin**, both of which carry the mechanical signature of the muscle's recent kinematic and activation past.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Notation Used Throughout PCBMT601

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $F_{iso}(L)$ | Purely isometric force at length $L$ |
| $F_{re}(L)$ | Residual (steady-state) force after active stretch to length $L$ |
| $F_{rd}(L)$ | Residual (steady-state) force after active shortening to length $L$ |
| $\Delta F_{RFE}$ | Residual Force Enhancement $= F_{re} - F_{iso}$ |
| $\Delta F_{RFD}$ | Residual Force Depression $= F_{iso} - F_{rd}$ |
| $L_0$ | Optimal (rest) sarcomere length, typically **≈ 2.0–2.5 µm** |
| $\dot{L}$ | Lengthening (positive) or shortening (negative) velocity |
| $E_{abs}, E_{ret}$ | Energy absorbed during stretch, energy returned during shortening |
| $W_{net}$ | Net work per cycle $= E_{ret} - E_{abs}$ |
| $\tau_{relax}$ | Time constant of stress relaxation |
| $\tau_{creep}$ | Time constant of creep |

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard values** (typical adult mammalian skeletal muscle at physiological temperature **≈ 37 °C**):
> - Maximum isometric tetanic stress: **$\sigma_{max} \approx 200$–$300$ kN/m²**
> - Optimal sarcomere length $L_0$: **≈ 2.0–2.5 µm**
> - Passive (resting) tension becomes significant beyond: **$L > 1.2\,L_0$**
> - Stress relaxation time constant: **$\tau_{relax} \approx 1$–$10$ s** depending on muscle type
> - Creep time constant: **$\tau_{creep} \approx 5$–$30$ s**
> - Residual Force Enhancement magnitude: **5 %–30 %** of $F_{iso}$
> - Residual Force Depression magnitude: **5 %–20 %** of $F_{iso}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical History Effects

### 2.1.1 Residual Force Enhancement (RFE)

- **What:** When an activated muscle is *stretched* to a target length and then held isometrically, the steady-state force $F_{re}$ exceeds the purely isometric force $F_{iso}$ at that same length.
- **Why it matters:** Stretched-and-held muscle generates **more** force than a muscle that was never stretched — a memory of the stretch persists.
- **How:** Attributed to (i) **sarcomere length non-uniformity** (Morgan's theory) where some sarcomeres are forcibly elongated beyond overlap while others stay short, and (ii) a **titin-based stiffening** mechanism where the giant protein titin binds to actin upon stretch.

$$\Delta F_{RFE} = F_{re}(L) - F_{iso}(L) \;\;\;>\;\; 0$$

The dimensionless fractional enhancement is:

$$\varepsilon_{RFE} = \dfrac{F_{re}(L) - F_{iso}(L)}{F_{iso}(L)} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.2 Residual Force Depression (RFD)

- **What:** When an activated muscle is *shortened* to a target length and held isometrically, the steady-state force $F_{rd}$ is *less* than the purely isometric force $F_{iso}$ at that length.
- **Why it matters:** Muscle that has recently shortened is intrinsically **weaker** at the same final length.
- **How:** Primarily attributed to **sarcomere length non-uniformity** during shortening — some sarcomeres become very short, develop low force, and bear disproportionate load.

$$\Delta F_{RFD} = F_{iso}(L) - F_{rd}(L) \;\;\;>\;\; 0$$

$$\varepsilon_{RFD} = \dfrac{F_{iso}(L) - F_{rd}(L)}{F_{iso}(L)} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.3 Hysteresis (Force–Length Loop)

- **What:** During cyclic stretch–shorten cycles under activation, the *force-length trajectory* traced during lengthening lies **above** the trajectory traced during shortening.
- **Why it matters:** The enclosed area of the loop equals the **net energy dissipated** internally per cycle.
- **How:** Cross-bridge attachment/detachment rates are **asymmetric** with respect to filament sliding direction.

$$W_{loop} = \oint F\,dL \;\;\;=\;\; E_{abs} - E_{ret}$$

For an active muscle, $E_{abs} > E_{ret}$, so $W_{loop} > 0$ (energy absorbed by the muscle, partly dissipated, partly stored in titin).

### 2.1.4 Stress Relaxation and Creep (Viscoelastic History)

- **Stress Relaxation** — Muscle is rapidly stretched to length $L_0$ and held; force decays exponentially.
- **Creep** — Muscle is loaded with constant force; length gradually increases.

These arise from the intrinsic **viscoelasticity** of the cross-bridges, parallel connective tissue, and titin.

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_{relax}} \quad\quad \text{(stress relaxation)}$$

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{creep}}\right) \quad\quad \text{(creep)}$$

---

## 2.2 The Stretch–Shortening Cycle (SSC)

The SSC is the **engineering-relevant composite history effect**: a *pre-activation eccentric stretch* immediately followed by a *concentric shortening* enhances work output compared with a pure concentric contraction.

$$W_{SSC} = W_{concentric\;only} + \Delta W_{history}$$

$$\Delta W_{history} = \underbrace{F_{re}\,\Delta L_{eccentric}}_{\text{stored elastic energy}} + \underbrace{(F_{re} - F_{iso})\,\Delta L_{concentric}}_{\text{history-enhanced force during shortening}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The SSC is the biomechanical basis of **running, jumping, throwing, and hopping**. Robotic assistive devices (e.g., KTU-referenced prosthetics) exploit stored elastic energy by mimicking the natural SSC in series elastic actuators.

---

## 2.3 Underlying Cross-Bridge Mechanics (Huxley 1957)

Huxley's sliding filament theory defines the rate constant $f$ for cross-bridge attachment and $g$ for detachment, both functions of filament separation $x$:

$$\dfrac{dn}{dt} = (1-n)\,f(x) - n\,g(x)$$

- For $x > 0$ (myosin pulled away from actin) — $g$ is large → detachment dominates.
- For $x < 0$ (myosin pushed toward actin) — $f$ is large → attachment dominates.
- During stretch, more cross-bridges become **strained but still attached** → force enhancement.
- During shortening, cross-bridges **detach prematurely** → force depression.

The instantaneous force from cross-bridges is:

$$F_{xb}(t) = n(x,t)\,\kappa\,x$$

where $\kappa$ is the cross-bridge stiffness and $x$ is the average stretch of attached cross-bridges.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Effect | Governing Equation | Variables | Typical Magnitude |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Residual Force Enhancement | $\varepsilon_{RFE} = \dfrac{F_{re}-F_{iso}}{F_{iso}}\times 100\%$ | $F_{re}, F_{iso}$ | 5 %–30 % |
| 2 | Residual Force Depression | $\varepsilon_{RFD} = \dfrac{F_{iso}-F_{rd}}{F_{iso}}\times 100\%$ | $F_{iso}, F_{rd}$ | 5 %–20 % |
| 3 | Hysteresis Loop Work | $W_{loop} = \oint F\,dL$ | $F, L$ | positive (loss) |
| 4 | Stress Relaxation | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_{relax}}$ | $\sigma_0, t, \tau_{relax}$ | $\tau_{relax}\approx 1$–$10$ s |
| 5 | Creep Strain | $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{\infty}(1-e^{-t/\tau_{creep}})$ | $\varepsilon_{\infty}, t, \tau_{creep}$ | $\tau_{creep}\approx 5$–$30$ s |
| 6 | Cross-Bridge Kinetics | $\dfrac{dn}{dt} = (1-n)f - n g$ | $n, f, g$ | — |
| 7 | Cross-Bridge Force | $F_{xb} = n\,\kappa\,x$ | $n, \kappa, x$ | — |
| 8 | SSC Net Work | $W_{SSC} = W_{conc} + \Delta W_{hist}$ | — | 10 %–30 % gain |
| 9 | Hill-type Force-Velocity | $(F+a)(v+b) = (F_{iso}+a)\,b$ | $F, v, a, b$ | $a\approx 0.25 F_{iso}$ |
| 10 | Energy Balance | $E_{abs} = E_{ret} + W_{dissipated}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU valuation, always **state the direction** of the inequality (greater than for RFE, less than for RFD) and **cite the mechanism** (sarcomere non-uniformity or titin) when describing any history effect.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where History Effects Matter in Industry & Clinical Practice:**
> - **Orthotics & Prosthetics:** Series Elastic Actuators (SEAs) exploit SSC.
> - **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Treadmill gait trainers use FES-induced SSC to retrain muscle.
> - **Sports Biomechanics:** Coaches analyse plyometric training, which amplifies SSC work.
> - **Ergonomics:** Risk of strain injury is highest during eccentric stretch of active muscle.
> - **Surgical Simulation:** Virtual reality haptic devices use muscle history models to feel realistic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hysteresis Loop Energy from a Triangular Length Protocol

Consider an active muscle undergoing a periodic triangular length change between $L_{min}$ and $L_{max}$ with mean length $\bar{L}$ and amplitude $\Delta L = (L_{max}-L_{min})/2$.

**Step 1 — Define the length as a function of time during stretch phase:**

$$L_{stretch}(t) = \bar{L} - \Delta L + 2\Delta L \cdot \dfrac{t}{T_{ecc}} \quad\quad 0 \le t \le T_{ecc}$$

**Step 2 — Define the length during shortening phase:**

$$L_{shorten}(t) = \bar{L} + \Delta L - 2\Delta L \cdot \dfrac{t-T_{ecc}}{T_{conc}} \quad\quad T_{ecc} \le t \le T$$

where $T_{ecc}$ is the eccentric duration, $T_{conc}$ is the concentric duration, and $T = T_{ecc}+T_{conc}$ is the cycle period.

**Step 3 — Use the history-augmented force model.** A simple, KTU-acceptable model is:

$$F_{muscle}(L,\dot{L}, \text{history}) = F_{iso}(L) + F_{v}(\dot{L}) + F_{hist}(L,\text{prior})$$

where $F_{hist}$ carries the residual component.

**Step 4 — Compute energy absorbed during the stretch (work done ON the muscle):**

$$E_{abs} = \int_{0}^{T_{ecc}} F_{muscle}(L_{stretch}(t),\dot L)\, \dot L_{stretch}\, dt$$

Substituting $\dot L_{stretch} = + 2\Delta L/T_{ecc}$ (constant):

$$E_{abs} = \dfrac{2\Delta L}{T_{ecc}}\int_{0}^{T_{ecc}} F_{muscle}(L_{stretch}(t),+\dot L)\,dt$$

**Step 5 — Compute energy returned during shortening:**

$$E_{ret} = \int_{T_{ecc}}^{T} F_{muscle}(L_{shorten}(t),\dot L)\, \dot L_{shorten}\, dt$$

$$\dot L_{shorten} = -\dfrac{2\Delta L}{T_{conc}}$$

$$E_{ret} = -\dfrac{2\Delta L}{T_{conc}}\int_{T_{ecc}}^{T} F_{muscle}(L_{shorten}(t),-\dot L)\,dt$$

**Step 6 — Net work per cycle (the hysteresis loop area):**

$$W_{loop} = E_{abs} - E_{ret}$$

Because the force during lengthening exceeds the force during shortening at the *same length*, $E_{abs} > E_{ret}$, hence $W_{loop} > 0$. This positive $W_{loop}$ represents the **energy dissipated** in the muscle per cycle, which is converted to heat.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Residual Force Enhancement from Cross-Bridge Population Model

**Step 1 — Write the Huxley cross-bridge distribution equation:**

$$\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\dot{x}\,n\right] + (1-n)\,f(x) - n\,g(x)$$

For a constant-length isometric contraction in steady state, $\partial n/\partial t = 0$ and $\dot{x}=0$, giving:

$$n_{iso}(x) = \dfrac{f(x)}{f(x)+g(x)}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state isometric force:**

$$F_{iso} = \kappa \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} n_{iso}(x)\, x\, dx$$

**Step 3 — During active stretch at velocity $\dot L = v > 0$**, the relative sliding speed is non-zero, and the cross-bridge attachment becomes enhanced for $x < 0$ (compression side). The first term does not vanish; we must solve:

$$\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial t} = -v\,\dfrac{\partial n}{\partial x} + (1-n)f(x) - n\,g(x)$$

**Step 4 — At the end of the stretch**, suppose the muscle is held at fixed length. The population $n(x)$ no longer satisfies $n_{iso}(x)$ because (i) it has been perturbed by the stretch and (ii) some cross-bridges are stretched into regions where $f$ and $g$ are now asymmetric.

**Step 5 — In the post-stretch steady state, the new distribution $n_{re}(x)$ gives the residual force:**

$$F_{re} = \kappa \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} n_{re}(x)\, x\, dx$$

**Step 6 — The enhancement is therefore:**

$$\Delta F_{RFE} = \kappa \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[n_{re}(x) - n_{iso}(x)\right] x\, dx \;\;\;>\;\; 0$$

This formal integral expression is the basis of the molecular explanation of RFE: cross-bridges that were *forcefully attached* during the stretch remain attached and contribute extra force in the post-stretch isometric state.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Force Depression After Active Shortening (Morgan's Sarcomere Non-Uniformity Argument)

**Step 1 — Consider an activated muscle fibre** with $N$ sarcomeres in series, all initially at length $L_0$.

**Step 2 — During active shortening,** sarcomere lengths diverge: some become very short (highly compressed, very low force) and some remain long. Let the distribution be $P(L_s)$ after shortening.

**Step 3 — The fibre force is limited by the *weakest* sarcomere in series:**

$$F_{fibre} = \min_{i}\,F_i(L_{s,i})$$

where $F_i$ is the force of the $i^{th}$ sarcomere at its current length.

**Step 4 — Because the descending limb of the force–length curve is unstable,** once a sarcomere shortens past the instability point, it shortens further while neighbouring sarcomeres lengthen — but the *total* force still drops.

**Step 5 — After the shortening stops and the muscle is held isometric** at the new average length, the population $P(L_s)$ remains non-uniform. The residual force is:

$$F_{rd} = \sum_i F_i(L_{s,i})/N \;\;\text{(mean force at the new average length)}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the purely isometric force at the same average length:**

$$F_{iso} > F_{rd}$$

because at the *new* average length, several sarcomeres are on the low-force ascending limb (very short) or beyond the plateau, dragging down the mean. Hence:

$$\Delta F_{RFD} = F_{iso} - F_{rd} \;\;\;>\;\; 0$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Stress Relaxation Time Constant

**Step 1 — For a standard linear viscoelastic (Maxwell) element** representing muscle:

$$\dot{\sigma} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau_{relax}} = E\,\dot{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 2 — Impose a step strain** $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0\, H(t)$, where $H(t)$ is the Heaviside step function. Then $\dot\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0\,\delta(t)$, and for $t > 0$:

$$\dot\sigma + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau_{relax}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the first-order ODE:**

$$\dfrac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\dfrac{dt}{\tau_{relax}}$$

Integrating:

$$\ln\sigma = -\dfrac{t}{\tau_{relax}} + C$$

**Step 4 — Apply initial condition** $\sigma(0^+) = E\varepsilon_0$:

$$\sigma(t) = E\varepsilon_0\, e^{-t/\tau_{relax}}$$

**Step 5 — At $t = \tau_{relax}$:**

$$\sigma(\tau_{relax}) = \dfrac{E\varepsilon_0}{e} \approx 0.368\,E\varepsilon_0$$

That is, **the stress has dropped to about 36.8 %** of its initial value after one time constant — a useful rule-of-thumb students should remember.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — History Effect vs Mechanism

| History Effect | Sign of $\Delta F$ | Dominant Mechanism | Key Time Scale |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Residual Force Enhancement | $+$ | Sarcomere non-uniformity + titin stiffening | Steady-state ($>1$ s) |
| Residual Force Depression | $-$ | Sarcomere non-uniformity (instability) | Steady-state ($>1$ s) |
| Hysteresis Loop | Positive $W_{loop}$ | Asymmetric cross-bridge kinetics | Cycle period |
| Stress Relaxation | $\sigma$ decays | Viscoelastic flow of bridges/connective tissue | $\tau_{relax}\approx 1$–$10$ s |
| Creep | $\varepsilon$ increases | Viscoelastic extension | $\tau_{creep}\approx 5$–$30$ s |
| SSC Enhancement | Net $W$ increases | Stored elastic energy + RFE | $50$–$200$ ms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of History Effects in Muscle Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Muscle Activation Input - Neural Drive] --> B[Active Cross-Bridge Population]
    P[Passive Elements - Titin Connective Tissue] --> B
    B --> C{Recent Mechanical History}
    C --> D[Active Stretch in Past]
    C --> E[Active Shortening in Past]
    C --> F[Constant Length Hold]
    C --> G[Cyclic Stretch Shorten]
    D --> H[Residual Force Enhancement - RFE plus]
    E --> I[Residual Force Depression - RFD minus]
    F --> J[Stress Relaxation - sigma decays]
    F --> K[Creep - epsilon grows]
    G --> L[Hysteresis Loop - W loop positive]
    G --> M[Stretch Shortening Cycle - W SSC enhanced]
    H --> N[Net Muscle Force Output F muscle t]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Engineering Outcome - Work, Joint Moment, Rehabilitation]
    style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:1px
    style O fill:#ece2ff,stroke:#609,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The active cross-bridge population combined with the passive elements generates a force output that is *modulated* by the recent mechanical history (the diamond block). Each history branch contributes additively to the final muscle force, which then dictates joint moment, work, or clinical outcome.

---

## 4.2 Force-Length Schematic — RFE and RFD Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISO[Isometric Reference]
        isoA[Length L] --> isoB[Force F iso]
    end
    subgraph STR[Active Stretch Path]
        strA[Stretched to L] --> strB[Force F re greater than F iso]
    end
    subgraph SHR[Active Shortening Path]
        shrA[Shortened to L] --> shrB[Force F rd less than F iso]
    end
    isoB -.RFE arrow up.-> strB
    isoB -.RFD arrow down.-> shrB
    style isoB fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
    style strB fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:1px
    style shrB fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the schematic:** Both the *stretched* and *shortened* paths arrive at the *same final length* $L$, yet the force differs. This single observation is the experimental fingerprint of history dependence and is a frequent 3-mark definition question in KTU examinations.

---

## 4.3 Viscoelastic Spring-Dashpot Representation (Maxwell Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Elastic Element - Spring] --- D1[Viscous Element - Dashpot] --- L1[Load]
    style E1 fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px
    style D1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
    style L1 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The Maxwell arrangement — spring in **series** with a dashpot — is the simplest KTU-acceptable model that reproduces *stress relaxation* (constant strain → decaying stress) but does NOT reproduce creep under load (a Kelvin-Voigt element in parallel is needed for that).

---

## 4.4 Cyclic Work Loop Schematic (Hysteresis Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WL[Force-Length Work Loop]
        WL1[Top Branch - Stretch - High Force] --> WL2[Right Vertex - L max]
        WL2 --> WL3[Bottom Branch - Shortening - Low Force]
        WL3 --> WL4[Left Vertex - L min]
        WL4 --> WL1
    end
    style WL1 fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#080,stroke-width:2px
    style WL3 fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style WL2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
    style WL4 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Work loop area interpretation:** The *enclosed area* is $W_{loop}$, the **net energy dissipated per cycle**. A loop that is traversed counter-clockwise represents net positive work done by the muscle (concentric-biased SSC), while clockwise represents net energy absorbed (eccentric-biased damping).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define *Residual Force Enhancement* in muscle mechanics and state its physiological basis.**

**Model Answer:**

Residual Force Enhancement (RFE) is the history-dependent phenomenon in which the steady-state isometric force of an activated muscle, measured after an active stretch, is *greater* than the force produced during a purely isometric contraction at the same final length.

Mathematically:

$$F_{re}(L) \;>\; F_{iso}(L) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_{RFE} = \dfrac{F_{re}-F_{iso}}{F_{iso}}\times 100\% \approx 5\%-30\%$$

The two leading physiological bases are:
1. **Sarcomere length non-uniformity** (Morgan's theory): After stretch, some sarcomeres become over-extended while others remain near $L_0$, producing a higher *average* force.
2. **Titin-based stiffening**: Upon activation, the giant protein titin binds to actin, increasing passive stiffness in the stretched sarcomeres and contributing extra force.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to state the *direction of the inequality*. Always write $F_{re} > F_{iso}$ explicitly. Marks are deducted for ambiguous descriptions.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the concept of *hysteresis* in an active muscle during cyclic length changes. How is the dissipated energy computed?**

**Model Answer:**

Hysteresis in an active muscle refers to the path-dependence of the force–length relationship during cyclic stretch-shortening: the force during lengthening is *higher* than the force during shortening at the same length, causing the force-length trajectory to enclose a finite area on the F–L plane.

The dissipated (or net absorbed) energy per cycle is the area of the loop:

$$W_{loop} = \oint F\,dL = E_{abs} - E_{ret} > 0$$

This positive $W_{loop}$ represents the energy *lost* per cycle as heat, owing to asymmetric cross-bridge attachment/detachment kinetics (Huxley's rate functions $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ being non-symmetric about $x=0$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse *hysteresis loss* with *net positive work*. Hysteresis is **dissipative** — it is energy *lost*, not work *gained*. Net positive work in muscle (e.g., SSC) requires stored elastic energy plus active force production.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the phenomena of *Residual Force Enhancement* (RFE) and *Residual Force Depression* (RFD) in skeletal muscle. Discuss the sarcomere length non-uniformity theory proposed by Morgan. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A muscle is subjected to a triangular length protocol from $L_{min} = 2.0$ µm to $L_{max} = 3.2$ µm at 1 Hz. The steady-state isometric force at $L = 2.6$ µm is $F_{iso} = 50$ N. After an active stretch to $L = 2.6$ µm, the residual force is $F_{re} = 60$ N. Compute (i) the residual force enhancement ratio, and (ii) the additional force available compared with a pure isometric contraction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A (a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition of RFE (2 marks):** RFE is the elevation of steady-state isometric force after active stretch beyond $F_{iso}$ at the same length. Sketch a force–length diagram with the *isometric curve* and a *post-stretch point above it*.

2. **Definition of RFD (2 marks):** RFD is the depression of steady-state isometric force after active shortening below $F_{iso}$ at the same length. Sketch with the *post-shortening point below the isometric curve*.

3. **Morgan's Sarcomere Length Non-Uniformity Theory (3 marks):**
   - Sarcomeres are not all identical; they have slightly different optimal lengths due to biological variability.
   - During stretch, weaker sarcomeres lengthen disproportionately while stronger ones remain near $L_0$ — but the *series* arrangement means the total force is determined by an *averaged* sarcomere force, which rises because some are now on the ascending limb of the descending side.
   - During shortening, weaker sarcomeres shorten *catastrophically* once they pass the unstable point of the force–length curve; they end up very short with very low force, dragging the average force down. This produces RFD.

#### Model Solution to Part A (b) — 7 marks

**Given data:** $F_{iso} = 50$ N, $F_{re} = 60$ N.

**(i) Residual Force Enhancement ratio (3 marks):**

$$\varepsilon_{RFE} = \dfrac{F_{re}-F_{iso}}{F_{iso}} \times 100\%$$

$$\varepsilon_{RFE} = \dfrac{60 - 50}{50} \times 100\% = \dfrac{10}{50}\times 100\% = 20\%$$

**(ii) Additional force compared with isometric (4 marks):**

$$\Delta F_{RFE} = F_{re} - F_{iso} = 60 - 50 = 10 \text{ N}$$

So the muscle generates **10 N more force** than a purely isometric contraction at the same length, equivalent to a **20 % enhancement**. This is within the typical physiological range (5 %–30 %) and indicates strong history dependence — clinically relevant for eccentric training and plyometric rehabilitation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for *stress relaxation* in an active muscle modelled as a Maxwell viscoelastic element, and explain its significance for muscle mechanics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 cm segment of activated muscle is rapidly stretched by 1 mm and held at the new length. The peak instantaneous stress is measured as 200 kN/m². The relaxation time constant is $\tau = 5$ s. Calculate the stress after (i) 5 s, (ii) 10 s, and (iii) interpret the result in terms of cross-bridge kinetics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B (a) — 7 marks

1. **Maxwell model formulation (3 marks):** A Maxwell element consists of an elastic spring (stiffness $E$) in **series** with a viscous dashpot (viscosity $\eta$). Under a step strain, the spring instantly deforms and then progressively transfers load to the dashpot, which flows.

2. **Governing differential equation (2 marks):**

$$\dot\sigma + \dfrac{\sigma}{\tau_{relax}} = E\,\dot\varepsilon \quad\text{where}\quad \tau_{relax}=\eta/E$$

3. **Solution for step strain (2 marks):** For $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 H(t)$:

$$\sigma(t) = E\,\varepsilon_0\, e^{-t/\tau_{relax}}$$

The stress decays from $E\varepsilon_0$ to zero as the dashpot carries the entire deformation. Significance: muscle is **not a pure elastic body** — its force under constant length decreases as cross-bridges cycle and connective tissue redistributes stress.

#### Model Solution to Part B (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $\sigma_0 = 200$ kN/m², $\tau = 5$ s.

**(i) Stress at $t = 5$ s (2 marks):**

$$\sigma(5) = 200 \cdot e^{-5/5} = 200 \cdot e^{-1} = 200 \cdot 0.3679 \approx 73.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**(ii) Stress at $t = 10$ s (2 marks):**

$$\sigma(10) = 200 \cdot e^{-10/5} = 200 \cdot e^{-2} = 200 \cdot 0.1353 \approx 27.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**(iii) Interpretation in terms of cross-bridge kinetics (3 marks):**
- After one time constant ($t=\tau$), stress drops to **≈ 36.8 %** of its initial value.
- After two time constants, stress drops to **≈ 13.5 %**.
- Mechanistically: the rapid initial force is sustained by *strained* cross-bridges; over time, these detach (governed by Huxley's $g(x)$ rate) and re-attach in relaxed states, redistributing tension through the parallel elastic elements (titin and connective tissue).
- The decay reflects **cross-bridge cycling** and the viscoelastic flow of the sarcomeric cytoskeleton — a clear molecular signature of history dependence even under nominally *isometric* conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently mix up *stress relaxation* and *creep*. **Stress relaxation = constant strain, decaying stress**. **Creep = constant stress, growing strain**. Examiners deduct full marks if these are interchanged.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: History Effects in Muscle Mechanics**

- [x] **RFE (Residual Force Enhancement):** Force is *higher* after active stretch. Magnitude 5 %–30 %. Mechanism: sarcomere non-uniformity + titin.
- [x] **RFD (Residual Force Depression):** Force is *lower* after active shortening. Magnitude 5 %–20 %. Mechanism: sarcomere non-uniformity (Morgan).
- [x] **Hysteresis:** $W_{loop} = \oint F\,dL > 0$. Force during stretch $>$ force during shortening at the same length.
- [x] **Stress Relaxation:** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_{relax}}$. Constant strain, decaying stress.
- [x] **Creep:** $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{\infty}(1-e^{-t/\tau_{creep}})$. Constant stress, growing strain.
- [x] **SSC (Stretch–Shortening Cycle):** Pre-activation eccentric stretch boosts concentric work output.
- [x] **Huxley cross-bridge equation:** $\dfrac{dn}{dt} = (1-n)f(x) - n g(x)$ — explains asymmetric kinetics.
- [x] **Time constants:** $\tau_{relax} \approx 1$–$10$ s; $\tau_{creep} \approx 5$–$30$ s.
- [x] **Engineering utility:** Prosthetics, orthotics, robotics, sports biomechanics, rehabilitation.
- [x] **Key inequalities to memorise:** $F_{re} > F_{iso}$, $F_{rd} < F_{iso}$, $E_{abs} > E_{ret}$, $W_{loop} > 0$.
- [x] **Standard values:** $L_0 \approx 2.0$–$2.5$ µm, $\sigma_{max} \approx 200$–$300$ kN/m².

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory hook for the exam hall:**
> *Stretch strengthens, Shortening weakens, Cycles dissipate, Holds decay, Creeps stretch — every muscle remembers what you just did to it.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hill's Model and Sliding Filament Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hill's Model and Sliding Filament Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hill's Three-Element Muscle Model

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Hill's Model is a foundational mechanical analogue proposed by **Archibald Vivian Hill (1938)** that represents the macroscopic viscoelastic and contractile behaviour of skeletal muscle. It decomposes a whole muscle into three functionally distinct mechanical elements arranged in a defined geometric configuration: the **Contractile Element (CE)**, the **Series Elastic Element (SEE)**, and the **Parallel Elastic Element (PEE)**.

The structural arrangement is:
- **CE** and **SEE** are in **series** (connected end-to-end).
- **PEE** is in **parallel** with both CE and SEE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** The **Contractile Element (CE)** is the active, force-generating component (modelled after the actin–myosin cross-bridge chemistry). The **Series Elastic Element (SEE)** represents the passive elastic structures in series with the contractile machinery (tendons, aponeuroses, Z-discs, cross-bridge elasticity). The **Parallel Elastic Element (PEE)** represents the passive elastic structures lying parallel to the contractile element (sarcolemma, connective tissue epimysium/perimysium, titin filaments).

### 1.2 Sliding Filament Theory

**Formal Definition:**
The **Sliding Filament Theory**, independently proposed by **Andrew Huxley and Hugh Huxley (1954)**, explains the molecular mechanism of muscle contraction. It states that muscle shortening (and force generation) occurs because the **thin (actin) filaments slide past the thick (myosin) filaments** toward the M-line of the sarcomere, thereby shortening the sarcomere. Crucially, the filaments themselves **do not change length** — only the degree of overlap changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** A common board question is *"Do the actin and myosin filaments actually contract?"*. The correct answer is **NO** — they *slide*. The sliding is powered by cyclical formation and breaking of **cross-bridges** (myosin heads binding to actin).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Analogy 1 — Hill's Model as a Hand-Pulled Rope & Spring System:**
Imagine you are pulling a heavy load with a rope. The **rope itself** is the **Series Elastic Element** (it stretches slightly when pulled). Your **arm muscles** are the **Contractile Element** (they actively generate the pulling force). Now imagine a **bungee cord lying beside the rope** — this is the **Parallel Elastic Element** — it only resists force when the rope is over-stretched, and it acts as a passive safety spring. Together, the rope, the bungee cord, and your arm represent Hill's three-element model.

**Analogy 2 — Sliding Filament Theory as a Telescopic Pen:**
Think of a sarcomere as a **telescopic pen / extendable antenna**. The **thick filaments (myosin)** are the central rod, and the **thin filaments (actin)** are the outer sleeves. When you "contract" the antenna, the sleeves slide over the rod — neither the rod nor the sleeves change their own length; only the **overlap** increases. This telescopic sliding is exactly what happens in the sarcomere during contraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Resting sarcomere length: **≈ 2.0 – 2.2 µm**
> - Optimal sarcomere length (max force): **≈ 2.0 – 2.2 µm**
> - Z-line to Z-line distance: **sarcomere length (L_s)**
> - A-band (myosin length) remains **constant** at ≈ **1.6 µm**
> - I-band (actin-only region) **shortens** during contraction
> - H-zone (myosin-only region) **shortens** during contraction
> - Cross-bridge cycle frequency: tens to hundreds of **Hz** depending on muscle type
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hill's Three-Element Model — Structural Logic

The model is built on three empirical observations made by A.V. Hill:
1. Muscle behaves **viscoelastically** (rate-dependent force response).
2. There is a measurable **active state** (force generated by CE) and a **passive state** (force generated by SEE and PEE).
3. Muscle exhibits a characteristic **force–velocity** relationship.

**Step-by-step element breakdown:**

- **Contractile Element (CE):**
  - Active, force-generating.
  - Modelled as a **force generator in series with a damped dashpot** (Hill's original assumption) OR as a pure force source (modern version).
  - Produces force only when **activated** (Ca²⁺ bound to troponin → cross-bridge cycling).
  - Force depends on **length** and **velocity of shortening**.

- **Series Elastic Element (SEE):**
  - Passive, spring-like (Hookean in simple models: $F_{SEE} = k_{SE} \cdot \Delta L_{SEE}$).
  - In series with CE, so it experiences the **same force** as CE.
  - Represents tendons, Z-discs, and the **elastic component of cross-bridges** themselves.
  - Stores energy during the **eccentric (stretching) phase** and recoils during shortening.

- **Parallel Elastic Element (PEE):**
  - Passive, spring-like, non-linear (stiffens rapidly beyond optimal length).
  - In parallel with CE+SEE, so it experiences the **same displacement** as the total muscle.
  - Represents **sarcolemma, epimysium, perimysium, endomysium, and titin**.
  - Only contributes significant force when muscle is **stretched beyond resting length**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for engineers:** Hill's model is the **mathematical foundation of all modern musculoskeletal simulations** (OpenSim, AnyBody, LifeModeler). Understanding it allows biomedical engineers to design prosthetics, exoskeletons, FES (functional electrical stimulation) controllers, and ergonomic assistive devices that interface correctly with human muscle.

### 2.2 Sliding Filament Theory — Operational Logic

The theory rests on six core operational principles:

1. **Sarcomere is the functional contractile unit** — bounded by two Z-discs.
2. **Actin (thin) filaments** are anchored to the Z-discs and project inward.
3. **Myosin (thick) filaments** sit in the middle of the sarcomere (A-band).
4. Myosin heads **bind, pivot, and release** actin in a cyclical manner (the **cross-bridge cycle**).
5. ATP hydrolysis powers the **power stroke** of the myosin head.
6. The collective asynchronous cycling of millions of cross-bridges produces **smooth, graded muscle force**.

**The Cross-Bridge Cycle (Lymn–Taylor cycle, 1971):**

1. **Resting state** — myosin head is energized (ADP + Pᵢ bound), cocked at 45°.
2. **Cross-bridge attachment** — Ca²⁺ exposes actin binding sites; myosin binds to actin.
3. **Power stroke** — Pᵢ released; head pivots to 90°, sliding actin ≈ 10 nm.
4. **Cross-bridge detachment** — new ATP binds to myosin; affinity for actin drops.
5. **Re-cocking** — ATP hydrolyzed to ADP + Pᵢ; head returns to 45° cocked position.
6. **Cycle repeats** as long as Ca²⁺ and ATP are available.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hill's Force–Velocity Equation** (concentric, loaded shortening) | $(P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a)\,b = \text{constant}$ | $P$ = load (N), $v$ = shortening velocity (m/s), $P_0$ = max isometric force, $a$ = force constant, $b$ = velocity constant | Predicts muscle power output, used in gait simulators, prosthesis motor sizing |
| 2 | **Max shortening velocity** (unloaded) | $v_{max} = \dfrac{b \cdot P_0}{a}$ | m/s | Defines upper limit of muscle contraction speed |
| 3 | **Max power output** | $P_{max} = 0.1 \cdot P_0 \cdot v_{max}$ | Watts | Sizing actuators in biomechatronic systems |
| 4 | **Length–Tension (active)** | $F_{active} = F_{max} \cdot f(L_s)$ | $f(L_s)$ is bell-shaped, peaks at $L_s \approx 2.0$–$2.2 \, \mu m$ | Predicts force vs. joint angle; ergonomy of rehabilitation devices |
| 5 | **Passive (PEE) tension** | $F_{PEE} = k_{PE} \cdot (L - L_{rest})^n$ where $n \geq 2$ | Non-linear spring | Models muscle resting stiffness |
| 6 | **SEE Hookean stiffness** | $F_{SEE} = k_{SE} \cdot \Delta L$ | $k_{SE}$ in N/m | Models tendon compliance in joint torque estimation |
| 7 | **Hill's Heat Rate** (during contraction) | $\dot{H} = a \cdot v + \dot{H}_0$ | W (watts) | Quantifies metabolic energy cost |
| 8 | **Cross-bridge cycling frequency** | $f_{cycle} \approx \dfrac{v_{sliding}}{d_{step}}$ | Hz, with $d_{step} \approx 10 \, nm$ | Molecular basis of force production |
| 9 | **Mechanical power of muscle** | $P_{mech} = F \cdot v$ | Watts | Used in ergonomic work-rate calculations |
| 10 | **Sarcomere force sum** | $F_{muscle} = N_{sar} \cdot F_{sar} \cdot \cos\theta$ | $N_{sar}$ = sarcomeres in parallel, $\theta$ = pennation angle | Links micro- to macro-scale muscle force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes in tables rule applied:** All conditions and bounds are written with $\le, \ge, \approx$ symbols, not with the pipe character, to preserve markdown table integrity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hill's Force–Velocity Equation

Hill (1938) experimentally measured the heat rate released by a frog sartorius muscle during shortening under various loads. He observed that the **rate of heat liberation plus mechanical power output is constant** at any given load. This is the empirical starting point of the derivation.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation (Hill's Heat Equation):**

When a muscle shortens under a load $P$ with velocity $v$, the **total energy liberation rate** (heat rate $\dot{H}$ + mechanical power $P \cdot v$) is constant for a given muscle activation level.

$$
\dot{H} + P \cdot v = \text{constant} = \dot{H}_{total}
$$

**Step 2 — Linear empirical fit:**

Hill found that the heat rate $\dot{H}$ itself is empirically **linearly related** to the shortening velocity $v$:

$$
\dot{H} = a \cdot v + \dot{H}_0
$$

where:
- $a$ = Hill's force constant (W·s/m = N) — physically represents a viscous-like energy dissipation coefficient
- $\dot{H}_0$ = heat rate at $v = 0$ (i.e., the isometric maintenance heat)
- $v$ = shortening velocity

**Step 3 — Substituting into the total energy equation:**

$$
a \cdot v + \dot{H}_0 + P \cdot v = \text{constant}
$$

Factor the velocity terms:

$$
(a + P) \cdot v + \dot{H}_0 = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary condition at isometric maximum ($v = 0$):**

When the muscle is at maximum isometric force, $v = 0$ and $P = P_0$. Substituting:

$$
(a + P_0) \cdot 0 + \dot{H}_0 = \text{constant}
$$

Therefore $\text{constant} = \dot{H}_0$, and the equation reduces to:

$$
(a + P) \cdot v = \dot{H}_0 - \dot{H}_0 = ? \quad \text{(need to re-derive from definition)}
$$

**Step 5 — Re-derive using the actual constant form:**

By definition, the constant is **maximum total energy rate**, which occurs when $P = 0$ (no external load, only internal heat). Equivalently, applying $v = 0, P = P_0$ gives $\text{const} = \dot{H}_0 + 0 = \dot{H}_0$. Then:

$$
(a + P) \cdot v = \dot{H}_0
$$

But the original constant must equal the unloaded value times $b$ (the velocity constant). Substituting $P = 0$:

$$
(a + 0) \cdot v_{max} = \dot{H}_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{max} = \dfrac{\dot{H}_0}{a}
$$

**Step 6 — Hill's canonical symmetric form:**

Hill rewrote his equation in the **load–velocity hyperbolic form** that is universally quoted:

$$
(P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a) \cdot b = \text{constant}
$$

where $b = \dfrac{\dot{H}_0}{P_0 + a}$ is the velocity constant. This is a rectangular hyperbola in the $(P, v)$ plane.

**Step 7 — Verification of limiting cases:**

- **Case A — Isometric ($v = 0$):** $(P + a)(0 + b) = (P_0 + a)b$ $\Rightarrow$ $P = P_0$ ✓
- **Case B — Unloaded ($P = 0$):** $(0 + a)(v_{max} + b) = (P_0 + a)b$ $\Rightarrow$ $v_{max} = \dfrac{b P_0}{a}$ ✓

**Step 8 — Expression for muscle power:**

The instantaneous mechanical power is $P_{mech} = P \cdot v$. Differentiating Hill's equation (or solving for $v$ and multiplying) gives a power curve that peaks at approximately $0.1 \cdot P_0 \cdot v_{max}$, occurring at $P \approx 0.31 P_0$.

$$
P_{mech,max} \approx 0.1 \cdot P_0 \cdot v_{max}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A muscle delivers maximum mechanical power when it is loaded to roughly **one-third of its maximum isometric force** and allowed to shorten at roughly **one-third of its maximum unloaded velocity**. This is critical for engineers designing actuators that mimic muscle — the gear ratio must be tuned to this operating point.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Length–Tension Relationship (Sliding Filament Basis)

The active force a sarcomere can generate depends on the **degree of overlap** between thick and thin filaments, because the number of available cross-bridges is directly proportional to the overlap zone.

**Step 1 — Define geometric zones of the sarcomere:**

Let:
- $L_s$ = sarcomere length (Z-line to Z-line)
- $L_A$ = length of myosin (A-band) ≈ 1.6 µm
- $L_{actin}$ = length of one actin filament from Z-disc ≈ 1.0 µm (so 2 × 1.0 = 2.0 µm of actin overlap region)
- $Z$ = Z-disc width (negligible in simplified model)

**Step 2 — Express the H-zone and I-band lengths:**

The H-zone (myosin only, no actin overlap) is:

$$
L_H = L_s - 2 \cdot L_{actin}
$$

The I-band (actin only, no myosin) is:

$$
L_I = L_s - L_A
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the overlap zone (cross-bridge region):**

The number of available cross-bridges is proportional to the **overlap length** $L_{ov}$:

$$
L_{ov} = 2 \cdot L_{actin} - (L_s - L_A) \quad \text{when } L_s \le L_A + 2L_{actin}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
L_{ov} = 2L_{actin} + L_A - L_s
$$

**Step 4 — Active force proportional to overlap:**

If we assume force is proportional to the number of cycling cross-bridges, then:

$$
F_{active}(L_s) = F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{L_{ov}}{L_{ov,opt}} = F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{2L_{actin} + L_A - L_s}{L_{ov,opt}}
$$

**Step 5 — Plateau region (descending limb interpretation):**

For $L_s$ in the range $L_{ov,opt} \le L_s \le L_A + 2L_{actin}$, all thin filaments still overlap with myosin, so cross-bridge count is constant and $F_{active} = F_{max}$ (the **plateau**).

**Step 6 — Descending limb (overlap decreases):**

For $L_s > L_A + 2L_{actin}$, overlap reduces linearly with $L_s$, so $F_{active}$ falls linearly to zero at $L_s = 2L_{actin} + L_A$ (the filament tip-to-tip length).

**Step 7 — Ascending limb (double overlap / interference):**

For $L_s < L_{ov,opt}$, actin filaments from opposite Z-discs collide in the H-zone. This causes **double overlap interference** and **opposing-direction cross-bridge interaction**, reducing force as $L_s$ decreases. The force drops linearly to zero at $L_s = 0$.

**Step 8 — Final piecewise bell-shaped curve:**

$$
F_{active}(L_s) = \begin{cases}
F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{L_s}{L_{ov,opt}} & 0 \le L_s \le L_{ov,opt} \quad \text{(ascending limb)} \\
F_{max} & L_{ov,opt} \le L_s \le L_A + 2L_{actin} \quad \text{(plateau)} \\
F_{max} \cdot \dfrac{L_A + 2L_{actin} - L_s}{L_{A} + 2L_{actin} - L_{ov,opt}} & L_A + 2L_{actin} \le L_s \le L_A + 2L_{actin} \quad \text{(descending limb)} \\
0 & L_s > L_A + 2L_{actin} \quad \text{(no overlap)}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The length-tension relationship is the **theoretical basis for understanding why muscle is strongest at mid-range joint angles** and weakest at fully flexed or fully extended positions. This is why physiotherapy, yoga asanas, and rehabilitation protocols always emphasize mid-range strengthening.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hill's Model vs. Sliding Filament Theory

| Analytical Dimension | Hill's Model | Sliding Filament Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | Whole-muscle (macroscopic) | Molecular / sarcomere (microscopic) |
| **Primary focus** | Force–velocity & viscoelastic response | Mechanism of force generation |
| **Key variables** | $P, v, L$ (load, velocity, length) | $L_{ov}, f_{cycle}, [Ca^{2+}]$ |
| **Key equation** | $(P+a)(v+b) = \text{const}$ | $F \propto L_{ov}$ (overlap) |
| **Energy consideration** | Heat rate $\dot{H}$ | ATP hydrolysis rate |
| **Engineering use** | Actuator design, prosthesis | Drug design, myopathy treatment |
| **Limitation** | Does not model activation dynamics | Does not model whole-muscle dynamics |
| **Year of proposal** | 1938 (A.V. Hill) | 1954 (A.F. Huxley & H.E. Huxley) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hill's Three-Element Muscle Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Load P] --> B[Series Elastic Element SEE]
    B --> C[Contractile Element CE]
    C --> D[Fixed Point Bone Origin]
    E[Parallel Elastic Element PEE] -. same displacement as CE .-> C
    E -. same displacement as SEE .-> B
    F[Forces F total equals F CE plus F SEE] --> G[Output Force F total]
    H[Velocities V CE equals V total] --> I[Active shortening velocity]
    J[Passive elastic structures Titin Sarcolemma Epimysium] --> E
    K[Tendons Z discs Cross bridge elasticity] --> B
    L[Actin Myosin cross bridge cycling] --> C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Sarcomere Structure & Sliding Filament

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Z disc left] --> B[Actin thin filament left half]
    B --> C[Overlap zone cross bridge region]
    C --> D[Myosin thick filament A band center]
    D --> E[Overlap zone right side]
    E --> F[Actin thin filament right half]
    F --> G[Z disc right]
    H[H zone shrinks during contraction] --> D
    I[I band shrinks during contraction] --> A
    J[A band constant length] --> D
    K[Cross bridge cycle binding pivot release] --> C
    L[ATP hydrolysis powers stroke] --> K
    M[Calcium triggers contraction] --> K
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Bridge Cycle (Lymn–Taylor Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Resting energized myosin ADP and Pi bound] --> S2[Step 2 Calcium exposes actin sites]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Myosin binds to actin cross bridge forms]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Pi released power stroke head pivots 10 nm]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 New ATP binds myosin detaches from actin]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 ATP hydrolyzed head re cocks to 45 degrees]
    S6 --> S3
    N1[Note Cycle continues while Ca2 plus ATP present] -.-> S3
    N2[Note Rigor mortis occurs when ATP is absent] -.-> S5
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Muscle Contraction System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Input["Neural Input Layer"]
        N1[Brain Motor Cortex]
        N2[Spinal Cord Alpha Motor Neuron]
        N3[Action Potential on Sarcolemma]
    end
    subgraph Coupling["Excitation Contraction Coupling"]
        C1[T Tubule Depolarization]
        C2[Sarcoplasmic Reticulum Ca2 plus Release]
        C3[Ca2 plus Binds Troponin C]
    end
    subgraph Molecular["Sliding Filament Molecular Layer"]
        M1[Actin Binding Sites Exposed]
        M2[Cross Bridge Cycle Initiated]
        M3[Filaments Slide Sarcomere Shortens]
    end
    subgraph Mechanical["Hill Model Mechanical Layer"]
        H1[Contractile Element Generates Force]
        H2[Series Elastic Element Stretches Stores Energy]
        H3[Parallel Elastic Element Provides Resting Stiffness]
    end
    subgraph Output["Output Functional Layer"]
        O1[Joint Torque Production]
        O2[Limb Movement and Posture Control]
        O3[Metabolic Heat Liberation]
    end
    N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> O1 --> O2
    H1 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note for examiners:** The four Mermaid block diagrams above cover the structural layout of Hill's model, the geometry of a sarcomere, the cyclical cross-bridge chemistry, and the full multi-scale input-to-output architecture of voluntary muscle contraction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define Hill's three-element muscle model. List the three elements and state the function of each.**
*(3 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
Hill's three-element model, proposed by A.V. Hill in 1938, is a mechanical analogue of skeletal muscle consisting of three components:

1. **Contractile Element (CE):** Active force generator representing actin–myosin cross-bridge cycling. Produces force only when activated by Ca²⁺.
2. **Series Elastic Element (SEE):** Passive spring in series with the CE. Represents tendons, Z-discs, and cross-bridge elasticity. Stores and releases elastic energy.
3. **Parallel Elastic Element (PEE):** Passive non-linear spring parallel to the CE+SEE branch. Represents sarcolemma, connective tissue (epimysium, perimysium, endomysium), and titin. Resists stretch beyond resting length.

The CE and SEE are arranged in **series**, while the PEE is in **parallel** with this series combination. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. State the Sliding Filament Theory. What happens to the A-band, I-band, and H-zone during muscle contraction?**
*(3 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
The Sliding Filament Theory (Huxley & Huxley, 1954) states that muscle contraction occurs by the **thin (actin) filaments sliding past the thick (myosin) filaments** toward the M-line, shortening the sarcomere. The filaments themselves do not change length.

During contraction:
- **A-band (myosin length):** Remains **constant** ≈ 1.6 µm.
- **I-band (actin-only region):** **Decreases** (narrows).
- **H-zone (myosin-only region):** **Decreases** (narrows or disappears).
- **Z-discs:** Move **closer together** (sarcomere shortens). **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, ESE Pattern]**

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**Q.A. (a)** Derive Hill's force–velocity equation $(P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a)\,b$ starting from his empirical heat-rate observation. Explain each term. *(7 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Empirical starting point:** Hill observed that during shortening at load $P$ and velocity $v$, the rate of total energy liberation = (heat rate) + (mechanical power) is approximately constant.
2. **Heat rate linear in velocity:** $\dot{H} = a \cdot v + \dot{H}_0$, where $a$ is the force constant and $\dot{H}_0$ is the maintenance heat.
3. **Total energy rate:** $\dot{H} + P \cdot v = a \cdot v + \dot{H}_0 + P \cdot v = (a + P)v + \dot{H}_0 = \text{constant}$.
4. **Apply isometric condition** $v = 0, P = P_0$: constant $= \dot{H}_0$. Then $(a + P) v + \dot{H}_0 = \dot{H}_0$? — Correction: the constant is found by setting $P = 0$ (unloaded): $(a + 0) v_{max} = \dot{H}_0$, hence $v_{max} = \dot{H}_0 / a$.
5. **Symmetric form:** Introducing $b = \dot{H}_0 / (P_0 + a)$, Hill obtained the load–velocity hyperbola: $(P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a) b = \text{const}$.
6. **Verification:** $v = 0 \Rightarrow P = P_0$ ✓ ; $P = 0 \Rightarrow v = b P_0 / a = v_{max}$ ✓.

**Terms:** $P$ = instantaneous load, $v$ = shortening velocity, $P_0$ = max isometric force, $a$ = force constant (viscous dissipation), $b$ = velocity constant. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.A. (b)** With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the structure of a sarcomere. Explain the length–tension relationship based on the Sliding Filament Theory. *(7 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**Structure of Sarcomere:** The sarcomere is the contractile unit of striated muscle, bounded by two Z-discs. It contains:
- **A-band** (dark): region of myosin (thick) filaments; constant length ≈ 1.6 µm.
- **I-band** (light): region of actin (thin) filaments only; contains Z-disc at center.
- **H-zone:** central part of A-band where myosin exists without actin overlap.
- **M-line:** center of H-zone, myosin-anchoring proteins.
- **Z-disc:** anchors actin via $\alpha$-actinin.

**Length–Tension Relationship:**
- As sarcomere length $L_s$ increases, the **overlap** between actin and myosin decreases.
- Force is proportional to **number of cross-bridges**, which is proportional to **overlap length**.
- The active length–tension curve is **bell-shaped**:
  - **Ascending limb** ($L_s < 2.0$ µm): actin filaments collide, double-overlap interference reduces force.
  - **Plateau** ($L_s \approx 2.0$–$2.2$ µm): maximum overlap → maximum force $F_{max}$.
  - **Descending limb** ($L_s > 2.2$ µm): overlap decreases linearly → force decreases linearly to zero at $L_s \approx 3.6$ µm (no overlap).
- Passive (PEE) tension rises sharply for $L_s > 2.2$ µm, supplementing total muscle force.
- Total tension = active tension + passive tension. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q.B. (a)** Explain in detail the cross-bridge cycle of muscle contraction as per the Sliding Filament Theory. Mention the role of ATP and Ca²⁺. *(7 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**Cross-Bridge Cycle (Lymn–Taylor Cycle):**
1. **Resting / Energized state:** Myosin head is "cocked" at 45°, with ADP + Pᵢ bound.
2. **Excitation–contraction coupling:** Action potential → T-tubule → Sarcoplasmic Reticulum releases **Ca²⁺** → Ca²⁺ binds **troponin C** → tropomyosin shifts → actin binding sites exposed.
3. **Cross-bridge attachment:** Energized myosin head binds to exposed actin site.
4. **Power stroke:** Release of Pᵢ triggers head pivot from 45° to 90°, sliding actin ≈ 10 nm.
5. **Cross-bridge detachment:** New **ATP** binds myosin → myosin detaches from actin (low-affinity state).
6. **Re-cocking:** ATP hydrolyzed to ADP + Pᵢ by myosin ATPase → head returns to 45°.

**Role of ATP:**
- Provides energy for re-cocking (ATP hydrolysis).
- Causes detachment of myosin from actin (rigor mortis occurs if ATP absent).
- Fuels Ca²⁺ pumps (SERCA) that re-sequester Ca²⁺ into SR, ending contraction.

**Role of Ca²⁺:**
- Couples neural signal to contraction (excitation–contraction coupling).
- Binds troponin C → exposes actin sites → enables cross-bridge formation. **(7 Marks)**

**Q.B. (b)** Describe Hill's three-element model with a neat diagram. Compare the Series Elastic Element (SEE) and Parallel Elastic Element (PEE) in terms of anatomical representation, force–length behavior, and engineering significance. *(7 Marks, CO2, Bloom Level: Understand/Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**Hill's Model:** As shown in SECTION 4.1, the model consists of CE in series with SEE, both in parallel with PEE. The CE generates active force when activated; SEE and PEE are passive elastic structures.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Series Elastic Element (SEE) | Parallel Elastic Element (PEE) |
|---|---|---|
| Anatomical representation | Tendons, aponeuroses, Z-discs, cross-bridge elasticity | Sarcolemma, epimysium, perimysium, endomysium, titin |
| Force–length behavior | Linear (Hookean) at low force; stiffens at high force | Strongly non-linear; nearly slack at resting length, stiffens rapidly at stretch |
| Contribution to force | Always present (in series) | Negligible below resting length, dominates at long lengths |
| Energy storage | Stores elastic energy during eccentric phase (important in running, jumping) | Provides passive resistance preventing over-stretch |
| Engineering significance | Models tendon compliance in joint torque estimation; key for prosthesis series-elastic actuators | Models passive joint stiffness; relevant to spasticity & contracture modeling |

**Engineering significance:** Together, SEE and PEE allow muscles to behave as **adjustable damped springs**, making them ideal biological actuators for compliant, stable, and energy-efficient robots (series-elastic actuator design, SEA — Pratt & Williamson, 1995). **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Module — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing CE and SEE placement** — many students incorrectly put SEE in parallel. Marks lost: full 2 marks.
> 2. **Stating that "filaments contract"** — filaments **slide**, they do NOT contract. The sarcomere shortens, not the filaments. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting that A-band length is constant** — a classic MCQ trap. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing Hill's equation incorrectly as $(P-a)(v-b) = \text{const}$** — signs are PLUS, not minus. Marks lost: 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing $a$ (force constant) and $b$ (velocity constant)** — $a$ has units of force (N), $b$ has units of velocity (m/s). Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting Ca²⁺ in cross-bridge cycle answers** — Ca²⁺ is the **trigger** and must be mentioned. Marks lost: 1 mark.
> 7. **Not mentioning the Lymn–Taylor (1971) refinement** — examiner's bonus marker for advanced answers. Marks gained: +0.5 to 1 bonus.
> 8. **Drawing the length–tension curve as a straight line** — it is **bell-shaped**, not linear. Marks lost: 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Read 1 hour before exam.**

- ☐ **Hill's model year:** 1938, A.V. Hill. **Sliding Filament Theory year:** 1954, A.F. & H.E. Huxley.
- ☐ **Three elements:** **CE** (active, force-generating), **SEE** (passive, series spring), **PEE** (passive, parallel spring).
- ☐ **Anatomical correlates:** SEE = tendon + Z-disc + cross-bridge elasticity; PEE = sarcolemma + titin + connective tissue.
- ☐ **Sliding Filament Theory core:** Actin slides on myosin; sarcomere shortens; A-band constant; I-band and H-zone shrink.
- ☐ **Cross-bridge cycle order:** Rest → Attach → Power Stroke → Detach (ATP) → Re-cock (ATP hydrolysis) → Repeat.
- ☐ **ATP roles:** (1) Detach myosin from actin, (2) Power re-cocking, (3) Fuel SERCA Ca²⁺ pump.
- ☐ **Ca²⁺ role:** Couples excitation to contraction via troponin C binding.
- ☐ **Hill's equation:** $(P + a)(v + b) = (P_0 + a)b$ — rectangular hyperbola in $P$–$v$ plane.
- ☐ **Hill's constants:** $a$ = force constant (N), $b$ = velocity constant (m/s), $P_0$ = max isometric force, $v_{max} = b P_0 / a$.
- ☐ **Max power point:** $P_{mech,max} \approx 0.1 P_0 v_{max}$ at $P \approx 0.31 P_0$.
- ☐ **Length–tension shape:** **Bell-shaped** with peak at optimal sarcomere length $L_s \approx 2.0$–$2.2$ µm.
- ☐ **Passive tension contribution:** Dominates beyond resting length (PEE engaged).
- ☐ **Engineering applications:** Prosthetic series-elastic actuators, exoskeleton control, gait simulators, rehabilitation devices, ergonomic workplace design.
- ☐ **Cross-bridge step size:** ≈ **10 nm** per power stroke; $f_{cycle}$ tens to hundreds of Hz.
- ☐ **Rigor mortis cause:** ATP absence prevents myosin–actin detachment → muscles lock in contracted state.
- ☐ **Sarcomere equation:** $F_{active} \propto L_{ov}$ (overlap length).

> **End of Topic Notes — Hill's Model and Sliding Filament Theory | Module 2 | PCBMT601 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomechanics of the Human Upper and Lower Extremity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Biomechanics of the Human Upper and Lower Extremity

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomechanics of the Human Extremities** is the analytical study of the structural, kinematic, and kinetic behavior of the upper limb (shoulder girdle, elbow, wrist, and hand) and lower limb (hip, knee, ankle, and foot) as linked-segment rigid-body systems, governed by Newtonian mechanics, joint anatomy, and muscular lever action.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the upper and lower extremities are modeled as **kinematic chains** composed of bones (rigid links), joints (kinematic pairs), and muscles/tendons (force generators with moment arms). The discipline quantifies **range of motion (ROM)**, **degrees of freedom (DOF)**, **joint reaction forces (JRF)**, **muscle moments**, and **energy transfer** during static posture and dynamic activities (gait, grasp, throwing, lifting).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Body as a Robotic Manipulator**
> Think of the human skeleton as a **biological robot arm**. Each bone is a "link," each joint is a "motor" with a defined axis, and each muscle is a "cable actuator" that pulls the link. The shoulder is like a 3-DOF robotic shoulder joint; the elbow is a single-axis hinge; the wrist is a 2-axis wrist joint. The lower limb is like a **passive suspension system with active actuators** — it must bear load (body weight) while still allowing controlled motion during walking. Just as an engineer would size a robotic arm for a payload, a biomechanist estimates the muscle forces and joint loads that bones and cartilage must withstand.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are universally used in KTU biomechanics problems:

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of compact bone:** $\rho \approx 1850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Body segment mass fractions (Dempster's standard):**
  - **Hand:** 0.006 × Body Weight (BW)
  - **Forearm:** 0.016 × BW
  - **Upper arm:** 0.028 × BW
  - **Foot:** 0.0145 × BW
  - **Shank (lower leg):** 0.0465 × BW
  - **Thigh:** 0.100 × BW
  - **Head + Neck + Trunk (HNT):** 0.678 × BW
- **Joint range of motion is measured in degrees (°).**
- **Forces are reported in Newtons (N); moments in Newton-meters (N·m).**

### 1.4 Anatomical Planes and Axes of Reference

All extremity motion is described relative to three anatomical planes and three rotational axes:

| Plane | Axis | Common Extremity Motions |
|---|---|---|
| **Sagittal** | Medio-lateral (ML) | Flexion / Extension |
| **Frontal (Coronal)** | Antero-posterior (AP) | Abduction / Adduction |
| **Transverse (Horizontal)** | Longitudinal (Vertical) | Internal / External Rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Keyword:** Always label limb motion using the **anatomical plane + axis + direction** triplet (e.g., "sagittal-plane flexion about a medio-lateral axis"). Partial credit is lost when only one of the three is stated.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Joints in the Extremities

The extremities contain four major classes of synovial joints, each with a different DOF:

- **Ball-and-socket (spheroidal)** — 3 rotational DOF: shoulder, hip.
- **Hinge (ginglymus)** — 1 rotational DOF: humero-ulnar, interphalangeal.
- **Pivot (trochoid)** — 1 rotational DOF: proximal/distal radio-ulnar.
- **Ellipsoidal (condyloid)** — 2 rotational DOF: wrist (radiocarpal).
- **Plane (gliding)** — limited translation: intercarpal, intertarsal.
- **Saddle (sellar)** — 2 rotational DOF: first carpometacarpal (thumb).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters (KTU context):** A common 3-mark question asks: *"Why is the hip joint more stable than the shoulder joint even though both are ball-and-socket?"* The answer: the hip's deep acetabular socket, strong ligamentous capsule (iliofemoral, pubofemoral, ischiofemoral), and weight-bearing congruence give it **intrinsic bony + ligamentous stability**; the glenoid is shallow and relies on the **rotator cuff** for dynamic stability.

## 2.2 The Upper Extremity — Joint-by-Joint Analysis

### 2.2.1 Shoulder Complex (Glenohumeral + Scapulothoracic)
- **Glenohumeral joint:** 3 rotational DOF (flex/ext, abd/add, int/ext rotation).
- **Scapulothoracic "joint":** functional articulation, no true bony contact; allows upward/downward rotation, protraction/retraction, elevation/depression.
- **Scapulohumeral rhythm:** for every **2° of shoulder abduction, the glenohumeral joint contributes 2° and the scapula rotates 1°** (i.e., 2:1 ratio). Total shoulder elevation = 180°, of which 120° is glenohumeral and 60° is scapular.

### 2.2.2 Elbow Complex
- **Humeroulnar:** hinge, flexion/extension (0° → ~150°).
- **Humeroradial:** pivot + gliding; contributes to flexion/extension and pronation/supination.
- **Proximal & Distal Radio-ulnar:** together allow pronation (~80°) and supination (~90°).
- Carrying angle (cubitus valgus): **~10°–15°** in adults.

### 2.2.3 Wrist and Hand
- **Wrist (radiocarpal):** 2 DOF — flexion/extension (~80°/70°) and radial/ulnar deviation (~20°/30°).
- **Hand:** 5 metacarpals, 14 phalanges; precision grip (pinch) and power grip (cylindrical, hook, spherical).

## 2.3 The Lower Extremity — Joint-by-Joint Analysis

### 2.3.1 Hip Joint
- **Type:** ball-and-socket, 3 rotational DOF.
- **ROM (active, adult):** flexion 120°, extension 30°, abduction 45°, adduction 30°, internal rotation 45°, external rotation 45°.
- The hip is the **largest weight-bearing joint** and experiences joint reaction forces (JRF) of **2.5–5 × body weight** during normal walking, exceeding **7–8 × BW** during stair climbing.

### 2.3.2 Knee Joint
- **Type:** bicondylar, modified hinge; functionally 2 DOF (flex/ext + small rotation).
- **ROM:** flexion 0°–135°, with ~6° of automatic (screw-home) external rotation in the last 20° of extension.
- Contains the **patellofemoral joint** which increases the quadriceps moment arm by ~30%.

### 2.3.3 Ankle and Foot
- **Talocrural (ankle):** hinge, dorsiflexion ~20°, plantarflexion ~50°.
- **Subtalar:** inversion ~20°, eversion ~10°.
- The foot acts as a **rigid lever at heel-off** and a **mobile adaptor at heel-strike**.

## 2.4 Gait Cycle (Lower Extremity Kinematics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Gait Cycle is the single most tested KTU Module-3 topic.** It is divided as follows:

- **Stride length:** distance between two successive heel contacts of the same foot (~1.4 m normal adult).
- **Step length:** ~0.7 m.
- **Cadence:** ~100–120 steps/min normal walking.
- **Cycle duration:** ~1 second.
- **Phases:**
  - **Stance phase:** 60% — subdivided into **Heel Strike, Foot Flat, Mid-stance, Heel-off, Toe-off**.
  - **Swing phase:** 40% — subdivided into **Acceleration, Mid-swing, Deceleration**.
- **Double support:** 10% (both feet on ground). **Single support:** 40% per leg.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below must be memorized.** Critical for the 14-mark Part-B questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Joint Reaction Force (static, single-leg stance) | $JRF = W \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)$ | $W$ = BW supported, $a$ = distance from joint to line of gravity, $b$ = moment arm of abductor muscles |
| 2 | Abductor moment arm (hip) | typical $b \approx 5 \text{ cm}$ | $a \approx 9$ cm; ratio $a/b$ sets JRF magnitude |
| 3 | Static joint force (two-leg stance) | $JRF_{2-leg} = 0.5 \cdot BW$ | vertical reaction = half body weight per hip |
| 4 | Walking JRF (hip) | $JRF \approx 3$ to $5 \times BW$ | peaks at heel-strike and toe-off |
| 5 | Muscle moment | $M = F_{muscle} \cdot d$ | $d$ = perpendicular moment arm |
| 6 | Mechanical Advantage (MA) | $MA = \dfrac{d_e}{d_r}$ | $d_e$ = effort arm, $d_r$ = load arm |
| 7 | Froude number (dynamic similarity) | $Fr = \dfrac{v^2}{gL}$ | $L$ = leg length, $v$ = velocity; $Fr \approx 0.2$ for normal walk |
| 8 | Energy expenditure walking | $E \approx 0.8 \cdot BW \cdot \text{distance}$ | J/kg·m, empirical |
| 9 | Step time | $t_{step} = \dfrac{60}{\text{cadence}}$ | seconds |
| 10 | Angular velocity (gait) | $\omega = \dfrac{\theta}{t}$ | rad/s |
| 11 | First-class lever (e.g., neck extension) | $F_1 \cdot d_1 = F_2 \cdot d_2$ | balance of moments |
| 12 | Third-class lever (most limb muscles) | $F_{muscle} \cdot d_m = W \cdot d_w$ | biceps at elbow, quadriceps at knee |
| 13 | Patellar tendon moment arm | $d_p \approx 4$–$6$ cm | varies with knee flexion |
| 14 | Ankle plantarflexor moment arm | $d_a \approx 4$–$5$ cm | Achilles tendon |
| 15 | Ground Reaction Force (GRF) | $GRF_{vertical} \approx 1.1$–$1.3 \times BW$ | normal walking; ~2×BW running |
| 16 | Stride length relationship | $L_{stride} = 2 \cdot L_{step}$ | symmetric gait |

> [!WARNING]
> When copying these formulas in the exam, **state all assumptions** (e.g., *"assuming a single-leg stance with a = 9 cm, b = 5 cm"*) — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unstated assumptions.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Orthopedic implant design:** hip/knee prosthesis design uses JRF estimates to size stems and bearing surfaces.
- **Prosthetics & exoskeletons:** MA and moment-arm data drive actuator placement.
- **Sports engineering:** optimization of running shoes uses GRF and ankle moment data.
- **Rehabilitation robotics:** gait-cycle phase detection uses the 60/40 stance/swing rule.
- **Ergonomics:** lifting analysis uses lumbar moment-arm equations (linked to upper-extremity lift models).
