# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exhaustive** — every algebraic transition and assumption is written out. Use these as the gold-standard template for your 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Derivation: Static Hip Joint Reaction Force in Single-Leg Stance

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a person of body weight $W$ standing on **one leg**. The center of mass of the head-arms-trunk (HAT) and the contralateral (lifted) leg is supported by the stance leg's hip. Let:

- $a$ = horizontal distance from the hip joint center to the line of gravity of HAT (≈ 9 cm)
- $b$ = perpendicular moment arm of the **hip abductor muscles** (gluteus medius/minimus) about the hip joint (≈ 5 cm)
- $F_{abd}$ = tension in the abductor muscles
- $J$ = hip joint reaction force (resultant magnitude we seek)

### 3.1.2 Free-Body Diagram (verbal)

- The hip joint supports the **weight** $W$ acting downward through the line of gravity located distance $a$ medial to the joint.
- The **abductors** pull on the greater trochanter with force $F_{abd}$ at perpendicular distance $b$ from the joint.
- The **joint reaction force** $J$ acts at the joint center in an unknown direction.

### 3.1.3 Moment Equilibrium (about the hip joint)

Taking moments about the hip joint eliminates $J$ and gives a single equation:

$$
\sum M_{hip} = 0
$$

The abductors create a **counter-clockwise** moment (lateral pull) that balances the **clockwise** moment of body weight:

$$
F_{abd} \cdot b - W \cdot a = 0
$$

Solving for $F_{abd}$:

$$
F_{abd} = W \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}
$$

### 3.1.4 Force Equilibrium

Summing forces vertically:

$$
J_{vertical} - W + F_{abd,vertical} = 0
$$

Because the abductor line of action has a small vertical component, to a first approximation:

$$
J_{vertical} = W - F_{abd} \quad \text{(when abductors are nearly horizontal)}
$$

Substituting $F_{abd}$:

$$
J_{vertical} = W - W \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} = W \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{b}\right)
$$

### 3.1.5 Resultant JRF Magnitude

Combining vertical and horizontal components, the standard simplified textbook expression is:

$$
J = W \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right)
$$

> Derivation note: the "**+**" sign arises because the horizontal component of the abductor force is $F_{abd} \cdot \cos\theta$ and combines vectorially with the vertical reaction. In the simplified 1-D moment-arm model the result is the well-known formula.

### 3.1.6 Numerical Example (KTU-typical)

Take $W = 700 \text{ N}$, $a = 9 \text{ cm}$, $b = 5 \text{ cm}$:

$$
F_{abd} = 700 \times \dfrac{9}{5} = 1260 \text{ N}
$$

$$
J = 700 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{9}{5}\right) = 700 \times 2.8 = 1960 \text{ N}
$$

Therefore the **hip JRF ≈ 1960 N ≈ 2.8 × BW**, consistent with measured in-vivo values of 2.5–5 × BW during single-leg stance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the abductors matter so much:** because $b$ is much smaller than $a$, the abductors must generate forces **larger than body weight** to keep the pelvis level, and the joint must pay back a reaction that is also larger than body weight. This is the central reason the hip is the largest load-bearing joint in the body.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mechanical Advantage of the Biceps at the Elbow

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A person holds a weight $W$ in the hand with the forearm horizontal. The biceps inserts on the radial tuberosity at a perpendicular moment arm $d_b \approx 5 \text{ cm}$ from the elbow joint center. The weight is held at a distance $L$ from the elbow (assume $L = 30 \text{ cm}$ from elbow to hand).

### 3.2.2 Moment Equilibrium

The biceps must produce a flexion moment equal to the extension moment of the load:

$$
F_{biceps} \cdot d_b = W \cdot L
$$

$$
F_{biceps} = W \cdot \dfrac{L}{d_b} = W \cdot \dfrac{30}{5} = 6W
$$

So the biceps must pull **6 times the weight** to hold the forearm horizontal — a clear example of **speed-and-range lever trade-off** (third-class lever).

### 3.2.3 Mechanical Advantage

$$
MA = \dfrac{d_b}{L} = \dfrac{5}{30} = 0.167
$$

A small MA means the muscle sacrifices force for **velocity and displacement** at the hand: the hand moves 6 times faster and 6 times farther than the biceps shortens.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Knee Quadriceps Force Using Patellar Moment Arm

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A person extends the knee against a load. The patellar tendon applies a force $F_q$ at a moment arm $d_q$ from the knee center. The external load creates a flexion moment $M_{ext}$ about the knee.

### 3.3.2 Equilibrium

$$
F_q = \dfrac{M_{ext}}{d_q}
$$

If $M_{ext} = 50 \text{ N·m}$ and $d_q = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$:

$$
F_q = \dfrac{50}{0.05} = 1000 \text{ N}
$$

### 3.3.3 Patellofemoral Joint Reaction Force

The patella "wraps" around the femoral trochlear groove, effectively doubling the quadriceps force on the patella at typical flexion angles:

$$
F_{PFJ} \approx 2 F_q \cdot \cos\theta
$$

At $\theta = 60°$ knee flexion:

$$
F_{PFJ} = 2 \times 1000 \times \cos 60° = 2 \times 1000 \times 0.5 = 1000 \text{ N}
$$

This explains why **patellofemoral pain is most common in deep-squat activities** — the patella bears nearly 2× the quadriceps force.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Gait Cycle Velocity and Stride Length

### 3.4.1 Given Data
- Stride length: $L_{stride} = 1.4 \text{ m}$
- Cadence: $c = 110 \text{ steps/min}$
- Step length: $L_{step} = 0.7 \text{ m}$

### 3.4.2 Step Time

$$
t_{step} = \dfrac{60}{c} = \dfrac{60}{110} = 0.545 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.4.3 Stride Time (two steps)

$$
t_{stride} = 2 \cdot t_{step} = 1.09 \text{ s}
$$

### 3.4.4 Walking Velocity

$$
v = \dfrac{L_{stride}}{t_{stride}} = \dfrac{1.4}{1.09} = 1.285 \text{ m/s} \approx 4.63 \text{ km/h}
$$

### 3.4.5 Froude Number Check

With leg length $L = 0.9 \text{ m}$:

$$
Fr = \dfrac{v^2}{gL} = \dfrac{(1.285)^2}{9.81 \times 0.9} = \dfrac{1.651}{8.829} = 0.187
$$

This is in the **walk-regime** ($Fr < 0.5$), confirming the calculation is consistent with normal walking.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Joint Comparison

| Joint | Type | DOF | Stabilizers | Typical JRF (× BW) | Common Injury |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Glenohumeral | Ball-socket | 3 | Rotator cuff, labrum, capsule | 0.9 | Dislocation, rotator-cuff tear |
| Elbow (humeroulnar) | Hinge | 1 | Ulnar/radial collaterals | 0.5–1.0 | Lateral epicondylitis |
| Wrist | Ellipsoidal | 2 | TFCC, extrinsic ligaments | 0.3–0.5 | Carpal tunnel syndrome |
| Hip | Ball-socket | 3 | Iliofemoral, labrum | 2.5–5.0 | Osteoarthritis, fracture |
| Knee | Modified hinge | 2 | Cruciates, collaterals, menisci | 2.0–4.0 (walking) | ACL tear, meniscal tear |
| Ankle | Hinge | 1 | Deltoid, lateral ligaments | 1.5–3.0 | Sprain (inversion) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define the term "Degrees of Freedom" (DOF) as applied to human joints. Give the DOF for the hip joint and elbow joint. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** DOF is the number of independent rotational (and/or translational) movements a joint can perform. In biomechanics, it is the minimum number of coordinates required to fully specify the position of one body segment relative to another.
- **Hip joint (ball-and-socket):** **3 rotational DOF** — flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, internal/external rotation.
- **Elbow joint (humeroulnar):** **1 rotational DOF** — flexion/extension only.

> [!NOTE]
> Mention the planes and axes for full credit.

---

### Question 2. List the three major sub-phases of the **stance phase** of the human gait cycle, and state the percentage of the gait cycle occupied by the entire stance phase. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
- The stance phase occupies **60%** of the gait cycle.
- Three major sub-phases:
  1. **Heel strike / Initial contact (0%)**
  2. **Mid-stance (~12–31%)**
  3. **Toe-off / Terminal contact (~50–60%)**
- (Optionally: also Foot Flat and Heel Off, giving the full five divisions.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation, you must answer **one full question (14 marks)**, and each question has internal choice. Model solutions for both options A and B are given below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain with neat sketches the kinematics of the shoulder complex. Discuss the "scapulohumeral rhythm" and its clinical significance. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Anatomical components of the shoulder complex:**
1. **Glenohumeral joint** — ball-and-socket between the humeral head and glenoid fossa of the scapula; 3 DOF.
2. **Acromioclavicular joint** — between acromion and clavicle; allows scapular rotation and tilting.
3. **Sternoclavicular joint** — saddle-type between manubrium and clavicle; only bony link of upper limb to axial skeleton.
4. **Scapulothoracic articulation** — functional (not a true synovial joint) gliding of the scapula on the thoracic wall.

**Kinematic contributions during abduction (0° → 180°):**
- 0°–30°: predominantly glenohumeral motion.
- 30°–90°: combined glenohumeral + scapular motion begins.
- 90°–180°: full combined motion; clavicular elevation and posterior rotation of the clavicle contribute.

**Scapulohumeral rhythm:**
- The classic ratio is **2 : 1**, meaning for every **2°** of glenohumeral abduction, the scapula rotates **1°** in the same direction (upward rotation).
- Total of 180° shoulder elevation = **120° glenohumeral + 60° scapulothoracic rotation**.

**Clinical significance:**
- Disruption of the 2:1 rhythm (called **scapular dyskinesis**) is associated with **impingement syndrome**, **rotator-cuff tears**, and **frozen shoulder**.
- Therapists use the rhythm to identify which component (glenohumeral or scapular) is restricted in patients with shoulder pain.
- A shoulder that achieves 180° of elevation with only 80° of glenohumeral motion (i.e., a 1.33:1 rhythm) is compensating via excessive scapular motion — a red flag for joint pathology.

#### (b) A man weighing 700 N stands on one leg. The center of gravity of the head, arms, trunk and the non-weight-bearing leg is 9 cm medial to the hip joint center. The abductor muscles (gluteus medius and minimus) have a perpendicular moment arm of 5 cm. Calculate (i) the abductor muscle force, and (ii) the magnitude of the hip joint reaction force. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $W = 700 \text{ N}$
- $a = 9 \text{ cm} = 0.09 \text{ m}$
- $b = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Moment equilibrium about the hip joint:**

$$
\sum M_{hip} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{abd} \cdot b = W \cdot a
$$

$$
F_{abd} = \dfrac{W \cdot a}{b} = \dfrac{700 \times 0.09}{0.05} = \dfrac{63}{0.05} = 1260 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Joint reaction force:**

$$
J = W \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{b}\right) = 700 \left(1 + \dfrac{9}{5}\right) = 700 \times 2.8 = 1960 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- Abductor force = **1260 N (≈ 1.8 × BW)**
- Hip JRF = **1960 N (≈ 2.8 × BW)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly forget the "+1" inside the parenthesis and write $J = W \cdot (a/b) = 1260 \text{ N}$. That is wrong. The +1 accounts for the body-weight component that the joint must also support. Deduct 2 marks for this omission.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Describe the human gait cycle with a labelled phase diagram. Explain the functional role of the stance and swing phases, and the significance of "double support." [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The gait cycle is the time interval between two successive occurrences of the same event during walking — typically **heel-strike to the next heel-strike of the same foot**. It has a normal duration of **~1 second**.

**Major divisions (with percentages):**
- **Stance phase (60%)** — the reference foot is in contact with the ground.
- **Swing phase (40%)** — the reference foot is off the ground and advancing.

**Stance sub-phases:**
1. **Heel strike (0%)** — initial contact; vertical GRF rises rapidly to ~1.1 × BW.
2. **Foot flat (0–12%)** — loading response; absorption of impact by ankle dorsiflexors and knee flexors.
3. **Mid-stance (12–31%)** — body passes over the supporting foot; the contralateral leg is in mid-swing.
4. **Heel-off (31–50%)** — ankle plantarflexors generate forward propulsion.
5. **Toe-off (50–60%)** — final release of GRF; transition to swing.

**Swing sub-phases:**
1. **Acceleration (60–73%)** — hip flexors (iliopsoas) drive the limb forward.
2. **Mid-swing (73–87%)** — limb passes under the body; passive pendulum motion.
3. **Deceleration (87–100%)** — hamstrings eccentrically contract to slow the leg for heel-strike.

**Functional role:**
- Stance provides **support, stability, and propulsion**; it is the only phase generating GRF.
- Swing provides **limb advancement and foot clearance**.

**Double support significance:**
- The two periods when **both feet are on the ground** together (10% of cycle) define walking.
- **Running eliminates double support** (replaced by a "float phase"), making it the kinematic criterion separating walk from run.
- Double support is critical for **balance in pathological gait** (e.g., cerebral palsy) — patients prolong it for stability.

#### (b) A patient performs knee-extension exercise. The external flexion moment about the knee is 60 N·m. The patellar tendon has a moment arm of 4 cm. Calculate (i) the quadriceps tendon force, and (ii) the patellofemoral joint reaction force at 60° of knee flexion. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $M_{ext} = 60 \text{ N·m}$
- $d_q = 4 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- $\theta = 60°$

**Step 1 — Quadriceps tendon force from moment equilibrium:**

$$
F_q = \dfrac{M_{ext}}{d_q} = \dfrac{60}{0.04} = 1500 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Patellofemoral joint reaction force:**

The patella is pulled by both the quadriceps tendon (above) and the patellar tendon (below), and is pressed against the femoral trochlear groove. At knee flexion angle $\theta$:

$$
F_{PFJ} \approx 2 F_q \cos\theta
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_{PFJ} = 2 \times 1500 \times \cos 60° = 2 \times 1500 \times 0.5 = 1500 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

- Quadriceps force = **1500 N**
- Patellofemoral JRF at 60° flexion = **1500 N** (≈ 2× BW for a 75 kg person)
- This is why **deep-squatting and stair descent** are commonly prescribed carefully in patients with patellofemoral pain — the PFJ force can exceed 3× BW at 90° flexion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write $F_{PFJ} = F_q$ instead of $2 F_q \cos\theta$. The factor **2** arises from the two-tendon pull on the patella, and $\cos\theta$ projects the patellar-tendon component into the joint-compression direction. Omitting either is a 1-mark deduction per term.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Upper & Lower Extremity Biomechanics**

### Quick-Recall Tables

| Item | Key Value / Rule |
|---|---|
| Hip joint type | Ball-and-socket, 3 DOF |
| Elbow joint type | Hinge (humeroulnar), 1 DOF |
| Knee joint type | Modified hinge, 2 DOF |
| Wrist joint type | Ellipsoidal, 2 DOF |
| Ankle joint type | Hinge, 1 DOF |
| Scapulohumeral rhythm | **2 : 1** (GH : scapular) |
| Carrying angle | **10°–15°** |
| Stance phase | **60%** of gait cycle |
| Swing phase | **40%** of gait cycle |
| Double support | **10%** of cycle |
| Single support | **40%** of cycle per leg |
| Stride length | **~1.4 m** (normal adult) |
| Cadence | **100–120 steps/min** |
| Walking JRF at hip | **2.5–5 × BW** |
| Running GRF | **~2 × BW** |
| Walking GRF | **~1.1–1.3 × BW** |
| Froude number (walk) | **< 0.5** |
| Biceps moment arm | **~5 cm** |
| Quadriceps moment arm | **~4–6 cm** |
| Achilles moment arm | **~4–5 cm** |

### Top 10 Things You Must Not Forget

1. **Always state planes and axes** when describing joint motion (sagittal/frontal/transverse + ML/AP/vertical).
2. **JRF formula $J = W(1 + a/b)$** assumes single-leg stance; for two-leg stance, halve the body weight first.
3. **The hip abductors must generate force > BW** because $b < a$ — this is a frequent 3-mark question.
4. **Gait cycle = 60% stance + 40% swing = 10% double support + 40% single support per leg** (5% from each leg).
5. **Scapulohumeral rhythm 2:1** is the gold standard; total elevation 180° = 120° glenohumeral + 60° scapular.
6. **Third-class lever** dominates in limb muscles (biceps, quadriceps, Achilles) — mechanical advantage is small (<0.2), but speed and displacement are amplified.
7. **Patellofemoral force $\approx 2 F_q \cos\theta$** — must be quoted as a vector, not just a scalar.
8. **Froude number < 0.5** confirms walking; **> 0.5** indicates running.
9. **Bones of the upper limb:** humerus, radius, ulna, 8 carpals, 5 metacarpals, 14 phalanges.
10. **Bones of the lower limb:** femur, patella, tibia, fibula, 7 tarsals, 5 metatarsals, 14 phalanges.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Warning — Module 3 Common Deductions:**
> - Forgetting to draw a labelled free-body diagram in 14-mark numericals: **−2 marks**.
> - Mixing "hip joint" and "knee joint" moment-arm values: **−1 mark each occurrence**.
> - Confusing **degree** of motion with **radian** in angular-velocity problems: **−1 mark**.
> - Failing to convert cm to m in force-moment calculations: **−1 mark per error**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loads on the Upper Extremity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Loads on the Upper Extremity**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loads on the Upper Extremity** refers to the complete set of internal and external **forces, moments, joint reaction forces, and muscle tensions** acting on the anatomical structures of the shoulder girdle, upper arm, elbow, forearm, wrist, and hand during static postures and dynamic activities.

In KTU 2024 Scheme biomechanics terminology, the "upper extremity" is treated as a **kinematic chain** of four rigid bodies (upper arm, forearm, hand, and the load being manipulated) connected by three primary joints — the **glenohumeral**, **humeroulnar/radiohumeral (elbow)**, and **radiocarpal (wrist)** joints. Loads are classified as:

| Load Type | Description | Typical Source |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Body load (intrinsic)** | Gravitational force of the limb segments | Weight of arm, forearm, hand |
| **External load (extrinsic)** | Force applied by objects, tools, or environment | Carried weight, push/pull force |
| **Muscle load** | Active tension generated by contractile elements | Biceps, triceps, deltoid, rotator cuff |
| **Joint reaction load** | Internal force transmitted across articular surfaces | Humeroulnar compression, glenohumeral compression |
| **Inertial load** | Dynamic force due to acceleration of segments | Throwing, swinging, lifting |

> [!NOTE]
> The **gravitational acceleration** constant used universally in upper-extremity analysis is **g = 9.81 m/s²**, while limb segment weights are expressed as a percentage of **Total Body Weight (TBW)** — a standard convention adopted in anthropometric tables for biomechanics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Crane Model

Imagine the upper limb as a **construction crane mounted on a tower**:

- The **trunk** is the tower (fixed base).
- The **humerus** is the primary boom.
- The **forearm** is the secondary boom hinged at the elbow.
- The **hand** is the hook and pulley at the end.
- The **muscles** (biceps, triceps, deltoid) are the steel cables powered by winches.
- The **external object held** is the payload hanging from the hook.

Just as a crane engineer must calculate the **tension in the cables**, the **force on the hinge pins (joints)**, and the **moment the tower must resist**, a biomechanist must calculate the **muscle force**, **joint reaction force**, and **shoulder torque** for any given load in the hand. A startling fact emerges in both systems: **the force inside the cables/joints is often many times greater than the weight of the payload** — a phenomenon called **mechanical disadvantage** of the musculoskeletal lever system.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based framework expects PCBMT601 students to **apply Newton's laws of statics to biological lever systems**, which is the core analytical skill tested in Module 3 problems on the upper extremity. This topic directly maps to **CO3: Apply static and dynamic analysis techniques to human joint systems.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Free Body Diagram (FBD) Approach — Step-by-Step Logic

The systematic method for analyzing loads on the upper extremity is identical to engineering statics applied to a multi-link mechanism. The logical sequence is:

**Step 1 — Segment Selection**
Isolate one rigid body (e.g., the forearm) by passing a section through the joints above and below it. Treat all other structures (muscles, adjacent bones, external objects) as **external forces and moments** acting on the chosen segment.

**Step 2 — Force Inventory**
Enumerate every force and moment acting on the segment:
- Gravitational force of the segment (W_seg = m_seg × g) acting at the **center of mass (CoM)**.
- Gravitational force of any held object (W_ext) acting at the grip point.
- Active muscle force (F_m) acting along the line of pull at its **line of action**.
- Joint reaction force (R_j) at the proximal and distal joints (unknown magnitude and direction).
- Applied external forces (push, pull, contact with surface).

**Step 3 — Coordinate System**
Establish a 2D Cartesian frame (usually x horizontal, y vertical) consistent with the limb's anatomical orientation. For 3D problems, add a z-axis.

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Equations**
Apply the three scalar equations of static equilibrium:

$$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M_O = 0$$

These three equations suffice to solve for **three unknown force/moment components** per isolated segment.

**Step 5 — Successive Segments**
Repeat the FBD for the adjacent segment (e.g., the upper arm) using the joint reaction found in Step 4 as a known applied force (Newton's third law reverses its direction).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** The musculoskeletal system is mechanically a **statically determinate structure** in many common postures (especially when a single prime mover dominates), which is why simple 2D free-body analysis yields closed-form solutions — a fact heavily exploited in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.2 Anthropometric Reference Data for the Upper Extremity

These are the standard segment parameters used universally in upper-limb biomechanics:

| Segment | Mass (% of TBW) | CoM Location (from proximal end, as % of segment length) |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Upper arm (humerus + soft tissue)** | **2.8 %** | **43.6 %** |
| **Forearm (radius + ulna + soft tissue)** | **1.6 %** | **43.0 %** |
| **Hand (carpals + metacarpals + phalanges)** | **0.6 %** | **50.6 %** (approx. at the middle) |
| **Total upper limb (one side, including shoulder)** | **~5.0 – 5.1 %** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **moment arm** (perpendicular distance from joint center to the line of muscle action) is a critical geometric parameter. Standard values frequently used in KTU problems:
> - **Biceps brachii at the elbow** ≈ **5 cm** (line of action passes ~5 cm anterior to the trochlear axis)
> - **Deltoid at the shoulder** ≈ **5 cm** (line of action passes ~5 cm lateral to the glenohumeral axis when the arm is at ~90° abduction)
> - **Brachialis at the elbow** ≈ **2 – 3 cm** (deeper, shorter moment arm)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|----------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $W_{seg} = m_{seg} \cdot g$ | Gravitational weight of a limb segment | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | $m_{seg} = p \cdot m_{body}$ | Segment mass from body mass and percentage p | kg |
| 3 | $\sum F_x = 0$ | Horizontal force equilibrium | N |
| 4 | $\sum F_y = 0$ | Vertical force equilibrium | N |
| 5 | $\sum M_O = 0$ | Moment equilibrium about point O | N·m |
| 6 | $F_m = \dfrac{\sum W_i \cdot d_i}{d_m}$ | Muscle force from moment balance | N |
| 7 | $R_j = \sqrt{R_{jx}^2 + R_{jy}^2}$ | Resultant joint reaction force magnitude | N |
| 8 | $\theta_R = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R_{jy}}{R_{jx}}\right)$ | Direction of joint reaction force | degrees or radians |
| 9 | $F_{compress} = \dfrac{F_m}{2 \sin(\theta/2)}$ | Joint compression in a two-bone/muscle system | N |
| 10 | $M_{muscle} = F_m \cdot d_m$ | Muscle moment (torque) about the joint | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical sign convention:** The pipe symbol `\vert` is forbidden inside KTU markdown table cells. The absolute value of x must be written as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ — **never with the raw character $\vert$** which would break the table parser.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of This Analysis

The methods taught in this module are not academic exercises. They are the **direct foundation** of:

- **Orthopedic implant design** — knowing the joint reaction force determines the required strength of a hip, shoulder, or elbow prosthesis.
- **Ergonomics and occupational health** — repetitive loads on the upper limb cause **cumulative trauma disorders** (e.g., carpal tunnel syndrome, rotator cuff tendinopathy, lateral epicondylitis). Quantifying loads informs workstation design and tool weight limits.
- **Rehabilitation engineering** — designing exoskeletons, orthoses, and resistance-training protocols.
- **Sports biomechanics** — analyzing the throwing motion, tennis serve, or weightlifting technique to prevent injury and optimize performance.
- **Forensic biomechanics** — reconstructing the loads sustained during a fall, assault, or vehicular accident.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step below is shown explicitly. The KTU 2024 valuation scheme allocates **partial marks for each correctly written equilibrium equation and substitution**, so a fully written derivation is essential.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Biceps Tension and Elbow Joint Reaction (Forearm Horizontal, Holding a Load)

### Problem Statement

A subject stands with the **upper arm vertical** and the **forearm horizontal**, holding a **mass of 5 kg** in the hand. The biceps muscle inserts at a perpendicular distance of **5 cm** from the elbow joint axis. The **center of mass of the forearm** is **15 cm** from the elbow. The **grip point** (where the load acts) is **35 cm** from the elbow. The weight of the forearm is **15 N**. Determine the **force in the biceps** and the **magnitude of the elbow joint reaction force**.

### Step 1 — Construct the Free Body Diagram of the Forearm

Isolate the forearm as a rigid body. Identify the four external forces acting on it (gravitational acceleration **g = 9.81 m/s²**):

1. **W_F** = weight of forearm = **15 N**, acting downward at the forearm CoM, **15 cm** from the elbow.
2. **W_L** = weight of the load in hand = 5 × 9.81 = **49.05 N**, acting downward at the grip, **35 cm** from the elbow.
3. **F_B** = biceps tension, acting vertically upward along the muscle's line of action, at a perpendicular distance of **5 cm** anterior to the elbow.
4. **R_E** = elbow joint reaction force, acting at the elbow joint (unknown direction and magnitude — to be solved).

### Step 2 — Take Moments About the Elbow Joint

Choosing the elbow as the moment center eliminates the joint reaction **R_E** from the moment equation, which is the standard KTU trick. Taking counterclockwise as positive:

$$\sum M_E = 0$$

Listing each moment individually:

$$+F_B \cdot (0.05\text{ m}) \;-\; W_F \cdot (0.15\text{ m}) \;-\; W_L \cdot (0.35\text{ m}) \;=\; 0$$

> The biceps moment is **counterclockwise** (it tends to rotate the forearm upward, i.e., flex the elbow). The two weight moments are **clockwise** (they tend to extend the elbow downward).

### Step 3 — Solve for the Biceps Force F_B

Rearrange the moment equation to isolate F_B:

$$F_B \cdot 0.05 \;=\; W_F \cdot 0.15 \;+\; W_L \cdot 0.35$$

$$F_B \;=\; \frac{W_F \cdot 0.15 \;+\; W_L \cdot 0.35}{0.05}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$F_B \;=\; \frac{(15)(0.15) \;+\; (49.05)(0.35)}{0.05}$$

$$F_B \;=\; \frac{2.25 \;+\; 17.1675}{0.05}$$

$$F_B \;=\; \frac{19.4175}{0.05}$$

$$\boxed{F_B \;=\; 388.35\text{ N}}$$

**Interpretation:** The biceps must generate about **388.35 N** of force to hold just **5 kg** in the hand. The mechanical disadvantage (MA = 5 cm ÷ 35 cm = 0.143) means the muscle force is roughly **7 times** the load.

### Step 4 — Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium to Find the Elbow Reaction

$$\sum F_y \;=\; 0$$

$$R_{Ey} \;+\; F_B \;-\; W_F \;-\; W_L \;=\; 0$$

Solving for R_Ey:

$$R_{Ey} \;=\; W_F \;+\; W_L \;-\; F_B$$

$$R_{Ey} \;=\; 15 \;+\; 49.05 \;-\; 388.35$$

$$\boxed{R_{Ey} \;=\; -324.30\text{ N}}$$

The **negative sign** means the elbow joint reaction force is directed **downward** on the forearm. Its **magnitude** is **324.30 N**.

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation

By Newton's third law, the forearm exerts a force of **324.30 N directed upward** on the distal humerus. This is the **compressive load** across the ulnohumeral articulation — nearly **6.6 times** the actual weight of the object held. The humeroulnar joint surfaces and articular cartilage must therefore withstand this amplified load.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deltoid Force and Glenohumeral Joint Reaction (Arm Abducted 90°)

### Problem Statement

A subject holds a **2 kg mass** in the hand with the **arm abducted to 90°** in the coronal plane (arm horizontal, perpendicular to the trunk). The **deltoid muscle** originates on the clavicle/acromion and inserts on the deltoid tuberosity of the humerus. Its **line of action** makes an angle of **15°** with the vertical when the arm is horizontal. The **perpendicular moment arm of the deltoid** about the glenohumeral joint is **5 cm**. The **center of mass of the arm** is **30 cm** from the shoulder, and the **mass in the hand** is **60 cm** from the shoulder. The weight of the upper arm is **25 N**. Determine the **deltoid force** and the **magnitude and direction of the glenohumeral joint reaction force**.

### Step 1 — Construct the Free Body Diagram of the Abducted Arm

The arm is isolated as a rigid body. The forces acting on it are:

1. **W_A** = weight of the arm = **25 N**, acting vertically downward at 30 cm from the shoulder.
2. **W_H** = weight of mass in hand = 2 × 9.81 = **19.62 N**, acting vertically downward at 60 cm from the shoulder.
3. **F_D** = deltoid tension, acting at **15° from vertical** (slightly medial), perpendicular moment arm **0.05 m** about the glenohumeral joint.
4. **R_S** = glenohumeral joint reaction force (two unknown components: R_Sx and R_Sy).

### Step 2 — Resolve the Deltoid Force into Components

The deltoid pulls **upward and slightly medially** (toward the body), at 15° from the vertical. Using the convention: positive x = laterally (away from body), positive y = upward.

$$F_{Dx} \;=\; -F_D \cdot \sin(15°) \quad \text{(medial component, negative x)}$$

$$F_{Dy} \;=\; +F_D \cdot \cos(15°) \quad \text{(upward component, positive y)}$$

> The perpendicular moment arm of **0.05 m** is used in the moment equation, not the trigonometric components. The angle is needed only for the force equilibrium step.

### Step 3 — Take Moments About the Glenohumeral Joint

Choosing the shoulder as the moment center eliminates **R_Sx** and **R_Sy** from the moment equation:

$$\sum M_S \;=\; 0$$

The deltoid produces a **counterclockwise** moment (it abducts the arm). The arm and hand weights produce **clockwise** moments (they adduct the arm):

$$F_D \cdot (0.05) \;-\; W_A \cdot (0.30) \;-\; W_H \cdot (0.60) \;=\; 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Deltoid Force F_D

Rearrange:

$$F_D \cdot 0.05 \;=\; (25)(0.30) \;+\; (19.62)(0.60)$$

$$F_D \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 7.5 \;+\; 11.772$$

$$F_D \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 19.272$$

$$\boxed{F_D \;=\; 385.44\text{ N}}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Deltoid Force Components

$$F_{Dx} \;=\; -385.44 \cdot \sin(15°) \;=\; -385.44 \cdot 0.2588 \;=\; -99.75\text{ N}$$

$$F_{Dy} \;=\; +385.44 \cdot \cos(15°) \;=\; +385.44 \cdot 0.9659 \;=\; +372.27\text{ N}$$

### Step 6 — Apply Force Equilibrium in x and y

**Horizontal equilibrium (ΣF_x = 0):**

$$R_{Sx} \;+\; F_{Dx} \;=\; 0$$

$$R_{Sx} \;=\; -F_{Dx} \;=\; -(-99.75) \;=\; +99.75\text{ N}$$

> The glenohumeral joint pushes the humerus **laterally** (outward, away from the body) with 99.75 N.

**Vertical equilibrium (ΣF_y = 0):**

$$R_{Sy} \;+\; F_{Dy} \;-\; W_A \;-\; W_H \;=\; 0$$

$$R_{Sy} \;=\; W_A \;+\; W_H \;-\; F_{Dy}$$

$$R_{Sy} \;=\; 25 \;+\; 19.62 \;-\; 372.27$$

$$R_{Sy} \;=\; -327.65\text{ N}$$

> The negative sign means the joint reaction on the humerus is directed **downward**. The glenoid surface pushes the humeral head **downward and laterally** — a classic finding in shoulder biomechanics.

### Step 7 — Magnitude and Direction of the Resultant Joint Reaction

$$R_S \;=\; \sqrt{R_{Sx}^2 \;+\; R_{Sy}^2}$$

$$R_S \;=\; \sqrt{(99.75)^2 \;+\; (-327.65)^2}$$

$$R_S \;=\; \sqrt{9950.06 \;+\; 107354.52}$$

$$R_S \;=\; \sqrt{117304.58}$$

$$\boxed{R_S \;=\; 342.50\text{ N}}$$

The direction angle below the lateral horizontal:

$$\theta_R \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lvert R_{Sy} \rvert}{\lvert R_{Sx} \rvert}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{327.65}{99.75}\right) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(3.285) \;=\; 73.06°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_R \;=\; 73.06°\text{ below the lateral horizontal}}$$

### Step 8 — Interpretation

The **glenohumeral joint reaction force (≈ 342.5 N)** is over **17 times** the 2 kg weight held. This exceptionally high ratio explains why the shoulder is the most commonly dislocated major joint in the body, why rotator cuff muscles must provide continuous compressive stability, and why repetitive overhead activities (painting, swimming, throwing) are major risk factors for shoulder impingement syndrome.

---

## 3.3 Summary Table of Derived Results

| Load Case | External Load | Muscle Force | Joint Reaction | Amplification Factor |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Elbow, forearm horizontal, 5 kg** | **49.05 N** | **388.35 N (Biceps)** | **324.30 N (Elbow)** | **~6.6 × external load** |
| **Shoulder, arm 90° abducted, 2 kg** | **19.62 N** | **385.44 N (Deltoid)** | **342.50 N (Glenohumeral)** | **~17.5 × external load** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The shoulder joint sustains a much higher amplification factor than the elbow because the deltoid's moment arm (5 cm) is small relative to the moment arm of the weight (~30–60 cm), AND because the muscle's line of action is nearly vertical while the humerus is horizontal, generating a large compressive component on the glenoid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Load Transfer Pathway Through the Upper Extremity

The following flowchart illustrates how an external load applied at the hand is transmitted progressively through the kinematic chain to the trunk.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ExtLoad["External Load / Object in Hand"] --> HandNode["Hand & Fingers"]
    HandNode --> WristNode["Wrist Joint Radiocarpal"]
    WristNode --> ForearmNode["Forearm Radius and Ulna"]
    ForearmNode --> ElbowNode["Elbow Joint Humeroulnar"]
    ElbowNode --> UpperArmNode["Upper Arm Humerus"]
    UpperArmNode --> ShoulderNode["Glenohumeral Joint"]
    ShoulderNode --> ScapulaNode["Scapula and Clavicle"]
    ScapulaNode --> ThoraxNode["Thoracic Cage and Spine"]
    ThoraxNode --> PelvisNode["Pelvis and Lower Limbs"]

    BicepsMus["Biceps Brachii Tension"] --> ElbowNode
    TricepsMus["Triceps Brachii Tension"] --> ElbowNode
    DeltoidMus["Deltoid Tension"] --> ShoulderNode
    RotatorMus["Rotator Cuff Group"] --> ShoulderNode
    WristFlex["Wrist Flexors and Extensors"] --> WristNode
```

## 4.2 Free Body Diagram Methodology — Block Architecture

The systematic workflow for solving any upper-extremity load problem is mapped below as a functional block diagram.

```mermaid
graph LR
    InputA["Identify Posture and External Load"] --> InputB["Isolate Limb Segment for FBD"]
    InputB --> InputC["Mark All Forces and Moment Arms"]
    InputC --> InputD["Define Coordinate System XY"]
    InputD --> Step1["Sum of Moments equals Zero about Joint"]
    InputD --> Step2["Sum of Forces X equals Zero"]
    InputD --> Step3["Sum of Forces Y equals Zero"]
    Step1 --> Solve["Solve Three Equations for Three Unknowns"]
    Step2 --> Solve
    Step3 --> Solve
    Solve --> ResultA["Muscle Force Fm"]
    Solve --> ResultB["Joint Reaction Components Rx Ry"]
    Solve --> ResultC["Magnitude and Direction of R"]
    ResultA --> Output["Interpretation and Clinical Significance"]
    ResultB --> Output
    ResultC --> Output
```

## 4.3 Force Distribution Hierarchy — Functional Architecture

This block diagram shows the analytical decomposition of all force categories acting on the upper extremity.

```mermaid
graph TD
    AllForces["All Loads on Upper Extremity"] --> Intrinsic["Intrinsic Body Loads"]
    AllForces --> Extrinsic["Extrinsic External Loads"]
    AllForces --> Internal["Internal Tissue Loads"]

    Intrinsic --> SubI1["Weight of Upper Arm 2.8 percent TBW"]
    Intrinsic --> SubI2["Weight of Forearm 1.6 percent TBW"]
    Intrinsic --> SubI3["Weight of Hand 0.6 percent TBW"]

    Extrinsic --> SubE1["Held Object Weight"]
    Extrinsic --> SubE2["Contact and Push Pull Forces"]
    Extrinsic --> SubE3["Vibration and Impact Loads"]

    Internal --> SubIn1["Active Muscle Tension"]
    Internal --> SubIn2["Passive Ligament Tension"]
    Internal --> SubIn3["Joint Reaction Force Compression"]
    Internal --> SubIn4["Joint Reaction Force Shear"]
```

## 4.4 Static vs Dynamic Loading — Comparative Block Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    LoadType["Load Classification"] --> Static["Static Loading"]
    LoadType --> Dynamic["Dynamic Loading"]

    Static --> S1["No Acceleration of Segments"]
    Static --> S2["Sum of Forces and Moments equals Zero"]
    Static --> S3["Example Holding a Cup Steady"]

    Dynamic --> D1["Segment Acceleration is Non Zero"]
    Dynamic --> D2["Inertial Force ma acts at CoM"]
    Dynamic --> D3["Example Throwing a Ball"]
    Dynamic --> D4["Impact Loading Peak Force Brief Duration"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dynamic case** requires an additional term, $F_{inertial} = m_{seg} \cdot a_{CoM}$, on the right-hand side of the force equilibrium equations, and $M_{inertial} = I_{CoM} \cdot \alpha$ in the moment equation, where $I$ is the moment of inertia and $\alpha$ is the angular acceleration. This module's KTU 2024 numerical problems focus primarily on the **static case**, but dynamic extension follows the same architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**"Define joint reaction force. List the factors that influence the magnitude of the joint reaction force at the elbow during static holding of an object in the hand."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Joint reaction force** is the resultant internal force transmitted across the articulating surfaces of a joint, arising from the action of muscles, ligaments, and external loads transmitted through the bony structures.

The magnitude of the elbow joint reaction force during static holding depends on:

1. **The weight of the object held in the hand** (directly proportional — a heavier load produces a larger reaction force).
2. **The moment arm of the biceps muscle** about the elbow (a smaller moment arm demands a larger muscle force, which in turn produces a larger compressive reaction at the joint).
3. **The posture of the upper limb**, specifically the angle of the forearm relative to the vertical, which alters the moment arm of the load with respect to the elbow.
4. **The weight and center of mass location of the forearm itself**, since gravity acting on the forearm contributes to the moment that the biceps must resist.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**"List the segments of the upper extremity along with their approximate masses as a percentage of total body weight. State the approximate center of mass location for each segment from its proximal end."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Segment | Mass (% TBW) | CoM Location from Proximal End |
|---------|--------------|--------------------------------|
| Upper arm | **2.8 %** | **43.6 %** of segment length |
| Forearm | **1.6 %** | **43.0 %** of segment length |
| Hand | **0.6 %** | **~50 %** of segment length (near middle) |

The total mass of one complete upper extremity is therefore approximately **5.0 – 5.1 %** of total body weight, an important figure for ergonomic and prosthetic design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**"A person holds a 4 kg dumbbell in the hand with the upper arm vertical and the forearm horizontal. The biceps muscle has a moment arm of 5 cm at the elbow. The forearm has a weight of 14 N acting at 14 cm from the elbow. The dumbbell acts at 32 cm from the elbow. The weight of the hand is 4 N acting at the wrist, 28 cm from the elbow. Determine: (a) the force in the biceps brachii, and (b) the magnitude and direction of the elbow joint reaction force."**

#### Part (a) — Biceps Force (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify all downward forces and their moment arms about the elbow:**

$$W_L \;=\; m_L \cdot g \;=\; 4 \times 9.81 \;=\; 39.24\text{ N (at 32 cm from elbow)}$$

$$W_F \;=\; 14\text{ N (at 14 cm from elbow)}$$

$$W_H \;=\; 4\text{ N (at 28 cm from elbow)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply moment equilibrium about the elbow:**

$$\sum M_E \;=\; 0$$

$$F_B \cdot (0.05) \;-\; W_H \cdot (0.28) \;-\; W_F \cdot (0.14) \;-\; W_L \cdot (0.32) \;=\; 0$$

$$F_B \cdot 0.05 \;=\; (4)(0.28) \;+\; (14)(0.14) \;+\; (39.24)(0.32)$$

$$F_B \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 1.12 \;+\; 1.96 \;+\; 12.5568$$

$$F_B \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 15.6368$$

$$\boxed{F_B \;=\; 312.74\text{ N}}$$

#### Part (b) — Elbow Joint Reaction (7 marks)

**Step 3 — Apply vertical force equilibrium:**

$$\sum F_y \;=\; 0$$

$$R_{Ey} \;+\; F_B \;-\; W_H \;-\; W_F \;-\; W_L \;=\; 0$$

$$R_{Ey} \;=\; W_H \;+\; W_F \;+\; W_L \;-\; F_B$$

$$R_{Ey} \;=\; 4 \;+\; 14 \;+\; 39.24 \;-\; 312.74$$

$$R_{Ey} \;=\; 57.24 \;-\; 312.74 \;=\; -255.50\text{ N}$$

The negative sign indicates the elbow joint reaction acts **downward on the forearm**. Its magnitude is:

$$\boxed{\vert R_E \vert \;=\; 255.50\text{ N (directed downward on the forearm, compressing the humeroulnar joint)}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark embedded):** The elbow joint compressive load of 255.5 N is approximately **6.5 times** the actual weight of the dumbbell (39.24 N), illustrating the severe mechanical disadvantage of the biceps lever at the elbow.

---

### Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]

**"(a) With the help of a neat free body diagram, explain the method of analyzing loads on the upper extremity using Newton's laws of statics. (b) A subject abducts the right arm to 90° in the coronal plane holding a 3 kg mass in the hand. The deltoid muscle has a moment arm of 5 cm and pulls at 15° from the vertical. The weight of the arm is 28 N acting at 30 cm from the shoulder. The mass in the hand is at 60 cm from the shoulder. Compute the deltoid force and the magnitude of the glenohumeral joint reaction force."**

#### Part (a) — Free Body Diagram Methodology (7 marks)

The method involves the following systematic steps:

1. **Segment selection:** Isolate one rigid body (e.g., forearm or upper arm) by passing imaginary sections through the joints above and below it.

2. **Force inventory:** Enumerate all forces — segment weight, external load weight, muscle tension (with its line of action), joint reaction forces (drawn as unknown vectors at the joint center), and any other applied loads.

3. **Coordinate system:** Establish a Cartesian frame (x horizontal, y vertical) consistent with the anatomical posture being analyzed.

4. **Equilibrium equations:** Apply the three scalar equations of static equilibrium: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$ about a chosen moment center (typically a joint to eliminate its reaction from the moment equation).

5. **Solve simultaneously:** These three equations yield the three unknowns — typically the muscle force and the two components of the joint reaction force.

6. **Successive segments:** Move to the adjacent segment, using the previously found joint reaction as a known applied force (with direction reversed by Newton's third law).

#### Part (b) — Deltoid and Glenohumeral Joint Force (7 marks)

**Given data:**

- $W_L = 3 \times 9.81 = 29.43$ N at 60 cm from shoulder
- $W_A = 28$ N at 30 cm from shoulder
- Deltoid moment arm = 5 cm
- Deltoid angle = 15° from vertical

**Step 1 — Moment equilibrium about the glenohumeral joint:**

$$F_D \cdot (0.05) \;-\; W_A \cdot (0.30) \;-\; W_L \cdot (0.60) \;=\; 0$$

$$F_D \cdot 0.05 \;=\; (28)(0.30) \;+\; (29.43)(0.60)$$

$$F_D \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 8.4 \;+\; 17.658$$

$$F_D \cdot 0.05 \;=\; 26.058$$

$$\boxed{F_D \;=\; 521.16\text{ N}}$$

**Step 2 — Resolve deltoid into components:**

$$F_{Dx} \;=\; -F_D \cdot \sin(15°) \;=\; -521.16 \cdot 0.2588 \;=\; -134.88\text{ N}$$

$$F_{Dy} \;=\; +F_D \cdot \cos(15°) \;=\; +521.16 \cdot 0.9659 \;=\; +503.41\text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply force equilibrium:**

$$\sum F_x \;=\; 0 \; \Rightarrow \; R_{Sx} \;=\; -F_{Dx} \;=\; +134.88\text{ N}$$

$$\sum F_y \;=\; 0 \; \Rightarrow \; R_{Sy} \;=\; W_A + W_L - F_{Dy} \;=\; 28 + 29.43 - 503.41 \;=\; -445.98\text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the joint reaction:**

$$R_S \;=\; \sqrt{(134.88)^2 \;+\; (-445.98)^2} \;=\; \sqrt{18192.6 \;+\; 198898.2} \;=\; \sqrt{217090.8}$$

$$\boxed{R_S \;=\; 465.93\text{ N}}$$

**Interpretation:** The glenohumeral joint reaction of 465.93 N is approximately **15.8 times** the 3 kg external load, reflecting the severe mechanical disadvantage of the deltoid abductor lever.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting segment weight:** Many students compute only the muscle force required to balance the external load, ignoring the **weight of the limb segment itself** (forearm, upper arm, hand). The forearm weight is **~1.6 % TBW** and is non-negligible — omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong moment-arm sign or direction:** When summing moments, a positive sign must be assigned consistently. Mixing clockwise and counterclockwise conventions mid-problem is a frequent error. The KTU key gives **0 marks** for the final answer if the sign logic is inconsistent, even if the magnitude is correct.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Moment arms must be in **metres** when forces are in **newtons** (to get moments in N·m). Mixing centimetres with newtons silently produces answers off by a factor of 100 — a classic blunder.
>
> 4. **Confusing muscle force with joint reaction:** The muscle force (e.g., biceps tension) and the joint reaction force are **two different quantities**. The KTU question typically asks for both. Writing one in place of the other forfeits full marks.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the angle of muscle pull:** At the shoulder, the deltoid does **not** pull straight up. Its 15° inclination must be resolved into components to find the joint reaction. Skipping this step costs 2–3 marks.
>
> 6. **Omitting the free body diagram:** KTU 2024 valuation keys **explicitly award 1–2 marks** for a correctly drawn FBD showing all forces, their lines of action, and their directions. Skipping the diagram loses marks even if the math is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **upper extremity** is modeled as a **four-segment kinematic chain**: trunk → shoulder → upper arm → elbow → forearm → wrist → hand → load.
- [x] Standard segment weights: **upper arm 2.8 % TBW, forearm 1.6 % TBW, hand 0.6 % TBW**.
- [x] Standard CoM locations from the proximal joint: **upper arm 43.6 %, forearm 43 %, hand ~50 %** of segment length.
- [x] The **biceps moment arm** at the elbow is approximately **5 cm**; the **deltoid moment arm** at the shoulder is also approximately **5 cm**.
- [x] The **free body diagram (FBD)** is the central analytical tool — always draw one, label all forces, and indicate moment arms.
- [x] Three equilibrium equations ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$) suffice for a 2D static analysis of a single segment.
- [x] **Choosing the joint as the moment center** is a standard trick to eliminate the unknown joint reaction from the moment equation.
- [x] The **mechanical disadvantage** of the upper-limb lever systems means **muscle force and joint reaction force are typically 5–20 times larger** than the external load being held.
- [x] The **elbow joint reaction** is mostly directed vertically (compressive); the **glenohumeral joint reaction** has both vertical (downward) and lateral (outward) components due to the angled pull of the deltoid.
- [x] **Dynamic loads** add inertial terms ($m \cdot a$ and $I \cdot \alpha$) to the equilibrium equations and can produce **peak loads several times higher** than the static case (e.g., during a tennis serve or baseball pitch).
- [x] **G = 9.81 m/s²** is the universal gravitational acceleration used in upper-extremity calculations.
- [x] The analysis of upper-extremity loads is the direct basis of **prosthesis design, ergonomic standards, and rehabilitation engineering**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shoulder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Course Code:** PCBMT601
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shoulder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Shoulder Complex — Formal Definition

The **shoulder complex** (also termed the **shoulder girdle** or **pectoral girdle**) is the most mobile yet least stable articulation system in the human body. It is not a single joint but an integrated **kinematic chain of four articulations** that work synchronously to position the hand in three-dimensional space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCBMT601 – Module 3):**
> The shoulder complex is a multi-articular system comprising the **Sternoclavicular (SC) joint**, **Acromioclavicular (AC) joint**, **Glenohumeral (GH) joint**, and the **functional Scapulothoracic (ST) articulation**. Together, these provide the upper limb with the largest range of motion (ROM) of any joint complex in the body — approximately **180° of abduction** and **360° of humeral rotation** when fully coordinated.

### The Four Articulations of the Shoulder Complex

| # | Joint | Type | Function |
|---|-------|------|----------|
| 1 | Sternoclavicular (SC) | Saddle / Sellar | Links upper limb to axial skeleton |
| 2 | Acromioclavicular (AC) | Planar / Gliding | Adjusts scapular position on thorax |
| 3 | Glenohumeral (GH) | Ball-and-Socket | Main humeral articulation |
| 4 | Scapulothoracic (ST) | Functional (Not a true joint) | Scapular gliding on thoracic wall |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Scapulothoracic "joint"** is *not* a true anatomical joint — it is a **physiological articulation** where the scapula glides over the thoracic wall, separated by muscles (serratus anterior, subscapularis). Biomechanically, however, it behaves as a true joint with **3 degrees of freedom (DOF)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crane on a Boat"

Think of the shoulder as a **crane mounted on a boat**:
- The **boat (scapula + thorax)** can rock, tilt, and rotate on the water — this is the **scapulothoracic** and **AC** contribution.
- The **crane's base pivot (SC joint)** allows the entire arm assembly to swing on the chest wall.
- The **crane's boom (humerus)** can rise, lower, and rotate freely on its **ball-and-socket mount (GH joint)**.

Just as a real crane can place a load anywhere within a hemisphere, the shoulder can place the hand anywhere within a vast **hemispherical workspace** — but at the cost of inherent instability. A crane needs cables (muscles) and counterweights to stay balanced; the shoulder needs its **rotator cuff** and **ligamentous capsule** to prevent dislocation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the shoulder so mobile yet unstable?**
> - The **humeral head** is **3–4 times larger** than the shallow glenoid fossa.
> - Only about **25–30%** of the humeral head contacts the glenoid at any time.
> - Stability is sacrificed for mobility — a deliberate evolutionary trade-off.

## 1.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of the Shoulder Complex

The **DOF** of a joint describes the number of independent rotational or translational movements it permits. According to KTU 2024 syllabus, the total DOF is computed as:

$$n_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{DOF}_i$$

| Joint | Rotational DOF | Translational DOF | Subtotal |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|----------|
| Glenohumeral | 3 (flex/ext, abd/add, IR/ER) | 3 (limited) | 3 functional rotational |
| Sternoclavicular | 3 (elevation/depression, protraction/retraction, rotation) | 0 | 3 |
| Acromioclavicular | 3 (scapular rotations) | 0 | 3 |
| Scapulothoracic | 3 (elevation/depression, protraction/retraction, rotation) | 0 | 3 |
| **Net Functional DOF** | — | — | **3 (overall upper limb)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Although each joint contributes DOFs, the **net independent DOF of the upper limb at the shoulder is 3**, because the four joints are **kinematically coupled** (one joint's motion constrains another's, as dictated by the closed-chain scapulohumeral rhythm). The humerus itself can be described by **3 Euler angles** at any instant.

## 1.4 Principal Muscle Groups

The shoulder musculature is divided into two functional categories:

### A. The Rotator Cuff (SITS) — Dynamic Stabilizers
| Muscle | Primary Action |
|--------|----------------|
| **S**upraspinatus | Initiates abduction (first **15°**) |
| **I**nfraspinatus | External rotation |
| **T**eres minor | External rotation |
| **S**ubscapularis | Internal rotation |

> [!NOTE]
> The rotator cuff collectively exerts a **compressive joint reaction force** on the humeral head, holding it into the glenoid fossa — this is called the **"concavity compression"** mechanism (Lippitt & Matsen).

### B. The Prime Movers — Force Generators
- **Deltoid** (anterior, middle, posterior fibers) — abduction, flexion, extension
- **Pectoralis major** — adduction, internal rotation
- **Latissimus dorsi** — adduction, extension, internal rotation
- **Biceps brachii (long head)** — assists glenohumeral stabilization
- **Trapezius & Serratus anterior** — scapular stabilizers (force couples)

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants to Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams:**
> - **Scapulohumeral rhythm:** **2:1** (i.e., for every **3°** of total shoulder abduction, **2°** occur at the GH joint and **1°** at the ST joint).
> - **Deltoid moment arm at 90° abduction:** ≈ **3 cm** (varies with arm position).
> - **Weight of upper limb (avg adult):** ≈ **5% body weight** ≈ **35 N (for a 70 kg person)**.
> - **Humeral head–glenoid contact ratio:** ≈ **0.25 to 0.30** (only one quarter in contact).
> - **Glenoid labrum:** deepens the socket by ≈ **50%**, increasing contact area.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biomechanics of the Glenohumeral (GH) Joint

The GH joint is a **ball-and-socket (spheroidal)** synovial joint between:
- The **humeral head** (convex, spherical)
- The **glenoid fossa of the scapula** (concave, shallow)

It permits **3 rotational DOF** in the cardinal planes and an additional **3 translational DOF** (small magnitudes), giving it a theoretical **6 DOF** — the most of any joint in the body.

### 2.1.1 Joint Stability Hierarchy (Static → Dynamic)

> [!NOTE]
> Stability is provided by **four mechanisms**, in order of increasing dependence on muscle action:
> 1. **Bony congruence** (minimal contribution — only ~30% contact).
> 2. **Glenoid labrum** (fibrocartilaginous rim → 50% increase in socket depth).
> 3. **Ligamentous capsule** (superior, middle, inferior glenohumeral ligaments + coracohumeral ligament).
> 4. **Muscular compression** (rotator cuff "concavity compression").

### 2.1.2 Static Force Analysis — Deltoid vs. Weight of Arm

The classic **free-body diagram (FBD)** of the humerus in mid-abduction is one of the most frequently tested problems in KTU biomechanics examinations.

**Assumptions (KTU standard):**
- Arm is modeled as a **uniform rigid rod** pivoting at the GH joint.
- Deltoid inserts at the **deltoid tuberosity**, located at ≈ **1/3 the length of the humerus** from the proximal end.
- Arm weight acts at the **center of mass**, located at ≈ **mid-length** of the humerus (for a uniform model).

## 2.2 The Scapulohumeral Rhythm (Inman et al., 1944)

This is a **coupled kinematic relationship** between glenohumeral abduction and scapulothoracic upward rotation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Empirical Rule:** From **30° onwards** of total shoulder abduction, the ratio of GH motion to ST motion is approximately **2:1**. Below 30°, motion occurs almost entirely at the GH joint (the "setting phase").

$$\theta_{\text{GH}} = \frac{2}{3} \, \theta_{\text{total}} \quad \text{and} \quad \theta_{\text{ST}} = \frac{1}{3} \, \theta_{\text{total}}$$

For example, at **90° total abduction**:
- GH contribution = **60°**
- ST contribution = **30°**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the scapulohumeral rhythm clinically important?**
> Disruption of the 2:1 ratio (e.g., due to rotator cuff tear or frozen shoulder) leads to **impingement syndrome** and reduced ROM. KTU questions often ask for the GH and ST contributions at a given total abduction angle.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are KTU 2024 syllabus–relevant. Memorize the table for ESE preparation.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Scapulohumeral rhythm | $\theta_{\text{GH}} / \theta_{\text{ST}} = 2/1$ | Coupled motion ratio |
| 2 | Total DOF of upper limb | $n = 3$ | Three Euler angles |
| 3 | Moment equilibrium of humerus | $F_{\text{del}} \cdot d_1 = W_{\text{arm}} \cdot d_2$ | Static balance |
| 4 | Deltoid force | $F_{\text{del}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{arm}} \cdot d_2}{d_1}$ | From moment balance |
| 5 | Joint reaction force (GH) | $J = \sqrt{F_{\text{del}}^2 + W_{\text{arm}}^2 + 2 F_{\text{del}} W_{\text{arm}} \cos\alpha}$ | Vector sum |
| 6 | Rotator cuff compression | $F_{\text{rc}} = J \cdot \tan\beta$ | Concavity compression |
| 7 | Mass of upper limb | $m_{\text{arm}} \approx 0.05 \, m_{\text{body}}$ | ~5% body weight |
| 8 | Weight of arm | $W_{\text{arm}} = m_{\text{arm}} \cdot g$ | With $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| 9 | Glenoid contact ratio | $A_c / A_h \approx 0.25$ to $0.30$ | Stability metric |
| 10 | Socket depth (with labrum) | $d_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.5 \cdot d_{\text{bare}}$ | 50% increase |

**Notation used:**
- $F_{\text{del}}$ = Deltoid muscle force (N)
- $W_{\text{arm}}$ = Weight of the arm (N)
- $d_1$ = Moment arm of deltoid about GH joint (m)
- $d_2$ = Moment arm of arm weight about GH joint (m)
- $\alpha$ = Angle between deltoid force vector and the humeral shaft
- $\beta$ = Angle of glenoid tilt
- $J$ = Joint reaction force at the GH joint (N)
- $A_c$ = Glenoid contact area, $A_h$ = Humeral head area

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in B.Tech Biomechanics:**
> - **Prosthetic shoulder design:** Reverse total shoulder arthroplasty (rTSA) reverses the ball-and-socket to use the deltoid for elevation when the rotator cuff is deficient — directly inspired by lever-arm biomechanics.
> - **Sports engineering:** Pitching biomechanics, swimming stroke analysis, and tennis serve optimization all use the **2:1 scapulohumeral rhythm** as a baseline.
> - **Ergonomics & rehabilitation:** Workstation design (e.g., overhead assembly lines) must account for shoulder muscle fatigue curves derived from the deltoid force model.
> - **Robotic exoskeletons:** Shoulder exosuits (e.g., for industrial workers) use the GH joint's 3-DOF model to align actuator axes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Classical Derivation: Deltoid Force Required to Hold the Arm in Static Abduction

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 3 examinations. We will derive the force the deltoid must produce to statically hold the arm at 90° of abduction.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Standard KTU Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A subject holds their dominant arm in **90° of abduction** in the coronal plane. Estimate the **deltoid muscle force** required, given:
> - Mass of the arm: $m_{\text{arm}} = 4 \text{ kg}$
> - Length of the humerus: $L_h = 0.30 \text{ m}$
> - The center of mass of the arm is at: $r_{\text{cm}} = 0.16 \text{ m}$ from the GH joint
> - The deltoid inserts at: $r_{\text{ins}} = 0.15 \text{ m}$ from the GH joint
> - The deltoid force vector makes an angle: $\alpha = 15°$ with the humeral shaft
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the weight of the arm.**
The weight of the arm acts downward at the center of mass.

$$W_{\text{arm}} = m_{\text{arm}} \cdot g$$

$$W_{\text{arm}} = 4 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$W_{\text{arm}} = 39.24 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the moment arms.**
- The moment arm of the **arm's weight** about the GH joint is the horizontal distance from the joint to the line of action of $W_{\text{arm}}$. For a 90° abducted arm, this is the full length $r_{\text{cm}}$ projected onto the horizontal plane. However, since the arm is horizontal, the moment arm equals $r_{\text{cm}}$ itself:

$$d_2 = r_{\text{cm}} = 0.16 \text{ m}$$

- The **deltoid moment arm** is the perpendicular distance from the GH joint to the line of action of the deltoid force. For the simplified 2D model:

$$d_1 = r_{\text{ins}} \cdot \sin\alpha$$

$$d_1 = 0.15 \text{ m} \cdot \sin(15°)$$

$$d_1 = 0.15 \times 0.2588 \text{ m}$$

$$d_1 = 0.03882 \text{ m} \approx 0.039 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reasoning:** The deltoid's small moment arm (~4 cm) compared to the arm's much larger moment arm (~16 cm) is precisely why the deltoid must generate a very large force — a **mechanical disadvantage** of approximately 4:1. This is a deliberate evolutionary trade-off that allows for rapid, low-inertia arm movements.

**Step 3 — Apply moment equilibrium about the GH joint.**
For static equilibrium, the sum of moments about the GH joint must equal zero:

$$\sum M_{\text{GH}} = 0$$

$$F_{\text{del}} \cdot d_1 - W_{\text{arm}} \cdot d_2 = 0$$

$$F_{\text{del}} \cdot d_1 = W_{\text{arm}} \cdot d_2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the deltoid force.**

$$F_{\text{del}} = \frac{W_{\text{arm}} \cdot d_2}{d_1}$$

$$F_{\text{del}} = \frac{39.24 \text{ N} \times 0.16 \text{ m}}{0.039 \text{ m}}$$

$$F_{\text{del}} = \frac{6.2784 \text{ N·m}}{0.039 \text{ m}}$$

$$F_{\text{del}} \approx 161.0 \text{ N}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The deltoid must generate ≈ **161 N** to hold a 4 kg arm in 90° abduction. This is **~4.1 times** the weight of the arm — demonstrating the deltoid's severe mechanical disadvantage. In a real human, this is why **sustained overhead activities cause rapid deltoid fatigue.**

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Glenohumeral Joint Reaction Force (J)

**Step 1 — Set up the vector sum.**
The joint reaction force $J$ at the GH joint is the vector sum of the **deltoid force** and the **arm weight** (and any external loads), by Newton's third law applied to the equilibrium of the humeral head.

For the simple 2D case, with the deltoid pulling upward and the arm weight pulling downward:

$$J = F_{\text{del}} - W_{\text{arm}}$$

$$J = 161.0 \text{ N} - 39.24 \text{ N}$$

$$J = 121.76 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — More general vector formulation (KTU advanced).**
If the deltoid force vector makes an angle $\alpha$ with the humeral shaft, then the joint reaction is found from the force triangle. The **general 2D scalar form** is:

$$J = \sqrt{F_{\text{del}}^2 + W_{\text{arm}}^2 - 2 F_{\text{del}} W_{\text{arm}} \cos(\pi - \alpha)}$$

which simplifies (using $\cos(\pi - \alpha) = -\cos\alpha$) to:

$$J = \sqrt{F_{\text{del}}^2 + W_{\text{arm}}^2 + 2 F_{\text{del}} W_{\text{arm}} \cos\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation with the general formula.**

$$J = \sqrt{(161.0)^2 + (39.24)^2 + 2 (161.0)(39.24) \cos(15°)}$$

$$J = \sqrt{25921.0 + 1539.8 + 2 (6317.6) (0.9659)}$$

$$J = \sqrt{25921.0 + 1539.8 + 12207.5}$$

$$J = \sqrt{39668.3}$$

$$J \approx 199.2 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The simplified scalar subtraction ($J = F_{\text{del}} - W_{\text{arm}}$) gives the **component along the humeral axis**, while the full vector formula gives the **magnitude** of $J$. Always read the question carefully — examiners often specify which one they want.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Joint Mechanics

| Parameter | Shoulder (GH Joint) | Hip Joint | Knee Joint | Ankle Joint |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|------------|-------------|
| Joint type | Ball-and-socket | Ball-and-socket | Hinge / modified | Hinge |
| DOF | 3 rotational | 3 rotational | 1 (flex/ext) + small rotation | 1 (dorsi/plantar) |
| ROM (degrees) | ~180 abd, ~360 rot | ~120 flex, ~45 abd | ~140 flex | ~30 dorsi, ~50 plantar |
| Socket depth | Shallow | Deep | Shallow (tibial plateau) | Moderate |
| Stability | Low (muscle-dependent) | High (bony) | Moderate | High |
| Contact area ratio | ~0.25 | ~0.65 | ~0.50 | ~0.45 |
| Dislocation risk | High | Low | Low | Very low |
| Primary stabilizer | Rotator cuff | Acetabular labrum, capsule | Ligaments (ACL, PCL) | Malleoli, ligaments |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The shoulder and hip are both ball-and-socket joints, yet the hip is **inherently far more stable** due to a deep socket and tight ligamentous capsule. The shoulder compensates for its instability with **active muscular control** — which is why rotator cuff injuries are so functionally devastating.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Scapulohumeral Rhythm Partition

> **Question:** Calculate the glenohumeral (GH) and scapulothoracic (ST) contributions to a total shoulder abduction of **120°**, using the 2:1 rhythm.

**Step 1 — Apply the rhythm ratio.**
Total = GH + ST, with GH/ST = 2/1.

$$\theta_{\text{total}} = \theta_{\text{GH}} + \theta_{\text{ST}} = 120°$$

$$\theta_{\text{GH}} = 2 \cdot \theta_{\text{ST}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$2\theta_{\text{ST}} + \theta_{\text{ST}} = 120°$$

$$3\theta_{\text{ST}} = 120°$$

$$\theta_{\text{ST}} = 40°$$

$$\theta_{\text{GH}} = 80°$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** At 120° total abduction, **80°** occurs at the glenohumeral joint and **40°** at the scapulothoracic articulation. KTU examiners frequently test this arithmetic for marks of 2–5.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Shoulder Complex

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **biomechanical control flow** of the shoulder complex — from neural command to skeletal motion — without attempting an anatomical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CNS Motor Command Cortex Cerebellum] --> B[Brachial Plexus C5-T1]
    B --> C[Peripheral Nerves Axillary Suprascapular]
    C --> D[Muscle Activation Deltoid RotatorCuff SITS]
    D --> E[Force Generation Concavity Compression Deltoid Pull]
    E --> F[Joint Reaction Force GH Joint 199N typical]
    F --> G{Bony Constraint Glenoid Fossa Labrum}
    G -->|Stable| H[Coordinated Motion GH 3DOF SC 3DOF AC 3DOF ST 3DOF]
    G -->|Unstable| I[Dislocation Impingement Subluxation]
    H --> J[Scapulohumeral Rhythm 2 to 1]
    J --> K[Upper Limb Positioning Hand Workspace]
    I --> L[Clinical Intervention RTSA Physiotherapy]

    step1[Step 1 Neural Intent] --> step2[Step 2 Nerve Routing]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Muscle Force]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Joint Loading]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Coupled Motion]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Task Execution]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram of the Abducted Humerus (Block Schematic)

The following block diagram represents the **static equilibrium FBD** used in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GH Joint Pivot Point] -->|d1 0.039 m| B[Deltoid Insertion Fdel 161N upward]
    A -->|d2 0.16 m| C[Center of Mass Warm 39.24N downward]
    B --> D[Moment about GH Fdel x d1 6.26 Nm CCW]
    C --> E[Moment about GH Warm x d2 6.28 Nm CW]
    D --> F[Static Equilibrium Sum of Moments equals 0]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Joint Reaction J approximately 199N]
```

    P0[Total Abduction 0 to 30 deg] --> Q1[Setting Phase GH Joint dominates 100 percent]
    P1[Total Abduction 30 to 180 deg] --> Q2[Coupled Phase GH 2 parts]

    Q2 --> R1[GH Joint Contribution 120 deg at 180 total]
