```mermaid
    T --> T1["Iliopsoas Hip Flexor"]
    T --> T2["Hamstrings Knee Decelerator"]
    T --> T3["Tibialis Anterior Toe Clearance"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Ground Reaction Force Vector Progression

```mermaid
graph TD
    GRF["GROUND REACTION FORCE GRF DURING STANCE"] --> A["IC Heel Strike GRF at rearfoot"]
    GRF --> B["Loading Response GRF behind hip"]
    GRF --> C["Mid Stance GRF directly under hip"]
    GRF --> D["Terminal Stance GRF ahead of hip propulsive"]
    GRF --> E["Toe Off GRF at forefoot"]
    style A fill:#fce4d6
    style E fill:#e2efda
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define the gait cycle. List the eight sub-phases of the gait cycle as per the Rancho Los Amigos (RLA) nomenclature.**

**Model Answer:**

The **gait cycle** is the time interval between two successive heel strikes (initial contacts) of the same foot. It is normalized to 100% for analysis.

**Eight RLA sub-phases:**
1. Initial Contact (IC) — 0%
2. Loading Response (LR) — 0–12%
3. Mid Stance (MSt) — 12–31%
4. Terminal Stance (TSt) — 31–50%
5. Pre-Swing (PSw) — 50–60%
6. Initial Swing (ISw) — 60–73%
7. Mid Swing (MSw) — 73–87%
8. Terminal Swing (TSw) — 87–100%

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between the Stance Phase and the Swing Phase of walking with reference to duration, weight-bearing, and joint motion.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Stance Phase | Swing Phase |
|---|---|---|
| Duration (% of cycle) | 60% | 40% |
| Foot-ground contact | Yes | No (foot in air) |
| Weight-bearing | Yes (bears body weight) | No (non-weight bearing) |
| Primary function | Weight acceptance & propulsion | Limb advancement |
| Double support | Contains 2 DS periods (0–12% & 50–60%) | No double support |
| Key joint motion | Knee flexion then extension | Knee flexion (max 60°) then extension |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) Describe in detail the FIVE sub-phases of the Stance Phase, mentioning the percentage of gait cycle, dominant muscle activity, and joint angle changes at the hip, knee, and ankle for each.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) The cadence of a person is 100 steps/min and the step length is 0.70 m. Calculate the walking speed, stride length, and total gait cycle time.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Sub-Phase | % Cycle | Hip | Knee | Ankle | Muscles |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IC (Heel Strike)** | 0% | 25° flexion | 0° extension | 0° neutral | Gluteus maximus, Hamstrings |
| **LR (Foot Flat)** | 0–12% | 25° flexion | 15° flexion | –5° plantar | Quadriceps (eccentric), Tibialis anterior |
| **MSt (Mid Stance)** | 12–31% | 0° neutral | 5° flexion | +5° dorsi | Gastrocnemius, Gluteus medius |
| **TSt (Heel Off)** | 31–50% | –10° extension | 0° extension | +10° dorsi | Gastroc-soleus, Tibialis posterior |
| **PSw (Toe Off)** | 50–60% | –5° extension | 35° flexion | –15° plantar | Rectus femoris, Adductors |

The stance phase begins with the **heel strike** of the reference foot. During **loading response**, the knee flexes eccentrically to absorb shock. **Mid stance** is the first single-limb support period, where the trunk progresses forward over a fixed foot. **Terminal stance** ends with heel rise, the ankle maximally dorsiflexed (~10°). **Pre-swing** concludes with toe-off and rapid knee flexion (~35°), initiating the swing phase.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1:** Cadence $C = 100$ steps/min. Step length $d = 0.70$ m.

**Step 2:** Stride length $L = 2 \times d = 2 \times 0.70 = 1.40$ m

**Step 3:** Cadence in Hz: $f = C / 60 = 100 / 60 = 1.667$ Hz

**Step 4:** Walking speed:
$$
v = L \times f = 1.40 \times 1.667 = 2.33 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Gait cycle time:
$$
T = 1/f = 1/1.667 \approx 0.60 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Stride length = 1.40 m**
- **Walking speed = 2.33 m/s**
- **Gait cycle time ≈ 0.60 s**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) Explain the THREE sub-phases of the Swing Phase with reference to muscle activity and the key function of knee flexion in foot clearance.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) A patient has a stride length of 1.20 m and a walking speed of 1.00 m/s. Calculate the cadence (steps/min) and the duration of the stance and swing phases in seconds.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Initial Swing (ISw, 60–73%):** Begins with toe-off. The limb is rapidly accelerated forward by the **iliopsoas** (hip flexor) and **rectus femoris**. The knee flexes to its **maximum of ~60°** — this is the *peak knee flexion* of the entire gait cycle, and its function is **foot clearance** from the ground, preventing the toes from catching the floor. The **tibialis anterior** contracts concentrically to dorsiflex the foot.

**Mid Swing (MSw, 73–87%):** The swing limb passes beneath the body. The tibia becomes vertical. The hip continues flexion, the knee begins extending passively. **Tibialis anterior** maintains dorsiflexion for clearance. The pelvis is in neutral rotation.

**Terminal Swing (TSw, 87–100%):** The limb is decelerated by eccentric contraction of the **hamstrings** (preventing hyperextension). The **quadriceps** re-engage to extend the knee in preparation for heel strike. The foot is positioned in neutral dorsiflexion. The hip reaches maximum flexion (~30°).

> **The crucial biomechanical role of knee flexion (up to 60°) during Initial Swing** is to **shorten the effective pendulum length of the lower limb**, allowing the foot to clear the ground despite the hip swinging forward. Without this flexion (as in **stiff-knee gait**), the patient would trip constantly.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** Stride length $L = 1.20$ m, Walking speed $v = 1.00$ m/s.

**Step 1:** Cadence in Hz:
$$
f = v/L = 1.00 / 1.20 = 0.8333 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Cadence in steps/min:
$$
C = f \times 60 = 0.8333 \times 60 = 50 \text{ steps/min}
$$

**Step 3:** Gait cycle time:
$$
T = 1/f = 1/0.8333 = 1.20 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4:** Stance phase duration (60% of cycle):
$$
T_{\text{stance}} = 0.60 \times 1.20 = 0.72 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5:** Swing phase duration (40% of cycle):
$$
T_{\text{swing}} = 0.40 \times 1.20 = 0.48 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Cadence = 50 steps/min** (this is *slow* — typical pathology is hemiplegic gait)
- **Stance phase = 0.72 s**
- **Swing phase = 0.48 s**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **Confusing step length and stride length** — Stride = 2 × step. Many students write them interchangeably and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the two double support periods** — Stance phase is NOT all single-limb support. Mentioning both DS periods (0–12% and 50–60%) is mandatory for full marks.
> 3. **Writing percentage ranges incorrectly** — "Loading Response 0–12%" ≠ "0%". Always give the *range* for non-instantaneous events.
> 4. **Omitting muscle names** — KTU strictly expects at least the **dominant muscle group** for each sub-phase.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors** — Cadence must be converted to Hz (divide by 60) before applying $v = Lf$.
> 6. **Stating "Knee bends" without numerical angles** — Always cite angles (e.g., 60° peak flexion) for credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⏱ **Gait cycle = 100%** → **Stance 60% + Swing 40%**
- 🦶 **Stance has 5 sub-phases** (IC, LR, MSt, TSt, PSw); **Swing has 3 sub-phases** (ISw, MSw, TSw)
- 🔁 **Two double support periods** (0–12% and 50–60%) — *the hallmark of walking*
- 🏃 **No double support in running** — replaced by a *float phase* (both feet airborne)
- 📐 **Peak knee flexion ~60°** occurs in **Initial Swing** → essential for foot clearance
- 📐 **Peak hip extension ~10°** occurs at **Terminal Stance** (just before heel-off)
- 📐 **Peak ankle dorsiflexion ~10°** occurs at **Terminal Stance**
- 🔢 **Stride length = 2 × step length**
- 🔢 **Walking speed $v = L \times f$**, where $f$ = cadence in Hz (= steps/min ÷ 60)
- 🔢 **Gait cycle time $T = 1/f$**
- ⏲ **Two DS + One SS (of reference limb) = Stance**; **Swing of reference = SS of opposite limb**
- 💪 **Gluteus maximus & hamstrings** dominate Initial Contact (deceleration of trunk)
- 💪 **Gastrocnemius-soleus** dominate Terminal Stance (push-off)
- 💪 **Iliopsoas** dominates Initial Swing (hip flexion initiation)
- 💪 **Hamstrings** dominate Terminal Swing (deceleration of forward-swinging leg)
- 🏥 **Clinical correlate:** Stiff-knee gait (loss of ISw knee flexion), crouch gait (excess MSt knee flexion), equinus gait (loss of heel strike).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporal Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temporal Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⏱️ TEMPORAL FACTORS IN BIOMECHANICS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporal Factors** are the time-based parameters that characterize the duration, rate, sequencing, and rhythmicity of human motion and force application. They form the fourth dimension (time, $t$) of any biomechanical analysis, complementing the spatial factors (linear $x$ and angular $\theta$ displacements) and mass/inertial factors ($m$, $I$).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, temporal factors include:

- **Time ($t$)** — measured in **seconds (s)**, the fundamental scalar
- **Linear velocity ($v$)** — $\text{m/s}$
- **Linear acceleration ($a$)** — $\text{m/s}^2$
- **Angular velocity ($\omega$)** — **rad/s**
- **Angular acceleration ($\alpha$)** — $\text{rad/s}^2$
- **Reaction time ($RT$)** — **s** or **ms**
- **Movement time ($MT$)** — **s**
- **Rate of Force Development ($RFD$)** — **N/s**
- **Cadence / Frequency ($f$)** — **Hz** or **steps/min**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Clock" of Movement

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine watching a 100 m sprinter on slow-motion replay. The *spatial* factors tell you **where** the runner is (distance covered). The *temporal* factors tell you **when** and **how fast** they get there. Just as a clock has hands, gears, and a ticking rhythm, every human movement has a hidden internal clock — the temporal pattern of muscle activation, joint rotation, and force buildup.

**Plain English Intuition:** If biomechanics were a movie, spatial factors describe the *frames* (the picture), while temporal factors describe the *frame rate* and the *storyboard* — the sequencing and timing that makes the motion meaningful. A soccer kick and a ballet pirouette may travel through similar angles, but the *temporal signature* (how fast, when, in what rhythm) is completely different.

## 1.3 Why Temporal Factors Matter (KTU Engineering Perspective)

| Domain | Application of Temporal Factors |
|---|---|
| **Gait Analysis** | Stride time, step time, cadence, swing-to-stance ratio |
| **Sports Engineering** | Sprint start reaction time, bat-swing duration |
| **Rehabilitation** | Time-to-peak torque in post-ACL patients |
| **Ergonomics** | Fitts' Law for optimal control-display ratio |
| **Prosthetics** | Gait cycle temporal symmetry index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Temporal factors are essential for analyzing the **kinematic chain of motion** and the **dynamic loading profile** of biological tissues over time. They are the bridge between *kinematics* (geometry of motion) and *kinetics* (forces causing motion).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Temporal Concepts

### 🔹 Level 1: Primitive Temporal Quantities

The most basic temporal factor is **time ($t$)** itself — a scalar quantity measured along the continuum of motion. Every other temporal factor is mathematically derived from $t$ or its derivatives.

### 🔹 Level 2: First Derivative — Velocity (The "Rate" Family)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why first derivative?** Velocity tells us *how fast* position changes — it is the slope of the position-time graph. In biomechanics, we are usually more interested in the rate of change than in absolute time.

$$v = \frac{ds}{dt}, \qquad \omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

### 🔹 Level 3: Second Derivative — Acceleration (The "Change-in-Rate" Family)

Acceleration is the rate at which velocity itself changes. It is critical in biomechanics because **Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$) links acceleration directly to the net force.

$$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{d^2 s}{dt^2}, \qquad \alpha = \frac{d\omega}{dt} = \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2}$$

### 🔹 Level 4: Integrated Temporal Quantities — Impulse and Work

> [!NOTE]
> **Integration over time** converts instantaneous rates into cumulative effects. Impulse (change in momentum) and work-energy relationships are the integrated cousins of acceleration.

$$J = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} F \, dt = \Delta p = m \Delta v$$

### 🔹 Level 5: Neuromuscular Temporal Factors

These describe the **time delay** between stimulus and response:

- **Reaction Time ($RT$):** Latency from stimulus onset to first detectable muscle activation.
- **Movement Time ($MT$):** Time from initiation to completion of voluntary motion.
- **Response Time = $RT + MT$**

## 2.2 The Kinematic Equations (Constant Acceleration) — Adapted for Biomechanics

For a body segment moving with constant acceleration, the **SUVAT** equations form the analytical backbone:

$$v = u + a t$$

$$s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$$

$$v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$$

$$s = \tfrac{1}{2}(u + v)\, t$$

Where:
- $u$ = initial velocity, $v$ = final velocity
- $a$ = constant acceleration
- $s$ = displacement
- $t$ = elapsed time

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in Engineering:** These equations are routinely used to model the **flight phase of a jump**, the **pendulum-like swing of a limb**, and the **acceleration phase of sprinting**, where acceleration is approximately constant over short intervals.

## 2.3 Angular Kinematic Equations (KTU 2024 — High Weightage)

For rotating body segments (e.g., forearm during a tennis serve):

$$\omega_f = \omega_i + \alpha t$$

$$\theta = \omega_i t + \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^2$$

$$\omega_f^2 = \omega_i^2 + 2 \alpha \theta$$

$$\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(\omega_i + \omega_f) t$$

## 2.4 Fitts' Law — The Speed-Accuracy Trade-Off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fitts' Law (1954)** is a fundamental predictive model in motor control and human factors engineering, stating that the **Movement Time ($MT$)** required to move to a target is a logarithmic function of the **Index of Difficulty ($ID$)**:

$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)$$

Where:
- $A$ = distance (amplitude) to the target center
- $W$ = width of the target
- $a, b$ = empirically determined regression constants
- $\log_2(2A/W) = ID$ = Index of Difficulty (measured in **bits**)

**Throughput ($TP$)** is the information-processing rate:

$$TP = \frac{ID}{MT} \quad \text{[bits/s]}$$

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | Linear velocity | $\text{m/s}$ | Center-of-mass motion |
| 2 | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$ | Linear acceleration | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Sprint start phase |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Angular velocity | $\text{rad/s}$ | Joint rotation rate |
| 4 | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt}$ | Angular acceleration | $\text{rad/s}^2$ | Wrist snap in throwing |
| 5 | $v = u + a t$ | SUVAT — 1st eqn. | mixed | Constant-accel. motion |
| 6 | $s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2$ | SUVAT — 2nd eqn. | $\text{m}$ | Jump height analysis |
| 7 | $v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s$ | SUVAT — 3rd eqn. | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | Velocity at landing |
| 8 | $\omega_f = \omega_i + \alpha t$ | Angular SUVAT-1 | $\text{rad/s}$ | Joint kinematics |
| 9 | $J = \int F \, dt = m \Delta v$ | Impulse-momentum | $\text{N·s}$ | Foot-ground impact |
| 10 | $RFD = \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta t}$ | Rate of Force Development | $\text{N/s}$ | Explosive strength |
| 11 | $MT = a + b \log_2(2A/W)$ | Fitts' Law | $\text{s}$ | Motor control design |
| 12 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency–Period relation | $\text{Hz}$ | Gait cadence |
| 13 | $TP = ID / MT$ | Fitts' Throughput | $\text{bits/s}$ | HCI / ergonomics |
| 14 | $v_{tan} = r \omega$ | Tangential velocity | $\text{m/s}$ | Circular limb motion |
| 15 | $a_{tan} = r \alpha$ | Tangential acceleration | $\text{m/s}^2$ | Circular limb motion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 EXHAUSTIVE STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Goal:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No logical leaps. The derivations follow the exact pattern expected in KTU 14-mark answers, where marks are awarded for **every meaningful transition**.

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Position-Time Relationship from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** Velocity is the time-rate of change of position.

$$v(t) = \frac{ds(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1 — Isolate $ds$:**

$$ds(t) = v(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides between initial time $t_0$ and final time $t$:**

$$\int_{s_0}^{s(t)} ds' = \int_{t_0}^{t} v(t') \, dt'$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left integral (s is the variable of integration):**

$$s(t) - s_0 = \int_{t_0}^{t} v(t') \, dt'$$

**Step 4 — Define $s_0 = s(t_0)$ as the initial position. If motion starts at $t_0 = 0$ with $s_0 = 0$:**

$$s(t) = \int_{0}^{t} v(t') \, dt'$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Position is the **time-integral** of velocity. This is why a velocity-time graph's *area* gives displacement.

## 3.2 Derivation #2: SUVAT Equation $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$

**Starting Premise:** Assume *constant* acceleration $a$. Then velocity is a linear function of time:

$$v(t) = u + a t$$

**Step 1 — Apply definition of velocity:**

$$\frac{ds}{dt} = u + a t$$

**Step 2 — Separate differentials:**

$$ds = (u + a t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $0$ to $t$ (with $s(0) = 0$):**

$$\int_{0}^{s(t)} ds' = \int_{0}^{t} (u + a t') \, dt'$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate RHS term by term:**

$$s(t) = \int_{0}^{t} u \, dt' + \int_{0}^{t} a t' \, dt'$$

**Step 5 — Constants pulled out; standard power rule applied:**

$$s(t) = u \big[ t' \big]_{0}^{t} + a \left[ \frac{t'^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{t}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute limits:**

$$s(t) = u(t - 0) + a \left( \frac{t^2 - 0^2}{2} \right)$$

$$\boxed{s(t) = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation #3: SUVAT Equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ (Eliminating Time)

**Starting Premise:** Use the two fundamental kinematic identities.

$$v = u + a t \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 1 — From (i), solve for $t$:**

$$t = \frac{v - u}{a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute this $t$ into (ii):**

$$s = u \cdot \frac{v - u}{a} + \tfrac{1}{2} a \left( \frac{v - u}{a} \right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the second term:**

$$\left( \frac{v - u}{a} \right)^2 = \frac{(v - u)^2}{a^2}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} a \cdot \frac{(v - u)^2}{a^2} = \frac{(v - u)^2}{2 a}$$

**Step 4 — Common denominator $2a$ on the RHS:**

$$s = \frac{2u(v - u)}{2a} + \frac{(v - u)^2}{2a}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the numerators over the common denominator:**

$$s = \frac{2u(v - u) + (v - u)^2}{2a}$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $(v - u)$ from the numerator:**

$$s = \frac{(v - u)\big[ 2u + (v - u) \big]}{2a}$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$2u + v - u = u + v$$

**Step 8 — Substitute back:**

$$s = \frac{(v - u)(v + u)}{2 a}$$

**Step 9 — Apply the difference-of-squares identity: $(v-u)(v+u) = v^2 - u^2$:**

$$s = \frac{v^2 - u^2}{2a}$$

**Step 10 — Cross-multiply to obtain the final form:**

$$2 a s = v^2 - u^2$$

$$\boxed{v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s}$$

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Fitts' Law — Conceptual Derivation from Information Theory

**Premise:** Human motor system transmits information through movement. Targets are characterized by an **Index of Difficulty (ID)** measured in **bits**, following Shannon's channel-capacity theorem.

**Step 1 — Model the target as a spatial "channel":**

The target is a one-dimensional interval of width $W$, centered at distance $A$ from the start.

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's logarithmic measure of information:**

The number of *equally probable* target zones that fit along distance $A$:

$$N = \frac{2A}{W}$$

(The factor 2 accounts for movement in both directions.)

**Step 3 — Compute information content in bits:**

$$ID = \log_2 N = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Empirically (Fitts, 1954), Movement Time scales linearly with ID:**

$$MT = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Throughput (information rate) of the motor system:**

$$\boxed{TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{\log_2(2A/W)}{MT} \quad [\text{bits/s}]}$$

> **Engineering Note:** Fitts' Law is the cornerstone of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, **surgical robotics**, and **prosthetic limb design** — all assessed in KTU Module 3.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Temporal Factors to Motion Phases

| Movement Phase | Dominant Temporal Factor | Formula Link | Typical Magnitude (Human) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-motor (anticipation) | Reaction Time $RT$ | $RT = t_{response} - t_{stimulus}$ | **150 – 250 ms** |
| Initiation | Acceleration $a$ | $a = \Delta v / \Delta t$ | up to **40 m/s²** (sprint) |
| Steady state | Velocity $v$ | $v = \Delta s / \Delta t$ | **8 – 12 m/s** (sprint) |
| Deceleration | Negative $a$ | $a < 0$ | varies |
| Termination | Movement Time $MT$ | $MT = t_{end} - t_{start}$ | **100 – 500 ms** |
| Cyclic (gait) | Cadence $f$ | $f = 1/T$ | **100 – 120 steps/min** |
| Explosive force | Rate of Force Development | $RFD = \Delta F/\Delta t$ | **5000 N/s** (elite athletes) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram #1 — Hierarchical Concept Map of Temporal Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TEMPORAL FACTORS] --> B[INSTANTANEOUS]
    A --> C[INTEGRATED]
    A --> D[NEUROMUSCULAR]
    A --> E[CYCLIC]

    B --> B1[Time t in s]
    B --> B2[Linear Velocity v in m per s]
    B --> B3[Linear Acceleration a in m per s squared]
    B --> B4[Angular Velocity omega in rad per s]
    B --> B5[Angular Acceleration alpha in rad per s squared]

    C --> C1[Impulse J in Ns]
    C --> C2[Work W in J]
    C --> C3[Displacement from area under v t graph]

    D --> D1[Reaction Time RT]
    D --> D2[Movement Time MT]
    D --> D3[Response Time equals RT plus MT]

    E --> E1[Period T in s]
    E --> E2[Frequency f in Hz]
    E --> E3[Cadence in steps per min]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram #2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Temporal Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STAGE 1 RAW DATA ACQUISITION] --> S2[STAGE 2 NOISE FILTERING]
    S2 --> S3[STAGE 3 EVENT DETECTION]
    S3 --> S4[STAGE 4 TEMPORAL PARAMETER EXTRACTION]
    S4 --> S5[STAGE 5 INTERPRETATION AND CLINICAL OUTPUT]

    S1 --> S1A[High Speed Camera 200 fps]
    S1 --> S1B[Force Plate 1000 Hz]
    S1 --> S1C[IMU Sensors 100 Hz]
    S1 --> S1D[EMG 2000 Hz]

    S3 --> S3A[Heel Strike Marker]
    S3 --> S3B[Toe Off Marker]
    S3 --> S3C[Peak Force Marker]

    S4 --> S4A[Compute Reaction Time]
    S4 --> S4B[Compute Movement Time]
    S4 --> S4C[Compute Cadence and Stride Time]
    S4 --> S4D[Compute Rate of Force Development]

    S5 --> S5A[Compare to Normative Database]
    S5 --> S5B[Flag Asymmetry Index]
    S5 --> S5C[Generate Report for Clinician]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram #3 — Kinematic Relationship Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Position s of t] -->|d by dt| V[Velocity v of t]
    V -->|d by dt| A[Acceleration a of t]
    A -->|m times a| F[Net Force F equals m a]
    F -->|integrate over t| J[Impulse J]
    J -->|equals| MV[Change in Momentum m delta v]

    P -->|integrate over t| AREA[Area under v t curve]
    AREA --> DISP[Net Displacement]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU Exam:** When a question asks "relate temporal factors," start at the top of Diagram 1, traverse the hierarchy, and finish with the kinematic relationship diagram. Examiners award marks for **explicit linkages**, not just listing concepts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are mapped to **KTU Course Outcomes (CO3)** — *"Apply kinematic and kinetic principles to analyze human movement"* — and target **Bloom's Levels: Apply (L3) and Analyze (L4)**.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *(3 Marks)*

**Define temporal factors in biomechanics. List any four temporal parameters with their SI units.**

**Model Answer:**

> Temporal factors are time-based quantities that describe the duration, rate, and rhythmicity of human motion. They are essential for characterizing how fast, how long, and in what sequence movement occurs.

**Four temporal parameters:**

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Time | $t$ | second (s) |
| Linear velocity | $v$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| Linear acceleration | $a$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| Frequency | $f$ | hertz (Hz) |

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correct parameter list with units.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *(3 Marks)*

**State Fitts' Law. What does the Index of Difficulty represent?**

**Model Answer:**

> Fitts' Law states that the **Movement Time (MT)** required to acquire a target is a logarithmic function of the **distance to the target** and the **width of the target**:
> $$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)$$
> The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** = $\log_2(2A/W)$ represents the *information content* of the task, measured in **bits**. A higher ID means a harder task (larger distance or smaller target) and consequently a longer movement time.

*(Award 3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for explanation of ID, 1 for unit "bits".)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always include units** in the final answer. A correct numerical value *without* the unit loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Show all intermediate steps.** KTU examiners follow a *step-marking scheme* — partial credit is given for every correct algebraic transition. Skipping steps is the #1 cause of lost marks.
> 3. **State the governing equation** before substituting values. Students who jump directly to numbers get 0 for the "formula" credit.
> 4. **Sign convention matters** for direction (velocity sign, acceleration sign). Explicitly state "taking forward as positive."
> 5. In Fitts' Law questions, **clarify the base of the logarithm is 2** (i.e., ID is in bits). Many students incorrectly use base-10 or natural log.

---

### ✏️ **Question A — KTU University Exam Model** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Derive the kinematic equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ from first principles, showing every step. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A sprinter starting from rest accelerates uniformly at $2.5 \, \text{m/s}^2$ for the first $4 \, \text{s}$ of a race. Calculate:
   (i) The velocity reached at $t = 4 \, \text{s}$.
   (ii) The distance covered in this time.
   (iii) If the sprinter then decelerates uniformly to rest over the next $3 \, \text{s}$, what is the deceleration? *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1** *(1 mark)*: State the two governing kinematic equations for constant acceleration:
$$v = u + a t \quad \text{...(i)}$$
$$s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 2** *(1 mark)*: From (i), solve for $t$:
$$t = \frac{v - u}{a}$$

**Step 3** *(1 mark)*: Substitute this $t$ into (ii):
$$s = u \left( \frac{v - u}{a} \right) + \tfrac{1}{2} a \left( \frac{v - u}{a} \right)^2$$

**Step 4** *(1 mark)*: Simplify the second term:
$$\tfrac{1}{2} a \cdot \frac{(v - u)^2}{a^2} = \frac{(v - u)^2}{2 a}$$

**Step 5** *(1 mark)*: Put over common denominator $2a$:
$$s = \frac{2 u (v - u) + (v - u)^2}{2 a}$$

**Step 6** *(1 mark)*: Factor $(v - u)$ and simplify the bracket:
$$s = \frac{(v - u)(2u + v - u)}{2 a} = \frac{(v - u)(v + u)}{2 a}$$

**Step 7** *(1 mark)*: Apply difference of squares and rearrange:
$$2 a s = v^2 - u^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s}$$

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $u = 0$ (starts from rest), $a_1 = 2.5 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $t_1 = 4 \, \text{s}$, $v_f = 0$ at $t_2 = 7 \, \text{s}$ total (so deceleration phase $= 3 \, \text{s}$).

**(i) Velocity at $t = 4 \, \text{s}$** *(2 marks)*:
$$v = u + a_1 t_1 = 0 + (2.5)(4)$$
$$\boxed{v = 10 \, \text{m/s}}$$

**(ii) Distance covered in first 4 s** *(2 marks)*:
$$s = u t_1 + \tfrac{1}{2} a_1 t_1^2 = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(2.5)(4)^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(2.5)(16)$$
$$\boxed{s = 20 \, \text{m}}$$

**(iii) Deceleration over next 3 s** *(3 marks)*:
Using $v_f = v_i + a t$ with $v_f = 0$, $v_i = 10 \, \text{m/s}$, $t = 3 \, \text{s}$:
$$0 = 10 + a_d (3) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad a_d = -\frac{10}{3}$$
$$\boxed{a_d \approx -3.33 \, \text{m/s}^2 \quad \text{(deceleration magnitude } = 3.33 \, \text{m/s}^2\text{)}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates deceleration (opposite to the direction of motion).

---

### ✏️ **Question B — KTU University Exam Model (Internal Choice)** *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** What is Fitts' Law? Derive the relationship between Movement Time and the Index of Difficulty. Discuss its application in designing a computer mouse pointer. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** An athlete performs a vertical jump. From force-plate data, the ground reaction force rises from **0 N to 1800 N in 0.30 s** during the push-off phase. The athlete's body mass is **75 kg**. Calculate:
   (i) The Rate of Force Development (RFD).
   (ii) The net impulse delivered during this phase.
   (iii) The takeoff velocity (assuming the athlete starts from a crouch with $u = 0$). *(7 Marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Definition** *(1 mark)*: Fitts' Law is an empirical model in human motor control that quantifies the **speed-accuracy trade-off** in aimed movements. It states that the time to move to a target is a logarithmic function of the ratio of distance to target width.

**Statement of Fitts' Law** *(1 mark)*:
$$MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2A}{W} \right)$$

**Derivation of ID** *(3 marks)*:
- Model target as a "channel" of width $W$ at distance $A$ from start.
- The number of *independent* non-overlapping target zones along distance $A$ in either direction:
$$N = \frac{2A}{W}$$
- By Shannon's information theory, the information content of selecting one zone:
$$ID = \log_2 N = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{2A}{W} \right) \quad [\text{bits}]$$
- Empirically, $MT \propto ID$, leading to Fitts' linear regression form.

**Application in computer mouse design** *(2 marks)*:
- **CD ratio (Control-Display ratio)**: Distance moved on desk vs. cursor distance on screen.
- Designers use Fitts' Law to optimize the size of on-screen buttons — larger buttons (greater $W$) and shorter cursor travel (smaller $A$) reduce $ID$ and hence $MT$, improving usability.
- Microsoft's research on Windows pointer acceleration (the "enhanced pointer precision" feature) is directly derived from Fitts' Law to maximize **Throughput $TP$** (bits/s).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $F_i = 0 \, \text{N}$, $F_f = 1800 \, \text{N}$, $\Delta t = 0.30 \, \text{s}$, $m = 75 \, \text{kg}$, $u = 0$.

**(i) Rate of Force Development** *(2 marks)*:
$$RFD = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta t} = \frac{1800 - 0}{0.30}$$
$$\boxed{RFD = 6000 \, \text{N/s}}$$

**(ii) Net Impulse** *(2 marks)*:
Assuming approximately linear force rise (triangular profile):
$$J = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot F_{peak} \cdot \Delta t = \tfrac{1}{2}(1800)(0.30)$$
$$\boxed{J = 270 \, \text{N·s}}$$

*(Examiner note: If the student assumes a rectangular profile, $J = 1800 \times 0.30 = 540 \, \text{N·s}$. Either is acceptable with stated assumption.)*

**(iii) Takeoff velocity** *(3 marks)*:
By the impulse-momentum theorem:
$$J = m \Delta v = m(v - u)$$
$$270 = 75 \cdot (v - 0)$$
$$v = \frac{270}{75}$$
$$\boxed{v_{takeoff} = 3.6 \, \text{m/s} \quad \text{(directed upward)}}$$

**Verification (optional, +1 bonus):** The jump height can be computed as:
$$h = \frac{v^2}{2 g} = \frac{(3.6)^2}{2(9.81)} \approx 0.66 \, \text{m}$$

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Last-Minute" 60-Second Revision Card — Pin This On Your Wall!**

### ✅ Core Definitions to Memorize
- **Temporal factors** = time-based motion parameters
- **Reaction time** = stimulus-to-response latency
- **Movement time** = initiation-to-completion duration
- **Response time** = $RT + MT$
- **RFD** = $\Delta F / \Delta t$ — explosive strength metric
- **Fitts' ID** = $\log_2(2A/W)$ — measured in **bits**
- **Throughput** = $ID/MT$ — motor channel capacity in **bits/s**

### ✅ The 4 SUVAT Equations (Linear)
$$v = u + a t \qquad s = u t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2 \qquad v^2 = u^2 + 2 a s \qquad s = \tfrac{1}{2}(u+v)t$$

### ✅ The 4 SUVAT Equations (Angular)
$$\omega_f = \omega_i + \alpha t \qquad \theta = \omega_i t + \tfrac{1}{2} \alpha t^2 \qquad \omega_f^2 = \omega_i^2 + 2\alpha\theta \qquad \theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(\omega_i + \omega_f)t$$

### ✅ Key Units Cheat-Sheet
- $t$: **s (second)**
- $v$: **m/s**, $\omega$: **rad/s**
- $a$: **m/s²**, $\alpha$: **rad/s²**
- $J$: **N·s**, RFD: **N/s**
- $f$: **Hz**, $T$: **s**

### ✅ Derivations to Practice Until Fluency
- ☐ SUVAT: $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ (from $v = u + at$)
- ☐ SUVAT: $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ (eliminate $t$)
- ☐ Fitts' Law — from Shannon's information theorem
- ☐ Impulse-momentum: $J = m\Delta v$ from $F = ma$

### ✅ Common Engineering Applications
- **Gait analysis** — stride time, cadence, swing-to-stance ratio
- **Sports biomechanics** — sprint kinematics, bat-swing tempo
- **Prosthetics & orthotics** — temporal symmetry index
- **HCI & ergonomics** — Fitts' Law in interface design
- **Rehabilitation** — RFD in post-ACL recovery

### ✅ Exam Strategy Pointers
- Always define symbols **before** using them.
- Always state **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming constant acceleration").
- Always include **units** in the final boxed answer.
- Always show **all steps** — KTU uses step-marking.
- If deriving, **start from a known formula**, not from a memorized result.

> 🎯 **Final Tip:** Temporal factors are the *connective tissue* between kinematics (geometry) and kinetics (forces). Mastering them unlocks Module 3 entirely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Center of Mass Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Center of Mass Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Biomechanics (PCBMT601 – Module 3)**, the **Center of Mass (COM)** of a multi-segment biological system (e.g., the human body) is defined as the unique point in space where the **weighted average position** of all elemental masses (tissues, limbs, organs) is concentrated. Mathematically, it is the **mass-weighted mean position vector** of every particle constituting the system.

When the body or any of its segments undergo motion (walking, jumping, running, lifting), the **Center of Mass Displacement** refers to the **change in the spatial position of this mass-weighted point** between two instants of time. It is a **vector quantity** measured in **metres (m)** and constitutes the foundation for the application of Newton's Second Law in linked-segment biomechanical models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> The *Center of Mass Displacement* is the translational displacement of the body's net mass centre, governed by the equation of motion $\vec{F}_{ext} = M \cdot \vec{a}_{COM}$, where $M$ is the total body mass and $\vec{a}_{COM}$ is the acceleration of the centre of mass.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large inflated beach ball** floating on water. Even if the ball spins, tilts, or wobbles internally, there is **one single point** inside it that traces a perfectly smooth, parabolic path when you toss it. That point is its *Centre of Mass*.

Now, picture the **human body as a system of 14+ connected rigid segments** (head, trunk, upper arms, forearms, hands, thighs, shanks, feet). When you walk, your arms swing forward while your legs swing backward — a complicated mess of internal motion. However, your **whole-body COM** traces a remarkably smooth, wave-like curve through space. This smooth curve is what biomechanists study because:

1. It represents the **net translation** of your entire body.
2. It obeys **Newton's Laws** as if all your mass were concentrated at that single point.
3. It is **directly measurable** by force plates (via ground reaction force / body mass).

**Key Standard Metric (Anthropometric):**
- The COM of an **average adult human in anatomical (erect) posture** lies approximately **anterior to the second sacral vertebra (S2)**, roughly **55 % of the standing height** measured from the ground (per **Dempster's classic 1955 data**).
- This is the standard **anthropometric landmark** used by KTU examiners when referencing *segmental percentage distributions*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why COM Displacement Matters in KTU Biomechanics:**
> - It is the **only point** at which the *net external force* can be directly applied for translational analysis.
> - It eliminates the need to solve **rotational dynamics** of every individual joint when studying whole-body locomotion.
> - It is the basis of **inverse dynamics** and **gait analysis** problems frequently asked in KTU End Semester Examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

The displacement of the centre of mass is not a single formula but a **systematic reduction of a complex multi-body system into a single equivalent point mass**. The reasoning proceeds in six logical stages:

1. **Decomposition of the Body into Segments:** The human body is partitioned into a finite number of rigid links (typically 14 in a Hanavan model or 16 in a Dempster model). Each segment is treated as a rigid body with a known **mass (mᵢ)**, a known **centre of mass location (rᵢ)**, and a known **moment of inertia (Iᵢ)**.

2. **Determination of Individual Segment COMs:** Each segmental COM is located using **anthropometric tables** (e.g., Dempster, Winter). These tables express the segmental COM as a **percentage of segment length** from a proximal or distal joint.

3. **Mass-Weighted Vector Summation:** The position vector of the whole-body COM ($\vec{r}_{COM}$) is computed as the **mass-weighted average** of all individual segmental COM position vectors. The derivation of this formula follows directly from the requirement that the **net moment of the elemental masses about the COM is zero** by definition.

4. **Calculation of COM Displacement (ΔrCOM):** Displacement is defined as the **change in the COM position vector** between an initial state (t₁) and a final state (t₂). It is a **time-independent vector** quantity.

5. **Differentiation to Obtain Velocity and Acceleration:** Time derivatives yield the **velocity ($\vec{v}_{COM}$)** and **acceleration ($\vec{a}_{COM}$)** of the centre of mass. These are essential for applying Newton's Second Law.

6. **Application of Newton's Second Law (The "Why" of COM):** The total **external force** acting on the body equals the **total mass times the acceleration of the COM**. Internal forces (muscle, joint reactions) cancel out — this is the **fundamental reason** biomechanists use COM analysis; it bypasses the need to resolve internal force systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the COM Formula:**
> The COM formula is not arbitrary. It emerges from a single axiom: *The net angular momentum of the system about its COM, when no external forces act, must remain zero (or constant).* Solving this constraint yields the mass-weighted mean.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme. Memorise the units in bold.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Position of COM (Vector Form) | $\vec{r}_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Weighted mean of all particle positions | m |
| 2 | COM in 2D (x-coordinate) | $x_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i x_i}{M}$ | Horizontal COM position | m |
| 3 | COM in 2D (y-coordinate) | $y_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i y_i}{M}$ | Vertical COM position | m |
| 4 | COM Velocity | $\vec{v}_{COM} = \dfrac{d\vec{r}_{COM}}{dt} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{v}_i}{M}$ | Time derivative of COM position | m·s⁻¹ |
| 5 | COM Acceleration | $\vec{a}_{COM} = \dfrac{d\vec{v}_{COM}}{dt} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{a}_i}{M}$ | Time derivative of COM velocity | m·s⁻² |
| 6 | COM Displacement (Vector) | $\Delta \vec{r}_{COM} = \vec{r}_{COM,2} - \vec{r}_{COM,1}$ | Change in COM position | m |
| 7 | Newton's Second Law (Translational) | $\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = M \vec{a}_{COM}$ | Net external force on the system | N |
| 8 | Linear Momentum of the System | $\vec{P} = M \vec{v}_{COM}$ | Total linear momentum | kg·m·s⁻¹ |
| 9 | Kinetic Energy of COM Translation | $KE_{COM} = \tfrac{1}{2} M v_{COM}^{\,2}$ | Translational kinetic energy | J |
| 10 | Work–Energy on COM | $W = \vec{F}_{net} \cdot \Delta \vec{r}_{COM}$ | Net work done to displace COM | J |
| 11 | Impulse–Momentum on COM | $\vec{J} = M \Delta \vec{v}_{COM} = \int \vec{F}_{net}\,dt$ | Impulse changes COM momentum | N·s |
| 12 | Segmental COM Fraction (e.g., Head) | $r_{head} = 0.5 \times L_{head}$ | COM is at 50% of head length from vertex | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Prosthetics & Orthotics:** Engineers design above-knee prostheses by ensuring the *residual limb COM* aligns with the *socket centre* to minimise gait asymmetry.
> - **Sports Biomechanics:** Coaches analyse the COM trajectory of a high-jumper — a lower COM at take-off yields a higher parabolic flight path.
> - **Ergonomics & Occupational Safety:** When lifting a load, the **combined COM** of (person + load) determines the line of gravity and the resulting **anterior–posterior shear** on the lumbar spine (L5/S1 disc).
> - **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Exoskeletons use real-time COM estimation to predict and prevent falls in geriatric patients.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Position of the Centre of Mass (From First Principles)

**Statement to Prove:** The position vector of the COM of a system of $N$ particles is given by $\vec{r}_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i}$.

**Starting Concept:** By definition, the **net moment of the gravitational forces** (or equivalently, the net first moment of mass) about the COM must equal **zero**, because the COM is the **balance point** of the system.

**Step 1:** Let the COM be located at position $\vec{r}_{COM}$. The total moment of the mass elements about this point must vanish:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{COM}) = \vec{0}$$

This equation states that the sum of all mass-weighted displacements from the COM is zero (the "balance" condition).

**Step 2:** Expand the summation across the summation sign. This distributes the mass-weighted position vectors and the COM term:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_{COM} = \vec{0}$$

Here, the first term is the sum of mass-weighted positions, and the second term is the total mass $M$ multiplied by the constant vector $\vec{r}_{COM}$.

**Step 3:** Recognise that $\vec{r}_{COM}$ is **constant** with respect to the summation index $i$. Therefore, it can be factored out of the second summation:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i \;-\; \vec{r}_{COM} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i = \vec{0}$$

Define the total mass as $M = \sum m_i$. This is a standard abbreviation used throughout rigid-body dynamics.

**Step 4:** Move the COM term to the right-hand side by adding it to both sides of the equation:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i = \vec{r}_{COM} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i = M \vec{r}_{COM}$$

This isolates the sum of mass-weighted position vectors as equal to the total mass times the COM position.

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by the total mass $M$ to solve explicitly for the COM position vector:

$$\vec{r}_{COM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}$$

This is the fundamental formula used in all subsequent COM displacement analyses. Q.E.D.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Displacement of the COM Between Two Time Instants

**Statement to Prove:** $\Delta \vec{r}_{COM} = \vec{r}_{COM}(t_2) - \vec{r}_{COM}(t_1) = \dfrac{1}{M} \sum m_i [\vec{r}_i(t_2) - \vec{r}_i(t_1)]$.

**Step 1:** Apply the COM position formula derived above at the **initial time** $t_1$. Each segment $i$ has mass $m_i$ and position $\vec{r}_i(t_1)$:

$$\vec{r}_{COM}(t_1) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i(t_1)$$

This is the direct application of the formula from Derivation 1 at the first instant.

**Step 2:** Apply the same formula at the **final time** $t_2$:

$$\vec{r}_{COM}(t_2) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i(t_2)$$

This is the direct application of the formula from Derivation 1 at the second instant.

**Step 3:** Subtract the initial COM position from the final COM position. The scalar factor $\tfrac{1}{M}$ is common to both and can be factored out:

$$\Delta \vec{r}_{COM} = \vec{r}_{COM}(t_2) - \vec{r}_{COM}(t_1) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \left[ \vec{r}_i(t_2) - \vec{r}_i(t_1) \right]$$

Since the total mass $M$ does not depend on time, the $\tfrac{1}{M}$ factor distributes equally across both terms.

**Step 4:** Recognise that the term in brackets, $\vec{r}_i(t_2) - \vec{r}_i(t_1)$, is by definition the **displacement of the i-th segment** ($\Delta \vec{r}_i$). Substitute this to obtain the final compact form:

$$\Delta \vec{r}_{COM} = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \, \Delta \vec{r}_i$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The displacement of the whole-body COM is the **mass-weighted average of the displacements of all individual segments**. Lighter segments contribute less; heavier segments (e.g., trunk) dominate. Q.E.D.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — COM Displacement in a Lifting Task

**Problem (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style):**
A worker (body mass $M = 75$ kg) performs a squat-lift. The body is modelled as **two segments**:
- **Segment A (Trunk + Head + Arms):** mass $m_A = 45$ kg, COM located at $(x_A, y_A) = (0.30,\, 1.40)$ m at the start, and $(0.30,\, 1.20)$ m at the end of the lift.
- **Segment B (Thighs + Shanks + Feet):** mass $m_B = 30$ kg, COM located at $(x_B, y_B) = (0.20,\, 0.50)$ m at the start, and $(0.25,\, 0.70)$ m at the end.

**Find:** The magnitude and direction of the COM displacement of the worker.

**Step 1 — Verify total mass:** $M = m_A + m_B = 45 + 30 = 75$ kg. ✓ (consistent with the problem statement)

**Step 2 — Compute initial COM position** by applying the mass-weighted average formula to the x-coordinate. The factor $\tfrac{1}{M} = \tfrac{1}{75}$ kg⁻¹ converts mass-weighted sums to COM coordinates:

$$x_{COM,1} = \frac{m_A x_{A,1} + m_B x_{B,1}}{M} = \frac{(45)(0.30) + (30)(0.20)}{75} = \frac{13.5 + 6.0}{75} = \frac{19.5}{75}$$

$$x_{COM,1} = 0.260 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the initial y-coordinate** of the COM using the same mass-weighted average approach, this time applied to the vertical components:

$$y_{COM,1} = \frac{m_A y_{A,1} + m_B y_{B,1}}{M} = \frac{(45)(1.40) + (30)(0.50)}{75} = \frac{63.0 + 15.0}{75} = \frac{78.0}{75}$$

$$y_{COM,1} = 1.040 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final COM x-coordinate** by substituting the final segmental x-positions into the same mass-weighted formula. Note that the trunk x-coordinate is unchanged (0.30 m) while the legs shift forward slightly:

$$x_{COM,2} = \frac{m_A x_{A,2} + m_B x_{B,2}}{M} = \frac{(45)(0.30) + (30)(0.25)}{75} = \frac{13.5 + 7.5}{75} = \frac{21.0}{75}$$

$$x_{COM,2} = 0.280 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final COM y-coordinate** by applying the mass-weighted formula to the final vertical segmental positions. The trunk lowers from 1.40 m to 1.20 m while the legs rise from 0.50 m to 0.70 m:

$$y_{COM,2} = \frac{m_A y_{A,2} + m_B y_{B,2}}{M} = \frac{(45)(1.20) + (30)(0.70)}{75} = \frac{54.0 + 21.0}{75} = \frac{75.0}{75}$$

$$y_{COM,2} = 1.000 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the COM displacement vector** by subtracting the initial COM position from the final COM position. The vector has two components representing horizontal and vertical shifts:

$$\Delta x_{COM} = x_{COM,2} - x_{COM,1} = 0.280 - 0.260 = +0.020 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta y_{COM} = y_{COM,2} - y_{COM,1} = 1.000 - 1.040 = -0.040 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the magnitude** of the COM displacement using the Pythagorean theorem applied to the two orthogonal components:

$$|\Delta \vec{r}_{COM}| = \sqrt{(\Delta x_{COM})^{2} + (\Delta y_{COM})^{2}} = \sqrt{(0.020)^{2} + (-0.040)^{2}}$$

$$|\Delta \vec{r}_{COM}| = \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.0016} = \sqrt{0.0020} \approx 0.0447 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the direction** of the COM displacement vector using the inverse tangent of the vertical-to-horizontal ratio. The negative y-component indicates a downward shift, so the angle is measured below the horizontal:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Delta y_{COM}}{\Delta x_{COM}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.040}{0.020}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-2.0) \approx -63.4^{\circ}$$

**Final Answer:** The worker's COM moved **44.7 mm** in a direction **63.4° below the horizontal** (i.e., slightly forward and downward — consistent with a lowering phase of a squat lift).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from this Example:** The COM moved only **4.47 cm** even though the trunk segment alone moved **20 cm vertically**. This is because the **legs moved upward**, partially cancelling the trunk's downward displacement. This is the **mass-weighting effect** that makes COM analysis so powerful in biomechanics.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — COM Velocity from Newton's Second Law

**Problem:** A 70 kg sprinter's COM accelerates from $2.0$ m/s to $8.0$ m/s in $1.2$ s during a 100 m race. Find (a) the change in COM momentum and (b) the average net horizontal force from the ground.

**Step 1 — Change in COM velocity:** Subtract the initial velocity from the final velocity to obtain the scalar change in speed along the horizontal direction:

$$\Delta v_{COM} = 8.0 - 2.0 = 6.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Change in COM momentum:** Apply the linear momentum formula $P = M v_{COM}$ and take the difference between the final and initial momenta. The factor $M = 70$ kg is the body mass:

$$\Delta P = M \, \Delta v_{COM} = (70)(6.0) = 420 \text{ kg·m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Average net force from Newton's Second Law:** By the impulse–momentum theorem, the average net force equals the change in momentum divided by the time interval over which the force acts:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta t} = \frac{420}{1.2} = 350 \text{ N}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The ground must push the sprinter forward with a net **350 N** of force to achieve this COM acceleration. This value can be directly verified by a force plate placed on the running track.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — COM Computation Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of computing the Center of Mass Displacement from raw segmental data. This is the standard sequence used in any motion-capture–based biomechanics analysis (e.g., Vicon, Qualisys, OptiTrack).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Anthropometric Data] --> B[Segment Mass Fractions]
    B --> C[Segment COM Locations from Anthropometric Tables]
    C --> D[Acquire 3D Marker Positions from Motion Capture]
    D --> E[Compute Global Position of Each Segmental COM]
    E --> F[Apply Mass Weighted Average Formula]
    F --> G[Whole Body COM Position r COM at time t]
    G --> H{Difference Between Two Time Frames?}
    H -- Yes t2 minus t1 --> I[COM Displacement Vector Delta r COM]
    H -- No Single Frame --> J[Time Derivative of r COM]
    J --> K[COM Velocity v COM]
    K --> L[COM Acceleration a COM]
    I --> M[Final Output Displacement Magnitude and Direction]
    L --> N[Apply Newton Second Law F net equals M times a COM]
    N --> O[Final Output Net External Force]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - The upper branch (**A → G → I → M**) yields the **displacement** of the COM — this is the primary topic of this module.
> - The lower branch (**G → J → K → L → N → O**) yields the **velocity, acceleration, and net force** — these are downstream quantities used in KTU gait analysis problems.
> - Both branches **originate from the same COM position formula** — highlighting its central importance.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Human Body Segmental COM Map (Schematic)

The following block diagram shows the **hierarchical segmental model** of the human body used in COM computation. Each block represents a rigid segment with its known **mass fraction** and **COM location** as a percentage of segment length.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Head and Neck] --> H2[Mass 0.081 M COM at 50 percent of head height from vertex]
    T1[Thorax plus Abdomen plus Pelvis Trunk] --> T2[Mass 0.497 M COM at 63 percent of trunk length from shoulder]
    UA1[Upper Arm Left and Right] --> UA2[Mass 0.028 M each COM at 43.6 percent from shoulder]
    FA1[Forearm Left and Right] --> FA2[Mass 0.016 M each COM at 43 percent from elbow]
    HND1[Hand Left and Right] --> HND2[Mass 0.006 M each COM at 50 percent from wrist]
    TH1[Thigh Left and Right] --> TH2[Mass 0.100 M each COM at 43.3 percent from hip]
    SH1[Shank Left and Right] --> SH2[Mass 0.0465 M each COM at 43.3 percent from knee]
    FT1[Foot Left and Right] --> FT2[Mass 0.0145 M each COM at 50 percent from heel]
    H2 --> Z[Combine All Segmental Mass Weighted Vectors]
    T2 --> Z
    UA2 --> Z
    FA2 --> Z
    HND2 --> Z
    TH2 --> Z
    SH2 --> Z
    FT2 --> Z
    Z --> W[Output Total Body COM Position r COM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the Anthropometric Data Above:** The segmental mass fractions shown are the **standard values reported by Winter (2009, "Biomechanics and Energetics of Human Movement")** and are widely accepted in KTU-approved biomechanics textbooks. Students should remember the **trunk = ~50 % of body mass**, since the trunk dominates the COM position calculation.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Relationship Between COM Kinematics and Newton's Laws

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Body Mass M] --> B[COM Position r COM]
    A --> C[COM Velocity v COM]
    A --> D[COM Acceleration a COM]
    B -->|Time Derivative| C
    C -->|Time Derivative| D
    D -->|Multiply by M| E[Net External Force F ext]
    C -->|Multiply by M| F[Linear Momentum P]
    C -->|Half M v squared| G[Translational KE]
    B -->|Delta r| H[Work Energy on COM]
    E --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram is the **conceptual summary** of the entire Module-3 topic. Every arrow represents a **differentiation, integration, or multiplication** operation. In KTU ESE problems, you will often be asked to "work backwards" — i.e., given the force from a force plate, deduce the COM acceleration and velocity.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Define the Centre of Mass (COM) of a multi-segment system. Why is the concept of COM displacement significant in biomechanical analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Centre of Mass (COM)** of a multi-segment system is the unique point in space where the **mass-weighted average position** of all particles of the system is concentrated. For a discrete system of $N$ segments, it is mathematically defined as $\vec{r}_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$, where $m_i$ is the mass and $\vec{r}_i$ the position of the $i^{th}$ segment. (1½ marks)

The **displacement of the COM** is the change in position of this single equivalent point over time. Its significance lies in the fact that **Newton's Second Law for the entire body reduces to** $\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = M \vec{a}_{COM}$. This means biomechanists can analyse **whole-body translation** without resolving complex internal joint forces, making COM displacement the **foundation of gait analysis, sports biomechanics, and ergonomics**. (1½ marks)

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**State and explain the impulse–momentum principle as applied to the displacement of the COM of the human body during walking.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **impulse–momentum principle** for the COM states that the impulse delivered by the **net external force** acting on the body over a time interval $\Delta t$ equals the **change in linear momentum of the COM** during that interval: $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}_{ext}\, dt = M \, \Delta \vec{v}_{COM}$. (1½ marks)

During walking, the **ground reaction force (GRF)** is the primary external force acting on the body. The **horizontal component of the GRF** generates the impulse that accelerates the COM forward during the push-off phase and decelerates it during heel-strike. The **vertical component** supports body weight and produces the vertical oscillation of the COM seen in normal gait (typically 4–5 cm). The product of the net force and the time interval over which it acts (i.e., the **impulse**) thus determines how much the COM velocity — and therefore its displacement — changes. (1½ marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question in the ESE carries **14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) carrying **7 marks** and (b) carrying **7 marks**. The student must answer **one full question** from the module's choice pair.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the formula for the position of the centre of mass of a system of $N$ particles. Clearly state the fundamental axiom (the "zero moment condition") on which the derivation rests. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation rests on the **fundamental axiom of the COM**: *The net first moment of mass of the system about its centre of mass is zero*. This is the mathematical statement of the "balance point" concept.

Let the COM be located at $\vec{r}_{COM}$. The weighted displacement of each particle from the COM is $m_i(\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{COM})$. By the axiom:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i (\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{COM}) = \vec{0} \quad \text{[Axiom: net moment about COM is zero]} \tag{1}$$

Expanding:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i - \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_{COM} = \vec{0} \tag{2}$$

Since $\vec{r}_{COM}$ is independent of $i$, it factors out:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_{COM} \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i = \vec{0} \tag{3}$$

Define total mass $M = \sum m_i$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i = M \vec{r}_{COM} \tag{4}$$

Dividing by $M$:

$$\boxed{\vec{r}_{COM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}} \tag{5}$$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Statement of the axiom (1 mark)
- Setup of moment equation (1 mark)
- Expansion and factoring (2 marks)
- Definition of $M$ and isolation of $\vec{r}_{COM}$ (2 marks)
- Final boxed answer with correct notation (1 mark)

---

**(b)** A gymnast of total body mass $M = 60$ kg is performing a backward somersault. At the start of the flight phase, her body is modelled as **three segments** with the following data:

| Segment | Mass (kg) | COM Position at Take-off (m) | COM Position at Peak (m) |
|---------|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Head + Arms + Trunk (HAT) | 42.0 | (0.10, 1.20) | (0.20, 1.80) |
| Right Thigh + Shank | 9.0 | (0.05, 0.50) | (0.15, 1.10) |
| Left Thigh + Shank | 9.0 | (-0.05, 0.50) | (-0.15, 1.10) |

Calculate:
1. The position of the whole-body COM at **take-off** and at **peak of flight**. **(3 marks)**
2. The **magnitude and direction** of the COM displacement between these two instants. **(1 mark)**
3. The **change in gravitational potential energy** of the COM. **(1 mark)**
4. Comment on the role of the **trunk segment** in determining the whole-body COM location. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(b.1) COM at take-off:**

Apply the mass-weighted formula separately to the x and y coordinates. The factor $\tfrac{1}{M} = \tfrac{1}{60}$ kg⁻¹ converts mass-weighted sums into COM coordinates.

For the **x-coordinate at take-off**, substitute the take-off x-positions of all three segments into the weighted mean:

$$x_{COM,1} = \frac{m_{HAT} x_{HAT,1} + m_{R} x_{R,1} + m_{L} x_{L,1}}{M} = \frac{(42)(0.10) + (9)(0.05) + (9)(-0.05)}{60}$$

$$x_{COM,1} = \frac{4.20 + 0.45 - 0.45}{60} = \frac{4.20}{60} = 0.070 \text{ m}$$

For the **y-coordinate at take-off**, substitute the take-off y-positions of all three segments into the same weighted mean. The trunk y-coordinate (1.20 m) is the dominant contribution due to its large mass:

$$y_{COM,1} = \frac{(42)(1.20) + (9)(0.50) + (9)(0.50)}{60} = \frac{50.40 + 4.50 + 4.50}{60} = \frac{59.40}{60} = 0.990 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, at take-off: $\vec{r}_{COM,1} = (0.070,\, 0.990)$ m. **(1.5 marks)**

**COM at peak of flight:**

For the **x-coordinate at peak**, substitute the peak x-positions of all three segments. The trunk shifts to 0.20 m while the legs shift outward to ±0.15 m:

$$x_{COM,2} = \frac{(42)(0.20) + (9)(0.15) + (9)(-0.15)}{60} = \frac{8.40 + 1.35 - 1.35}{60} = \frac{8.40}{60} = 0.140 \text{ m}$$

For the **y-coordinate at peak**, substitute the peak y-positions of all three segments. The trunk y-coordinate rises from 1.20 m to 1.80 m, the dominant vertical shift:

$$y_{COM,2} = \frac{(42)(1.80) + (9)(1.10) + (9)(1.10)}{60} = \frac{75.60 + 9.90 + 9.90}{60} = \frac{95.40}{60} = 1.590 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, at peak: $\vec{r}_{COM,2} = (0.140,\, 1.590)$ m. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b.2) COM Displacement:**

The displacement is the difference of the final and initial COM positions. The horizontal component comes from the trunk and leg movements, while the vertical component is the gain in height:

$$\Delta x = 0.140 - 0.070 = +0.070 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta y = 1.590 - 0.990 = +0.600 \text{ m}$$

The **magnitude** is the Euclidean norm of the displacement vector:

$$|\Delta \vec{r}_{COM}| = \sqrt{(0.070)^{2} + (0.600)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.0049 + 0.3600} = \sqrt{0.3649} \approx 0.604 \text{ m}$$

The **direction** below the horizontal is given by the arctangent of the ratio of the components:

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.600}{0.070}\right) = \tan^{-1}(8.571) \approx 83.3^{\circ} \text{ (above horizontal)}$$

Therefore, the COM displacement is **0.604 m at 83.3° above the horizontal** (essentially almost vertically upward). **(1 mark)**

**(b.3) Change in Gravitational Potential Energy:**

The change in potential energy depends only on the **vertical displacement of the COM** ($\Delta y = 0.600$ m), since gravity acts vertically. Use the standard formula $U = M g \Delta h$ with $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$\Delta U = M g \Delta y = (60)(9.81)(0.600) = 353.16 \text{ J} \approx 353 \text{ J}$$

**(1 mark)**

**(b.4) Role of the Trunk:**

The **HAT (Head–Arms–Trunk) segment** accounts for $\tfrac{42}{60} = 70\%$ of the total body mass. Because the COM formula is **mass-weighted**, the trunk's position **dominates** the calculation. Even though the legs moved a large relative distance, their low mass fraction (15% each) means they contribute only **30%** to the final COM position. This is a key biomechanical insight: **heavy segments govern the body's COM**. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **work–energy theorem** as applied to the displacement of the COM. A 65 kg athlete's COM rises by 0.25 m during a vertical jump. Calculate:
1. The **gravitational potential energy gained** by the COM. **(2 marks)**
2. The **minimum kinetic energy** the COM must have had at take-off. **(2 marks)**
3. The **minimum take-off speed** of the COM. **(1.5 marks)**
4. State the **assumption** that justifies "minimum" in the answers above. **(1.5 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **work–energy theorem** for the COM states that the **net work done by external forces** acting through the displacement of the COM equals the **change in the translational kinetic energy of the COM**:

$$W_{net} = \Delta KE_{COM} = \tfrac{1}{2} M v_{COM,2}^{\,2} - \tfrac{1}{2} M v_{COM,1}^{\,2}$$

For a vertical jump, gravity is the relevant external force during the flight phase. The work done by gravity through the vertical displacement $\Delta y$ is $W = -M g \Delta y$ (negative, since gravity opposes upward motion).

**(b.1) Potential Energy gained:** Apply the gravitational potential energy formula $\Delta U = M g \Delta h$ using the body mass $M = 65$ kg, gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s², and the vertical rise of the COM $\Delta h = 0.25$ m:

$$\Delta U = M g \Delta h = (65)(9.81)(0.25) = 159.41 \text{ J} \approx 159.4 \text{ J}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(b.2) Minimum kinetic energy at take-off:** The "minimum" condition corresponds to the case where **all** kinetic energy at take-off is converted to potential energy at peak (i.e., $v_{COM,2} = 0$ at peak). By conservation of energy, $KE_{take-off} \geq \Delta U$:

$$KE_{take-off,\,min} = 159.4 \text{ J}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(b.3) Minimum take-off speed:** Equate the kinetic energy expression $\tfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}$ to the minimum kinetic energy found in the previous step, and solve algebraically for the speed $v$:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} M v_{min}^{\,2} = 159.4 \implies v_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 159.4}{65}} = \sqrt{4.905} \approx 2.215 \text{ m/s}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(b.4) Assumption:** The "minimum" condition assumes **no energy losses** — i.e., the jump is performed in a vacuum, with no air resistance, and the take-off occurs from a rigid surface with no deformation or muscular co-contraction losses. In reality, ground contact time and joint stiffness reduce the effective transfer, so the *actual* take-off speed is **higher** than the calculated minimum. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(b)** A 70 kg patient begins to **stand up from a chair**. The COM moves from rest at point A (0.30 m anterior to the ankle, 0.45 m above the floor) to point B (0.05 m anterior to the ankle, 1.05 m above the floor) at the end of the sit-to-stand motion. The **ankle joint** is at (0, 0) m. Calculate: **(7 marks)**

1. The **horizontal and vertical components** of the COM displacement. **(2 marks)**
2. The **total work done against gravity** to lift the COM. **(2 marks)**
3. If the muscles deliver a **net mechanical energy** of 480 J in 1.5 s, find the **average power output** of the muscles. **(1.5 marks)**
4. Briefly explain why sit-to-stand is a **mechanically demanding** task for elderly patients. **(1.5 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(b.1) Components of COM displacement:** The horizontal displacement is the difference in anterior positions (note the sign — the COM moves *posteriorly*, toward the ankle, so the value is negative). The vertical displacement is the difference in heights above the floor:

$$\Delta x = x_B - x_A = 0.05 - 0.30 = -0.25 \text{ m (i.e., 25 cm posterior)}$$

$$\Delta y = y_B - y_A = 1.05 - 0.45 = +0.60 \text{ m (upward)}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(b.2) Work done against gravity:** Only the vertical component contributes to work against gravity, since gravity acts vertically. Apply $W = M g \Delta y$ with $M = 70$ kg and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$W_{gravity} = M g \Delta y = (70)(9.81)(0.60) = 411.96 \text{ J} \approx 412 \text{ J}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(b.3) Average power output:** Power is the rate of doing work, given by the total energy delivered divided by the time interval over which it is delivered. Use $P_{avg} = E / \Delta t$ with $E = 480$ J and $\Delta t = 1.5$ s:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{480}{1.5} = 320 \text{ W}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(b.4) Why sit-to-stand is mechanically demanding for the elderly:**
- The COM must be **lifted vertically by 60 cm** — this is a large displacement requiring substantial knee and hip extensor torque.
- The COM must simultaneously be **moved horizontally** to maintain balance over the feet — failure to align the COM over the base of support causes a fall.
- The elderly have **reduced muscle mass (sarcopenia)** and **slower reaction times**, so the **320 W** average power required may exceed their capacity.
- The task also requires precise **co-ordination of multiple joints** (ankle, knee, hip, trunk), which is impaired in conditions such as Parkinson's disease and osteoarthritis. **(1.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting vector nature of displacement:** Many students write $\Delta r_{COM} = r_2 - r_1$ as a scalar. Always treat it as a **vector** and compute both magnitude and direction.
> 2. **Sign errors in COM velocity/acceleration:** KTU examiners specifically look for correct **signs** in the x and y components. Recheck: a *downward* displacement of the COM has a **negative** $\Delta y$.
> 3. **Forgetting the total mass $M$ in the denominator:** The COM is the **weighted average** — students sometimes write $\sum m_i \vec{r}_i$ and call it the COM, which is a serious conceptual error costing **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Mixing up COM of segments vs whole body:** Each *segmental* COM is a fraction of segment length; the *whole-body* COM is the mass-weighted sum of segmental COMs. Confusing these is a common Part-A trap.
> 5. **In 14-mark problems, students skip showing the mass-weighting derivation.** Always show the formula substituted with values — partial credit is awarded for **method**, not just the final number.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Centre of Mass Displacement (Module 3)**

- **Core Definition:** The COM of a multi-body system is the **mass-weighted mean position vector**: $\vec{r}_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}$.
- **Displacement is a vector:** $\Delta \vec{r}_{COM} = \vec{r}_{COM,2} - \vec{r}_{COM,1}$ — always report both **magnitude and direction**.
- **Mass-Weighted Property:** Heavier segments (trunk ≈ 50% of body mass) dominate the COM location; lighter segments (hands ≈ 1% each) have negligible effect.
- **Newton's Second Law at COM:** $\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = M \vec{a}_{COM}$ — internal forces (muscle, joint reactions) **cancel out** automatically.
- **Momentum:** $\vec{P} = M \vec{v}_{COM}$ — change in momentum requires **net external impulse**.
- **Work–Energy:** $W_{net} = \Delta KE_{COM} = \tfrac{1}{2} M (v_2^{2} - v_1^{2})$; for vertical motion, $W_{gravity} = M g \Delta y$.
- **Anatomical Reference:** Whole-body COM of an erect adult lies **anterior to S2 vertebra**, about **55% of body height** from the ground.
- **Standard Anthropometric Data (Winter 2009):** Trunk mass ≈ 49.7%, Thighs ≈ 10% each, Shanks ≈ 4.65% each, Feet ≈ 1.45% each, Head ≈ 8.1%, Upper Arms ≈ 2.8% each, Forearms ≈ 1.6% each, Hands ≈ 0.6% each.
- **Measurement Methods:** (i) **Reaction Board Method** (static), (ii) **Force Plate Method** (dynamic, uses $a_{COM} = F/M$), (iii) **Segmental Method** (kinematic, uses anthropometric tables + motion capture).
- **KTU-Favourite Problems:** (1) 2-segment COM displacement calculation, (2) Vertical jump energy/speed, (3) Gait impulse–momentum using force plate data, (4) Work done in sit-to-stand.
- **Common Exam Keyword Triggers:** "Calculate the COM position", "Determine the displacement of the COM", "Find the work done on the COM", "Apply Newton's Second Law to the COM".
- **Critical Pitfall to Avoid:** Never confuse the **COM** of a segment with the **whole-body COM**. Always state which one the question is asking for.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Range of Motion at the Joints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Range of Motion at the Joints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Range of Motion (ROM)** is the measurable distance and direction a joint can move between its flexed and extended positions. It is a fundamental kinematic parameter in biomechanics and clinical rehabilitation that quantifies the maximum angular displacement (in degrees) or linear displacement (in centimeters) achievable at a synovial or cartilaginous joint, governed by the geometry of the articulating surfaces, the extensibility of periarticular soft tissues (ligaments, joint capsule, muscles, tendons, and skin), and the integrity of the neuromuscular control system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> Range of Motion at the joints refers to the distance and direction a joint can move between its flexed and extended position. ROM is fundamental to kinesiology, gait analysis, and rehabilitation engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Door Hinge Analogy:** Imagine a standard door hinge on a door. The door is restricted from rotating beyond a certain point by the physical structure of the hinge and the door frame. The angle through which the door can swing (typically 0° to 180°) is analogous to a joint's ROM. Just as a rusted, swollen, or poorly oiled hinge will have a reduced arc of motion compared to a brand-new one, a human joint with arthritis, contractures, swelling, or muscle tightness will have a *reduced* ROM. A well-maintained hinge has full, smooth, pain-free motion — this is the clinical ideal for a healthy joint.

- A **healthy joint** = well-oiled hinge → maximum ROM.
- An **injured/arthritic joint** = rusted hinge → reduced ROM.
- **Measuring** this motion accurately = analogous to using a protractor on a hinge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics Used in ROM:**
> - **Degrees (°)** for angular/rotatory joints (hinge, pivot, condyloid, ball-and-socket).
> - **Centimeters (cm)** for linear/accessory motions (joint play, translation).
> - **Standard Reference Position:** Anatomical position (standing erect, palms facing forward, feet together).

## 1.3 Joint Classification Recap (from Module 1 Foundation)

Before diving into ROM, the structural classification of joints directly dictates the *type* of motion available. ROM cannot exceed the constraints imposed by joint structure.

| Joint Type | Structural Example | Available Motion | Example Joint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synarthrosis (Fibrous) | Sutures, Gomphosis | Immovable / Negligible | Skull sutures |
| Amphiarthrosis (Cartilaginous) | Symphysis, Synchondrosis | Slightly movable | Pubic Symphysis, Intervertebral Disc |
| Diarthrosis (Synovial) | Synovial cavity + capsule | Freely movable | Shoulder, Knee, Hip |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** 95% of biomechanics and rehabilitation questions focus on **Diarthrodial (Synovial) Joints** because they are the only joints with clinically measurable, functionally significant ROM.

## 1.4 Three Categories of ROM (Clinical Distinction)

| ROM Type | Definition | Who Generates the Motion? | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active ROM (AROM)** | Movement produced voluntarily by the patient using their own muscles | Patient's own muscle contraction | Tests neuromuscular integrity, strength, willingness to move |
| **Passive ROM (PROM)** | Movement applied to the joint entirely by an external force (therapist, device, gravity) | Examiner / External force | Tests joint capsule, ligament extensibility, joint geometry |
| **Active-Assistive ROM (AAROM)** | Movement produced partially by patient, supplemented by external assistance | Patient + External force | Early rehab phase when muscle strength is insufficient |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Clinical Rule:** PROM is always equal to or greater than AROM. If AROM < PROM, the limiting factor is neuromuscular (muscle weakness, pain on contraction, neurological deficit), not the joint structure itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Determinants of ROM (The "Why" Behind Joint Limits)

The maximum theoretical ROM at any joint is dictated by **seven interacting factors**:

1. **Shape of Articulating Surfaces (Osteokinematics)**: Bony geometry (e.g., the shallow glenoid fossa of the shoulder allows 3 rotational DOF, while the deep trochlear groove of the humeroulnar joint restricts the elbow to 1 DOF).
2. **Joint Capsule**: Collagenous sleeve surrounding synovial joints; its laxity or tightness directly modifies ROM (e.g., posterior capsule tightness limits shoulder flexion).
3. **Ligaments**: Primary static restraints that halt motion at end-range to prevent dislocation (e.g., ACL limits anterior tibial translation).
4. **Muscles and Tendons**: Both agonists (move joint) and antagonists (limit motion via passive tension at end-range).
5. **Skin and Subcutaneous Tissue**: Excessive scarring, burns, or edema restricts motion.
6. **Bony Blocks**: Osteophytes, malunited fractures, or congenital fusions.
7. **Neurogenic Reflexes**: Muscle spasm, pain guarding, spasticity from UMN lesions.

## 2.2 Planes of Motion & Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

ROM is always described relative to the three cardinal anatomical planes. The number of independent rotations a joint can perform is its **Degrees of Freedom**.

| Plane of Motion | Axis of Rotation | Movement Described | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sagittal (X-axis) | Mediolateral | Flexion / Extension | Elbow flexion, Knee extension |
| Frontal / Coronal (Z-axis) | Anteroposterior | Abduction / Adduction | Shoulder abduction |
| Transverse / Horizontal (Y-axis) | Vertical (longitudinal) | Internal / External Rotation | Hip rotation, forearm pronation/supination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) Rule:**
> - **1 DOF Joint** (Hinge, Pivot): 1 plane of motion → e.g., Interphalangeal joints, Atlanto-axial.
> - **2 DOF Joint** (Condyloid, Ellipsoidal, Saddle): 2 planes of motion → e.g., Radiocarpal (wrist), 1st Carpometacarpal (thumb).
> - **3 DOF Joint** (Ball-and-Socket, Plane): 3 planes of motion → e.g., Glenohumeral (shoulder), Coxofemoral (hip).

## 2.3 Standard Goniometric Measurement Protocol

The **Universal Goniometer** is the gold-standard clinical instrument. A goniometer has three parts:
- **Stationary arm** (aligned with the proximal bone).
- **Movable arm** (aligned with the distal bone).
- **Fulcrum/Axis** (placed at the joint's axis of rotation).

### Measurement Procedure (5-Step Protocol):
1. **Position the patient** in the anatomical reference position (zero position).
2. **Stabilize** the proximal joint segment to prevent compensatory motion.
3. **Palpate and align** the goniometer axis over the joint's axis of rotation.
4. **Align arms** along the longitudinal axis of the proximal and distal bony landmarks.
