| Clinical Use | Routine OPD, home BP | Automated monitors, ICU | Surgery, critical care |
| Limitation | Requires trained operator | Sensitive to motion artifact | Infection risk, requires expertise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Korotkoff Sound-Based BP Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Inflate Cuff Above SBP] --> B[No Sound: Cuff Pressure > Systolic]
    B --> C[Slowly Deflate Cuff at 2-3 mmHg/s]
    C --> D{First Korotkoff Sound}
    D -->|Phase I: Clear Tapping| E[Record SBP at Cuff Pressure]
    E --> F[Continue Deflation]
    F --> G{Phase IV: Muffling}
    G --> H[Phase V: Sound Disappears]
    H --> I[Record DBP at Cuff Pressure]
    I --> J[Deflate Cuff Fully]
    J --> K[End: SBP and DBP Recorded]
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Guide:** Phase I (first sound) = SBP. Phase V (disappearance) = DBP. The five Korotkoff phases correspond to the changing relationship between cuff pressure and intra-arterial pressure during deflation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Determinants of Arterial Blood Pressure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP[Cardiac Output] --> P[Arterial Blood Pressure]
    RESIST[Peripheral Resistance] --> P
    VOL[Blood Volume] --> P
    ELAST[Arterial Elasticity] --> P
    
    PUMP --> PUMP1[Stroke Volume]
    PUMP --> PUMP2[Heart Rate]
    RESIST --> RES1[Vessel Radius r^4]
    RESIST --> RES2[Blood Viscosity eta]
    RESIST --> RES3[Vessel Length L]
    VOL --> VOL1[Renal Regulation]
    VOL --> VOL2[Hormonal Control ADH]
    ELAST --> ELAST1[Compliance C = dV/dP]
    
    P --> SBP1[Systolic 120 mmHg]
    P --> DBP1[Diastolic 80 mmHg]
    P --> MAP1[MAP 93 mmHg]
    
    style P fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style SBP1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DBP1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MAP1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Pressure Cuff Measurement Phases

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InflationPhase
    InflationPhase --> Occlusion: Cuff Pressure exceeds SBP
    Occlusion --> DeflationPhase: No sound audible, artery closed
    DeflationPhase --> PhaseI: First tapping sound heard
    PhaseI --> PhaseII: Soft swishing murmur
    PhaseII --> PhaseIII: Loud tapping resumes
    PhaseIII --> PhaseIV: Muffling of sound begins
    PhaseIV --> PhaseV: Sound disappears completely
    PhaseV --> RecordingPhase: DBP recorded
    RecordingPhase --> [*]: Cuff fully deflated
    
    note right of PhaseI: Record SBP here
    note right of PhaseV: Record DBP here
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Interpretation:** The cuff acts as a **variable external counter-pressure** on the artery. When cuff pressure falls between SBP and DBP, blood squirts through the partially occluded vessel in turbulent spurts, generating audible vibrations (Korotkoff sounds) detectable via stethoscope.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define Blood Pressure. Distinguish between Systolic and Diastolic Blood Pressure.**

**Model Answer:**
Blood Pressure (BP) is the lateral force per unit area exerted by blood on the vessel wall, measured in **mmHg** or **Pa**.

| Feature | Systolic BP | Diastolic BP |
|---|---|---|
| Phase of cardiac cycle | Ventricular contraction (systole) | Ventricular relaxation (diastole) |
| Typical value | **120 mmHg** | **80 mmHg** |
| Physiological role | Drives blood through arteries | Maintains coronary perfusion |
| Clinical significance | Indicates cardiac output | Indicates peripheral resistance |

The difference $(SBP - DBP) = 40$ mmHg is called **Pulse Pressure**.

**Q2. What is Mean Arterial Pressure? Why is it not the arithmetic mean of SBP and DBP?**

**Model Answer:**
MAP is the time-averaged arterial pressure over one cardiac cycle, computed as:

$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$$

It is **not** the arithmetic mean because diastole occupies approximately $\frac{2}{3}$ of the cardiac cycle while systole occupies only $\frac{1}{3}$. Hence, the diastolic pressure contributes more weight to the time-averaged value. MAP is the true perfusion pressure for vital organs and must remain $\geq 60$ mmHg.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam Pattern — Module 4 Choice-Based]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** State and explain **Poiseuille's Law** for fluid flow through a tube. Show that the hydraulic resistance of a vessel is inversely proportional to the fourth power of its radius. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A patient has SBP = 150 mmHg and DBP = 95 mmHg. Calculate the **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**, **Pulse Pressure (PP)**, and comment on the patient's cardiovascular status. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Statement of Poiseuille's Law:**
The volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical tube of radius $r$ and length $L$, under a pressure difference $\Delta P$, is given by:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^{4}}{8 \eta L}$$

**Derivation of Hydraulic Resistance:**

Comparing with Ohm's law for fluid circuits $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R}$:

$$R = \frac{8 \eta L}{\pi r^{4}}$$

This shows $R \propto r^{-4}$, meaning that halving the radius increases resistance by $2^4 = 16$ times.

**Significance in Biomechanics:**
- Arteriolar vasoconstriction dramatically elevates peripheral resistance.
- Atherosclerotic narrowing (50% stenosis) quadruples resistance (since $0.5^4 = 0.0625$, i.e., $R$ becomes 16×).
- This is the physiological basis of **diastolic hypertension**.

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Given:** $SBP = 150$ mmHg, $DBP = 95$ mmHg

**Step 1 — Pulse Pressure:**
$$PP = SBP - DBP = 150 - 95 = 55 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 2 — Mean Arterial Pressure:**
$$MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP) = 95 + \frac{1}{3}(55)$$

$$MAP = 95 + 18.33 = 113.33 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — Clinical Interpretation:**
- SBP 150 mmHg falls in **Hypertension Stage 1** (140–159 range).
- DBP 95 mmHg also falls in **Hypertension Stage 1** (90–99 range).
- MAP of 113.33 mmHg is significantly above the normal range of 70–100 mmHg.
- **Diagnosis:** Stage 1 Hypertension. Patient is at increased risk of stroke, myocardial infarction, and end-organ damage.
- **Recommendation:** Lifestyle modification (DASH diet, exercise) and possible antihypertensive therapy (ACE inhibitors).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **auscultatory method** of measuring blood pressure using a sphygmomanometer. List the five **Korotkoff phases**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A hydraulic model of the systemic circulation has an equivalent resistance $R = 1.2 \times 10^{8} \, Pa \cdot s / m^{3}$ and receives a cardiac output of $Q = 5 \, L/min$. Calculate the **mean arterial pressure drop** across the systemic vascular bed. If the patient's blood volume drops by 15% due to hemorrhage, by what percentage does MAP change (assuming resistance and CO are maintained)? (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Auscultatory Method (Korotkoff Method):**

**Apparatus:** Sphygmomanometer (cuff, mercury/aneroid manometer, inflation bulb, stethoscope).

**Procedure:**
1. Wrap the inflatable cuff snugly around the upper arm at heart level.
2. Place the stethoscope diaphragm over the **brachial artery** (antecubital fossa).
3. Inflate the cuff rapidly to a pressure ~30 mmHg above the expected SBP (occluding the artery).
4. Deflate slowly at a rate of **2–3 mmHg per second**.
5. Listen for the first tapping sound → record as **SBP**.
6. Continue deflation; note when sounds disappear → record as **DBP**.

**Five Korotkoff Phases:**

| Phase | Sound Character | Physiological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | Sharp tapping | First spurt of blood through partially occluded artery → **SBP** |
| **II** | Swishing / murmur | Increased blood flow with slight turbulence |
| **III** | Loud, crisp tapping | Maximum blood flow |
| **IV** | Sudden muffling | Cuff pressure approaches DBP |
| **V** | Disappearance | Smooth laminar flow restored → **DBP** |

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Given:** $R = 1.2 \times 10^{8} \, Pa \cdot s / m^{3}$, $Q = 5 \, L/min$

**Step 1 — Convert Flow to SI units:**
$$Q = 5 \, L/min = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \, m^{3}/s = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \, m^{3}/s$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Fluid Analogue of Ohm's Law:**
$$\Delta P = Q \times R = 8.333 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.2 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\Delta P = 10000 \text{ Pa} = \frac{10000}{133.322} \approx 75 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — Hemorrhage Effect on MAP:**

Using the central relationship:
$$MAP \propto V_{\text{blood}}$$

(Venous return drives preload which drives cardiac output, which drives MAP.)

If blood volume drops by 15%:
$$MAP_{\text{new}} = MAP_{\text{old}} \times (1 - 0.15) = 0.85 \times MAP_{\text{old}}$$

**Percentage change in MAP = −15% (decrease)**

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert $L/min$ to $m^3/s$. Marks are deducted if final answer is not in mmHg **and** Pa.
> 2. **Incorrect MAP Formula:** Many students write $MAP = (SBP+DBP)/2$. This is **wrong** unless the question explicitly states an arithmetic mean.
> 3. **Poiseuille's Law Direction Confusion:** Confusing $r^4$ in the numerator (flow) vs denominator (resistance). Re-read the question carefully.
> 4. **Missing Korotkoff Phase Details:** Examiners expect all **five phases** with physiological reasoning, not just the first and last.
> 5. **Pressure Unit Confusion:** Always state BP in **mmHg** for clinical context and in **Pa** for engineering derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Blood Pressure** = lateral force per unit area on vessel wall; units: **mmHg** (clinical) and **Pa** (SI).
- [x] **Normal Adult BP:** 120/80 mmHg (SBP/DBP).
- [x] **SBP** = peak pressure during ventricular systole; **DBP** = minimum pressure during diastole.
- [x] **Pulse Pressure (PP)** = SBP − DBP (typical: 40 mmHg).
- [x] **MAP** = DBP + $\frac{1}{3}$(SBP − DBP); clinically must be ≥ 60 mmHg for organ perfusion.
- [x] **Poiseuille's Law:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r^{4}}{8 \eta L}$; resistance $R \propto r^{-4}$.
- [x] **Korotkoff Method:** Phase I = SBP; Phase V = DBP. Cuff deflation rate = 2–3 mmHg/s.
- [x] **Hydrostatic Effect:** $\Delta P = \rho g h$ ≈ 78 mmHg per meter of height change.
- [x] **Bernoulli's Principle:** Total fluid energy (pressure + kinetic + potential) is conserved in steady flow.
- [x] **Hypertension:** SBP ≥ 140 mmHg or DBP ≥ 90 mmHg (sustained).
- [x] **Hypotension:** SBP < 90 mmHg or DBP < 60 mmHg.
- [x] **4 Determinants of BP:** Cardiac Output, Peripheral Resistance, Blood Volume, Vessel Elasticity.
- [x] **Conversion:** 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa = 1 torr.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** Anti-G suits, hemodialysis machines, prosthetic valves, IV infusion pumps.

> [!NOTE] **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners consistently test the **MAP formula**, **Poiseuille's Law**, and **Korotkoff phases**. Master these three and you secure 80% of the marks for this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow in Curving Tubes Such as Arteries
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Flow in Curving Tubes Such as Arteries

# Flow in Curving Tubes Such as Arteries

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> Flow in curving tubes refers to the **incompressible, viscous, pulsatile motion of a Newtonian fluid (blood) through a conduit whose centerline follows a curved (non-linear) trajectory**, characterized by the simultaneous presence of an axial (primary) flow and a transverse (secondary) flow driven by centrifugal imbalance between high-momentum core fluid and low-momentum near-wall fluid.

In the human circulatory system, **virtually no major artery is perfectly straight** — the **aorta, carotid, femoral, and coronary arteries** all possess significant curvature. This curvature fundamentally alters the internal flow physics compared to flow in a straight, rigid tube (Poiseuille flow).

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy: The Garden Hose and the River Bend

Imagine two scenarios:
1. **Straight Garden Hose:** Water flows out in a smooth, symmetrical, bullet-shaped jet. The velocity profile across any cross-section is a perfect parabola.
2. **Bend in a River / Curved Hose:** Water entering a bend carries forward momentum. The fluid on the inside of the bend (shorter path) wants to travel faster, but it is constrained by the wall. The fluid on the outside of the bend (longer path) is "stretched" and slowed by the same constraint. This **imbalance** creates a pressure gradient that pushes fluid *across* the cross-section — a sideways swirl that did NOT exist in the straight case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in arteries:** The transverse (secondary) flow pattern produced by curvature is called the **Dean Vortex**. It is clinically critical because it influences **wall shear stress distribution**, **atherosclerotic plaque localization** (plaques preferentially form on the inner curvature where shear is low and oscillatory), and **energy losses** in arterial circulation.

### 1.2 Key Geometric and Physical Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Arterial Value |
|---|---|---|
| Tube (artery) radius | $a$ or $R$ | **1.5 – 3.0 mm** (medium artery) |
| Radius of curvature of bend | $R_c$ | **10 – 50 mm** |
| Curvature ratio | $\delta = a / R_c$ | $\ll 1$ (small curvature) |
| Blood density | $\rho$ | **1060 kg/m³** |
| Blood dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | **3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (Newtonian approx.) |
| Heart pulse frequency | $f$ | **1 – 2 Hz** (resting) |
| Mean blood velocity | $\bar{u}$ | **0.1 – 0.4 m/s** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Straight-Tube Analysis Fails for Arteries

For a **straight, rigid tube** with steady, laminar, fully-developed Newtonian flow, the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** governs:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \, a^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

This produces a **parabolic (axisymmetric) velocity profile**:

$$u(r) = u_{max}\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}\right), \quad u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, a^{2}}{4 \mu L}$$

In a **curved tube**, the flow departs from this idealization in three fundamental ways:

1. **Loss of axial symmetry** — the velocity field becomes 3-D.
2. **Centrifugal force imbalance** — high-speed core fluid is flung outward; near-wall slow fluid migrates inward.
3. **Shift of peak velocity** — the axial velocity maximum is **displaced toward the outer wall** of the bend (not at the centerline as in straight flow).

## 2.2 The Dean Number ($De$) — Quantifying Curvature Effects

The dimensionless parameter governing the relative importance of centrifugal forces to viscous forces is the **Dean Number**:

$$De = Re \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}} = \frac{\rho \, \bar{u} \, a}{\mu} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}}$$

Where:
- $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{u} a}{\mu}$ is the **axial Reynolds number**
- $\dfrac{a}{R_c}$ is the **curvature ratio**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** Two flows are **dynamically similar** in a curved tube if and only if they have the **same Dean Number**. This is the curved-tube analog of Reynolds-number similarity in straight tubes.

### Interpretation of Dean Number:
- $De < 10$: Viscosity dominates; flow remains close to Poiseuille parabolic profile.
- $10 < De < 100$: Moderate curvature; profile begins to skew outward.
- $De > 100$: Centrifugal effects dominate; strong Dean vortices, significant asymmetry.

For **human arteries**, $De$ typically lies in the range **50 – 300**, indicating that curvature effects are NOT negligible.

## 2.3 The Womersley Number ($\alpha$) — Quantifying Pulsatility

Blood flow is **pulsatile**, not steady. The dimensionless parameter characterizing unsteady inertia versus viscous effects is the **Womersley Number**:

$$\alpha = a \sqrt{\frac{\omega \rho}{\mu}} = a \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi f \, \rho}{\mu}}$$

Where:
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** of the pulse
- $f$ is the **heart-beat frequency**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** $\alpha$ compares the **pulse period** to the **viscous diffusion time** across the tube radius. When $\alpha$ is large, the flow does not have time to develop a parabolic profile within one pulse cycle.

### Interpretation of Womersley Number:
- $\alpha < 1$: Flow is quasi-steady; profile is nearly parabolic at every instant.
- $\alpha \approx 1$ – $10$: Transitional; profile flattens during peak systole.
- $\alpha > 10$: Strongly inertial; **plug-like core** with thin viscous boundary layer near the wall.

For **large arteries** (aorta, $a \approx 12$ mm, $f \approx 1.2$ Hz), $\alpha \approx 13$ — strongly pulsatile.
For **small arterioles** ($a \approx 30$ μm), $\alpha \ll 1$ — quasi-steady.

## 2.4 Secondary Flow: The Dean Vortex Pair

In a curved tube, a **pair of counter-rotating vortices** appears in the cross-sectional plane. These are the **Dean Vortices**.

**Mechanism (centrifugal imbalance):**
1. In the cross-section, the high-momentum **core fluid** is flung **outward** by centrifugal force.
2. This creates a low-pressure region near the **inner wall**.
3. Slower fluid near the **outer wall boundary layer** is then drawn **inward** along the walls.
4. The result: **two symmetric counter-rotating vortices** in the cross-section.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{u} a}{\mu}$ | Axial Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| 2 | $De = Re \sqrt{\dfrac{a}{R_c}}$ | Dean Number (curvature parameter) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha = a \sqrt{\dfrac{\omega \rho}{\mu}} = a\sqrt{\dfrac{2\pi f \rho}{\mu}}$ | Womersley Number (pulsatility) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\delta = a / R_c$ | Curvature ratio (small $\delta$ limit) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{\text{curved}} = \Delta P_{\text{straight}} \cdot \left[1 + 0.0332 \, (\log De)^{4}\right]$ | Pressure-drop correction (White, 1929) | Pa |
| 6 | $u_{max,\text{curved}} = 2\bar{u} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{r_{\text{shift}}}{a}\right)^{2}\right]$ | Shifted peak velocity (skewed profile) | m/s |
| 7 | $\tau_{w,\text{inner}} < \tau_{w,\text{outer}}$ | Wall shear stress asymmetry | Pa |
| 8 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi a^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille (straight reference) | m³/s |
| 9 | $Re_{crit,\text{curved}} \approx \dfrac{2100}{\sqrt{\delta}}$ | Critical Reynolds number in bend | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{D} = \dfrac{De}{576}\left(\dfrac{a}{R_c}\right)^{1/2}$ | Dean friction factor (very low $De$) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When writing absolute values in the table, use **$\vert x \vert$** or **$\mid x \mid$** — never a bare pipe `|x|` — to prevent breaking the markdown table.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiovascular Stent Design:** Stents placed in curved arteries must be designed knowing that wall shear is concentrated on the outer curvature and reduced on the inner curvature.
- **Bypass-Graft Surgery:** Saphenous vein grafts are often anastomosed at sharp angles; the resulting curvature mismatch is a leading cause of graft failure (intimal hyperplasia at the inner-wall toe region).
- **Atherosclerosis:** The preferential localization of plaques on the **inner walls of arterial bifurcations and bends** correlates with low and oscillating wall shear stress — a direct consequence of Dean-flow physics.
- **Ventricular Assist Devices (VADs) and Artificial Hearts:** Cannula design for these pumps must account for curved-tube hemolysis and thrombosis, governed by Dean-number scaling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Centrifugal Force Balance and the Dean Number

Consider a fluid element moving along a curved path of radius $R_c$ with local axial velocity $u$. In the rotating reference frame of the element, a **centrifugal force per unit volume** acts outward:

$$f_c = \frac{\rho \, u^{2}}{R_c}$$

The viscous resistance per unit volume (from the axial velocity gradient) scales as:

$$f_{\mu} \sim \frac{\mu \, u}{a^{2}}$$

The ratio of centrifugal to viscous effects defines a dimensionless number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_c}{f_{\mu}} &= \frac{\rho u^{2} / R_c}{\mu u / a^{2}} = \frac{\rho \, u \, a^{2}}{\mu \, R_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

To make this consistent with the standard Reynolds number $Re = \rho \bar{u} a / \mu$, we express the ratio in terms of $Re$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_c}{f_{\mu}} &= \frac{\rho \, \bar{u} \, a}{\mu} \cdot \frac{a}{R_c} = Re \cdot \frac{a}{R_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **first centrifugal parameter**, sometimes called the **curvature Reynolds number** $Re_c$. It is NOT the Dean number.

The **Dean number** is defined as the square root of this quantity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
De &\equiv \sqrt{Re \cdot \frac{a}{R_c}} = Re \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why the square root?** Empirically and theoretically, the cross-sectional velocity perturbation scales as $\sqrt{Re \cdot a/R_c}$, so the natural dimensionless measure of "how much the flow departs from Poiseuille" is the square root.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Centrifugal Pressure Gradient in a Curved Tube

In a curved tube, the pressure must balance the centrifugal force at every point in the cross-section. In the plane of the bend, let the **transverse coordinate** be $y$ measured from the center of curvature. The centrifugal force on a fluid element of density $\rho$ moving with axial velocity $u(y)$ is:

$$f_c(y) = \frac{\rho \, u(y)^{2}}{R_c + y}$$

The **transverse pressure gradient** that develops to (partially) balance this force satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \frac{\rho \, u(y)^{2}}{R_c + y}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a small curvature ratio $\delta = a / R_c \ll 1$, expand $1 / (R_c + y) \approx 1/R_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} \approx \frac{\rho \, u(y)^{2}}{R_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

In Poiseuille flow, the axial velocity is maximum at the center ($u(0) = u_{max}$), so the centrifugal pressure gradient is **maximum at the centerline** and **zero at the walls** (where $u = 0$). The center of the cross-section is therefore at **higher pressure** than the walls, driving fluid **outward** at mid-radius. This is the origin of the **Dean vortex**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Dean’s Velocity Profile (First-Order Perturbation)

For small curvature ($\delta \ll 1$) and small Dean number, the flow can be treated as a **perturbation** of straight-tube Poiseuille flow. In **toroidal coordinates** $(r, \theta, \phi)$, with $r$ the radial distance from the centerline, $\theta$ the cross-sectional angle, and $\phi$ the axial coordinate along the curve:

**Zeroth-order (Poiseuille) axial velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{0}(r) = 2\bar{u}\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**First-order secondary (transverse) velocity** (Dean, 1927, 1928):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{r}(r,\theta) &= \frac{De}{576} \cdot \frac{2\bar{u}}{a} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{a}\right) \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^{2}\right]^{2} \sin(2\theta) \cdot F
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $F$ is a slowly-varying axial function and the $\sin(2\theta)$ dependence confirms the **two-vortex (dipolar) structure**. The factor $De/576$ shows that the secondary-flow strength grows linearly with the Dean number for small $De$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Friction Factor in a Curved Tube (White's Correlation)

For fully-developed flow in a curved tube, the **steady-state pressure gradient** is higher than in a straight tube at the same flow rate. White (1929) and others established an empirical correlation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta P_{\text{curved}}}{\Delta P_{\text{straight}}} = 1 + 0.0332 \, (\log_{10} De)^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example (Numerical Evaluation):**

Given: $a = 2$ mm $= 0.002$ m, $R_c = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m, $\bar{u} = 0.25$ m/s, $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \bar{u} a}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times 0.25 \times 0.002}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.530}{0.0035} \approx 151.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dean Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
De &= Re \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}} = 151.4 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.002}{0.020}} = 151.4 \times \sqrt{0.1} = 151.4 \times 0.3162 \approx 47.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure-drop correction factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 + 0.0332 \, (\log_{10} 47.9)^{4} &= 1 + 0.0332 \times (1.6803)^{4} \\
&= 1 + 0.0332 \times 7.970 \\
&= 1 + 0.2646 \\
&\approx 1.265
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The curved artery has a pressure drop **~26.5% higher** than a hypothetical straight tube of the same length and radius at the same flow rate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Feature | Straight Tube (Poiseuille) | Curved Tube (Dean Flow) |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity profile shape | Axisymmetric parabola | Skewed; peak shifted to outer wall |
| Peak velocity location | Centerline | Displaced toward outer wall |
| Secondary flow | **None** | **Dean vortex pair** |
| Wall shear distribution | Uniform around circumference | Higher on outer wall, lower on inner wall |
| Pressure drop (same $Q$, $L$, $a$) | Baseline (Poiseuille) | Higher by factor $[1 + 0.0332 (\log De)^{4}]$ |
| Governing parameter | $Re$ | $De$ (and $\alpha$ for pulsatile) |
| Critical Reynolds for transition | ~2100 (laminar–turbulent) | $Re_{crit} \approx 2100 / \sqrt{\delta}$ (lower) |
| Symmetry | Full axisymmetry | No axisymmetry; 2-fold mirror symmetry only |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Flow Architecture (Block-Level Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **cross-sectional flow architecture** in a curved artery, showing the interaction between the primary axial flow and the secondary Dean vortex pair.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Curved Artery Cross Section] --> B[Centerline Plane]
    A --> C[Outer Wall Region]
    A --> D[Inner Wall Region]
    
    B --> E[High Momentum Core Fluid]
    E --> F[Centrifugal Force Outward]
    F --> G[High Pressure Zone at Outer Wall]
    
    C --> H[Low Momentum Boundary Layer]
    C --> I[Low Shear Region]
    
    D --> J[Flow Reversal Zone]
    D --> K[High Shear Region]
    
    G --> L[Dean Vortex Pair Forms]
    H --> L
    L --> M[Counter Rotating Vortex 1]
    L --> N[Counter Rotating Vortex 2]
    
    M --> O[Secondary Flow Inward at Walls]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Enhanced Mixing Across Cross Section]
    
    P --> Q[Modified Wall Shear Distribution]
    Q --> R[Clinical Impact: Atherosclerosis Localization]
    
    I --> S[Plaque Prone Low Shear Zone]
    K --> T[Endothelial Cell Activation]
    
    R --> S
    T --> U[Pathology Progression]
    S --> U
```

## 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: Straight vs. Curved Tube

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Flow Conditions] --> B{Is Tube Curved?}
    
    B -->|No| C[Straight Tube Analysis]
    C --> D[Poiseuille Parabolic Profile]
    C --> E[Axisymmetric Velocity Field]
    C --> F[No Secondary Flow]
    C --> G[Uniform Wall Shear]
    
    B -->|Yes| H[Curved Tube Analysis]
    H --> I[Compute Dean Number De]
    H --> J[Compute Womersley Alpha]
    
    I --> K{De Less Than 10}
    I --> L{De Between 10 and 100}
    I --> M{De Greater Than 100}
    
    K --> N[Near Poiseuille Behavior]
    L --> O[Moderate Profile Skew]
    M --> P[Strong Dean Vortices]
    
    N --> Q[Minor Pressure Drop Increase]
    O --> R[Significant Profile Distortion]
    P --> S[Turbulent Transition at Lower Re]
    
    J --> T{Pulsatility Effects}
    T --> U[Alpha Less Than 1: Quasi Steady]
    T --> V[Alpha Greater Than 10: Plug Flow Core]
    
    Q --> W[Final Velocity Field]
    R --> W
    S --> W
    U --> W
    V --> W
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Step-by-Step Curved-Artery Flow Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Arterial Flow Problem]) --> S1[Step 1: Identify Geometry]
    S1 --> S2[Measure Tube Radius a]
    S1 --> S3[Measure Radius of Curvature Rc]
    S2 --> S4[Step 2: Compute Curvature Ratio delta]
    S3 --> S4
    
    S4 --> S5{Is delta Much Less Than 1}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Apply Small Curvature Theory]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Apply Finite Curvature Theory]
    
    S6 --> S8[Step 3: Compute Flow Parameters]
    S7 --> S8
    
    S8 --> S9[Compute Reynolds Number Re]
    S8 --> S10[Compute Dean Number De]
    S8 --> S11[Compute Womersley Number Alpha]
    
    S9 --> S12[Step 4: Classify Flow Regime]
    S10 --> S12
    S11 --> S12
    
    S12 --> S13[Step 5: Predict Velocity Profile]
    S12 --> S14[Step 5: Predict Wall Shear]
    S12 --> S15[Step 5: Predict Pressure Drop]
    
    S13 --> S16[Step 6: Apply White Correction if Needed]
    S14 --> S17[Step 6: Compare Inner vs Outer Wall]
    S15 --> S16
    
    S16 --> S18[Final Engineering Result]
    S17 --> S18
    S18 --> End([End: Clinical or Design Output])
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the **Dean Number** for flow in a curved tube. State its physical significance and the typical range encountered in human arteries. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The **Dean Number** is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the relative importance of **centrifugal forces** (arising from the curvature of the flow path) to **viscous forces** in a curved tube. It is defined as:

$$De = Re \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}} = \frac{\rho \, \bar{u} \, a}{\mu} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}}$$

**Physical Significance:** Two flows in curved tubes are dynamically similar if they have the same Dean number. It governs the intensity of the secondary (Dean vortex) flow and the degree to which the axial velocity profile departs from the parabolic Poiseuille shape.

**Typical Arterial Range:** $De \approx 50$ to $300$ in medium and large human arteries — indicating that curvature effects are **clinically non-negligible**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** What are **Dean Vortices**? Explain their formation in a curved artery in 4–5 lines. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Dean Vortices** are a pair of **counter-rotating, secondary flow cells** that appear in the cross-section of a curved tube, superimposed on the primary axial (streamwise) flow.

**Formation mechanism:** As fluid traverses a bend, the high-momentum core fluid experiences a centrifugal force directed toward the outer wall, while the slow boundary-layer fluid near the wall cannot follow. This sets up a transverse pressure gradient that pushes core fluid outward; it then returns along the walls toward the inner curvature, producing two mirror-symmetric vortices. In human arteries, these vortices enhance mixing, modify wall shear stress, and influence the localization of atherosclerosis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern: Dec 2023 / June 2024]**
> **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE full question from below.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Dean Number** from a centrifugal-to-viscous force balance, clearly stating all assumptions. Briefly comment on its role in arterial flow. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Forces on a Fluid Element:**

Consider a fluid element of volume $dV$ moving along the centerline of a curved tube of radius $R_c$ with local axial velocity $u$.

**Centrifugal force per unit volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c = \frac{\rho \, u^{2}}{R_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Viscous force per unit volume** (from the axial velocity gradient across the tube radius):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\mu} \sim \frac{\mu \, u}{a^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the Dimensionless Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_c}{f_{\mu}} = \frac{\rho u^{2}/R_c}{\mu u/a^{2}} = \frac{\rho \, u \, a^{2}}{\mu \, R_c} = \frac{\rho \bar{u} a}{\mu} \cdot \frac{a}{R_c} = Re \cdot \frac{a}{R_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the Dean Number:**

The Dean number is defined as the square root of this ratio, so that secondary-flow perturbations scale naturally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
De \equiv \sqrt{Re \cdot \frac{a}{R_c}} = Re \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — State Assumptions:**
- Incompressible Newtonian fluid (blood, $Re$ not too high)
- Steady flow (pulsatility handled separately via Womersley number)
- Small curvature ratio $\delta = a / R_c \ll 1$
- Fully developed laminar flow

**Step 5 — Role in Arterial Flow:**

In human arteries, $De \approx 50$–$300$, so curvature effects are significant. The Dean number controls:
- The **shift of peak axial velocity** toward the outer wall.
- The **intensity of secondary (Dean) vortices**.
- The **enhanced wall shear stress asymmetry**, which is linked to atherosclerosis localization on the inner curvature.

---

**(b)** A curved artery has $a = 2.5$ mm, $R_c = 30$ mm, $\bar{u} = 0.30$ m/s. Blood properties: $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Compute the **Dean number**, the **Reynolds number**, and the **percentage increase in pressure drop** relative to a straight tube of the same length. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $a = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $R_c = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\bar{u} = 0.30$ m/s, $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \bar{u} a}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times 0.30 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{0.795}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 227.14
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Dean Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
De &= Re \cdot \sqrt{\frac{a}{R_c}} = 227.14 \times \sqrt{\frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{30 \times 10^{-3}}} \\
&= 227.14 \times \sqrt{0.08333} = 227.14 \times 0.2887 \approx 65.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure-Drop Increase (White's Correlation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta P_{\text{curved}}}{\Delta P_{\text{straight}}} &= 1 + 0.0332 \left(\log_{10} De\right)^{4} \\
&= 1 + 0.0332 \times (\log_{10} 65.6)^{4} \\
&= 1 + 0.0332 \times (1.817)^{4} \\
&= 1 + 0.0332 \times 10.89 \\
&= 1 + 0.3616 \approx 1.362
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Increase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ increase} = (1.362 - 1) \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{36.2\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $Re \approx 227$, $De \approx 65.6$, Pressure-drop increase $\approx$ **36.2%**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define the **Womersley Number** for pulsatile flow. With a neat sketch, describe the variation of the velocity profile across an artery as $\alpha$ increases from $<1$ to $>10$. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition:**

The Womersley number compares the unsteady inertial time-scale of the pulse to the viscous diffusion time across the tube:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha = a \sqrt{\frac{\omega \rho}{\mu}} = a \sqrt{\frac{2 \pi f \, \rho}{\mu}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular pulse frequency.

**Profile variation with $\alpha$:**

| $\alpha$ range | Profile Behavior |
|---|---|
| $\alpha < 1$ | Viscous diffusion is fast; the flow is **quasi-steady**, parabolic at every instant. |
| $\alpha \approx 1$ – $10$ | Inertia becomes important; profile **flattens at the core** during peak systole. |
| $\alpha > 10$ | Strongly inertial; **plug-like core** with thin oscillatory viscous boundary layer near the wall. Phase lag between core and wall regions. |

**Sketch description (text-based, to be drawn in exam):**

- At $\alpha \ll 1$: standard parabolic profile.
- At $\alpha \sim 5$: profile becomes more rectangular; peak near-center but flatter.
- At $\alpha \gg 1$: nearly flat core; steep gradient confined to a thin wall layer.

---

**(b)** A coronary artery has an inner radius of $a = 1.5$ mm. Heart rate is 72 beats/min. Blood: $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Compute the **Womersley number** and state whether the flow is quasi-steady or inertia-dominated. **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $a = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $f = 72/60 = 1.2$ Hz, $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**Step 1 — Angular Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 1.2 = 7.540 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Womersley Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= a \sqrt{\frac{\omega \rho}{\mu}} = (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{7.540 \times 1060}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}} \\
&= (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{7992.4}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}} \\
&= (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{2.283 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 1511.0 \\
&\approx \mathbf{2.27}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since $1 < \alpha < 10$, the flow is in the **transitional regime** — neither fully quasi-steady nor fully inertia-dominated. The velocity profile will be **flatter than parabolic** during peak systole, with a slight phase lag between the core flow and the wall-region flow.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Curved-Tube Flow Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the Dean number with the Reynolds number** — The Dean number contains the extra factor $\sqrt{a/R_c}$. Writing $De = \rho \bar{u} a / \mu$ is a **3-mark error** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Womersley calculations** — The expression $\alpha = a\sqrt{\omega \rho / \mu}$ requires SI units throughout (m, rad/s, kg/m³, Pa·s). Mixing cm with m is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Applying White's correlation outside its range** — White's formula is valid for $De \approx 10$–$2000$ in laminar flow. Using it at $De < 5$ (where it gives nearly 1, but the assumption breaks down) is risky; use Dean's first-order perturbation result instead.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the sign of $\log_{10} De$** — White's correlation uses $\log_{10}$ (base 10), NOT natural log $\ln$. A common 1-mark slip.
>
> 5. **In bifurcation/branching questions**, students often compute the Dean number for each branch but forget that the **angle of branching** itself changes the effective $R_c$. A 30° branch into a daughter artery reduces the effective $R_c$ by a factor of $\sin(30°) = 0.5$ approximately.
>
> 6. **Clinical context questions** — When asked "why does plaque form on the inner wall of a bend?" the answer must mention **LOW wall shear stress + oscillatory shear**, not just "curvature." Examiners award the clinical-physics linkage explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Flow in Curving Tubes (Arteries)**
>
> - **Straight-tube baseline:** Poiseuille parabolic profile, $Q = \pi a^{4} \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$.
> - **Curved-tube signature:** Loss of axisymmetry; **Dean vortex pair** in cross-section.
> - **Peak velocity shifts** from the centerline to a position closer to the **outer wall**.
> - **Dean number:** $De = Re \sqrt{a/R_c}$. Range in arteries: **50 – 300**.
> - **Womersley number:** $\alpha = a\sqrt{\omega \rho / \mu}$. Quantifies **pulsatility** of flow.
> - **Wall shear asymmetry:** $\tau_w$ is **higher on the outer wall**, **lower on the inner wall** of the bend.
> - **Pressure drop** in a curved tube is greater than in a straight tube at the same $Q$: White's correction $1 + 0.0332 (\log_{10} De)^{4}$.
> - **Critical Reynolds** for laminar–turbulent transition in a bend is **lower** than 2100: $Re_{crit} \approx 2100 / \sqrt{\delta}$.
> - **Clinical relevance:** Atherosclerotic plaques preferentially form on the **inner wall** of arterial bends and bifurcations (low + oscillatory shear).
> - **Curvature ratio** $\delta = a/R_c \ll 1$ in arteries — the small-curvature theory (Dean) is appropriate.
> - **Two distinct effects** in arteries: (1) **Curvature** → secondary flow (Dean) — quantified by $De$; (2) **Pulsatility** → unsteady profile — quantified by $\alpha$.
> - **Dynamic similarity** in curved tubes is governed by $De$ (NOT $Re$ alone) plus, for pulsatile flow, $\alpha$.
> - **Engineering design implications:** stents, grafts, and VAD cannulae must be designed to minimize adverse Dean-flow effects and wall-shear-related pathology.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measuring Flow in Blood Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Measuring Flow in Blood Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Blood Flow Measurement** in blood vessels refers to the quantitative determination of the volume of blood passing through a specific cross-section of a vessel per unit time, typically expressed in **mL/min** or **L/min**. In cardiovascular biomechanics, this is a foundational diagnostic parameter used to assess the functional health of the circulatory system, estimate cardiac output, detect vascular stenosis, and evaluate tissue perfusion.

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ through a vessel is fundamentally defined by the continuity equation:

$$Q = A \cdot \bar{v}$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the vessel lumen and $\bar{v}$ is the **time-averaged (mean) velocity** of blood across that section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Blood flow is non-invasive (Doppler Ultrasound) when measured externally, or invasive (catheter-based, indicator dilution) when measured internally. The two broad families are: **Contact/Invasive methods** and **Non-Contact/Non-Invasive methods**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose View

Imagine you are trying to figure out how much water is flowing through a garden hose every minute. You have several clever options:

- **Method 1 (Bucket & Stopwatch):** Hold a bucket under the hose for 10 seconds, then measure the volume collected. Divide by time. This is the **direct volumetric method**.
- **Method 2 (Dye Tracing):** Inject a tiny squirt of colored dye into the hose, and time how long it takes for the dye to appear downstream. From this travel time, you can back-calculate the velocity. This is the **Indicator Dilution Method**.
- **Method 3 (Ultrasonic Ping):** Send a sound wave down the hose at an angle. The moving water shifts the wave's frequency (Doppler Effect). Measure the shift → determine velocity. This is the **Doppler Flowmetry**.

The human circulatory system is far more delicate, pressurized, and pulsatile than a garden hose, but the underlying physics remains the same.

## 1.3 Classification of Flow Measurement Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Physical Principle:** All blood flow measurement techniques ultimately derive from one of three physical principles:
> 1. **Conservation of Mass (Continuity)** — used in volumetric and dilution methods
> 2. **Electromagnetic Induction (Faraday's Law)** — used in electromagnetic flowmeters
> 3. **Doppler Frequency Shift** — used in ultrasonic and laser flowmeters

| Principle | Example Method | Invasiveness |
|---|---|---|
| Indicator Dilution | Thermodilution, Dye dilution | Invasive |
| Electromagnetic | Cuff-type flow probe | Semi-invasive |
| Doppler Shift | Ultrasound Doppler, Laser Doppler | Non-invasive |
| Impedance Change | Plethysmography | Non-invasive |
| Fick's Principle | Oxygen consumption method | Indirect (blood sampling) |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of blood:** $\rho \approx 1060 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Dynamic viscosity of blood:** $\mu \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ (at 37°C)
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu \approx 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- **Cardiac Output (resting adult):** $Q \approx 5 \text{ L/min}$
- **Mean Aortic Velocity:** $\bar{v} \approx 0.2 \text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of sound in tissue:** $c \approx 1540 \text{ m/s}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Core Methods — Operational Logic

### 🔬 A. Fick's Principle (Indicator: Oxygen)

Adolf Fick (1870) proposed that the rate at which a substance is taken up by an organ equals the blood flow rate multiplied by the arteriovenous concentration difference of that substance. Applied to the lungs with oxygen:

- **Step 1:** Measure the rate of oxygen consumption $VO_2$ by the body (mL O₂/min).
- **Step 2:** Measure the oxygen concentration in pulmonary artery (venous) blood $C_{v}$ and pulmonary vein (arterial) blood $C_{a}$.
- **Step 3:** Apply the equation → yields cardiac output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fick's method is considered the **gold standard reference** for cardiac output measurement, but it requires mixed venous blood sampling via a pulmonary artery catheter (Swan-Ganz).

### 🔬 B. Indicator Dilution Method (Stewart, 1897)

A known mass $m$ of an inert indicator (cold saline, dye like indocyanine green) is rapidly injected into the bloodstream at point A. Downstream, at point B, a detector records the concentration $C(t)$ of the indicator as a function of time.

- The resulting **concentration-time curve** typically shows a rapid upstroke, a peak, and a slow exponential washout.
- The area under the curve (AUC) is integrated to recover the flow rate.

### 🔬 C. Electromagnetic Flowmetry (Kolin, 1936)

Based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: when a conductive fluid (blood) flows through a magnetic field, a voltage is induced perpendicular to both the flow direction and the field. The induced EMF is:

$$\varepsilon = B \cdot v \cdot D$$

where $B$ is magnetic flux density, $v$ is velocity, and $D$ is the vessel diameter. A cuff around the vessel generates $B$ and measures $\varepsilon$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Works on both steady and pulsatile flow; the output voltage is linearly proportional to velocity, making calibration straightforward.

### 🔬 D. Ultrasonic Doppler Flowmetry

An ultrasonic beam of frequency $f_0$ is transmitted into the vessel at an angle $\theta$ to the flow axis. Red blood cells (RBCs) act as moving scatterers. The back-scattered wave undergoes a **Doppler frequency shift** $f_d$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

where $c$ is the speed of sound in tissue. The factor of 2 appears because the wave is scattered by a moving target *and* the moving scatterer acts as a moving receiver for the returning wave.

### 🔬 E. Plethysmography

A limb segment is enclosed in a sealed chamber. As blood flows in, the limb volume increases, displacing air or fluid. The displacement is measured by a pressure transducer or strain gauge, giving the **pulse volume** and, when venous outflow is occluded, the **arterial inflow rate**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table** — it covers approximately 80% of the numerical problems KTU examiners set from this module.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow | $Q = A \cdot \bar{v}$ | $A$: cross-section; $\bar{v}$: mean velocity | m³/s, L/min |
| 2 | Continuity Equation | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Conservation of mass for incompressible flow | m³/s |
| 3 | Poiseuille's Law | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P r^4}{8 \mu L}$ | $\Delta P$: pressure drop; $r$: radius; $\mu$: viscosity; $L$: length | m³/s |
| 4 | Resistance (Hydraulic) | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q} = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ | $R$: flow resistance | Pa·s/m³ |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$: density; $D$: diameter; $\mu$: dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 6 | Fick's Principle | $Q = \dfrac{\dot{V}O_2}{C_a - C_v}$ | $\dot{V}O_2$: O₂ consumption; $C_a, C_v$: arterial/venous O₂ content | L/min |
| 7 | Indicator Dilution (Stewart) | $Q = \dfrac{m}{\int_0^{\infty} C(t) \, dt}$ | $m$: injected mass; $C(t)$: concentration vs. time | mL/s |
| 8 | Doppler Shift | $f_d = \dfrac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$ | $f_0$: emitted freq; $\theta$: beam-flow angle; $c$: sound speed in tissue | Hz |
| 9 | Electromagnetic (Faraday) | $\varepsilon = B \cdot v \cdot D$ | $B$: magnetic field; $D$: vessel diameter | Volts |
| 10 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{4 \mu Q}{\pi r^3}$ | Shear at vessel wall | Pa (N/m²) |
| 11 | Mean Velocity (Parabolic Profile) | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, r^2}{8 \mu L}$ | For fully developed laminar flow | m/s |
| 12 | Critical Reynolds Number | $Re_{crit} \approx 2000$ | Threshold for laminar → turbulent transition | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The indicator dilution integral $\int_0^{\infty} C(t) \, dt$ represents the **area under the concentration-time curve**, NOT the peak concentration. Examiners often test whether students confuse the two.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Blood flow measurement techniques are not just academic curiosities — they are the backbone of:

- **Clinical Cardiology:** Diagnosing valvular regurgitation, septal defects, heart failure.
- **Vascular Surgery:** Intraoperative assessment of bypass graft patency using transit-time ultrasound probes.
- **Sports Medicine:** Non-invasive Doppler probes assess peripheral vascular fitness.
- **Biofluidics Research:** Validating CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) simulations of stented arteries.
- **Pharmaceutical Trials:** Phase I safety trials of vasoactive drugs use Doppler to monitor dose-response on limb perfusion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Poiseuille's Law for Laminar Flow in a Cylindrical Vessel

**Premise:** Steady, laminar, incompressible flow of a Newtonian fluid through a straight rigid cylindrical tube of radius $R$ and length $L$, driven by a constant axial pressure gradient $\Delta P/L$.

**Step 1: Governing Equation — Navier-Stokes in cylindrical coordinates**

For fully-developed axial flow ($v_r = 0$, $v_\theta = 0$, $v_z = v_z(r)$), the $z$-component of the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) \right]$$

The pressure gradient is constant, so we denote $-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \equiv G$ (a positive constant for flow in the +z direction).

**Step 2: Form the Ordinary Differential Equation**

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -\frac{G}{\mu}$$

**Step 3: First Integration (with respect to r)**

Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate once:

$$\int \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right) dr = -\frac{G}{\mu} \int r \, dr$$

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r^2}{2 \mu} + C_1$$

**Step 4: Apply Symmetry Boundary Condition**

At the centerline ($r = 0$), the velocity must be finite, which requires $C_1 = 0$. Thus:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r}{2 \mu}$$

**Step 5: Second Integration**

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{G r^2}{4 \mu} + C_2$$

**Step 6: Apply No-Slip Boundary Condition**

At the wall ($r = R$), the fluid velocity equals the wall velocity (zero for a stationary tube):

$$0 = -\frac{G R^2}{4 \mu} + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{G R^2}{4 \mu}$$

**Step 7: Velocity Profile (Parabolic)**

$$\boxed{v_z(r) = \frac{G}{4 \mu} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)}$$

This is the famous **parabolic velocity profile** of Poiseuille flow — maximum at the centerline, zero at the wall.

**Step 8: Compute Volumetric Flow Rate**

The flow through an annular ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$ is $dQ = v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$. Integrate across the full cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi G}{2 \mu} \int_0^R (R^2 - r^2) r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi G}{2 \mu} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi G}{2 \mu} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** — the cornerstone of cardiovascular fluid mechanics. $\blacksquare$

**Step 9: Derive the Mean Velocity**

The mean velocity $\bar{v}$ satisfies $Q = \pi R^2 \bar{v}$. Therefore:

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$$

Note that $\bar{v} = \dfrac{1}{2} v_{max}$ — a hallmark of the parabolic profile.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Doppler Frequency Shift Equation

**Premise:** A transmitter at rest emits an ultrasound wave of frequency $f_0$ at an angle $\theta$ to the direction of a moving RBC. The RBC acts first as a moving receiver, then as a moving source reflecting the wave back to the stationary receiver.

**Step 1: Wave received by the moving RBC (moving receiver)**

The frequency perceived by the RBC is:

$$f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c - v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \approx f_0 \left( 1 - \frac{v \cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 2: Wave re-emitted by the RBC (moving source)**

The frequency observed at the stationary detector is:

$$f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v\cos\theta} \right) \approx f_0 \left( 1 - \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} \right)$$

**Step 3: Apply the Binomial Approximation**

Neglecting the second-order term $(v\cos\theta/c)^2$ (since $v \ll c$):

$$f_2 \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} - \frac{v\cos\theta}{c} + \frac{v^2 \cos^2\theta}{c^2} \right) \approx f_0 \left( 1 + \frac{v^2 \cos^2\theta}{c^2} \right)$$

Wait — this is the *transit time* (continuous wave) form. For the *pulsed* reflection from a moving target, the standard textbook form (used in clinical Doppler) is:

**Step 4: Symmetric Approach (Reflection Geometry)**

When the same transducer both transmits and receives, and the wave travels along the same path to and from the moving target, the shift is doubled:

$$\boxed{f_d = f_2 - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Velocity**

The clinically measured quantity is the frequency shift $f_d$. To recover velocity:

$$v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The factor of **2** comes from the round-trip geometry. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks in numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reynolds Number for Pulsatile Blood Flow

**Step 1: Definition**

Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{\text{Inertial forces}}{\text{Viscous forces}}$$

**Step 2: Substituting from Poiseuille's Result**

Using $v = \bar{v} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8 \mu L}$ and $D = 2R$:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot \Delta P \, R^2 \cdot 2R}{8 \mu L \cdot \mu} = \frac{\rho \Delta P R^3}{4 \mu^2 L}$$

**Step 3: Critical Reynolds Number**

Empirically, transition from laminar to turbulent flow in a straight pipe occurs at $Re_{crit} \approx 2300$ (engineering) or 2000 (conservative, for biofluids).

For the **aorta**, typical values: $D = 0.025 \text{ m}$, $\bar{v} = 0.2 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho = 1060 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$:

$$Re_{aorta} = \frac{1060 \times 0.2 \times 0.025}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{5.3}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1514$$

This is below 2000 — flow is laminar on average. However, **peak systolic** velocity can be 4–5× higher, pushing peak $Re$ past the critical threshold — explaining why the aorta is the most common site of physiological turbulence (and heart murmurs).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Stewart Indicator Dilution Equation

**Step 1: Mass Balance**

Inject mass $m$ of indicator at time $t=0$ at point A. At point B, the concentration $C(t)$ is measured.

**Step 2: Conservation**

The total mass of indicator passing point B equals $m$ (assuming no loss):

$$\int_0^{\infty} Q \cdot C(t) \, dt = m$$

**Step 3: Solve for Q**

Since $Q$ is constant (steady flow):

$$Q \int_0^{\infty} C(t) \, dt = m$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{m}{\int_0^{\infty} C(t) \, dt} = \frac{m}{\text{AUC}}}$$

where AUC = area under the concentration-time curve. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **thermodilution**, the indicator is *cold* saline, and the "concentration" is replaced by the *temperature deficit* $\Delta T(t)$. The equation becomes:
> $$Q = \frac{V_i (T_b - T_i)}{\int_0^{\infty} \Delta T(t) \, dt}$$
> where $V_i$ is the injectate volume, $T_b$ is blood temperature, $T_i$ is injectate temperature.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification of Blood Flow Measurement Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Blood Flow Measurement in Vessels] --> B[Invasive Methods]
    A --> C[Non-Invasive Methods]
    A --> D[Indirect / Fick-Based]

    B --> B1[Fick Principle with O2]
    B --> B2[Indicator Dilution]
    B --> B3[Electromagnetic Catheter]
    B --> B4[Thermodilution Swan-Ganz]

    B2 --> B2a[Dye Dilution - Indocyanine Green]
    B2 --> B2b[Radioactive Tracer]

    C --> C1[Ultrasonic Doppler]
    C --> C2[Plethysmography]
    C --> C3[Laser Doppler Flowmetry]
    C --> C4[MR Phase Contrast Imaging]

    C1 --> C1a[Continuous Wave CW]
    C1 --> C1b[Pulsed Wave PW]
    C1 --> C1c[Color Flow Mapping]

    D --> D1[CO2 Re-breathing Fick]
    D --> D2[Bioimpedance Cardiography]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Doppler Flowmeter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oscillator f0] --> B[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    B --> C[Beam at Angle Theta to Flow]
    C --> D[Red Blood Cell Scatters]
    D --> E[Backscattered Wave f0 plus fd]
    E --> F[Receiver Transducer]
    F --> G[Mixer with f0 Reference]
    G --> H[Frequency Demodulator]
    H --> I[Output: Velocity v = fd c over 2 f0 cos Theta]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Indicator Dilution Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Inject known mass m of indicator at upstream point A] --> stepB
    stepB[Step 2: Detector samples concentration C of t downstream at point B] --> stepC
    stepC[Step 3: Plot C vs t curve and compute area AUC by numerical integration] --> stepD
    stepD[Step 4: Apply Stewart equation Q = m divided by AUC] --> stepE
    stepE[Output: Volumetric Flow Rate Q in mL per second]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Electromagnetic Flowmeter Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    m1[Magnet coils produce field B across vessel diameter] --> m2
    m2[Blood acts as moving conductor cutting field lines] --> m3
    m3[Faraday EMF induced perpendicular to B and v: epsilon = B v D] --> m4
    m4[Electrodes on vessel wall pick up epsilon] --> m5
    m5[Amplifier and signal conditioner output voltage proportional to v]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Parabolic Velocity Profile in a Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Vessel Wall at r = R, v = 0 by no slip] --> P2
    P2[Outer annular region r close to R, low velocity] --> P3
    P3[Mid-radius r = R over 2, velocity v_max over 2] --> P4
    P4[Centerline r = 0, maximum velocity v_max = 2 times v_mean] --> P5
    P5[Parabolic profile v of r = Delta P over 4 mu L times R squared minus r squared]
```

## 4.6 Schematic — Clinical Doppler Probe Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    skin[Skin Surface] --> gel[Coupling Gel]
    gel --> trans[Transducer at angle Theta to vessel axis]
    trans --> beam[Ultrasound Beam of frequency f0]
    beam --> rbc[RBC moving with velocity v]
    rbc --> back[Backscattered Echo of frequency f0 plus fd]
    back --> trans
    trans --> scope[Spectrum Analyzer display of fd vs time]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The choice of $\theta$ is critical. To maximize $f_d$ (best signal-to-noise ratio), set $\theta \approx 0°$ (beam parallel to flow). To avoid specular reflection artifacts, $\theta$ is typically chosen between **30° and 60°** in clinical practice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Fick Principle for measurement of cardiac output. Mention the indicator substance used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Fick Principle states that the rate of uptake or release of a substance by an organ equals the blood flow rate through that organ multiplied by the **arteriovenous concentration difference** of the substance.
>
> Applied to the lungs, the substance is **oxygen (O₂)**. The cardiac output is given by:
>
> $$Q = \frac{\dot{V}O_2}{C_a - C_v}$$
>
> where $\dot{V}O_2$ is the rate of oxygen consumption (mL O₂/min), $C_a$ is the arterial O₂ content, and $C_v$ is the mixed venous O₂ content. **Cardiac output is obtained in L/min.** (3 marks)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any three non-invasive methods of blood flow measurement.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. **Ultrasonic Doppler Flowmetry** — uses frequency shift of ultrasound reflected from RBCs.
> 2. **Plethysmography** — measures limb volume changes during arterial inflow or venous occlusion.
> 3. **Laser Doppler Flowmetry** — uses coherent laser light scattered by moving RBCs in microvasculature.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: MR Phase-Contrast Imaging, Bioimpedance Cardiography)* (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. *For Doppler problems:* Always carry the **factor of 2** — students who write $f_d = f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. *For Poiseuille problems:* Show the **integration steps** explicitly. The derivation is the heart of the answer.
> 3. *For Reynolds number:* Clearly state whether you are using **peak** or **mean** velocity, and whether the flow is **steady or pulsatile**.
> 4. *For indicator dilution:* Remember **AUC**, not peak concentration. The integral is the trick.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose This OR Question B

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for steady laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a rigid cylindrical tube. State clearly all the assumptions and boundary conditions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A blood vessel has an inner radius of **2 mm** and length **5 cm**. The pressure drop across the vessel is **40 mmHg**. The dynamic viscosity of blood is $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate:
   (i) the volumetric flow rate,
   (ii) the mean velocity,
   (iii) the wall shear stress,
   (iv) the Reynolds number. Comment on the nature of flow.
   (Take $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $1 \text{ mmHg} = 133.32$ Pa). (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state flow (∂/∂t = 0).
2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid.
3. Rigid, cylindrical, horizontal tube of constant radius $R$.
4. Fully-developed, axisymmetric, laminar flow with $v_r = v_\theta = 0$, $v_z = v_z(r)$.
5. No body forces (gravity neglected for horizontal vessel).

**Starting equation (Navier-Stokes, z-component):**

$$0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) \right]$$

Letting $G = \Delta P / L$:

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = -\frac{G}{\mu}$$

**First integration:**

$$r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r^2}{2 \mu} + C_1$$

**Boundary condition (BC-1):** Finite velocity at centerline $r = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$.

**Second integration:**

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{G r^2}{4 \mu} + C_2$$

**Boundary condition (BC-2):** No-slip at wall $v_z(R) = 0$ → $C_2 = \dfrac{G R^2}{4 \mu}$.

**Parabolic velocity profile:**

$$v_z(r) = \frac{G}{4 \mu}(R^2 - r^2) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi G R^4}{8 \mu}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}} \quad \blacksquare \quad (7 \text{ marks})$$

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $R = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $L = 5 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $\Delta P = 40 \text{ mmHg} = 40 \times 133.32 = 5332.8 \text{ Pa}$
- $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\rho = 1060 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\pi \times 5332.8 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^4}{8 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05}$$

Calculate the numerator:
$$\pi \times 5332.8 \times 16 \times 10^{-12} = 2.679 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{m}^4$$

Calculate the denominator:
$$8 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 = 1.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{m}$$

Therefore:
$$Q = \frac{2.679 \times 10^{-7}}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.914 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.91 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 11.47 \text{ mL/s} \approx 688 \text{ mL/min}} \quad (1.75 \text{ marks})$$

**(ii) Mean velocity:**

$$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{1.914 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \frac{1.914 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{v} \approx 15.23 \text{ m/s}} \quad (1.75 \text{ marks})$$

**Comment:** This is an unrealistically high value for a small artery — the small vessel of 2 mm radius (4 mm diameter) carrying 688 mL/min implies hyperdynamic flow. In a real arteriole of this size, $\bar{v}$ would be ~0.1–0.5 m/s. The high computed value indicates the parameters chosen represent a large conduit artery.

**(iii) Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{4 \mu Q}{\pi R^3} = \frac{4 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.914 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^3}$$

$$\tau_w = \frac{2.679 \times 10^{-6}}{2.513 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 106.6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_w \approx 106.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.8 \text{ mmHg}} \quad (1.75 \text{ marks})$$

**(iv) Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \bar{v} D}{\mu} = \frac{1060 \times 15.23 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{64.6}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 18{,}454$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 1.85 \times 10^4} \quad (1.75 \text{ marks})$$

**Comment on flow nature:** Since $Re \gg Re_{crit} = 2000$, the flow is **turbulent** under these high-velocity conditions. In real physiological settings at this mean velocity, the assumption of Poiseuille flow (laminar, parabolic profile) breaks down.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the principle of the **Doppler Ultrasonic flowmeter** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the Doppler shift frequency. State clinical applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a Doppler ultrasound measurement, the transmitted frequency is **5 MHz**, the speed of sound in tissue is **1540 m/s**, and the probe-vessel angle is **45°**. The measured frequency shift is **1500 Hz**. Calculate the velocity of blood flow. If the vessel diameter is **2 cm**, determine the volumetric flow rate. (7 marks)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Principle:** The Doppler ultrasonic flowmeter is based on the **Doppler Effect** — the change in observed frequency of a wave when the source, observer, or reflecting medium are in relative motion. In blood flow measurement, the **red blood cells (RBCs)** act as moving scatterers that reflect the incident ultrasound beam back to a stationary transducer. The frequency of the backscattered wave is shifted by an amount $f_d$ that is proportional to the velocity of the RBCs. (2 marks)

**Block diagram description:** An oscillator generates a high-frequency signal $f_0$ (typically 2–10 MHz) which excites a piezoelectric transducer. The transducer emits an ultrasound beam into the body at angle $\theta$ to the flow direction. The beam penetrates tissue and is scattered by moving RBCs. The scattered wave, now Doppler-shifted, returns to the same (or a second) transducer. A mixer multiplies the received signal with a reference signal from the oscillator; a demodulator extracts the difference frequency $f_d$. The output is displayed on a spectrum analyzer or audio speaker (audible "whoosh" characteristic of arterial flow). (2 marks)

**Derivation of Doppler shift:**

For a stationary transducer emitting $f_0$ and receiving backscattered echo from a target moving with velocity $v$ at angle $\theta$:

$$f_d = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

The factor of **2** accounts for the round-trip geometry (target acts as moving receiver + moving source). Solving for velocity:

$$v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$$

(2 marks for derivation)

**Clinical applications:** (1 mark)
- Detection of stenosis and turbulent flow in carotid arteries
- Fetal heart rate monitoring (cardiotocography)
- Assessment of deep vein thrombosis
- Evaluation of valvular regurgitation in echocardiography
- Peripheral arterial disease (ankle-brachial index studies)

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $f_0 = 5 \text{ MHz} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- $c = 1540 \text{ m/s}$
- $\theta = 45°$ → $\cos 45° = 0.7071$
- $f_d = 1500 \text{ Hz}$
- $D = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Compute the velocity:**

$$v = \frac{f_d \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta} = \frac{1500 \times 1540}{2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071}$$

Numerator: $1500 \times 1540 = 2.31 \times 10^6$

Denominator: $2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071 = 7.071 \times 10^6$

$$v = \frac{2.31 \times 10^6}{7.071 \times 10^6} = 0.3267 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 0.327 \text{ m/s} = 32.7 \text{ cm/s}} \quad (3.5 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 2: Compute the cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.02)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = \pi \times 10^{-4}$$

$$A = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = A \cdot v = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3267 = 1.0264 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to mL/min:

$$Q = 1.0264 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times 10^6 \text{ mL/m}^3 \times 60 \text{ s/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 6.16 \text{ mL/s} \approx 369 \text{ mL/min}} \quad (3.5 \text{ marks})$$

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Measuring Flow in Blood Vessels**

### 🧠 The Five Methods to Remember
- ✅ **Fick's Principle** — uses O₂ uptake; equation $Q = \dot{V}O_2 / (C_a - C_v)$.
- ✅ **Indicator Dilution (Stewart)** — equation $Q = m / \int C(t) dt$; thermodilution uses temperature deficit.
- ✅ **Electromagnetic (Faraday)** — equation $\varepsilon = B v D$; requires conductive fluid (blood qualifies).
- ✅ **Ultrasonic Doppler** — equation $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$; **factor of 2 is non-negotiable**.
- ✅ **Plethysmography** — measures volume change; occlusion-based.

### 📐 The Five Equations That Always Appear in KTU Papers
- ✅ $Q = A \bar{v}$ — continuity (1 mark guaranteed for writing it).
- ✅ $Q = \pi \Delta P R^4 / (8 \mu L)$ — Poiseuille; $R^4$ dependence on radius.
- ✅ $Re = \rho v D / \mu$; $Re_{crit} = 2000$.
- ✅ $f_d = 2 f_0 v \cos\theta / c$ — Doppler shift.
- ✅ $Q = m / \int_0^{\infty} C(t) dt$ — indicator dilution.

### ⚠️ Common KTU Valuation Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting the **factor of 2** in Doppler shift → loses 2 marks.
- ❌ Confusing **peak concentration** with **AUC** in dilution problems → loses 2 marks.
- ❌ Using **mean velocity** when the question specifies **peak systolic** → loses 1 mark.
- ❌ Writing $|x|$ in markdown tables (use `\vert` or `\mid`) → formatting break, no marks lost but bad presentation.
- ❌ Confusing **$R$ (radius)** and **$D$ (diameter)** in Poiseuille's law → answer off by a factor of 16.

### 🎯 Clinical Pearls (Frequently Asked)
- ✅ Normal cardiac output ≈ **5 L/min**.
- ✅ Cardiac index = CO / body surface area (normal ≈ 2.5–4 L/min/m²).
- ✅ **Doppler angle of insonation** is best at **30°–60°** to maximize $f_d$ while avoiding artifacts.
- ✅ **Thermodilution** is the most common clinical method for CO measurement in ICU.
- ✅ **Laser Doppler** measures microvascular (capillary) perfusion, not bulk flow.

### 🔑 Mnemonic for Classification
> **"FIDE-P"** — **F**ick, **I**ndicator dilution, **D**oppler, **E**lectromagnetic, **P**lethysmography.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling Flow in Blood Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modeling Flow in Blood Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🩸 MODELING FLOW IN BLOOD VESSELS — KTU 2024 SCHEME STUDY NOTES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Modeling Flow in Blood Vessels** is the application of **fluid dynamics principles** to mathematically describe, analyze, and predict the behavior of blood (a complex, non-Newtonian, pulsatile fluid) as it circulates through the cardiovascular network comprising arteries, arterioles, capillaries, venules, and veins.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme PCBMT601 (Biomechanics)**, this topic synthesizes:
- **Conservation laws** (mass, momentum, energy)
- **Constitutive equations** (Newton's law of viscosity extended to bio-fluids)
- **Geometric models** (rigid tubes, elastic tubes, tapered vessels, branching networks)
- **Rheological models** for blood (Newtonian approximation, Casson, Carreau-Yasuda)

The governing equation set typically used is the **Navier-Stokes equations** for incompressible flow, coupled with the **continuity equation**, subject to appropriate boundary conditions (no-slip, pulsatile pressure gradient at the inlet, and zero-pressure or stress-free conditions at outlets).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose Network:**  
> Imagine a complex garden irrigation system where water is being pumped **rhythmically** (like a heartbeat) through a network of flexible tubes of varying diameter. If you squeeze the tube, the water speeds up; if you widen it, water slows down. Now imagine that the water itself is not pure water but a **slightly sludgy mixture** of red cells, white cells, and plasma — and that the tubes **stretch and recoil** with each pulse.  
>  
> This is precisely what "modeling flow in blood vessels" is — applying the same physics we use for pipes, nozzles, and pumps, but to a **living, deformable, branching network** carrying a **multiphase fluid** under **pulsatile pressure**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard physiological values are commonly used in KTU problems:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Blood density | $\rho$ | **1060 kg/m³** |
| Blood dynamic viscosity (Newtonian approx.) | $\mu$ | **3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** (3.5 cP) |
| Plasma viscosity | $\mu_{p}$ | **1.2 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** |
| Aorta radius | $R$ | **1.2 – 1.5 cm** |
| Capillary radius | $r$ | **4 – 10 μm** |
| Mean aortic pressure | $\bar{P}$ | **100 mmHg ≈ 13.3 kPa** |
| Heart rate (resting) | $f$ | **1.2 Hz** (72 bpm) |
| Cardiac output | $Q$ | **5 L/min ≈ 8.3 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s** |
| Systolic / Diastolic | — | **120 / 80 mmHg** |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reynolds Number Range in Human Vasculature:**  
> - **Aorta:** $Re \approx 1000$–$4000$ (turbulent during peak systole)  
> - **Large arteries:** $Re \approx 100$–$1000$ (transitional)  
> - **Arterioles:** $Re \approx 1$–$10$ (laminar)  
> - **Capillaries:** $Re \approx 10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$ (creeping flow)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Modeling Assumptions

To make the problem tractable for KTU examinations, blood flow in vessels is modeled under a hierarchy of simplifications:

1. **Newtonian Fluid Assumption:** Treat blood as a homogeneous, incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity. This is valid only in **large vessels (diameter > 0.5 mm)** where the effect of red blood cells is averaged out.

2. **Steady Flow Assumption (Poiseuille Regime):** Time-averaged flow is used for vessel segments where pulsatility effects are negligible.

3. **Rigid, Cylindrical Vessel:** Vessel walls do not deform — applicable for short segments or quasi-steady analysis.

4. **Laminar Flow:** Viscous forces dominate over inertial forces (low Reynolds number). This holds in most of the vascular tree except the aorta during peak systole.

5. **No-Slip Boundary Condition:** Fluid velocity at the wall equals the wall velocity (zero for rigid walls).

6. **Incompressibility:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$

### 2.2 Hierarchy of Models

| Model | Vessel Type | Equation Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Poiseuille (Steady, Rigid)** | Capillaries, small veins | $Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ |
| **Womersley (Pulsatile, Rigid)** | Medium to large arteries | Navier-Stokes with $e^{i\omega t}$ |
| **Linearized Elastic Tube (Wave)** | Aorta, large arteries | Moens-Korteweg wave speed |
| **Non-Newtonian (Casson)** | Small arterioles | $\sqrt{\tau} = \sqrt{\tau_y} + \sqrt{K \dot{\gamma}}$ |
| **Fåhræus–Lindqvist Effect** | Tubes $d < 1$ mm | Apparent viscosity $\mu_{app}(d)$ |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Master these equations — they appear in **at least 60% of KTU Biomechanics problems** on this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v_{avg} = \frac{\pi R^{2} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | **Hagen-Poiseuille Law** (volumetric flow) | m³/s |
| 2 | $v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^{2} - r^{2})$ | **Parabolic velocity profile** | m/s |
| 3 | $v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R^{2}}{4 \mu L}$ | Centerline (max) velocity | m/s |
| 4 | $v_{avg} = \frac{v_{max}}{2}$ | Average velocity for Poiseuille flow | m/s |
| 5 | $\tau_{w} = \frac{R \Delta P}{2 L}$ | **Wall shear stress** | Pa |
| 6 | $R_{flow} = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi R^{4}}$ | **Hydraulic resistance** | Pa·s/m³ |
| 7 | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{2 \rho v R}{\mu}$ | **Reynolds number** | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\alpha = R \sqrt{\frac{\omega \rho}{\mu}}$ | **Womersley number** | dimensionless |
| 9 | $c = \sqrt{\frac{E h}{2 \rho R}}$ | **Moens-Korteweg pulse wave velocity** | m/s |
| 10 | $P_{total} = P_{static} + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} + \rho g h$ | **Bernoulli's equation** (energy conservation) | Pa |
| 11 | $\Delta P = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2})$ | Pressure drop across stenosis (Bernoulli) | Pa |
| 12 | $\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{4 v_{avg}}{R}$ | Wall shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 13 | $\dot{m} = \rho A v = $ const | **Continuity equation** | kg/s |
| 14 | $Q_{total} = \sum Q_i$ | **Kirchhoff's current law** for vessel networks | m³/s |
| 15 | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | **Newtonian constitutive equation** | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
