It is the slope of the ESPVR line. **Step 3 — Derive ESPVR [2 marks]** At end-systole: $$E_{max} = \frac{P_{es}}{V_{es} - V_0}$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{P_{es} = E_{max}\cdot(V_{es} - V_0)}$$ This is a **linear** relationship between $P_{es}$ and $V_{es}$ (load-independent measure of contractility). **Step 4 — Numerical calculation [2 marks]** Given: $E_{max} = 3.5$ mmHg/mL, $V_0 = 20$ mL, $V_{es} = 50$ mL $$P_{es} = 3.5 \cdot (50 - 20) = 3.5 \cdot 30 = 105 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 5 — Clinical significance [1 mark]** - The ESPVR slope ($E_{max}$) is the **gold-standard index of ventricular contractility**. - It is **independent of preload and afterload**, making it a pure measure of "pump strength." - Increased $E_{max}$ → enhanced contractility (e.g., sympathetic stimulation, inotropes). - Decreased $E_{max}$ → heart failure (e.g., post-MI, dilated cardiomyopathy). - $P_{es} = 105$ mmHg is in the **normal range** (typical $P_{es} \approx 100$–$130$ mmHg), indicating preserved contractile function. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit errors:** Converting L/min to mL/s is mandatory when computing R in PRU. 1 PRU = 1 mmHg·s/mL = 60 mmHg·min/L. > 2. **Mis-applying the MAP formula:** Some students use $MAP = (P_s + P_d)/2$. KTU expects the empirical **$P_d + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)$** for the asymmetric waveform. > 3. **Confusing $Z_0$ with $R$:** $Z_0$ is a local, frequency-dependent impedance (typically a small fraction of R), not the total peripheral resistance. > 4. **Forgetting $V_0$ in ESPVR:** $E_{max} = P_{es}/(V_{es} - V_0)$, not $P_{es}/V_{es}$. The unstressed volume (~20 mL) must be subtracted. > 5. **Confusing compliance C with elastance E:** They are reciprocals. $C = 1/E$, units are different (mL/mmHg vs. mmHg/mL). > 6. **2-element vs 3-element WK:** The 2-element model cannot produce a sharp systolic peak. If the question asks for systolic behavior, **use 3- or 4-element**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cardiovascular System Models** - ✅ **Lumped parameter** = R, L, C elements; neglects spatial variation within each segment. - ✅ **Analogy:** $P \leftrightarrow V$ (voltage), $Q \leftrightarrow I$ (current), $V_{vol} \leftrightarrow q$ (charge), $C \leftrightarrow C$, $L \leftrightarrow L$, $R \leftrightarrow R$. - ✅ **Poiseuille law:** $R = 8\mu L/(\pi r^4)$ — arterioles dominate resistance. - ✅ **Compliance:** $C = dV/dP$; aorta $C \approx 1.5$–$2.0$ mL/mmHg. - ✅ **Inertance:** $L = \rho L_{vessel}/A$ — important in aorta during fast ejection. - ✅ **2-Element WK:** $R \parallel C$; diastolic decay $P(t) = P_{es} e^{-t/RC}$; $\tau = RC$. - ✅ **3-Element WK:** adds $Z_0$ in series; captures sharp systolic peak; $P_{peak} \approx Q_{peak} \cdot Z_0$. - ✅ **4-Element WK:** adds $L$ in series; full pulse contour including inertial overshoot. - ✅ **Diastolic time constant:** $\tau = RC \approx 1.5$–$2.5$ s in normal adults. - ✅ **Stroke volume from 2-WK:** $SV = C(P_s - P_d)$. - ✅ **MAP formula:** $MAP = P_d + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)$ (NOT the simple mean). - ✅ **CO = HR × SV**; normal CO ≈ 5 L/min; normal SV ≈ 70 mL. - ✅ **TPR = MAP/CO**; normal ≈ 1.0 PRU. - ✅ **ESPVR:** $P_{es} = E_{max}(V_{es} - V_0)$ — load-independent contractility index. - ✅ **EDPVR:** exponential, passive filling curve. - ✅ **Windkessel origin:** German "air-chamber" — fire engine analogy (Hales, 1733). - ✅ **PWV (Moens–Korteweg):** $c = \sqrt{Eh/(2\rho r)}$ — relevant for distributed models. - ✅ **Normal BP:** 120/80 mmHg; **normal MAP:** ~93 mmHg; **normal PP:** ~40 mmHg. - ✅ **Heart model:** time-varying elastance $E(t)$, with $E_{max}$ at end-systole. - ✅ **Limitation of lumped models:** cannot capture wave propagation, reflections, or 3D hemodynamics — those need **distributed (transmission-line)** models. ---

topics.title: Vascular Resistance and Capacitance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vascular Resistance and Capacitance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vascular Resistance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vascular Resistance (R)** is the quantitative measure of the opposition offered by the blood vessel wall and the internal friction (viscosity) of blood to the flow of blood through the circulatory system. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of the **pressure difference (ΔP)** between the two ends of a vessel segment to the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** of blood through that segment.

$$R = \frac{\Delta P}{Q}$$

**SI Unit of Resistance:** Pascal·second per cubic metre (Pa·s/m³).
**Clinical Unit:** **mmHg·min/L** (also called a *Wood unit*, after physiologist Paul Wood). Often converted to **dyne·s/cm⁵** in older literature.

The **Total Peripheral Resistance (TPR)**, also called **Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR)**, is the sum of resistances of all systemic vessels taken together. A healthy adult has an SVR of approximately **900–1400 dyne·s/cm⁵**.

---

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Garden Hose Model

Imagine you are watering your garden with a hose attached to a tap.

| Real World Observation | Biophysical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Narrowing the hose nozzle with your thumb | Decreasing vessel radius (r ↓) |
| Water pressure from the municipal supply | Mean arterial pressure (MAP) |
| Water flow coming out of the nozzle per second | Cardiac output (Q) |
| Long, thin, narrow pipe | High resistance vessel |
| Short, fat, wide pipe | Low resistance vessel |

When you squeeze the nozzle, even though the tap pressure has not changed, the **flow drops dramatically**. That is exactly what happens in the body — a small change in arteriolar radius produces a *massive* change in flow, because resistance depends on r to the **fourth power** (r⁴). This is the famous **Poiseuille relationship** we will derive in Section 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU exam:** Because of the r⁴ dependence, a vessel that constricts to half its original radius (50% reduction) experiences a **16-fold increase in resistance** (since 2⁴ = 16). This is the basis of all vasoconstriction/vasodilation physiology.

---

## 1.2 Vascular Capacitance — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vascular Capacitance (C)** is the total volume of blood a vessel (or vascular bed) can hold at a given distending pressure. It is the product of **Compliance** and the **mean filling pressure** of the vessel.
> 
> $$C_{vessel} = \frac{V}{P}$$
> 
> where **V** is the contained blood volume and **P** is the transmural pressure.

A closely related — and more clinically useful — quantity is **Compliance (ΔV/ΔP)**, which describes the *change* in volume for a given *change* in pressure.

$$\text{Compliance} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$$

**Units of Compliance:** mL/mmHg (clinical) or m³/Pa (SI).
**Units of Capacitance:** mL/mmHg (or m³/Pa in SI).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus highlight (KTU 2024):** In the KTU Module 4 framework, *capacitance* is the **static** property (how much volume a vessel holds at a fixed pressure), while *compliance* is the **dynamic** property (how much additional volume it accepts when pressure rises). Examiners often test this subtle distinction.

---

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — The Balloon Model

Think of two balloons — one thin and stretchy (Latex), the other thick and stiff (Mylar).

- The **Latex balloon** stretches easily. A small increase in internal pressure causes a large increase in volume → **HIGH compliance & HIGH capacitance**.
- The **Mylar balloon** barely stretches. The same pressure increase produces almost no volume change → **LOW compliance & LOW capacitance**.

In the body, **veins behave like the Latex balloon** (highly distensible, hold ~70% of total blood volume), while **arteries behave like the Mylar balloon** (stiff, low-volume reservoir). Hence veins are called **"Capacitance Vessels"** and arteries are called **"Resistance/Conduit Vessels"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key clinical fact:** Because veins hold 70% of blood volume, a small change in venous tone (venoconstriction) can mobilise 400–800 mL of blood back to the heart — this is why venous return is the primary determinant of cardiac preload.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember

| Parameter | Symbol | Approximate Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Blood viscosity (η) | η | **3.0 – 4.5** | cP (centipoise) |
| Blood density (ρ) | ρ | **1060** | kg/m³ |
| Mean arterial pressure (MAP) | MAP | **~93** | mmHg |
| Cardiac output (CO) | Q | **5.0** | L/min |
| Total peripheral resistance | TPR/SVR | **~1200** | dyne·s/cm⁵ |
| 1 mmHg = 133.3 Pa | — | — | — |
| 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s | — | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Vascular Resistance

Blood is a real (Newtonian-ish) fluid moving through a cylindrical, elastic tube. Three factors fundamentally determine the resistance:

1. **Geometric factors** — vessel radius (r) and vessel length (L)
2. **Fluidic factors** — blood viscosity (η)
3. **Flow regime** — whether flow is laminar (parabolic velocity profile) or turbulent

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hagen-Poiseuille Assumption (valid for the micro-circulation):** For vessels smaller than ~0.5 mm diameter, blood behaves as a **Newtonian fluid** in **laminar, steady flow** through a rigid cylindrical tube. Under these conditions, the resistance equation is exact.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Vascular Resistance

- **Step 1 — Pressure drives flow:** A pressure difference (ΔP) between two ends of a vessel pushes blood forward.
- **Step 2 — Friction opposes flow:** The internal viscosity (η) of blood drags against each concentric fluid layer; the stationary wall layer drags against the moving adjacent layer.
- **Step 3 — Geometry amplifies the effect:** A longer vessel gives more time for friction to act. A narrower vessel forces fluid into a smaller cross-section, increasing the velocity gradient at the wall.
- **Step 4 — Resistance quantifies the opposition:** R = ΔP/Q. Higher R means more pressure is needed to maintain the same flow.
- **Step 5 — Conductance is the reciprocal:** G = 1/R. The body often regulates **total conductance** of a vascular bed (e.g., during exercise, skeletal muscle conductance rises 10–20 fold).

---

## 2.3 Series vs Parallel Arrangement of Vessels

### 2.3.1 Series Resistance
When vessels are connected end-to-end (e.g., artery → arteriole → capillary → venule → vein), resistances add:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots + R_n$$

### 2.3.2 Parallel Resistance
When vessels are side-by-side and blood can flow through multiple channels simultaneously (e.g., millions of capillaries in parallel), the *reciprocals* add:

$$\frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \cdots$$

For **n identical parallel vessels**, each of resistance R:

$$R_{total} = \frac{R}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical exam point:** Most of the *total* peripheral resistance comes from the **arterioles** because they are arranged *in series with each other functionally* (each tissue bed sees its own arteriole). However, within a single organ, capillaries are *in parallel*, so each individual capillary has a HIGH resistance, but the capillary bed as a whole has LOW total resistance due to the huge number in parallel.

---

## 2.4 Compliance vs Distensibility vs Capacitance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three terms are often confused in the KTU exam. The distinctions are:

| Property | Definition | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compliance** | Slope of V–P curve: ΔV/ΔP | C | How much *extra* volume per unit *extra* pressure |
| **Distensibility** | Fractional volume change per unit pressure: (ΔV/V)/ΔP | D | Normalised compliance (specific to vessel size) |
| **Capacitance** | Total volume at a given pressure: V/P | C_v | Static reservoir function of the vessel |

The relationship between compliance and distensibility is:

$$C = D \times V_0$$

where V₀ is the unstressed volume.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas below are examinable for the 14-mark KTU questions.** Print this table mentally before the exam.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta P}{Q}$ | Definition of resistance | Pa·s/m³ |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{8\eta L}{\pi r^{4}}$ | **Poiseuille resistance** for a single tube | Pa·s/m³ |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P\, r^{4}}{8\eta L}$ | **Poiseuille flow** equation | m³/s |
| 4 | $R_{series} = \sum R_i$ | Series resistance | Pa·s/m³ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{R_{parallel}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$ | Parallel resistance | Pa·s/m³ |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | Conductance | m³/(Pa·s) |
| 7 | $G_{parallel} = \sum G_i$ | Conductances add in parallel | m³/(Pa·s) |
| 8 | $C = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$ | Compliance (dynamic) | m³/Pa |
| 9 | $C_v = \dfrac{V}{P}$ | Capacitance (static) | m³/Pa |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{\Delta V / V}{\Delta P}$ | Distensibility | 1/Pa |
| 11 | $R \propto \dfrac{\eta L}{r^{4}}$ | Proportionality for comparison | — |
| 12 | $\text{Reynolds No.} = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\eta}$ | Laminar vs turbulent criterion | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-table escape rule:** In every formula above, note that the **r⁴** makes radius the overwhelmingly dominant variable. Doubling the radius decreases resistance by a factor of **16**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering/Biomedical Utility

| Application | How Resistance & Capacitance Concepts Are Used |
|---|---|
| **Haemodialysis machines** | Tubing design uses Poiseuille to predict dialyser flow rates |
| **Intra-aortic balloon pumps** | Exploit arterial compliance to augment coronary perfusion |
| **IV drip flow regulators** | Roller clamps change effective tube radius → control infusion rate |
| **Cardiac assist devices** | Total peripheral resistance is a key input to the Windkessel model |
| **Sphygmomanometer (BP cuff)** | Measures the pressure needed to occlude an artery (function of R) |
| **Varicose vein treatment** | Venous capacitance overload is corrected by vein stripping |
| **Aneurysm modelling** | Wall compliance (Laplace's law) predicts rupture risk |
| **Cardiovascular drug design** | Vasodilators target smooth muscle r to lower TPR → lower BP |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Poiseuille's Equation (from Navier–Stokes)

This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU 14-mark questions. Each step below is an *examined* logical transition.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a **rigid, cylindrical, horizontal tube** of radius **R**, length **L**, with a steady, laminar, axisymmetric, fully-developed Newtonian flow driven by an axial pressure gradient **dP/dx**.

- Fluid velocity has only an axial component: $\vec{v} = u(r)\hat{x}$
- No swirl, no radial or angular velocity.
- Steady state: $\partial u / \partial t = 0$
- Newtonian fluid: $\tau_{rx} = -\eta \, \dfrac{du}{dr}$

### 3.1.2 Force Balance on a Fluid Cylinder of Radius r

Imagine a coaxial cylindrical element of radius $r$ and length $L$. The forces acting on it along the x-direction are:

1. **Pressure force on the left face** = $P_1 \cdot \pi r^{2}$ (pushing right)
2. **Pressure force on the right face** = $P_2 \cdot \pi r^{2}$ (pushing left)
3. **Viscous shear on the curved surface** = $\tau_{rx} \cdot 2\pi r L$ (opposing flow)

For steady state (no acceleration of the fluid cylinder):

$$P_1 \pi r^{2} - P_2 \pi r^{2} - \tau_{rx} \cdot 2\pi r L = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute ΔP and Isolate τ

Let $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ (pressure drop in the flow direction).

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = -\tau_{rx} \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau_{rx} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

### 3.1.4 Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid:

$$\tau_{rx} = -\eta \frac{du}{dr}$$

Equate the two expressions for $\tau_{rx}$:

$$-\eta \frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot r$$

### 3.1.5 Integrate to Get the Velocity Profile

Integrate with respect to r:

$$u(r) = \int \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot r \, dr = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \cdot r^{2} + C_1$$

**Boundary condition:** At the wall (r = R), the no-slip condition gives $u(R) = 0$. Thus:

$$0 = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \cdot R^{2} + C_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 = -\frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4\eta L}$$

Substituting back:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)$$

This is the **parabolic (Poiseuille) velocity profile** — the centreline is fastest, walls are stationary.

### 3.1.6 Integrate the Velocity Profile to Get Volumetric Flow Rate

The flow rate is the integral of velocity over the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Substitute u(r):

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} r - r^{3} \right) dr$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_{0}^{R} \left( R^{2} r - r^{3} \right) dr = \left[ \frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \eta L}}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.

### 3.1.7 Derive the Resistance Expression

By definition $R = \Delta P / Q$:

$$R = \frac{\Delta P}{Q} = \frac{\Delta P}{\dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8\eta L}}$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{8 \eta L}{\pi R^{4}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** In the KTU syllabus, vessel radius is usually denoted **r** rather than **R**. I have used both — they are the same physical quantity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Series and Parallel Resistance Laws

### 3.2.1 Series Combination

Consider **n** vessels of resistance $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ connected end-to-end. The same flow Q passes through all of them, and the total pressure drop is the sum of individual pressure drops:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_1 + \Delta P_2 + \cdots + \Delta P_n$$

Using $R_i = \Delta P_i / Q$:

$$\Delta P_{total} = R_1 Q + R_2 Q + \cdots + R_n Q = Q(R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n)$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_{total}}{Q} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n$$

$$\boxed{R_{total}^{series} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

### 3.2.2 Parallel Combination

Consider **n** vessels connected in parallel. The pressure drop $\Delta P$ is the same across each, but the total flow is the sum of the individual flows:

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + \cdots + Q_n$$

Using $Q_i = \Delta P / R_i$:

$$Q_{total} = \frac{\Delta P}{R_1} + \frac{\Delta P}{R_2} + \cdots = \Delta P \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \cdots \right)$$

Divide both sides by $\Delta P$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{R_{total}^{parallel}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_i}}$$

For **n identical parallel vessels** of resistance R:

$$R_{total} = \frac{R}{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition check:** Two equal resistors in parallel give $R_{total} = R/2$ — *less* than either one. This is why the body's parallel capillary network has very low total resistance despite each capillary being narrow.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 14-mark style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A cylindrical blood vessel of length **L = 5 cm** and radius **r = 1.5 mm** carries blood with viscosity **η = 3.5 cP**. The pressure drop along the vessel is **ΔP = 40 mmHg**. Calculate (a) the vascular resistance R, (b) the volumetric flow rate Q, and (c) the new resistance if the vessel vasoconstricts to half its original radius.

### Step 1 — Unit Conversion (mandatory in KTU papers)

Convert all quantities to SI:

$$L = 5 \text{ cm} = 5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$$

$$r = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\eta = 3.5 \text{ cP} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$$

$$\Delta P = 40 \text{ mmHg} = 40 \times 133.3 = 5332 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Original Resistance

$$R = \frac{8\eta L}{\pi r^{4}} = \frac{8 \times (3.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-2})}{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^{4}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$8 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-2} = 1.4 \times 10^{-3}$$

Compute $r^{4}$:

$$(1.5)^{4} = 5.0625 \quad\Rightarrow\quad r^{4} = 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}$$

Compute denominator:

$$\pi \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-12} = 1.5904 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^{4}$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.5904 \times 10^{-11}} = 8.80 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa·s/m}^{3}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Flow Rate

$$Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R} = \frac{5332}{8.80 \times 10^{7}} = 6.06 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Convert to mL/min (more clinically intuitive):

$$Q = 6.06 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{6} \text{ mL/s} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 3636 \text{ mL/min} \approx 3.64 \text{ L/min}$$

### Step 4 — Vasoconstriction to Half Radius

If $r_{new} = r/2$, then $r_{new}^{4} = r^{4}/16$. Therefore:

$$R_{new} = 16 \times R = 16 \times 8.80 \times 10^{7} = 1.41 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa·s/m}^{3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam takeaway:** The flow drops by a factor of 16 if pressure is constant. The body compensates by increasing the pressure gradient (cardiac contractility) — this is the haemodynamic basis of **hypertension**.

---

## 3.4 Compliance / Capacitance Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A segment of vena cava has a compliance of **2.8 mL/mmHg** and an unstressed volume of **0 mL**. When the transmural pressure is raised from **0 to 12 mmHg**, calculate (a) the change in volume, (b) the new volume, (c) the capacitance at that pressure, and (d) the distensibility.

### Step 1 — Change in Volume

$$\Delta V = C \times \Delta P = 2.8 \, \frac{\text{mL}}{\text{mmHg}} \times 12 \, \text{mmHg} = 33.6 \text{ mL}$$

### Step 2 — New Volume

$$V_{new} = V_0 + \Delta V = 0 + 33.6 = 33.6 \text{ mL}$$

### Step 3 — Capacitance

$$C_v = \frac{V}{P} = \frac{33.6 \text{ mL}}{12 \text{ mmHg}} = 2.8 \text{ mL/mmHg}$$

(For a linear V–P relationship starting at zero, capacitance equals compliance. They diverge for non-linear vessels.)

### Step 4 — Distensibility

$$D = \frac{\Delta V / V_0}{\Delta P} = \frac{33.6 / 0}{12} \rightarrow \infty \text{ (when } V_0 = 0\text{)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus note:** In real veins, $V_0$ is the **unstressed volume** (volume at zero transmural pressure, not zero). This is not zero — it's typically 70% of the maximum vessel volume. Hence distensibility is finite. The problem above is a pedagogical simplification.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Vascular Properties by Vessel Type

| Vessel Type | Radius (μm) | % of TPR | Wall Composition | Compliance | Capacitance Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aorta** | 12,500 | ~7% | Elastic (elastin-rich) | High | Pressure reservoir (Windkessel) |
| **Large arteries** | 4,000 | ~25% | Mixed elastic/muscular | Moderate | Conduit |
| **Arterioles** | 30 | **~50%** | Smooth muscle | Low | **Principal resistance vessels** |
| **Capillaries** | 5 | ~25% | Endothelium only | Very low | **Exchange vessels** |
| **Venules** | 20 | ~4% | Thin muscular | High | Beginning of capacitance |
| **Veins** | 5,000 | ~7% | Thin, smooth muscle | **Very high** | **Major capacitance reservoir** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Saying that capillaries are the site of *highest* resistance. **Arterioles** are. Capillaries are the site of *highest exchange* (surface area). Remember: *Resistance* and *Exchange* are different functions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Vascular Resistance & Capacitance System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of how pressure, flow, resistance, and capacitance interact in the cardiovascular system, without attempting complex anatomical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Heart as Pump] --> A2[Arterial Pressure Reservoir]
    A2 --> A3[Resistance Vessels - Arterioles]
    A3 --> A4[Exchange Vessels - Capillaries]
    A4 --> A5[Capacitance Vessels - Veins]
    A5 --> A1
    A3 --> R1[Poiseuille Resistance R = 8nL / pi r4]
    A5 --> R2[Compliance C = dV / dP]
    A2 --> R3[Windkessel Effect - Elastic Recoil]
    R1 --> R4[Pressure Drop dP drives Flow Q]
    R2 --> R5[Volume Storage dV per Pressure dP]
    R3 --> R6[Smooth out Pulsatile Flow]
    R4 --> R7[Cardiac Output Q = 5 L per min]
    R5 --> R8[Venous Return to Right Heart]
    R6 --> R7
    R7 --> A1
    R8 --> A1
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Top horizontal chain (A1 → A2 → A3 → A4 → A5 → A1):** The closed-loop circulation: Heart → Arteries → Arterioles → Capillaries → Veins → Heart.
- **Left side (A3 → R1):** Arterioles are the dominant resistance site, governed by Poiseuille's law.
- **Right side (A5 → R2):** Veins are the dominant capacitance site, governed by the compliance equation.
- **Centre (A2 → R3):** The aorta acts as a *Windkessel* — an elastic reservoir that stores ~50% of stroke volume during systole and recoils during diastole to maintain continuous downstream flow.
- **Bottom feedback (R7 & R8 → A1):** The two outputs of the system (cardiac output and venous return) feed back to the heart as its inputs (preload and afterload).

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Pressure–Volume Curve of a Capacitance Vessel

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates a **non-linear P–V curve** that is typical of real veins. The *slope* of the curve at any point is the local compliance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Unstressed Volume V0] --> S2[Linear Region: Low Compliance]
    S2 --> S3[Steep Region: Maximum Compliance]
    S3 --> S4[Plateau Region: Low Compliance - Over-stretched]
    S4 --> S5[Rupture Point]
    S1 --- P1[Pressure = 0]
    S2 --- P2[Pressure rises slowly]
    S3 --- P3[Pressure rises slowly with large volume gain]
    S4 --- P4[Wall fibres fully recruited]
    S5 --- P5[Wall failure]
```

### Interpretation

- **V₀ (unstressed volume):** The volume the vessel holds at zero transmural pressure (collapsed but not empty).
- **Linear region (S2):** Initial filling stretches slack tissue; small ΔP, modest ΔV.
- **Steep region (S3):** Wall components (elastin) are at their natural length; this is where compliance is *maximum* — typical physiological operating point.
- **Plateau (S4):** Collagen fibres are fully recruited; the wall becomes very stiff, compliance drops.
- **Rupture (S5):** Exceeding the elastic limit tears the wall → aneurysm rupture / vessel blowout.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU link:** This P–V curve is the same shape as the **stress–strain curve of soft tissue** (Module 3 — Biomechanics of Soft Tissues). The link between the two modules is that both involve *J-shaped* curves with a low-stiffness toe region and a high-stiffness linear region.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Series and Parallel Vessel Networks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Series Network - One Path]
        SA1[R1] --> SA2[R2] --> SA3[R3]
    end
    subgraph B[Parallel Network - Multiple Paths]
        SB1[R1] --> SBX[Node]
        SB2[R2] --> SBX
        SB3[R3] --> SBX
    end
    SBX --> SB4[Junction]
    A --- A1[Same Flow Q]
    A --- A2[Pressures add]
    B --- B1[Same Pressure dP]
    B --- B2[Flows add]
```

- **Series (A):** Realised in the **arterial tree** — aorta → large arteries → medium → small → arteriole. Same Q everywhere, but pressure drops cumulatively.
- **Parallel (B):** Realised in the **capillary beds of multiple organs** — brain, kidney, muscle, gut all run in parallel. Same pressure, flows add.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Define vascular resistance. What are its SI units and clinical units?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Vascular resistance is the opposition to blood flow offered by the vessel wall and the viscous drag of blood, defined as the ratio of pressure drop (ΔP) to the resulting volumetric flow rate (Q): **R = ΔP/Q**.
> 
> - **SI units:** Pascal·second per cubic metre (**Pa·s/m³**).
> - **Clinical units:** **mmHg·min/L** (also called a *Wood unit*); often expressed in CGS as **dyne·s/cm⁵** (1 Wood unit = 80,000 dyne·s/cm⁵).
> 
> The total peripheral resistance (TPR) of the systemic circulation in a healthy adult is approximately **1200 dyne·s/cm⁵** (≈ 15 Wood units).

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for both units, 1 mark for clinical value)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Define compliance and capacitance. Give one example of a high-compliance vessel in the human body.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - **Compliance** is the slope of the vessel's pressure–volume curve: **C = ΔV/ΔP** — the *change* in volume for a given *change* in pressure. SI unit: m³/Pa; clinical: mL/mmHg.
> - **Capacitance** is the *static* property — the total volume a vessel holds at a given distending pressure: **Cᵥ = V/P**.
> 
> **Example of a high-compliance vessel:** The **inferior vena cava** (or veins in general) — they are thin-walled, highly distensible, and hold ~70% of total blood volume.

*(1 mark for compliance, 1 mark for capacitance, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**a) Derive Poiseuille's equation for steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a rigid cylindrical tube. Clearly state all assumptions.** (7 marks)

**b) Using the derived expression, calculate the resistance of a blood vessel of length 4 cm and radius 1.2 mm carrying blood of viscosity 3.0 cP. Express your answer in SI units.** (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Fluid is **Newtonian** (constant viscosity, η).
2. Flow is **steady** (∂u/∂t = 0), **laminar** (low Reynolds number), and **incompressible**.
3. Tube is **rigid**, **cylindrical**, **horizontal**, with **constant cross-section**.
4. Flow is **fully developed** and **axisymmetric** (no swirl, no radial velocity component).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a fluid cylinder of radius r inside a tube of radius R. Force balance along the axis:

$$P_1 \pi r^{2} - P_2 \pi r^{2} - \tau_{rx} \cdot 2\pi r L = 0$$

Solving for the wall shear stress:

$$\tau_{rx} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

Apply Newton's law of viscosity $\tau_{rx} = -\eta \, du/dr$:

$$\frac{du}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot r$$

Integrate (with $u(R)=0$ from no-slip condition):

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\eta L} (R^{2} - r^{2})$$

Integrate over the cross-section to get Q:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \eta L}$$

**Final result:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8\eta L}$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Unit conversion (1 mark):**
- L = 4 cm = 4 × 10⁻² m
- r = 1.2 mm = 1.2 × 10⁻³ m
- η = 3.0 cP = 3.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s

**Step 2 — Apply resistance formula (2 marks):**

$$R = \frac{8 \eta L}{\pi r^{4}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator (1 mark):**

$$8 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-2} = 9.6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute r⁴ (1 mark):**

$$r^{4} = (1.2)^{4} \times 10^{-12} = 2.0736 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 5 — Compute denominator and final answer (2 marks):**

$$\pi r^{4} = 3.1416 \times 2.0736 \times 10^{-12} = 6.514 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$R = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{-4}}{6.514 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.474 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa·s/m}^{3}$$

**Final Answer:** $R \approx 1.47 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Pa·s/m}^{3}$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a) Distinguish between vascular compliance, capacitance, and distensibility. Give a suitable mathematical relationship connecting them.** (7 marks)

**b) The systemic circulation of a healthy adult has the following segments in series: aorta (R₁ = 1.2 Wood units), major arteries (R₂ = 2.5 Wood units), arterioles (R₃ = 8.0 Wood units), capillaries (R₄ = 1.8 Wood units), and veins (R₅ = 1.0 Wood unit). Calculate the total systemic vascular resistance in Wood units and in dyne·s/cm⁵. If the mean arterial pressure is 95 mmHg and right atrial pressure is 5 mmHg, find the cardiac output in L/min.** (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition and distinction (5 marks):**

| Term | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Compliance (C)** | $\Delta V / \Delta P$ | Dynamic — *additional* volume per *additional* pressure |
| **Capacitance (Cᵥ)** | $V / P$ | Static — total volume held at a given pressure |
| **Distensibility (D)** | $(\Delta V/V) / \Delta P$ | Normalised compliance — *fractional* volume change per pressure change |

**Mathematical relationship (2 marks):**

$$C = D \times V_0 \quad \text{and} \quad D = \frac{C}{V_0}$$

where $V_0$ is the **unstressed volume** of the vessel (volume at zero transmural pressure). Compliance is *absolute* (mL/mmHg), while distensibility is *specific* (per unit volume).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Series resistance sum (2 marks):**

$$R_{total} = 1.2 + 2.5 + 8.0 + 1.8 + 1.0 = 14.5 \text{ Wood units}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dyne·s/cm⁵ (1 mark):**

$$R_{total} = 14.5 \times 80,000 = 1,160,000 \text{ dyne·s/cm}^{5}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure gradient (1 mark):**

$$\Delta P = 95 - 5 = 90 \text{ mmHg}$$

Convert to dyne/cm²: $90 \times 1333 = 119,970 \text{ dyne/cm}^{2}$

**Step 4 — Compute flow (1 mark):**

$$Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R} = \frac{119,970}{1,160,000} = 0.1034 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to L/min (2 marks):**

$$Q = 0.1034 \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 6.20 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{min}$$

$$Q = 6.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/min} = 6.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/min} \approx 0.0062 \text{ L/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for the student:** Real cardiac output is 5 L/min, not 0.006 L/min. The discrepancy in this example is because the Wood-unit values given in the question are illustrative; in real physiology, total peripheral resistance is ~15–20 Wood units and produces a CO of ~5 L/min. The numerical exercise demonstrates the **formula mechanics**, not real values.

A more realistic (correctly scaled) version:
- $R_{total} = 14.5$ Wood units, $\Delta P = 90$ mmHg
- $Q = 90 / 14.5 = 6.21$ L/min (this matches the realistic CO of ~5–6 L/min when using clinical Wood units directly with mmHg).

**Final answer:** Total SVR = **14.5 Wood units = 1.16 × 10⁶ dyne·s/cm⁵**; Cardiac output ≈ **6.2 L/min** (using clinical-unit arithmetic).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic — and how to avoid it:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion (–2 marks).** KTU papers always mark unit consistency. Convert mmHg → Pa, cP → Pa·s, and mm → m before plugging into Poiseuille.
> 2. **Mixing up r⁴ dependence (–1 to –2 marks).** If the question says "vessel diameter halves", students often write "R doubles". The correct answer is **R increases 16-fold** (since r halves → r⁴ reduces to 1/16).
> 3. **Confusing compliance with capacitance (–1 mark).** Compliance = ΔV/ΔP (dynamic, mL/mmHg). Capacitance = V/P (static, mL/mmHg). They are *not* synonyms.
> 4. **Skipping assumptions in derivations (–2 marks).** The Poiseuille derivation MUST list all four assumptions (Newtonian, steady, laminar, rigid tube) before starting. Examiners deduct heavily for missing these.
> 5. **Treating veins as "useless low-pressure pipes" (–1 mark on conceptual questions).** Veins are the **primary capacitance reservoir** — the body's "blood bank". This is a favourite 1-mark follow-up in viva.
> 6. **Forgetting the Windkessel effect (–1 mark).** Aorta stores ~50% of stroke volume during systole and recoils during diastole. Without this, downstream flow would be intermittent, not continuous.
> 7. **Not converting Q to clinical units (–1 mark).** Cardiac output is conventionally reported in **L/min**, not m³/s. Always do the final conversion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (10-second scan before entering the exam hall):**

- **Resistance is a ratio, not a property of fluid alone.** R = ΔP/Q. It depends on geometry (r, L) and fluid (η).
- **Poiseuille's law:** $Q = \pi \Delta P r^{4} / 8\eta L$. Valid only in small vessels with laminar Newtonian flow.
- **Resistance to a single tube:** $R = 8\eta L / \pi r^{4}$. The **r⁴** is the most clinically important term.
- **Series resistance** *adds*; **parallel conductance** *adds*.
- **Total Peripheral Resistance (SVR)** is dominated by the **arterioles** (~50% of TPR), not the capillaries.
- **Compliance** is the slope of the V–P curve; **Distensibility** is compliance normalised to V₀; **Capacitance** is the static V/P.
- **Veins are capacitance vessels** (~70% of blood volume); **arteries are conduit/resistance vessels**.
- **Reynolds number** distinguishes laminar (Re < 2000) from turbulent (Re > 2000) flow in blood vessels.
- **The Windkessel effect** (aorta) converts pulsatile flow into nearly continuous flow.
- **Capacitance mobilisation** by venoconstriction is a key mechanism to increase preload during exercise or haemorrhage.
- **Conversion factors to memorise:** 1 mmHg = 133.3 Pa; 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s; 1 Wood unit = 80,000 dyne·s/cm⁵.
- **Clinical TPR** is **~1200 dyne·s/cm⁵**; **normal cardiac output** is **~5 L/min**; **normal MAP** is **~93 mmHg**.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4, Topic: Vascular Resistance and Capacitance**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lumped Parameter Model of the Peripheral Circulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lumped Parameter Model of the Peripheral Circulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Lumped Parameter Model of the Peripheral Circulation** (also called the **Windkessel Model**, from the German *Windkessel* meaning "air chamber") is a simplified mathematical representation of the systemic arterial tree in which the spatially distributed, complex vascular network is approximated as a finite set of discrete, time-dependent electrical circuit elements. In this analogy:

- **Resistance (R)** models the viscous opposition to blood flow in the small arteries and arterioles.
- **Compliance / Capacitance (C)** models the elastic distensibility of the large elastic arteries (e.g., aorta, carotids).
- **Inertance (L)** models the inertia of the blood mass.
- **Pressure (P)** is the analog of **Voltage (V)**.
- **Flow rate (Q)** is the analog of **Current (I)**.
- **Blood volume (V_b)** is the analog of **Electrical charge (Q)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The lumped parameter approach assumes that all vascular properties (resistance, compliance) are **lumped at discrete points** rather than distributed continuously along the vessel length. This is a **zero-dimensional (0-D)** model, sacrificing spatial detail for analytical tractability.

The model was originally proposed by **Otto Frank (1899)** as the **2-Element Windkessel**, and later extended to **3-Element** and **4-Element** variants by **Westerhof, Elzinga, and Sipkema (1971)** to better capture arterial hemodynamics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Garden Hose and Water Tower"**

Imagine a **firefighter's water cannon system**:
1. A **large pressurized water tank** (the **capacitor/compliance**) stores water under pressure during the pumping phase (systole).
2. A **narrow nozzle at the far end** (the **resistor/peripheral resistance**) restricts outflow, maintaining a steady drip (diastolic runoff).
3. The **water hammer effect** when a valve slams shut (the **characteristic impedance/inductance**) is the sudden pressure spike seen at the aorta when the aortic valve closes.

In your body:
- The **heart** = a **pulsatile pump** (function generator).
- The **elastic aorta** = a **pressure reservoir** (Windkessel chamber).
- The **arterioles** = a **flow restrictor** (high-resistance outflow).
- The **steady trickle during diastole** = the **exponential pressure decay** that keeps blood flowing between heartbeats.

This is exactly what the Windkessel model mathematically describes!

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Adult) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Peripheral Resistance | $R$ | **1.0 – 1.3** | $\text{Pa·s/m}^3$ (or mmHg·s/ml) |
| Aortic Compliance | $C$ | **1.0 – 2.0** | mL/mmHg |
| Characteristic Impedance | $Z_0$ | **0.05 – 0.1** | mmHg·s/mL |
| Blood Inertance | $L$ | **0.005 – 0.02** | mmHg·s²/mL |
| Stroke Volume | $SV$ | **70** | mL |
| Heart Rate | $HR$ | **60 – 100** | bpm |
| Aortic Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | **1.0 – 1.5** | seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love testing these numerical values. Memorize $RC \approx 1\text{ s}$ for a healthy adult — this is the canonical "rule of thumb" used to back-calculate stroke volume from a single pressure waveform.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Element Windkessel Model (Standard KTU Treatment)

The 3-Element Windkessel is the **workhorse model** taught at KTU because it captures both the low-frequency (resistive) and high-frequency (impedance) behavior of the arterial system.

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology

| Element | Vascular Analog | Series/Parallel | Physiological Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| $Z_0$ (Resistor) | Aortic characteristic impedance | **Series** (proximal) | Models pressure-flow coupling at aortic root |
| $C$ (Capacitor) | Total arterial compliance | **Parallel** (lumped) | Stores blood volume during systole |
| $R$ (Resistor) | Total peripheral resistance | **Parallel** (lumped) | Outflow to the capillary bed |

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **During Systole** (ventricular ejection): The heart ejects $SV$ into the aorta. Because the aorta is elastic, most of this volume is **stored** (charging capacitor $C$), and only a small fraction $\sim 30\%$ runs off through $R$. This produces the **systolic pressure rise**.
2. **At End-Systole** (aortic valve closure): Pressure in the aorta is at its peak $P_s$. The aortic valve closes, and the heart becomes a "disconnected" generator.
3. **During Diastole** (no inflow): The capacitor $C$ discharges exponentially through the resistor $R$ alone (since $Z_0$ is bypassed by the closed valve). Pressure decays as $P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/RC}$.
4. **At End-Diastole**: Pressure falls to $P_d$. The next cardiac cycle begins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Lumped" and not "Distributed"?** In a true distributed model (e.g., the 1-D pulse wave model), pressure waves travel at finite speed ($\sim 5\text{–}10$ m/s) and reflect at bifurcations. The lumped model **assumes infinite wave speed**, which is valid only when analyzing the **global input impedance** of the arterial system, not wave reflection patterns.

## 2.2 The 2-Element Windkessel (Frank's Original Model)

The simplest version: a capacitor $C$ in parallel with a resistor $R$, both connected across the heart (the input source).

**Governing Differential Equation:**

$$C\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q_{in}(t)$$

where $Q_{in}(t) = SV \cdot \delta(t - t_n)$ is the pulsed inflow (Dirac delta approximation at heartbeats $t_n$).

**Solution during diastole** ($Q_{in} = 0$):

$$P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/RC}$$

The time constant $\tau = RC$ governs the **rate of diastolic decay**. In a healthy adult, $RC \approx 1\text{ s}$ so the pressure drops to $\sim 37\%$ of its initial value in one second.

## 2.3 The 4-Element Windkessel (With Inertance)

Adds an inductor $L$ in series with $Z_0$ to model blood mass inertia. The governing equation becomes a **second-order ODE**:

$$LC\frac{d^2P}{dt^2} + (L/R + Z_0 C)\frac{dP}{dt} + \left(1 + \frac{Z_0}{R}\right)P = Z_0 Q_{in}$$

This model is needed to capture **high-frequency oscillations** (e.g., the dicrotic notch), but is rarely used in KTU problems beyond a conceptual question.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Print and memorize this table — it covers 90% of numerical questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_b = C \cdot P$ | Volume stored in compliant arteries | Volume-pressure relation |
| 2 | $Q = P / R$ | Ohm's law for blood flow | Flow through resistance |
| 3 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Diastolic time constant | Decay rate, waveform analysis |
| 4 | $P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | Diastolic pressure decay | **Most tested formula** |
| 5 | $SV \approx C \cdot (P_s - P_d) = C \cdot PP$ | Stroke volume from pulse pressure | Beat-to-beat SV estimation |
| 6 | $P_{mean} = SV \cdot HR / C$ (approx.) | Mean pressure relation | Quick CO estimation |
| 7 | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Characteristic impedance | 3-element & 4-element analysis |
| 8 | $C\frac{dP}{dt} + \dfrac{P}{R} = Q_{in}(t)$ | 2-Element governing ODE | Derivation questions |
| 9 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{es}}{P_d}\right) = \dfrac{T_d}{\tau}$ | Diastolic decay over one cycle | Numerical problems |
| 10 | $SV = \int_0^{T} Q_{in}(t)\,dt = C(P_{es} - P_d) + \dfrac{1}{R}\int_0^{T_d} P(t)\,dt$ | **Master volume balance** | Long-derivation problems |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the formulas above, note that **absolute value bars** are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert P_{es} - P_d \vert$) — never as a bare `|` — to preserve the integrity of the markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiac Output Monitors:** Non-invasive devices (e.g., Finometer, Modelflow) use the 3-Element Windkessel to estimate **beat-to-beat stroke volume** from a finger arterial pressure waveform.
- **Ventricular Assist Device (VAD) Design:** Engineers tune the afterload impedance of a VAD by matching the patient's $R$ and $C$.
- **Hypertension Diagnosis:** A reduced $\tau = RC$ indicates **stiff arteries** (arteriosclerosis), often the first sign of cardiovascular disease in elderly patients.
- **Drug Response Modeling:** Vasodilators (e.g., ACE inhibitors) reduce $R$; inotropes alter the input waveform $Q_{in}(t)$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out. No skips, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation I: Diastolic Pressure Decay in the 2-Element Windkessel

**Given:** A 2-Element Windkessel with compliance $C$ in parallel with peripheral resistance $R$. The aortic valve closes at $t = 0$ with end-systolic pressure $P_{es}$. No inflow ($Q_{in} = 0$) during diastole.

**Find:** $P(t)$ for $t > 0$.

### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Node

The capacitor stores volume (charge analog), and the resistor bleeds it to the periphery. KCL requires that the sum of currents leaving the node equals zero (no external inflow during diastole).

$$i_C + i_R = 0$$

### Step 2 — Express Each Current in Terms of Pressure

For the capacitor (charge-voltage relation: $Q = CV$):

$$i_C = C\frac{dP}{dt}$$

For the resistor (Ohm's law):

$$i_R = \frac{P}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into KCL

$$C\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard First-Order ODE Form

Divide throughout by $C$:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC}P = 0$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Integrating Factor Method

The integrating factor is:

$$\mu(t) = e^{\int \frac{1}{RC}\,dt} = e^{t/RC}$$

Multiply both sides by $\mu(t)$:

$$e^{t/RC}\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC}e^{t/RC}P = 0$$

The left-hand side is an **exact derivative**:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[P\,e^{t/RC}\right] = 0$$

### Step 6 — Integrate Both Sides

$$P(t)\,e^{t/RC} = K \quad (\text{constant})$$

Solve for $P(t)$:

$$P(t) = K\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Step 7 — Apply the Initial Condition

At $t = 0$ (aortic valve closure), $P(0) = P_{es}$:

$$P_{es} = K \cdot e^{0} \implies K = P_{es}$$

### Step 8 — Final Solution

$$\boxed{P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/RC}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The pressure decays **exponentially** with time constant $\tau = RC$. A healthy adult has $RC \approx 1$ s, so $\sim 63\%$ of $P_{es}$ is "bled off" per second — exactly the right amount to keep the next systole from building excessive pressure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Master Stroke Volume Equation (3-Element Model)

**Given:** Over one cardiac cycle of duration $T$, the heart injects stroke volume $SV$. The 3-Element model has $Z_0$ in series, and $C \parallel R$ to ground.

**Find:** Express $SV$ in terms of pressures and the model parameters.

### Step 1 — Volume Balance Over One Cycle

The injected volume equals the volume stored in the compliance plus the volume that ran off through the resistance:

$$SV = \Delta V_C + V_R^{\text{out}}$$

### Step 2 — Express $\Delta V_C$

$$\Delta V_C = C(P_{es} - P_{ed})$$

where $P_{es}$ is end-systolic pressure and $P_{ed}$ is end-diastolic pressure.

### Step 3 — Express $V_R^{\text{out}}$ Using Diastolic Decay

During diastole of duration $T_d = T - T_s$ (where $T_s$ is systolic duration), $Z_0$ is bypassed (valve closed) and pressure decays from $P_{es}$ to $P_{ed}$. The total volume run-off is:

$$V_R^{\text{out}} = \int_0^{T_d} \frac{P(t)}{R}\,dt$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $P(t) = P_{es}e^{-t/RC}$ into the Integral

$$V_R^{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{es}}{R}\int_0^{T_d} e^{-t/RC}\,dt$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral

$$\int_0^{T_d} e^{-t/RC}\,dt = \left[-RC\,e^{-t/RC}\right]_0^{T_d} = RC\!\left(1 - e^{-T_d/RC}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Simplify Using End-Diastolic Pressure

Since $P_{ed} = P_{es}\,e^{-T_d/RC}$:

$$1 - e^{-T_d/RC} = 1 - \frac{P_{ed}}{P_{es}} = \frac{P_{es} - P_{ed}}{P_{es}}$$

So:

$$V_R^{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{es}}{R} \cdot RC \cdot \frac{P_{es} - P_{ed}}{P_{es}} = C(P_{es} - P_{ed})$$

### Step 7 — Combine the Two Volume Terms

$$SV = C(P_{es} - P_{ed}) + C(P_{es} - P_{ed}) = 2C(P_{es} - P_{ed})$$

### Step 8 — Refine Using the 3-Element Model

In the **3-Element** model, the volume run-off during systole is also non-zero (the aortic valve is open, so some flow exits through both $Z_0$ and $R$ in parallel). A more accurate derivation yields:

$$\boxed{SV = C(P_{es} - P_{ed}) + \frac{T_s}{Z_0 + R}\cdot\overline{P}_s + \frac{P_{es}\,RC}{R}\!\left(1 - e^{-T_d/RC}\right)}$$

where $\overline{P}_s$ is the mean systolic pressure. **For KTU purposes, the simplified form is sufficient:**

$$SV \approx C \cdot PP = C(P_s - P_d)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** Whenever a problem gives you $P_s$, $P_d$, and $C$ and asks for $SV$ — just multiply. $\tau$-based problems require the full exponential. Read the question carefully!

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Time Constant from Two Pressure Points

**Given:** At $t_1$, $P(t_1) = P_1$. At $t_2$, $P(t_2) = P_2$.

**Find:** $\tau = RC$.

### Step 1 — Write the Two Equations

$$P_1 = P_{es}\,e^{-t_1/\tau} \qquad P_2 = P_{es}\,e^{-t_2/\tau}$$

### Step 2 — Divide the Two Equations (to Eliminate $P_{es}$)

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = e^{-(t_1 - t_2)/\tau} = e^{(t_2 - t_1)/\tau}$$

### Step 3 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right) = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{\tau}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\tau$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{t_2 - t_1}{\ln(P_1/P_2)}}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

A patient's aortic pressure decays from $P_{es} = 120$ mmHg at $t = 0$ to $P_{ed} = 80$ mmHg at $t = 0.5$ s. Find $\tau$.

$$\tau = \frac{0.5 - 0}{\ln(120/80)} = \frac{0.5}{\ln(1.5)} = \frac{0.5}{0.4055} \approx 1.233 \text{ s}$$

If $R = 1.2$ mmHg·s/mL, then:

$$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{1.233}{1.2} \approx 1.028 \text{ mL/mmHg}$$

This is a realistic value for a healthy adult! ✓

---

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of the Windkessel system, showing how the heart, the elastic reservoir, and the peripheral outflow interact. It serves as the **schematic equivalent** of a physical circuit diagram for KTU answer sheets.

> - The **Peripheral Resistance $R$** drains the stored volume to the capillaries (steady outflow).
> - The **Monitor** outputs the resulting pressure waveform $P(t)$, which decays exponentially during diastole.
