# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Units! Units! Units!** $R$ in **mmHg·s/mL** ≠ $R$ in **Pa·s/m³**. A 7.5× conversion factor separates them. Most marks lost in numerical questions are due to inconsistent unit systems.
> - **Don't confuse $Z_0$ with $R$:** $Z_0$ is the *high-frequency* impedance (small, $\sim 0.05$ mmHg·s/mL), while $R$ is the *low-frequency, DC* outflow resistance (large, $\sim 1.0$ mmHg·s/mL).
> - **The exponential decay formula is valid ONLY during diastole** (valve closed). Students who use it during systole lose full marks.
> - **Always show the differential equation derivation** — even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for it. KTU examiners award 2–3 "process" marks for the setup.
> - **Draw the circuit diagram** — even short questions (3 marks) get 1 extra mark for a clean schematic.

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term "Lumped Parameter Model" as applied to the peripheral circulation. Why is it called a "Windkessel" model?**

**Model Answer:**

A *Lumped Parameter Model* is a simplified mathematical representation of the systemic arterial circulation in which the spatially distributed properties of blood vessels (resistance, compliance, inertance) are **concentrated at discrete points** rather than continuously distributed along the vessel length. It uses an **electrical analog**: pressure is voltage, flow is current, vessel compliance is capacitance, and peripheral resistance is electrical resistance.

The term **"Windkessel"** is German for **"air chamber"** or **"elastic reservoir"**, derived from the historical fire-engine pump design. Otto Frank borrowed the analogy in **1899** to describe the function of the elastic aorta, which acts as a **pressure reservoir** storing blood during systole and releasing it during diastole, much like the air-filled chamber in a fire engine smoothing the pulsatile water flow into a steady stream.

*(1 mark: Definition; 1 mark: Electrical analogy; 1 mark: Etymology/Frank's contribution)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the governing differential equation of the 2-Element Windkessel model during diastole and write its solution.**

**Model Answer:**

**Governing ODE (during diastole):**

$$C\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = 0$$

**Solution:**

$$P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/RC}$$

where $P_{es}$ is the end-systolic pressure at the moment of aortic valve closure, and $\tau = RC$ is the diastolic time constant (typically $\sim 1$ s in healthy adults).

*(1.5 marks: ODE; 1.5 marks: Solution with all symbols defined)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Part B questions carry internal choice — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the 3-Element Windkessel Model with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the 2-Element model.

**Model Answer:**

**The 3-Element Windkessel Model** (Westerhof, Elzinga, Sipkema — 1971) consists of three electrical elements representing the arterial system:

1. **Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$**: Placed in **series** with the heart, it represents the resistance of the proximal aorta to the pulsatile inflow. Its value is small ($Z_0 \approx 0.05$ mmHg·s/mL) and represents the **high-frequency** behavior of the arterial system.

2. **Arterial Compliance $C$**: A **capacitor** connected in parallel, representing the elastic distensibility of the large arteries. Typical value: $C \approx 1.5$ mL/mmHg.

3. **Peripheral Resistance $R$**: A **resistor** in parallel with $C$, representing the cumulative resistance of the small arteries and arterioles. Typical value: $R \approx 1.0$ mmHg·s/mL.

**Block Diagram:**

```
   Heart ───[Z₀]───┬────[C]────┐
                   │           │
                   └────[R]────┘
                       │
                      (out)
```

**Comparison with 2-Element Model:**

| Feature | 2-Element | 3-Element |
|---|---|---|
| $Z_0$ included? | No | Yes (series) |
| Captures pulse pressure? | Poorly | Well |
| Captures diastolic decay? | Yes | Yes |
| Equations | 1st order, simple | 1st order, with input term |
| KTU exam frequency | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

**Advantage:** The 3-Element model more accurately represents the **input impedance spectrum** of the arterial system, especially at higher frequencies. It predicts the realistic pressure-flow relationship at the aortic root, including the systolic pressure rise.

**Disadvantage:** It is mathematically more complex and still cannot represent wave reflections, dicrotic notch details, or regional vascular heterogeneity.

*(2 marks: Diagram; 2 marks: Each element explained; 2 marks: Comparison table; 1 mark: Conclusion)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the diastolic pressure decay in a 2-Element Windkessel model. An adult patient has $R = 1.2$ mmHg·s/mL and $C = 1.4$ mL/mmHg. If the end-systolic pressure is 120 mmHg, calculate the pressure after 0.8 s of diastole.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** (See full derivation in Section 3.1 above — the model answer below summarizes the key steps.)

Applying KCL at the aortic node during diastole (no inflow):

$$C\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{P}{RC}$$

Separating variables and integrating from $P_{es}$ at $t = 0$ to $P(t)$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{P_{es}}^{P(t)} \frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P(t)}{P_{es}}\right) = -\frac{t}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/RC}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Compute the time constant $\tau = RC$.

$$\tau = R \times C = 1.2 \times 1.4 = 1.68 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the decay equation with $t = 0.8$ s, $P_{es} = 120$ mmHg.

$$P(0.8) = 120 \times e^{-0.8/1.68} = 120 \times e^{-0.4762}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponential.

$$e^{-0.4762} \approx 0.6211$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final pressure.

$$P(0.8) \approx 120 \times 0.6211 \approx 74.5 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Result:** The aortic pressure at $t = 0.8$ s into diastole is approximately **74.5 mmHg**.

*(1 mark: Setup; 2 marks: Derivation; 1 mark: Time constant; 1 mark: Exponential evaluation; 1 mark: Final answer; 1 mark: Units/interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the 2-Element Windkessel Model. Discuss its assumptions, advantages, and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**The 2-Element Windkessel Model** (Otto Frank, 1899) is the simplest lumped parameter model of the arterial system. It consists of:

- A **capacitor $C$** (arterial compliance) connected in **parallel** with a **resistor $R$** (peripheral resistance).
- Both elements are connected **across the heart**, which acts as a periodic current source $Q_{in}(t)$.

**Schematic:**

```
        ┌────[C]────┐
        │           │
  (Q_in)            │
        │           │
        └────[R]────┘
            │
           (Out)
```

**Assumptions:**

1. The arterial system is **lumped** at one point — no spatial variation.
2. **Infinite pulse wave velocity** (no wave reflections).
3. **Linear** pressure-volume relationship: $V = C \cdot P$.
4. **Constant** peripheral resistance $R$ (no autoregulation).
5. **Steady-state periodic** operation — same waveform each cycle.
6. Blood is treated as a **Newtonian fluid** and the flow is **laminar**.

**Advantages:**

- **Mathematically simple** — closed-form analytical solution exists.
- Provides a **first-order approximation** of diastolic pressure decay.
- Useful for **non-invasive cardiac output estimation**.
- Easy to extract parameters $R$ and $C$ from a single pressure waveform.

**Limitations:**

- **No wave reflection** modeling → cannot predict dicrotic notch accurately.
- **Overestimates compliance** because the early rapid pressure drop (caused by $Z_0$) is wrongly attributed to compliance discharge.
- **Cannot represent the high-frequency input impedance** of the aorta.
- Treats the entire arterial system as a single compartment — ignores regional differences.
- **Linear assumption fails** in hypertension and aging (vessels become nonlinear and stiff).

*(2 marks: Diagram; 2 marks: Assumptions; 2 marks: Advantages; 1 mark: Limitations)*

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] The aortic pressure of a patient decays from 110 mmHg to 78 mmHg during a diastolic interval of 0.6 s. Assuming a 2-Element Windkessel model, calculate: (i) the time constant $\tau = RC$, and (ii) the total peripheral resistance $R$ if the compliance is $C = 1.1$ mL/mmHg.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $P_{es} = 110$ mmHg at $t = 0$
- $P_{ed} = 78$ mmHg at $t = 0.6$ s
- $C = 1.1$ mL/mmHg

**Part (i): Time Constant $\tau$**

Using the diastolic decay formula:

$$P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

At $t = 0.6$ s, $P = 78$ mmHg:

$$78 = 110\,e^{-0.6/\tau}$$

**Step 1:** Divide both sides by 110:

$$\frac{78}{110} = e^{-0.6/\tau} \implies 0.7091 = e^{-0.6/\tau}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(0.7091) = -\frac{0.6}{\tau}$$

$$-0.3436 = -\frac{0.6}{\tau}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{0.6}{0.3436} \approx 1.746 \text{ s}$$

**Part (ii): Peripheral Resistance $R$**

Using $\tau = RC$:

$$R = \frac{\tau}{C} = \frac{1.746}{1.1} \approx 1.587 \text{ mmHg·s/mL}$$

**Verification of Physiological Plausibility:** The value $R \approx 1.6$ mmHg·s/mL is slightly higher than the normal range, consistent with mild peripheral vasoconstriction (e.g., early hypertension). The patient warrants clinical follow-up.

*(2 marks: Setup; 1 mark: Log evaluation; 1 mark: Time constant; 1 mark: Resistance formula; 1 mark: Numerical answer; 1 mark: Clinical interpretation)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this and read it 30 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- **Lumped Parameter Model:** Spatially averaged 0-D model of the circulation using $R$, $C$, $L$ analogs.
- **Windkessel:** German for "air chamber" — the elastic reservoir function of the aorta.
- **Compliance $C$:** Slope of the pressure-volume curve of an artery ($\Delta V / \Delta P$).
- **Peripheral Resistance $R$:** Total opposition to flow in the small arteries and arterioles ($\Delta P / Q$).
- **Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$:** High-frequency input impedance of the aorta.
- **Time Constant $\tau = RC$:** Duration over which diastolic pressure falls to $\sim 37\%$ of its initial value.

### ✅ Must-Memorize Equations
- $V = C \cdot P$
- $Q = P / R$
- $\tau = R \cdot C$
- $P(t) = P_{es}\,e^{-t/\tau}$ (diastolic decay — the #1 most tested formula)
- $SV \approx C(P_s - P_d) = C \cdot PP$
- $\tau = (t_2 - t_1) / \ln(P_1 / P_2)$
- $C(dP/dt) + P/R = Q_{in}(t)$ (governing ODE)

### ✅ Must-Know Typical Values
- $R \approx 1.0$–$1.3$ mmHg·s/mL
- $C \approx 1.0$–$1.5$ mL/mmHg
- $Z_0 \approx 0.05$ mmHg·s/mL
- $\tau = RC \approx 1$ s
- Stroke Volume $SV \approx 70$ mL
- Pulse Pressure $PP \approx 40$ mmHg

### ✅ Must-Draw Diagrams
- 2-Element Windkessel circuit ($C \parallel R$)
- 3-Element Windkessel circuit ($Z_0$ in series with $C \parallel R$)
- 4-Element Windkessel circuit (adds $L$)
- Aortic pressure waveform with systolic peak, dicrotic notch, diastolic decay

### ✅ Examiner's Pet Topics
- **Derivation of diastolic decay** (always full marks if steps shown).
- **Calculation of $SV$ from $C$ and pulse pressure**.
- **Comparison table** of 2- vs 3- vs 4-Element models.
- **Numerical problem on time constant extraction** from two pressure points.
- **Clinical correlation** (e.g., reduced $\tau$ in arteriosclerosis).

### ✅ Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting that decay formula applies **only during diastole**.
- Mixing up $Z_0$ and $R$ (one is high-frequency, the other DC).
- Using the wrong time units ($R$ in mmHg·s/mL vs Pa·s/m³).
- Skipping the differential equation step in derivations.
- Drawing circuits without proper labels — KTU deducts for this.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:**
> 1. **Start with a circuit diagram** (1 free mark).
> 2. **State all given quantities with units** (builds examiner's confidence).
> 3. **Write the governing ODE** before plugging in numbers.
> 4. **Box the final answer** with explicit units.
> 5. **Add a 1-line physiological interpretation** — KTU awards "depth" marks for this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windkessel Simplification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Windkessel Simplification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — WINDKESSEL SIMPLIFICATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Windkessel Model** is a *lumped-parameter* hemodynamic model that simplifies the distributed, branching arterial tree of the systemic circulation into a finite number of discrete electrical-analog elements. It captures the pressure–flow relationship of the entire arterial system using combinations of **resistance (R)**, **compliance (C)**, **characteristic impedance (Z_c)**, and **inertance (L)**. The term *"Windkessel"* is German for **"air chamber"**, named after the historical fire-engine pump mechanism that used an air-filled dome to convert pulsatile pump output into steady outflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Windkessel model treats the systemic arteries as a single compliant chamber (the aorta and large elastic arteries) in parallel with the peripheral resistance. It is a **zero-dimensional (0-D) lumped model** of the cardiovascular system and represents the simplest tractable analog of arterial hemodynamics for engineering analysis.

The model was first introduced by the German physiologist **Otto Frank in 1899** to mathematically describe the diastolic pressure decay observed in the aorta. It is the foundational block upon which all modern distributed (1-D and 3-D) arterial models are conceptually built.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **fire-fighting water pump** in the 19th century. When the piston pushes, water spurts out in violent jerks. To smooth this into a continuous flow, engineers fitted a sealed dome containing **trapped air** above the water outlet.

*   **During the pump stroke (Systole):** Water rushes in. The trapped air compresses, *storing energy*. Pressure rises.
*   **Between strokes (Diastole):** The piston stops, but the compressed air *pushes the water out smoothly* through the hose.

The aorta behaves in exactly this way. Its elastic walls (similar to trapped air) stretch during ventricular ejection, storing blood and pressure energy. When the aortic valve closes, the recoil of the walls drives blood through the peripheral vessels in a more continuous stream.

**Blood Density Reference (for inertial calculations):**

> Standard physiological value: **ρ_blood ≈ 1060 kg/m³**
> Arterial blood viscosity: **μ ≈ 3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> Typical aortic radius: **r ≈ 1.2 cm**, length: **L ≈ 0.4 m**

### 1.3 Why Simplification is Needed

The human arterial tree contains **~10⁹** vessel segments in series–parallel arrangement. Solving Navier–Stokes equations for every segment is computationally impossible for clinical and physiological analysis. The Windkessel model reduces this to **2, 3, or 4 elements**, capturing the dominant physics:

*   Pressure drop across microcirculation → **R**
*   Elastic storage in large arteries → **C**
*   Inertial effects of blood mass → **L**
*   Local pressure–flow coupling at aortic root → **Z_c**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The Windkessel is the *equivalent circuit* of the arterial system, analogous to how a Thevenin equivalent replaces a complex resistor network with one voltage source and one resistance. It allows cardiac output estimation, compliance measurement, and pulse-contour analysis in clinical monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Windkessel Models

### A. Two-Element Windkessel (Frank, 1899)

The simplest model. It contains **only two elements** connected in parallel between the aorta and the venous return:

*   **R** — Total Peripheral Resistance (TPR)
*   **C** — Total Arterial Compliance

During **systole**, the heart ejects blood into the compliance C, raising the aortic pressure. During **diastole**, the aortic valve closes and C discharges exponentially through R.

> [!NOTE]
> Limitation: It cannot reproduce the high-frequency components of the pressure waveform (e.g., the systolic peak and dicrotic notch).

### B. Three-Element Windkessel (Westerhof et al., 1971)

Adds **characteristic impedance Z_c** in series with the parallel R–C combination.

*   **Z_c** represents the impedance of the aorta itself at high frequencies.
*   It correctly reproduces both the *low-frequency* (resistive) and *high-frequency* (impedance) behaviour.

### C. Four-Element Windkessel (Stergiopulos, 1992)

Adds **inertance L** in series with Z_c. This captures the inertial lag of blood mass during rapid ejection.

*   Most accurate of the lumped models.
*   Required for high-fidelity pulse-wave analysis.

## 2.2 Electrical–Hydraulic Analogy

| Electrical Quantity | Hydraulic Analog |
|---|---|
| Voltage (V) | Pressure (P, mmHg) |
| Current (I) | Volume flow (Q, mL/s) |
| Resistance (R) | Peripheral resistance (R = ΔP/Q) |
| Capacitance (C) | Arterial compliance (ΔV/ΔP) |
| Inductance (L) | Blood inertance (L = ρ·L_geom/A) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this analogy works:** Both systems obey the same differential equations derived from conservation of energy and continuity. Pressure is the driving "potential", and flow is the resulting "current".

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU Part B questions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(1)} \quad & \tau \;=\; R \cdot C \quad &&\text{Diastolic time constant (s)} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(2)} \quad & P(t) \;=\; P_d \, e^{-t/\tau} \quad &&\text{Diastolic pressure decay} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(3)} \quad & \text{MAP} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt \quad &&\text{Mean arterial pressure} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(4)} \quad & \text{MAP} \;=\; \text{CO} \cdot R_{\text{total}} \quad &&\text{Steady-flow relation} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(5)} \quad & C \;=\; \frac{\tau}{R} \quad &&\text{Compliance from decay} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(6)} \quad & \text{MAP} \;\approx\; P_d \;+\; \tfrac{1}{3}\,(P_s - P_d) \quad &&\text{Empirical shortcut} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(7)} \quad & Z_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho \cdot c}{A}} \quad &&\text{Characteristic impedance (3-elem.)} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(8)} \quad & L \;=\; \frac{\rho \cdot \ell}{A} \quad &&\text{Inertance (4-elem.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
* $P_d$ = end-diastolic pressure (mmHg)
* $P_s$ = peak systolic pressure (mmHg)
* $\tau$ = RC time constant (s)
* CO = cardiac output (L/min)
* $c$ = pulse wave velocity (m/s)
* $A$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
* $\rho$ = blood density (kg/m³)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Windkessel formulation is the cornerstone of:

*   **Cardiac output monitors** in ICUs (pulse-contour devices like PiCCO, LiDCO)
*   **Total artificial heart design** — for sizing compliance chambers
*   **Aortic stent-graft testing** — to simulate boundary conditions
*   **Hypertension modelling** — separating *resistive* vs *stiffness* components of elevated BP
*   **Biomedical signal processing** — for fitting pressure waveforms to estimate compliance and resistance

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Diastolic Pressure Decay Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** in Windkessel analysis and a guaranteed KTU favourite.

### Setup
After the aortic valve closes, no blood enters the aorta. The compliance C discharges through the peripheral resistance R. We apply **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the node between R and C.

### Step 1: Write the Conservation Equation

During diastole, the **inflow to the node is zero**. The flow leaving through R must equal the flow leaving the compliance C.

$$
Q_C(t) \;+\; Q_R(t) \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Element Relations

For a capacitor (compliance): the flow out equals the rate of decrease of stored volume, which by definition of compliance equals $C \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dt}$.

$$
Q_C(t) \;=\; C \cdot \frac{dP(t)}{dt}
$$

For a resistor (peripheral resistance): flow follows Ohm's law.

$$
Q_R(t) \;=\; \frac{P(t)}{R}
$$

### Step 3: Form the Differential Equation

$$
C \cdot \frac{dP(t)}{dt} \;+\; \frac{P(t)}{R} \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 4: Rearrange into Standard Separable Form

$$
\frac{dP}{P} \;=\; -\frac{1}{RC}\,dt
$$

### Step 5: Integrate Both Sides

Apply initial condition $P(0) = P_d$ (the pressure at the instant the aortic valve closes).

$$
\int_{P_d}^{P(t)} \frac{dP}{P} \;=\; -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P(t)}{P_d}\right) \;=\; -\frac{t}{RC}
$$

### Step 6: Exponentiate to Obtain Final Result

$$
\boxed{\;P(t) \;=\; P_d \, e^{-t/\tau}\quad \text{where} \quad \tau = RC\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The aortic pressure decays exponentially with time constant $\tau$. If you plot $\ln P(t)$ versus $t$ during diastole, you get a straight line whose slope equals $-1/\tau$. This is the basis of all clinical compliance estimation techniques.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Mean Arterial Pressure from the Windkessel Model

### Step 1: Average the Pressure Over One Cardiac Cycle

$$
\text{MAP} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt
$$

### Step 2: Use Steady-State Conservation

Over one cycle, the **net change in stored volume is zero** (compliance returns to its starting state). Therefore the total volume ejected by the heart (= CO $\times$ $T$) equals the total volume drained through R.

$$
\text{CO} \cdot T \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} \frac{P(t)}{R}\,dt
$$

### Step 3: Rearrange

$$
\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt \;=\; \text{CO} \cdot R
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MAP} \;=\; \text{CO} \cdot R_{\text{total}}\;}
$$

**Insight:** This is the **Ohm's law of the entire cardiovascular system**. Despite pulsatile flow, the average pressure equals average flow times resistance — a direct consequence of energy conservation over one cycle.

---

## 3.3 Compliance Estimation from a Pressure Trace

A KTU favourite: given the diastolic pressure at two time instants, find C.

### Step 1: Apply the Decay Equation Twice

$$
P(t_1) \;=\; P_d \, e^{-t_1/\tau}
$$

$$
P(t_2) \;=\; P_d \, e^{-t_2/\tau}
$$

### Step 2: Take the Ratio (eliminates $P_d$)

$$
\frac{P(t_2)}{P(t_1)} \;=\; e^{-(t_2 - t_1)/\tau}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\tau$

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{t_2 - t_1}{\ln\!\big(P(t_1)/P(t_2)\big)}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Compliance

Since $\tau = RC$, we get:

$$
\boxed{\;C \;=\; \frac{\tau}{R} \;=\; \frac{(t_2 - t_1)}{R \cdot \ln\!\big(P(t_1)/P(t_2)\big)}\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Empirical MAP Formula (Verification)

A useful sanity check often asked in KTU papers.

### Step 1: Split the Cardiac Cycle

Assume systole occupies $T_s$ and diastole $T_d = T - T_s$, with $T_s/T \approx 1/3$ for a healthy adult at rest.

### Step 2: Approximate the Pressure Waveform

Approximate the pressure during systole as a triangle rising to $P_s$ and falling linearly, and during diastole as the exponential decay derived above.

### Step 3: Compute the Time Average

After algebra (omitted for brevity in exam, but using $\int_{0}^{T_d} P_d e^{-t/\tau}dt = P_d \tau (1 - e^{-T_d/\tau})$ and assuming $\tau \gg T_d$), one obtains:

$$
\text{MAP} \;\approx\; P_d \;+\; \frac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)
$$

This is the **well-known clinical formula** that allows MAP to be estimated from a single cuff measurement of systolic and diastolic pressure.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem:** A patient has peripheral resistance $R = 1.2$ mmHg·s/mL, aortic compliance $C = 1.5$ mL/mmHg, and end-diastolic pressure $P_d = 80$ mmHg. The diastolic period lasts $T_d = 0.55$ s.

**Find:** (i) The diastolic time constant $\tau$. (ii) The pressure at the end of diastole.

### Solution:

**Step (i):**

$$
\tau \;=\; R \cdot C \;=\; (1.2)(1.5) \;=\; 1.8 \text{ s}
$$

**Step (ii):**

$$
P(T_d) \;=\; P_d \cdot e^{-T_d/\tau} \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-0.55/1.8}
$$

$$
P(T_d) \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-0.3056} \;=\; 80 \cdot 0.7367
$$

$$
\boxed{\;P(T_d) \;\approx\; 58.9 \text{ mmHg}\;}
$$

**Clinical interpretation:** The pressure dropped from 80 mmHg to ~59 mmHg during diastole — physiologically realistic for a healthy adult.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Windkessel Model Hierarchy and Function

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heart Left Ventricle] --> B[Aortic Valve]
    B --> C[Aortic Arch Windkessel Chamber]

    subgraph lumped["Lumped Arterial Model"]
        Zc[Characteristic Impedance Zc]
        L[Inertance L Blood Mass]
        C1[Compliance C Aortic Elasticity]
        R1[Peripheral Resistance R Microcirculation]
    end

    C --> Zc
    Zc --> L
    L --> C1
    C1 --> R1
    R1 --> D[Venous Return to Right Heart]

    subgraph phases["Operating Phases"]
        E1[SYSTOLE: Ventricle ejects blood into compliance C Pressure rises]
        E2[DIASTOLE: Aortic valve closes Compliance C discharges through R Pressure decays as P_d exp minus t by RC]
    end

    Zc --- phases
    R1 --- phases

    subgraph outputs["Clinical Outputs from Model"]
        O1[Total Peripheral Resistance R]
        O2[Aortic Compliance C]
        O3[Mean Arterial Pressure MAP]
        O4[Cardiac Output CO]
    end

    R1 --> O1
    C1 --> O2
    C1 --> O3
    R1 --> O4
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Electrical Analog Circuit Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Pressure Source P from Heart] --> Zc[Zc Characteristic Impedance]
    Zc --> N((Node Aorta))
    N --> C[C Compliance capacitor]
    N --> R[R Peripheral Resistance]
    C -.->|stores during systole| N
    R -.->|drains during diastole| N
    N --> O[Outflow to systemic circulation]

    style V fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style Zc fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style C fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style R fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#996600
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Windkessel Variants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Windkessel Family] --> B[2 Element Frank 1899]
    A --> C[3 Element Westerhof 1971]
    A --> D[4 Element Stergiopulos 1992]

    B --> B1[Parallel R and C only]
    B --> B2[Captures diastolic decay only]
    B --> B3[No systolic peak reproduction]

    C --> C1[Series Zc plus parallel R and C]
    C --> C2[Reproduces low and high frequency]
    C --> C3[Industry standard for clinical CO]

    D --> D1[Series L plus series Zc plus parallel R and C]
    D --> D2[Captures inertial lag]
    D --> D3[Used in research and high fidelity simulations]

    style A fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333
    style B fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#900
    style C fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#006
    style D fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#060
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each oval/block represents one lumped element. Flow direction follows blood: from left ventricle → through aortic valve → into the elastic aorta (C) → out through peripheral vessels (R). The exponential decay of pressure during diastole is the dominant transient response of the model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the Windkessel model. List the two elements used in the original 2-Element Windkessel and state their physiological significance. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Windkessel model is a *lumped-parameter* hemodynamic analog that simplifies the systemic arterial circulation into discrete electrical elements representing blood flow and pressure.

The two elements in the original Frank (1899) 2-Element Windkessel are:

| Element | Physiological Significance |
|---|---|
| **Resistance (R)** | Represents the total peripheral resistance (TPR) offered by arterioles and capillaries; converts mean pressure into mean flow (CO). |
| **Compliance (C)** | Represents the elastic storage capacity of the aorta and large arteries; stores blood during systole and releases it during diastole. |

Together, they produce the exponential pressure decay $P(t) = P_d \, e^{-t/RC}$ during diastole.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is meant by the "diastolic time constant" $\tau$ of the arterial system? Write its expression and state its typical physiological range in a healthy adult. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The diastolic time constant $\tau$ is the time required for the aortic pressure to decay to $1/e$ (approximately 36.8%) of its end-diastolic value after the aortic valve closes.

$$
\tau \;=\; R \cdot C
$$

* **Typical range:** $\tau \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ seconds in a healthy resting adult.
* **Clinical significance:** A *short* $\tau$ indicates stiff arteries (reduced compliance) or elevated peripheral resistance — both hallmarks of aging, hypertension, and arteriosclerosis. A *long* $\tau$ indicates a highly compliant, low-resistance system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for aortic pressure decay during diastole using the 2-Element Windkessel model. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A patient has the following hemodynamic parameters:
* Peripheral Resistance: $R = 1.0$ mmHg·s/mL
* Aortic Compliance: $C = 2.0$ mL/mmHg
* End-diastolic Pressure: $P_d = 80$ mmHg
* Heart Rate: 70 bpm
* Systolic Pressure: $P_s = 120$ mmHg

Calculate: (i) the diastolic time constant $\tau$, (ii) the pressure 0.4 s after valve closure, and (iii) the mean arterial pressure using the empirical formula. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Derivation of Aortic Pressure Decay:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The aortic valve is closed during diastole (no inflow from the heart).
2. The compliance C and resistance R are linear, time-invariant, and lumped.
3. Blood is incompressible; venous pressure ≈ 0 mmHg (reference).
4. No wave reflections (Windkessel ignores distributed effects).

**Derivation:**

Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law at the aortic node:

$$
C \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} \;+\; \frac{P}{R} \;=\; 0
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} \;=\; -\frac{1}{RC}\,dt
$$

Integrate with initial condition $P(0) = P_d$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_d}\right) \;=\; -\frac{t}{RC}
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
\boxed{\;P(t) \;=\; P_d \, e^{-t/\tau}\;,\qquad \tau = RC\;}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Diastolic time constant:**

$$
\tau \;=\; R \cdot C \;=\; (1.0)(2.0) \;=\; \mathbf{2.0 \;s}
$$

**(ii) Pressure after 0.4 s:**

$$
P(0.4) \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-0.4/2.0} \;=\; 80 \cdot e^{-0.2}
$$

$$
P(0.4) \;=\; 80 \cdot 0.8187 \;\approx\; \mathbf{65.5 \;\text{mmHg}}
$$

**(iii) Mean arterial pressure (empirical formula):**

$$
\text{MAP} \;\approx\; P_d \;+\; \tfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d) \;=\; 80 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{3}(120 - 80)
$$

$$
\text{MAP} \;=\; 80 \;+\; 13.3 \;\approx\; \mathbf{93.3 \;\text{mmHg}}
$$

**Verification via the model relation:** MAP = CO $\times$ R. Assuming CO = 5 L/min = 83.3 mL/s:

$$
\text{MAP} \;=\; 83.3 \times 1.0 \;\approx\; 83.3 \;\text{mmHg}
$$

The discrepancy arises because the empirical formula is an approximation valid when systole occupies ~1/3 of the cycle. The full Windkessel integral should be used for higher accuracy.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **3-Element Windkessel model**. How does it improve upon the 2-Element model? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **steady-state relation MAP = CO × R** for the systemic circulation. State its clinical significance in measuring cardiac output non-invasively. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) 3-Element Windkessel Model:**

The 3-Element Windkessel adds **characteristic impedance $Z_c$** of the proximal aorta in series with the parallel R-C combination of the 2-Element model.

**Circuit elements:**
* $Z_c$ (in series): Represents the local pressure–flow coupling at the aortic root. At high frequencies, the impedance seen by the heart approaches $Z_c$.
* $R$ (in parallel): Total peripheral resistance.
* $C$ (in parallel): Total aortic compliance.

**Improvement over the 2-Element model:**
1. The 2-Element model behaves as a *pure low-pass filter* — it cannot reproduce the systolic peak or high-frequency components of the aortic pressure waveform.
2. The 3-Element model reproduces both **low-frequency** behaviour (resistance dominates) and **high-frequency** behaviour (impedance $Z_c$ dominates), giving an accurate pressure waveform over the entire cardiac cycle.
3. It correctly predicts the initial systolic rise and the dicrotic notch, which are essential for accurate cardiac output estimation.

**Total input impedance of the 3-Element model:**

$$
Z_{\text{in}}(j\omega) \;=\; Z_c \;+\; \frac{R}{1 \;+\; j\omega R C}
$$

For $\omega \to 0$: $Z_{\text{in}} \to Z_c + R \approx R$ (since $Z_c \ll R$).
For $\omega \to \infty$: $Z_{\text{in}} \to Z_c$ (capacitor short-circuits R).

---

**(b) Steady-State MAP Relation:**

**Derivation:**

Over one cardiac cycle of duration $T$, conservation of volume demands:

$$
\text{Volume ejected} \;=\; \text{Volume drained through R}
$$

$$
\text{CO} \cdot T \;=\; \int_{0}^{T} \frac{P(t)}{R}\,dt
$$

Since $R$ is constant:

$$
\text{CO} \cdot T \;=\; \frac{1}{R}\int_{0}^{T} P(t)\,dt \;=\; \frac{\text{MAP} \cdot T}{R}
$$

Cancelling $T$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{MAP} \;=\; \text{CO} \cdot R_{\text{total}}\;}
$$

**Clinical Significance:**
1. Allows **non-invasive estimation of cardiac output** if MAP and peripheral resistance are measured: $\text{CO} = \text{MAP}/R$.
2. Forms the theoretical basis of **pulse-contour cardiac output devices** (PiCCO, LiDCO, FloTrac) used in ICU monitoring.
3. In **cardiac catheterization labs**, $R$ is determined from mean pressure and measured CO; deviations from normal $R$ flag vascular pathology.
4. Provides the **foundation for diagnosing hypertension**: separating *high-CO* (volume overload) from *high-R* (vasoconstriction) hypertension.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Windkessel Questions:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Pressure must be in **mmHg**, flow in **mL/s**, and resistance in **mmHg·s/mL**. Mixing SI (Pa) with clinical units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the initial condition $P_d$:** Always state $P(0) = P_d$ explicitly during the derivation.
> 3. **Confusing $\tau = RC$ with the systolic ejection time:** $\tau$ is the diastolic decay constant, NOT the duration of systole.
> 4. **Treating $Z_c$ and $R$ as interchangeable:** $Z_c$ is frequency-dependent and small (~0.05 mmHg·s/mL); $R$ is large (~1.0 mmHg·s/mL). They are physically different quantities.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption statement:** The derivation of $P(t) = P_d e^{-t/\tau}$ assumes a closed aortic valve — always mention this explicitly or lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Windkessel Simplification**

*   **Windkessel = German for "air chamber"** — the historical pump mechanism analogy.
*   **Introduced by Otto Frank in 1899** as a 2-element (R, C) model.
*   **Three variants exist:** 2-Element, 3-Element (adds $Z_c$), 4-Element (adds $L$).
*   **Core analogy:** Voltage ↔ Pressure; Current ↔ Flow; R ↔ Peripheral resistance; C ↔ Compliance; L ↔ Blood inertia.
*   **Master equation:** $P(t) = P_d \, e^{-t/\tau}$, where $\tau = RC$.
*   **Steady-state relation:** $\text{MAP} = \text{CO} \times R_{\text{total}}$ — Ohm's law of the cardiovascular system.
*   **Empirical shortcut:** $\text{MAP} \approx P_d + \tfrac{1}{3}(P_s - P_d)$.
*   **Typical $\tau$ in healthy adults:** $1.5$ to $2.5$ seconds.
*   **Typical blood density:** $\rho \approx 1060$ kg/m³ (use this constant when computing inertance $L$).
*   **Clinical relevance:** Pulse-contour cardiac output monitors, hypertension diagnosis, artificial heart design, stent-graft testing.
*   **Limitation:** Wave reflections and spatial pressure distribution are NOT captured — for those, use 1-D distributed models.
*   **Compliance formula from pressure trace:** $C = (t_2 - t_1) \,/\, [R \cdot \ln(P(t_1)/P(t_2))]$.
*   **2-Element Windkessel cannot reproduce the systolic peak** — this is the key reason Westerhof extended it.
*   **For 4 marks or above derivations:** Always (1) state assumptions, (2) write the KCL/node equation, (3) integrate with explicit limits, (4) solve for the constant, (5) interpret the result clinically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heart as a Pump - Variable Capacitor Model of the Heart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT601 - BIOMECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heart as a Pump - Variable Capacitor Model of the Heart # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Variable Capacitor Model of the Heart** is an electrical analog model used in biomechanics to represent the mechanical filling and ejection behavior of the cardiac chambers. In this model, each cardiac chamber (atria and ventricles) is represented as a **time-varying capacitor** whose capacitance changes with the contractile state of the myocardium. The heart is treated as a **pulsatile, variable-impedance pump** that stores blood (analogous to electrical charge) under pressure (analogous to voltage) and discharges it into the arterial system during systole. The variable nature of the capacitor arises because **ventricular compliance (C)** is not constant — it is high during diastole (relaxed myocardium, easy filling) and effectively reduced during systole (contracted myocardium, ejection). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCBMT601 Module 4):** > The heart is modeled as a **variable capacitor** because the ventricular wall stiffness changes dramatically between the relaxed (diastolic) and contracted (systolic) states. The compliance $C = dV/dP$ is high in diastole and low in systole — making $C$ a **time-varying parameter**, hence "variable" capacitor. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **flexible rubber balloon connected to a water tap and a narrow outlet pipe**: - When the balloon is **relaxed (diastole)**, it stretches easily. Water flows in and is stored in a large volume at relatively low pressure → this is like a **large capacitor** being **charged**. - When the balloon **contracts (systole)**, it becomes stiff and squeezes water out through the outlet → this is like a **small capacitor** being **discharged** rapidly. The balloon's ability to stretch is its **compliance** — exactly analogous to **capacitance** in an electrical circuit. A stretchier balloon (higher compliance) stores more fluid per unit pressure, just as a larger capacitor stores more charge per unit voltage. | Electrical Quantity | Cardiovascular Analog | |---|---| | Voltage ($V$) | Pressure ($P$) | | Charge ($Q$) | Blood Volume ($V_{blood}$) | | Capacitance ($C$) | Compliance ($C = \Delta V / \Delta P$) | | Current ($I = dQ/dt$) | Blood Flow ($Q_{flow} = dV_{blood}/dt$) | | Resistor ($R$) | Vascular Resistance ($R$) | | Time constant ($\tau = RC$) | Diastolic filling time constant | > [!NOTE] > **Why "Variable" and not just "Capacitor"?** > In a passive electrical capacitor, capacitance is a fixed geometric property ($C = \varepsilon A / d$). But the heart's compliance is **actively modulated by the muscle contractile state** — it changes every cardiac cycle. Therefore, the model is a *time-varying* capacitor governed by: $C = C(t)$. ## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters - **Normal cardiac output (CO):** $\mathbf{5 \text{ L/min}}$ at rest - **Heart rate (HR):** $\mathbf{60-100 \text{ bpm}}$ (resting $\approx 72$ bpm) - **Stroke volume (SV):** $\mathbf{\approx 70 \text{ mL/beat}}$ - **End-diastolic volume (EDV):** $\mathbf{\approx 120 \text{ mL}}$ - **End-systolic volume (ESV):** $\mathbf{\approx 50 \text{ mL}}$ - **Ventricular compliance (diastolic):** $\mathbf{\approx 5 \text{ mL/mmHg}}$ (highly variable) - **Mean aortic pressure:** $\mathbf{\approx 100 \text{ mmHg}}$ - **Mean atrial pressure:** $\mathbf{\approx 5-10 \text{ mmHg}}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The relationship $CO = HR \times SV$ is the foundational equation of cardiac pumping and is implicit in any capacitor-based model of the heart. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Cardiac Cycle in the Capacitor Framework The heart's pumping action is a two-phase cyclic process mapped onto a capacitor's charge-discharge cycle: ### Phase 1 — Diastole (Charging Phase, Capacitor "Charges Up") 1. The ventricular myocardium **relaxes** → wall becomes **compliant** (high $C$). 2. The atrioventricular (AV) valves (mitral/tricuspid) **open**. 3. Blood flows from the atria (fed by pulmonary veins / vena cavae) into the ventricles down a small pressure gradient. 4. **Analogy:** A capacitor with capacitance $C$ is connected to a voltage source (venous pressure $P_v$) through a series resistor (venous resistance $R_{in}$). Charge (blood) accumulates. 5. Filling follows an **exponential rise**: $V(t) = V_{\infty}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$. 6. The time constant $\tau_{fill} = R_{in} \cdot C$ determines how quickly the ventricle fills. ### Phase 2 — Systole (Discharge Phase, Capacitor "Discharges") 1. The ventricular myocardium **contracts** → wall becomes **stiff** (low $C$). 2. The AV valves **close** (first heart sound, S1); semilunar valves (aortic/pulmonic) **open**. 3. Blood is ejected into the aorta/pulmonary artery against high arterial pressure. 4. **Analogy:** The capacitor's capacitance suddenly decreases while the stored volume is forcibly ejected against a high-pressure load (arterial resistance $R_{out}$). 5. The Frank-Starling mechanism ensures the stroke volume is proportional to the end-diastolic volume (preload). ### Phase 3 — Isovolumetric Contraction/Relaxation (Brief "Open-Circuit" Intervals) - All four valves are closed; the ventricle acts as a **sealed capacitor** with no current flow (no volume change), but pressure rises (contraction) or falls (relaxation) rapidly. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Variable Nature The variable capacitor model captures a fundamental biophysical truth: **the same chamber has different pressure-volume (P-V) relationships in diastole vs. systole.** On a P-V diagram: - **Diastolic P-V curve:** Shallow slope (high compliance) → large $\Delta V$ per unit $\Delta P$. - **Systolic P-V curve:** Steep slope (low compliance) → small $\Delta V$ per unit $\Delta P$. This is why the compliance $C = dV/dP$ is a **function of time and contractile state**, not a constant. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are exam-ready for PCBMT601 Module 4 derivations and numerical problems. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V = C \cdot P$ | Volume-pressure relationship (capacitor analog) | mL, mL/mmHg, mmHg | | 2 | $C = dV/dP$ | Definition of compliance (chamber stiffness reciprocal) | mL/mmHg | | 3 | $C(t) = C_{diastole} \cdot f_{relax}(t) - C_{contract}(t)$ | Variable compliance time-function | mL/mmHg | | 4 | $Q_{flow} = dV/dt = \frac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{R}$ | Filling/ejection flow (Ohm's law analog) | mL/s, mmHg, mmHg·s/mL | | 5 | $V(t) = V_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)$ | Capacitor charging — diastolic filling | mL, s, mmHg·s/mL | | 6 | $V(t) = V_{0}\, e^{-t/(RC)}$ | Capacitor discharging — systolic ejection | mL, s, mmHg·s/mL | | 7 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Time constant of filling/ejection | s | | 8 | $t_{1/2} = 0.693 \cdot \tau$ | Time to half-fill (or half-empty) | s | | 9 | $SV = EDV - ESV$ | Stroke volume | mL | | 10 | $CO = HR \times SV$ | Cardiac output | mL/min or L/min | | 11 | $P(t) = P_{0} + \frac{V(t)}{C(t)}$ | Instantaneous ventricular pressure | mmHg | | 12 | $E(t) = \frac{P(t)}{V(t) - V_{0}}$ | Time-varying elastance (inverse of compliance) | mmHg/mL | | 13 | $W = \int P\, dV$ | Stroke work (area enclosed in P-V loop) | mmHg·mL = J (after conversion) | | 14 | $\eta = \frac{W_{useful}}{W_{total}} \approx 0.10-0.20$ | Cardiac efficiency (very low!) | dimensionless | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pacemaker & ICD Design:** Variable capacitor models are embedded in lumped-parameter cardiovascular simulators used to test implantable devices. - **Heart Failure Diagnosis:** A reduced diastolic compliance curve is the hallmark of **diastolic dysfunction (HFpEF)**. - **Cardiac Assist Devices (LVADs):** Engineers use the variable capacitor framework to model how a left ventricular assist device unloads the heart. - **Hemodynamic Simulation:** Whole-circulation lumped-parameter models (e.g., the 4-element Windkessel + heart) are built on this analog. - **Drug Response Modeling:** How inotropes (e.g., dobutamine) shift the systolic compliance curve can be simulated in this framework. > [!IMPORTANT] > The variable capacitor model is **the cornerstone of modern lumped-parameter cardiovascular simulation** and forms the mathematical basis of the famous **Suga-Sagawa time-varying elastance model** ($E_{max}$ concept), which is widely tested in KTU numerical problems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Basic Capacitor Analogy: $V = C \cdot P$ **Starting Premise:** A capacitor's fundamental defining equation in linear circuit theory is: $$Q = C \cdot V$$ **Step 1 — Substitute cardiovascular analogs.** We map charge $Q \rightarrow$ blood volume $V_{blood}$, voltage $V \rightarrow$ pressure $P$, and capacitance $C \rightarrow$ compliance $C$: $$V_{blood} = C \cdot P$$ **Step 2 — Interpret physically.** This equation says: *The volume of blood stored in a cardiac chamber is the product of the chamber's compliance and the transmural pressure across its wall.* **Step 3 — Differentiate to obtain compliance definition.** Taking the differential of both sides: $$dV_{blood} = C \cdot dP + P \cdot dC$$ For a constant compliance ($dC = 0$), this reduces to: $$C = \frac{dV_{blood}}{dP}$$ This is the **slope of the diastolic pressure-volume relationship** — exactly the definition of compliance used in clinical cardiology. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diastolic Filling Equation (Capacitor "Charging") **Starting Premise:** During diastole, the relaxed ventricle fills through the open AV valve. Blood flow from atrium to ventricle is governed by the pressure gradient divided by inflow resistance: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{P_{atrium} - P_{ventricle}(t)}{R_{in}}$$ **Step 1 — Relate ventricular pressure to current volume using the capacitor relation:** $$P_{ventricle}(t) = \frac{V(t)}{C_{diastole}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the flow equation:** $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{P_{atrium} - \dfrac{V(t)}{C_{diastole}}}{R_{in}}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange into a standard first-order linear ODE:** $$\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V(t)}{R_{in} \cdot C_{diastole}} = \frac{P_{atrium}}{R_{in}}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the time constant.** Let $\tau = R_{in} \cdot C_{diastole}$. The ODE becomes: $$\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V(t)}{\tau} = \frac{P_{atrium}}{R_{in}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve by separation of variables / integrating factor.** The standard solution of $\dot{x} + x/\tau = k$ is: $$x(t) = x_{\infty} + \left(x_{0} - x_{\infty}\right) e^{-t/\tau}$$ where $x_{\infty} = k\tau$ is the steady-state value. **Step 6 — Identify the steady-state volume.** At steady state, $dV/dt = 0$, so $V_{\infty} = P_{atrium} \cdot C_{diastole}$: $$\boxed{\,V(t) = P_{atrium} \cdot C_{diastole} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / (R_{in} C_{diastole})}\right)\,}$$ This is the **diastolic filling curve** — ventricular volume rises exponentially from $0$ toward $P_{atrium} \cdot C_{diastole}$ with time constant $\tau$. **Step 7 — At $t = \tau$, the ventricle is filled to $(1 - e^{-1}) \approx 63.2\%$ of its final volume.** At $t = 3\tau$, it is $\approx 95\%$ filled (effectively complete). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Systolic Ejection Equation (Capacitor "Discharging") **Starting Premise:** During systole, the contracted ventricle ejects blood into the aorta through the open semilunar valve against arterial pressure $P_{art}$: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{P_{ventricle}(t) - P_{art}}{R_{out}}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute $P_{ventricle}(t) = V(t) / C_{systole}$:** $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\dfrac{V(t)}{C_{systole}} - P_{art}}{R_{out}}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange:** $$\frac{dV}{dt} - \frac{V(t)}{R_{out} \cdot C_{systole}} = -\frac{P_{art}}{R_{out}}$$ Let $\tau_{ej} = R_{out} \cdot C_{systole}$: $$\frac{dV}{dt} - \frac{V(t)}{\tau_{ej}} = -\frac{P_{art}}{R_{out}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve using integrating factor $e^{-t/\tau_{ej}}$.** The general solution is: $$\boxed{\,V(t) = P_{art} \cdot C_{systole} + \left(V_{EDV} - P_{art} \cdot C_{systole}\right) e^{-t / (R_{out} C_{systole})}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Compute stroke volume.** $SV = V(0) - V(T_{sys}) = V_{EDV} - V_{ESV}$. The fraction of EDV ejected is the **ejection fraction**: $$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} = \frac{V_{EDV} - V_{ESV}}{V_{EDV}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on units of $P \cdot C$:** If $P$ is in mmHg and $C$ in mL/mmHg, then $P \cdot C$ is in mL, which is the correct unit for volume. The product is dimensionally consistent. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — The Variable (Time-Varying) Compliance Equation **Starting Premise:** Compliance is not constant; it changes with the contractile state. Let $C = C(t)$. **Step 1 — Start from the basic volume relation:** $$V(t) = C(t) \cdot P(t)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate using the product rule:** $$\frac{dV}{dt} = C(t) \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} + P(t) \cdot \frac{dC}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the flow equation $dV/dt = (P_{in} - P_{out})/R$:** $$C(t) \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} + P(t) \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{P_{in} - P(t)}{R}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $dP/dt$:** $$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{P_{in} - P(t)}{R \cdot C(t)} - \frac{P(t)}{C(t)} \cdot \frac{dC}{dt}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret each term:** | Term | Physical Meaning | |---|---| | $\dfrac{P_{in} - P(t)}{R \cdot C(t)}$ | Pressure change due to net inflow (charging effect) | | $-\dfrac{P(t)}{C(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dt}$ | Pressure change due to active wall stiffening (contraction) | **Step 6 — During systole, $dC/dt < 0$ (compliance is decreasing) → the second term is positive → pressure rises even without inflow** (this is the **isovolumetric contraction** phase). > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **key mathematical signature of the variable capacitor model**: it can explain how pressure rises inside a *sealed* chamber during contraction, which a constant capacitor cannot. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stroke Work (Area of P-V Loop) **Starting Premise:** Mechanical work done by the ventricle per beat equals the area enclosed by the P-V loop: $$W = \oint P \, dV$$ **Step 1 — Split into ejection and filling paths:** $$W = \underbrace{\int_{EDV}^{ESV} P_{systole}(V)\, dV}_{\text{Ejection (work done by heart)}} - \underbrace{\int_{ESV}^{EDV} P_{diastole}(V)\, dV}_{\text{Filling (work done on heart)}}$$ **Step 2 — Net work = area of P-V loop = stroke work × heart rate = external cardiac power.** $$W_{stroke} = \frac{1}{2}(P_{syst,max} - P_{diast,min}) \cdot (EDV - ESV) \quad \text{(triangular approx.)}$$ **Step 3 — Converting units:** $1 \text{ mmHg} \cdot \text{mL} = 0.133 \text{ joules}$ (since $1 \text{ mmHg} = 133.3 \text{ Pa}$ and $1 \text{ mL} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$). ## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A relaxed left ventricle has compliance $C = 8 \text{ mL/mmHg}$. Left atrial pressure is $P_{LA} = 10 \text{ mmHg}$. The inflow resistance is $R = 0.05 \text{ mmHg·s/mL}$. Find: (a) the time constant $\tau$, (b) the ventricular volume at $t = 0.4$ s, and (c) the stroke volume if end-systolic volume is $50$ mL. **Solution:** **(a) Time constant:** $$\tau = R \cdot C = 0.05 \times 8 = 0.4 \text{ s}$$ **(b) Volume at $t = 0.4$ s** (note: $t = \tau$, so we are at one time constant): $$V(0.4) = P_{LA} \cdot C \cdot \left(1 - e^{-1}\right) = 10 \times 8 \times (1 - 0.368) = 80 \times 0.632 = 50.56 \text{ mL}$$ **(c) Stroke volume:** $$SV = V(0.4) - ESV = 50.56 - 50 = 0.56 \text{ mL}$$ This unrealistically small SV indicates filling is **incomplete** at $0.4$ s — the model suggests either more filling time is needed, or compliance is higher, or atrial pressure is higher (as in exercise). > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** In real diastole ($\approx 0.5$ s at rest), ventricular filling reaches $\approx 95\%$ of $V_{\infty}$ within 3 time constants, giving realistic EDVs of $\approx 120$ mL. The capacitor model reproduces this naturally. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Electrical Analog Circuit of the Heart as a Variable Capacitor ```mermaid flowchart LR sourceV1[("P_ven
Venous Pressure
(Voltage Source)")] resR1[["R_in
Inflow Resistance
(Tricuspid/Mitral Valve)"]] capC1[("C_dia t
Variable Capacitor
(Relaxed Ventricle)")] resR2[["R_out
Outflow Resistance
(Aortic/Pulmonic Valve)"]] loadP1[("P_art
Arterial Pressure
(Aortic Load)")] sourceV1 -- "I_in = dV/dt" --> resR1 resR1 -- "Charges C(t)" --> capC1 capC1 -- "P_vent = V/C" --> resR2 resR2 -- "I_out = dV/dt" --> loadP1 style sourceV1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3 style loadP1 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#b30000 style resR1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style resR2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style capC1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#38761d ``` ## 4.2 Pressure-Volume Loop Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph "Diastole (Charging Phase)" A1["Phase: Isovolumetric Relaxation
All Valves Closed
P_vent drops sharply, V constant"] A2["Phase: Rapid Filling
AV Valves Open
V rises, P_vent low"] A3["Phase: Diastasis (Slow Filling)
Minimal flow
V nearly constant"] A4["Phase: Atrial Kick
Atria Contract
V reaches EDV"] end subgraph "Systole (Discharge Phase)" B1["Phase: Isovolumetric Contraction
All Valves Closed
P_vent rises sharply, V constant"] B2["Phase: Rapid Ejection
Semilunar Valves Open
V drops, P_vent high"] B3["Phase: Reduced Ejection
V reaches ESV
Stroke Volume = EDV - ESV"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 A4 -- "EDV = max Volume" --> B1 B1 --> B2 --> B3 B3 -- "ESV = min Volume
Loop closes" --> A1 style A1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#08519c style A2 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#08519c style A3 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#08519c style A4 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#08519c style B1 fill:#fee6ce,stroke:#d94801 style B2 fill:#fee6ce,stroke:#d94801 style B3 fill:#fee6ce,stroke:#d94801 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Variable Compliance Modulation ```mermaid flowchart LR input1["SA Node Trigger
(Electrical Pacemaker)"] process1["Myocardial
Contractile State
Modulator"] output1["C(t) - Time-Varying
Compliance Output"] effect1["Ventricular
Wall Stiffness"] feedback1["P-V Loop
Hemodynamic Feedback"] input1 -->|"Action Potential"| process1 process1 -->|"Active Modulation"| output1 output1 -->|"Determines"| effect1 effect1 -->|"Drives"| feedback1 feedback1 -.->|"Autonomic
Regulation"| process1 style input1 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000 style process1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style output1 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235 style effect1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6 style feedback1 fill:#ededed,stroke:#595959 ``` ## 4.4 Equivalent Circuit — Complete Cardiac Cycle as RC Network ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph systolePhase ["Systole Switch Position: SW1 OPEN, SW2 CLOSED"] sC["C_contract
LOW Capacitance
(Stiff Ventricle)"] sRout["R_out
(Aortic Valve)"] sArt[("P_aorta")] end subgraph diastolePhase ["Diastole Switch Position: SW1 CLOSED, SW2 OPEN"] dC["C_relax
HIGH Capacitance
(Compliant Ventricle)"] dRin["R_in
(Mitral Valve)"] dVein[("P_vein")] end HeartBeat["Cardiac Pacemaker
(SA Node Timing)"] HeartBeat -- "Drives Switches" --> systolePhase HeartBeat -- "Drives Switches" --> diastolePhase dVein -- "Charges" --> dRin dRin -- "Q_flow" --> dC dC -- "Ejects" --> sRout sRout --> sArt style HeartBeat fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#7f6000 style sC fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000 style dC fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The "switches" in this diagram represent the synchronized opening/closing of cardiac valves driven by the SA node. The pacemaker acts as the **timing element** that switches the heart between a high-capacitance (charging) and low-capacitance (discharging) configuration every cardiac cycle. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define "Variable Capacitor Model" of the heart. Why is the capacitor considered "variable"? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The variable capacitor model of the heart is an **electrical analog representation** in which each cardiac chamber is modeled as a **capacitor** whose capacitance corresponds to the chamber's **compliance** ($C = dV/dP$). The capacitor is called **"variable"** because the compliance of the ventricular wall is **not constant** — it is high during diastole (relaxed, easily distensible myocardium) and low during systole (contracted, stiff myocardium). Since the capacitance changes continuously with the **contractile state of the cardiac muscle** during each cycle, $C = C(t)$ is a **time-varying parameter**, justifying the term "variable capacitor." > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write "capacitor stores energy like the heart stores blood." This is **incomplete** — you must mention the **time-varying compliance** to earn the third mark. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] Write the differential equation governing diastolic filling of the ventricle in the variable capacitor model, and identify its time constant. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The diastolic filling of a relaxed ventricle, modeled as a capacitor being charged through a venous resistance, is governed by the first-order linear ODE: $$\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V(t)}{R_{in}\, C_{diastole}} = \frac{P_{atrium}}{R_{in}}$$ The **time constant** of filling is: $$\tau_{fill} = R_{in} \cdot C_{diastole}$$ where $R_{in}$ is the inflow (AV valve) resistance and $C_{diastole}$ is the relaxed ventricular compliance. Within one time constant, the ventricle fills to $\approx 63.2\%$ of its equilibrium volume $V_{\infty} = P_{atrium} \cdot C_{diastole}$; within $3\tau$, it is $\approx 95\%$ filled. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** With neat sketches and relevant derivations, explain the **variable capacitor model of the heart**. Include in your answer: **(a)** The electrical analog circuit of a single cardiac chamber, with clearly labeled variables. **(b)** Derivation of the **diastolic filling equation** and the **systolic ejection equation**, including expressions for the corresponding time constants. **(c)** A brief discussion of how the **Frank-Starling mechanism** emerges from this model. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 4 marks** Draw the equivalent circuit (see Section 4.1 of these notes): - **Atrial / venous pressure** $P_v$ acts as a **voltage source**. - **Inflow resistance** $R_{in}$ (AV valve) is the **series resistor** during filling. - **Ventricular compliance** $C(t)$ is the **time-varying capacitor** (large in diastole, small in systole). - **Outflow resistance** $R_{out}$ (semilunar valve) is the **series resistor** during ejection. - **Arterial pressure** $P_{art}$ acts as the **load voltage**. For the analogy: $V_{blood} \leftrightarrow Q$, $P \leftrightarrow V$, $C \leftrightarrow C$, $R \leftrightarrow R$, $Q_{flow} = dV/dt \leftrightarrow I$. **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Diastolic filling (charging):** Starting from the capacitor analogy $P_{vent}(t) = V(t)/C_{dia}$ and the flow equation: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{P_{atrium} - P_{vent}(t)}{R_{in}} = \frac{P_{atrium} - V(t)/C_{dia}}{R_{in}}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V(t)}{R_{in} C_{dia}} = \frac{P_{atrium}}{R_{in}}$$ With $\tau_{fill} = R_{in} C_{dia}$, the solution is: $$V(t) = V_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{fill}}\right), \quad \text{where } V_{\infty} = P_{atrium} C_{dia}$$ **Systolic ejection (discharging):** During systole, $C$ becomes small ($C_{sys}$) and ejection occurs through $R_{out}$ against $P_{art}$: $$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{V(t)/C_{sys} - P_{art}}{R_{out}}$$ $$\frac{dV}{dt} - \frac{V(t)}{R_{out} C_{sys}} = -\frac{P_{art}}{R_{out}}$$ With $\tau_{ej} = R_{out} C_{sys}$, the solution is: $$V(t) = P_{art} C_{sys} + \left(V_{EDV} - P_{art} C_{sys}\right) e^{-t/\tau_{ej}}$$ The stroke volume is $SV = V(0) - V(T_{sys}) = V_{EDV} - V_{ESV}$. **Part (c) — 3 marks** The Frank-Starling mechanism states that **the energy of contraction (and hence stroke volume) is proportional to the end-diastolic fiber length (i.e., preload volume).** In the variable capacitor model: - A higher venous return → higher $P_{atrium}$ → higher $V_{\infty} = P_{atrium} C_{dia}$ → larger $V_{EDV}$. - A larger $V_{EDV}$ stretches the ventricular wall more. - The greater stretch causes a **more vigorous contraction** (greater $C_{sys}$ reduction), ejecting a larger $SV$. - Hence, $SV \propto V_{EDV}$ emerges **naturally** from the model: increased preload (EDV) leads to increased output (SV) — the heart self-regulates output to match input. --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]** Derive the **governing differential equation for a time-varying compliance** $C(t)$ ventricle. Show that during isovolumetric contraction (no inflow/outflow), the **ventricular pressure can rise** purely due to a decrease in compliance. Discuss the engineering significance of this result for **heart-assist device design**. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks — Derivation of the variable-compliance equation** Start with the instantaneous volume-pressure relationship for a chamber with time-varying compliance: $$V(t) = C(t) \cdot P(t)$$ Differentiate using the product rule (since both $C$ and $P$ depend on time): $$\frac{dV}{dt} = C(t)\frac{dP}{dt} + P(t)\frac{dC}{dt}$$ Substitute the flow equation $dV/dt = (P_{in} - P_{out})/R$: $$C(t)\frac{dP}{dt} + P(t)\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{R}$$ Solving for $dP/dt$: $$\boxed{\,\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{R\,C(t)} - \frac{P(t)}{C(t)}\frac{dC}{dt}\,}$$ **Part (b) — 4 marks — Isovolumetric contraction analysis** During isovolumetric contraction, all valves are closed, so $dV/dt = 0$ and the volume $V$ is constant. From $V = C(t) P(t)$: $$P(t) = \frac{V}{C(t)}$$ Differentiating: $$\frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{V}{C(t)^2}\frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{P(t)}{C(t)}\frac{dC}{dt}$$ Since the myocardium is **contracting**, $C(t)$ is **decreasing**, so $dC/dt < 0$. Therefore: $$\frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{P}{C}\cdot(\text{negative}) = \text{positive}$$ The pressure rises even though **no blood enters or leaves** the chamber. This is impossible in a constant-capacitor model (where $dP/dt = 0$ when $dV/dt = 0$) but is naturally explained by the variable capacitor. **Part (c) — 3 marks — Engineering significance for heart-assist devices** Left Ventricular Assist Devices (LVADs) are designed to **unload** the ventricle by maintaining a continuous flow that keeps $dV/dt < 0$ throughout the cycle, reducing both EDV and the workload on the heart. The variable capacitor model: - Predicts that **reducing $V$** (unloading) reduces peak $P$ — a key therapeutic goal. - Shows that **stiffening the wall pharmacologically** (inotropes) is mathematically equivalent to **reducing $C_{dia}$** — a direct engineering handle. - Guides the design of **synchronization algorithms** for counterpulsation devices (e.g., IABP) that exploit the $dC/dt$ term to assist ejection. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** When deriving the variable capacitor equation, students often **forget the product rule** and write $dV/dt = C \cdot dP/dt$ only — losing the critical $P \cdot dC/dt$ term. This is the most important term (it explains isovolumetric pressure rise) and is the **#1 reason for losing 4-5 marks** in 14-mark derivation questions. Always write: $\frac{dV}{dt} = C\frac{dP}{dt} + P\frac{dC}{dt}$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Heart as a Pump: Variable Capacitor Model** - ⭐ **Core Analogy:** Heart chamber = **time-varying capacitor**; Compliance $C = dV/dP$ = capacitance; Pressure $P$ = voltage; Volume $V_{blood}$ = charge; Flow $dV/dt$ = current. - ⭐ **Variable Nature:** Compliance is **high in diastole** (relaxed wall) and **low in systole** (contracted wall) — hence $C = C(t)$. - ⭐ **Diastolic Filling Equation:** $V(t) = P_{atrium} C_{dia} \left(1 - e^{-t/(R_{in} C_{dia})}\right)$ with $\tau_{fill} = R_{in} C_{dia}$. - ⭐ **Systolic Ejection Equation:** $V(t) = P_{art} C_{sys} + (V_{EDV} - P_{art} C_{sys}) e^{-t/(R_{out} C_{sys})}$ with $\tau_{ej} = R_{out} C_{sys}$. - ⭐ **Variable-Capacitor Master Equation:** $C(t)\frac{dP}{dt} + P(t)\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{R}$ — derived via the **product rule** (don't skip it!). - ⭐ **Isovolumetric Phases:** Pressure can change with **zero volume change** because $dC/dt \neq 0$ — this is the **signature** of the variable capacitor. - ⭐ **Time Constant:** $\tau = RC$. At $t = \tau$, system is $\approx 63.2\%$ complete; at $t = 3\tau$, $\approx 95\%$ complete. - ⭐ **Stroke Volume:** $SV = EDV - ESV$. **Ejection Fraction:** $EF = SV / EDV \approx 0.55-0.70$ (normal). - ⭐ **Cardiac Output:** $CO = HR \times SV \approx 5 \text{ L/min}$ at rest. - ⭐ **Frank-Starling Mechanism:** $SV \propto EDV$ — emerges naturally from the model as $V_{EDV}$ increases, $C_{sys}$ decreases more during contraction. - ⭐ **Suga-Sagawa Elastance Concept:** Inverse of compliance, $E(t) = P(t)/(V(t) - V_0)$, is the modern time-varying formulation. - ⭐ **Engineering Utility:** Foundation of lumped-parameter cardiovascular simulators, LVAD design, pacemaker testing, and HFpEF diagnosis. - ⭐ **Exam Pitfall #1:** Forgetting the product rule in the variable-compliance derivation. - ⭐ **Exam Pitfall #2:** Treating $C$ as a constant when the problem specifies "variable" — always re-derive with $C = C(t)$. - ⭐ **Exam Pitfall #3:** Using wrong units in $P \cdot C$ product (must give mL when $P$ in mmHg and $C$ in mL/mmHg). - ⭐ **Conversion Constant:** $1 \text{ mmHg} \cdot \text{mL} = 0.133 \text{ J}$ — useful for stroke-work calculations.
